artists.movement: Pop Art · Ben-Day Dots · Comic Book
artists.bio: Born in New York City, Lichtenstein studied at the Art Students League before WWII service interrupted his training. After Ohio State University and teaching positions, he began in the late 1950s with Abstract Expressionist-influenced work before abruptly pivoting around 1961 to comic-book imagery. Where Warhol chose celebrity and consumer goods, Lichtenstein chose commercial printing — specifically the Ben-Day dot process used in cheap colour printing, enlarged and hand-applied to canvas. The 'romance comics' series, the war comics, the brushstroke paintings that turn the AbEx gesture into a printed cliché — all probe the distance between original and reproduction. His later work systematically parsed the history of modernism: Monet's haystacks, Picasso's cubism, Art Deco, Surrealism — each rendered in his flat, dot-screened vernacular. He produced one of the most coherent and formally rigorous bodies of work in American Pop Art and commanded premium prices throughout his career.

famous_works.title: Whaam!
famous_works.notes: A diptych reproducing a panel from DC Comics' All-American Men of War. Two metres wide. Now in the Tate Modern collection — the apotheosis of the war comic source material.

famous_works.title: Drowning Girl
famous_works.notes: A single panel from a romance comic. The woman's thought bubble — 'I don't care! I'd rather sink — than call Brad for help!' — is the complete text. One of the most debated works in Pop Art.

famous_works.title: Brushstroke
famous_works.notes: A large brushstroke rendered in Ben-Day dots. The gesture of action painting turned into a printed cliché — the most economical critique of Abstract Expressionism ever made.

famous_works.title: Reflections on Minerva
famous_works.notes: From the Reflections series, where printed imagery appears to reflect in glass or mirror. Meta-commentary on the relationship between image, reproduction, and surface.

lesser_known_works.title: Water Lily Pond with Reflections (Study)
lesser_known_works.notes: A late work from the Monet Haystack series — Monet's Impressionist shimmer flattened to dots and primary colours. Art history as subject matter and formal problem simultaneously.

lesser_known_works.title: Interior with Waterlilies
lesser_known_works.notes: Combines domestic interior with Monet's water-lily surface. The intersection of two pictorial traditions — modernist abstraction and decorative illusionism — made explicit.

lesser_known_works.title: The Ring
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work from the romance comic period. Less reproduced than Drowning Girl but structurally important — the source material still visible through the transformation.

artists.bio: Born in Matsumoto, Nagano, Kusama experienced hallucinatory visions from childhood — flowers speaking, patterns proliferating — which she began to externalise through drawing and painting. She studied at the Kyoto Municipal School of Arts and Crafts before moving to New York in 1957 with encouragement from Georgia O'Keeffe. Her Infinity Net paintings — large canvases covered edge-to-edge with obsessive, repetitive arcs — were among the most radical abstract paintings in New York by 1960, yet she received minimal critical attention compared to her male peers. Her happenings and installation works addressed the body, sexuality, and self-annihilation with a directness that influenced Fluxus, Claes Oldenburg, and others directly. She returned to Japan in 1973, voluntarily entering a psychiatric institution in Tokyo where she has lived and worked since. The late Infinity Mirror Rooms have become cultural phenomena; her pumpkin sculptures command the largest auction prices and the longest museum queues worldwide.

famous_works.title: Infinity Nets
famous_works.notes: The foundational series. Large canvases of repetitive arcs creating net-like patterns that extend beyond the picture plane in all directions — the earliest works in this mode predate the New York works.

famous_works.title: Infinity Mirror Room — Phalli's Field
famous_works.notes: A room of soft sculptures of white phalli with red dots, reflected in mirrors to apparent infinity. The first Infinity Mirror Room, installed in New York. Body, compulsion, and infinity collapsed into an environment.

famous_works.title: Pumpkin
famous_works.notes: The large yellow pumpkin covered in black dots installed on a pier in Naoshima, Japan. The most reproduced image in her late career — domesticity and monumentality fused.

lesser_known_works.title: Walking Piece
lesser_known_works.notes: A happening in Central Park in which Kusama and participants wore polka-dot clothing and moved through the public space. Photography and performance as artistic medium before it was named.

lesser_known_works.title: Accumulation No. 1
lesser_known_works.notes: An armchair upholstered entirely in white fabric soft penises. The compulsive proliferation of a single form — the logic of the Infinity Nets translated into three dimensions.

lesser_known_works.title: Dots Obsession — Infinity Mirrored Room
lesser_known_works.notes: Polka-dot spheres suspended from the ceiling, reflected to infinity. Less well-known than the LED Infinity Rooms of the 2010s but formally more rigorous.

top_ratio_lot.title: High Heels for Going to Heaven

artists.movement: Pop Art · West Coast · Language in Painting
artists.bio: Born in Omaha and raised in Oklahoma City, Ruscha moved to Los Angeles in 1956 to study at Chouinard Art Institute (now CalArts). He worked as a commercial graphic designer while developing his art practice — a double life whose formal tension never resolved but became the content of his work. His word-and-image paintings use commercial typography and signage as pictorial language: the Standard station burning, the Hollywood sign, gas stations documented with documentary flatness. Twenty-six Gasoline Stations (1963) is among the first artists' books in the modern sense — photographs of mundane subject matter presented without commentary or hierarchy. His later atmospheric word paintings place language — 'HONK,' 'BOSS,' 'ACTUAL SIZE' — against ambiguous atmospheric grounds, activating meaning against backdrop. He remains active in Los Angeles and is the most institutionally embedded living practitioner of West Coast Pop.

famous_works.title: Standard Station, Amarillo, Texas
famous_works.notes: The Standard Oil station rendered in diagonally dramatic commercial perspective. Diagonal motion versus static architecture: American drive-through culture as formal proposition.

famous_works.title: Twenty-six Gasoline Stations
famous_works.notes: An artist's book of 26 photographs of gas stations along Route 66. One of the founding documents of Conceptual art's use of documentary structure.

famous_works.title: Large Trademark with Eight Spotlights
famous_works.notes: The 20th Century Fox logo rendered in monumental scale. Corporation as monument, emblem as painting.

famous_works.title: OOF
famous_works.notes: Three letters on yellow ground. Meaning activated entirely by the reader's projection. MoMA acquired it in 1969 — the canonical word painting.

lesser_known_works.title: Actual Size
lesser_known_works.notes: A can of Spam in the lower right corner, the words 'ACTUAL SIZE' above. The joke about painting's relationship to reality is also a formal proposition.

lesser_known_works.title: News, Mews, Pews, Brews, Stews & Dues
lesser_known_works.notes: A book of colour screenprints — each a single word with suffix variation. Language as pure visual material, sound-similarity as compositional structure.

lesser_known_works.title: The Back of Hollywood
lesser_known_works.notes: The Hollywood sign from behind — the mechanical infrastructure visible, the facade stripped. Los Angeles as industry of image-making, literally reversed.

top_lot.title: That Was Then This Is Now

artists.bio: Born in New York to a Supreme Court justice, Frankenthaler studied under Hans Hofmann and Rufino Tamayo before arriving at her singular voice. In 1952, aged 23, she poured thinned paint onto raw unprimed canvas on her studio floor — the soak-stain technique — making colour integral to the support rather than sitting on it. Mountains and Sea (1952) was shown once, forgotten for years, then seen by Morris Louis and Kenneth Noland, who credited it as the catalyst for Colour Field painting. She was meticulous in the studio and resistant to the action-painter mythology. Married to Robert Motherwell from 1958 to 1971. Her late career yielded vast atmospheric works of near-pure chromatic intensity — the Arden and Eastern Light series — that consolidated her position as the most formally rigorous painter of her generation.

famous_works.title: Mountains and Sea
famous_works.notes: The founding gesture of Colour Field painting. Shown once, then stored; only its impact on Louis and Noland revealed its importance retroactively.

famous_works.title: Other Generations
famous_works.notes: A pivotal large-scale work demonstrating the expanded possibility of the stain beyond Mountains and Sea.

famous_works.title: Flood
famous_works.notes: A horizontal canvas of saturated blues and greens — the stain technique at its most atmospheric and emotionally sustained.

famous_works.title: Madame Butterfly
famous_works.notes: One of the greatest late works. A monumental canvas of vermilion and ochre in which the accumulated experience of fifty years is compressed into apparently effortless gesture.

lesser_known_works.title: Troilus
lesser_known_works.notes: A dense, multi-layered work that shows the tension between the poured method and deliberate compositional control.

lesser_known_works.title: Interior Landscape
lesser_known_works.notes: Combines stain with opaque passages — an early experiment with the limits of the technique she had established.

lesser_known_works.title: The Human Edge
lesser_known_works.notes: Less reproduced than Flood but structurally more complex: the colour bands force the eye to read the canvas as both flat field and receding space.

artists.bio: Born in Wyoming, Pollock trained under Thomas Hart Benton and worked for the Federal Art Project before Jungian analysis redirected him toward automatism and mythological imagery. The move to Springs, Long Island in 1945 and a barn studio freed him to pour. Working on canvas laid flat, he moved around and across the surface pouring and dripping industrial enamel, creating allover fields with no hierarchy, no fixed orientation. Clement Greenberg championed him; Hans Namuth photographed him in 1950, making the studio practice as famous as the paintings. The drip period (1947–50) ended abruptly; the subsequent black paintings are darker and more figurative. Alcoholism intensified. He died at 44 in a drunk-driving accident near his Long Island home. The mythology — the myth of the American original, the doomed genius — is structurally embedded in the prices.

famous_works.title: Full Fathom Five
famous_works.notes: An early poured work containing embedded physical detritus — cigarette butts, nails, a key — in the paint surface. Now in MoMA's collection.

famous_works.title: Number 31
famous_works.notes: One of the largest drip paintings, over five metres wide. The allover field at its most expansive and gravitationally assured.

famous_works.title: Lavender Mist (Number 1)
famous_works.notes: The most atmospheric of the drip paintings. Mist-coloured webs of paint suggest landscape without depicting it. National Gallery of Art, Washington.

famous_works.title: Blue Poles
famous_works.notes: The most controversial late work — eight blue vertical bars interrupt the dripped field. Purchased by the Australian government in 1973 for $2M, a scandal at the time.

lesser_known_works.title: The Moon-Woman
lesser_known_works.notes: Pre-drip figurative work drawing on Jungian archetypes. Shows the mythological strand that was never entirely absent even from the poured works.

lesser_known_works.title: Stenographic Figure
lesser_known_works.notes: A Mirò-influenced automatist composition. Important as evidence of the Surrealist lineage that Pollock absorbed and then violently exceeded.

lesser_known_works.title: Eyes in the Heat
lesser_known_works.notes: A transitional work where the impasto brushwork begins to cede to the poured gesture. The 'eyes' — circular forms — are among the last figurative traces.

artists.bio: Born in Chicago to a surgeon father and a poet mother who published in Poetry magazine, Mitchell studied at the Art Institute of Chicago and arrived in New York in 1947. She became part of the Cedar Tavern world but was consistently marginalised in critical accounts centred on the male abstract expressionists — a disparity between her critical standing and her market prices that persisted for decades. She moved to Paris in 1959, later settling in Vétheuil, the village where Monet had painted in the 1870s, a proximity she acknowledged obliquely. Her monumental polyptychs of the 1970s and 1980s — La Grande Vallée (1983–84), Chord (1987) — are among the most sustained achievements of late Abstract Expressionism, combining gestural intensity with a structural rigour that her male contemporaries rarely matched. Market revaluation began seriously in the 2010s and accelerated sharply after her 2021 retrospective at SFMOMA and the Guggenheim.

famous_works.title: Hemlock
famous_works.notes: A dense, agitated early painting named after the tree outside her New York studio. Establishes the memory-of-landscape approach that would define her mature work.

famous_works.title: George Went Swimming at Barnes Hole, But It Got Too Cold
famous_works.notes: The title signals her method: paintings encode remembered rather than observed experience. Among the most important second-generation AbEx works.

famous_works.title: La Grande Vallée XIV
famous_works.notes: Part of a sustained series named after the valley near Vétheuil. The polyptych format opens the painting to temporal extension — the work takes time to read.

famous_works.title: Chord
famous_works.notes: A triptych of extraordinary chromatic control. Yellow and blue fields generate a visual chord — the musical analogy is structural, not decorative.

lesser_known_works.title: Bracket
lesser_known_works.notes: A late diptych that shows the synthesis Mitchell achieved between gestural mark-making and architectural spatial organisation.

lesser_known_works.title: Sunflowers
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the last major series, made in failing health. The sunflower motif absorbs Van Gogh without quoting him — the yellow-black opposition is structural.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Black)
lesser_known_works.notes: A rare near-monochrome work that tests whether the energy of her gesture survives the removal of colour. It does.

artists.bio:
Yoshitomo Nara was born in Hirosaki, Aomori Prefecture, Japan in 1959 and studied at the Aichi Prefectural University of Fine Arts, subsequently spending twelve years in Germany at the Kunstakademie Düsseldorf before returning to Japan. His combination of Japanese manga and pop culture sensibility with a European training in drawing and painting, and his personal experience of being a foreigner in both cultural contexts, has shaped a practice of considerable emotional depth.

Nara's signature images are the children: large-headed, small-bodied figures with intense, often hostile or sullen expressions whose apparent cuteness is undercut by an emotional charge that ranges from vulnerability to menace. These figures — painted in flat, saturated colour — occupy simple grounds or minimal environments, their psychological states encoded in expression and posture with a precision that belies the apparent simplicity of the means. The works draw on both Japanese anime aesthetics and Western Pop Art while being reducible to neither.

Nara is also a prolific draughtsman, and his drawings maintain the same emotional directness as his paintings in a more intimate and exploratory register. He is represented by Pace Gallery and his work has been exhibited at major institutions worldwide, achieving significant auction prices. His practice connects with audiences who respond to his figures' emotional directness with remarkable immediacy.

famous_works.title: Knife Behind Back
famous_works.notes: A painting of Nara's characteristic child figure whose title — revealed only retrospectively — adds a menacing dimension to the apparent innocence of the depicted form.

famous_works.title: Little Sprout
famous_works.notes: A small sculptural figure whose combination of childlike form and sullen expression embodies the emotional ambivalence that characterises Nara's most resonant work.

famous_works.title: Midnight Rider
famous_works.notes: A large painting of a figure in nocturnal setting that demonstrates the full development of Nara's pictorial language across multiple decades.

lesser_known_works.title: A to Z (Collaboration)
lesser_known_works.notes: A collaborative installation project with the graphic design group Graf in which Nara's figures inhabit purpose-built architectural environments.

lesser_known_works.title: Star Lake series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works in which the outdoor natural setting of the Aomori region is incorporated into Nara's characteristic figuration.

top_lot.title: It's a Cold Day 寒冷的一天

top_ratio_lot.title: It's a Cold Day 寒冷的一天

artists.bio: Born in Concord, New Hampshire, Condo studied music and art before moving to New York in the early 1980s, where he worked briefly in Andy Warhol's Factory before establishing his own direction. He coined 'artificial realism' to describe his practice: figures derived from art-historical sources — Velázquez, Goya, Picasso, Rubens — are distorted, fragmented, and recombined in configurations that are simultaneously comic, disturbing, and technically accomplished. The faces are wrong in specific ways: features displaced, mouths enlarged, eyes asynchronous, foreheads compressed — a vocabulary of psychological disturbance rendered in traditional oil technique. He has lived and worked in London, Paris, and New York. His work is collected by Jay-Z and Kanye West (who commissioned his album artwork), and he commands consistently strong institutional prices. His technical virtuosity — the old-master surface over grotesque imagery — is the formal strategy: beauty and abjection held in the same gaze.

famous_works.title: The Fallen Butler
famous_works.notes: A large vertical canvas of a disintegrating figure — parts of the body in different states of dissolution. One of the defining works of Condo's mature period.

famous_works.title: Crazy Face
famous_works.notes: A portrait head in which multiple faces occupy the same skull simultaneously. Cubism's simultaneous viewpoints pushed into the register of psychological multiplicity.

famous_works.title: Sad Clown
famous_works.notes: From the Clown series — figures from commedia dell'arte traditions rendered with 17th-century technical authority and 21st-century psychological violence.

lesser_known_works.title: The Popeye Show
lesser_known_works.notes: Condo's engagement with cartoon characters — Popeye's physiognomy absorbed into his grotesque language. The border between the childlike and the disturbing is the subject.

lesser_known_works.title: The Big Bang Theory
lesser_known_works.notes: An early career work showing the development of the multiple-figure canvas before it reached the density of the mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Puppet Show
lesser_known_works.notes: Figures as marionettes — agency and control as explicit themes. The puppeteer's hand is absent but structurally present in every distorted pose.

artists.bio:
Anish Kapoor was born in Mumbai in 1954 and moved to London in the early 1970s, studying at Hornsey College of Art and the Chelsea School of Art. He came to prominence in the early 1980s with installations in which simple geometric forms — mounds, cones, hemispheres — were coated in pure pigment powder, often Indigo blue or red, blurring the boundaries between object, environment, and perception. The works seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it, creating voids that appeared to pull the viewer's gaze inward.

This investigation of void, depth, and the limits of visual cognition has remained central to Kapoor's practice across vastly different scales and materials. His reflective steel sculptures — concave dishes, distorting mirrors, the enormous Cloud Gate in Chicago's Millennium Park — turn the viewer's own image back as a distorted reflection, making self-perception the subject of the work. His wax-projectile machines, biological-seeming masses, and controversial acquisition of exclusive rights to Vantablack pigment have each provoked sustained critical debate.

Kapoor won the Turner Prize in 1991 and has been commissioned for major public installations worldwide. He is represented by Lisson Gallery and Gladstone Gallery, and his practice stands as one of the most sustained investigations in contemporary art of how sculpture can engage the body, perception, and the phenomenology of space.

famous_works.title: Cloud Gate
famous_works.notes: The enormous bean-shaped reflective steel sculpture in Chicago's Millennium Park, which distorts the city's skyline and the viewer's own image in its polished surface.

famous_works.title: My Red Homeland
famous_works.notes: A massive rotating mechanical arm that slowly ploughs a field of red wax in a circular trough, creating and destroying a blood-red landscape over time.

famous_works.title: Marsyas
famous_works.notes: A monumental PVC installation filling the Turbine Hall of Tate Modern with three steel rings and a skin of PVC stretched between them, creating a vast interior vessel.

lesser_known_works.title: 1000 Names
lesser_known_works.notes: An early installation of pigment-coated geometric forms on the floor and wall, establishing the void and pigment explorations that underpin Kapoor's subsequent practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Ghost
lesser_known_works.notes: A concave alabaster form that creates an interior void of uncanny depth, one of the key works in Kapoor's investigation of stone as a medium for constructing absence.

artists.bio: Born in Bradford, Yorkshire, Hockney won every prize at the Royal College of Art (1959–62) before moving to Los Angeles in 1964, drawn by the light, the swimming pools, and the openness. His California paintings of the mid-1960s — the pool series, the portraits of friends and collectors — used flat, clear light and compositional calm to depict a world of pleasure without irony. The domesticity was the content. His Cubist photo collages (the Joiners, 1982–83) were a rigorous investigation of how a moving eye perceives space: hundreds of photographs assembled into composite images that represent time as well as space. His more recent work — iPad drawings of Yorkshire landscapes, the Arrival of Spring in Woldgate (2011) — pursues the same perceptual problem across new media without anxiety. He is the most celebrated living British painter and commands institutional prices across all major formats.

famous_works.title: A Bigger Splash
famous_works.notes: Two weeks to paint the palm trees and architecture; thirty minutes to paint the splash. The paradox of depicting instantaneous movement through laborious technique. Tate Modern.

famous_works.title: Mr and Mrs Clark and Percy
famous_works.notes: A double portrait of the fashion designers Ossie Clark and Celia Birtwell. The compositional inversion — the wife seated, the husband standing — disturbs the conventional power dynamic.

famous_works.title: Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures)
famous_works.notes: A boy looks down at a figure swimming beneath him. Made in a feverish 48-hour session the day before the exhibition opening. Sold for $90.3M in 2018 — the highest price for a living artist at the time.

lesser_known_works.title: The Skater (Kasmin)
lesser_known_works.notes: A photo collage from the Joiners series. The gallery owner Lawrence Kasmin is shown from multiple angles simultaneously — the static photograph made temporally complex.

lesser_known_works.title: Sun on the Pool, Los Angeles April 13, 1982
lesser_known_works.notes: A pool painting from the later Los Angeles period. The light is more forensic, less atmospheric than the 1960s pool works — a different account of the same subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Garrowby Hill
lesser_known_works.notes: A Yorkshire road winding over the wolds — the return to English landscape before the major Yorkshire series. Less famous than the Arrival of Spring but more quietly remarkable.

top_lot.title: Green Pool with Diving Board and Shadow (Paper Pool 3)

artists.bio: Born Andrew Warhola in Pittsburgh to Slovak immigrant parents, Warhol studied at Carnegie Mellon before becoming the most successful commercial illustrator in New York (his blotted-line drawings for I. Miller shoes were ubiquitous by the late 1950s). The transition to fine art was a deliberate repudiation of the expressive myth: Campbell's soup cans, Brillo boxes, death-and-disaster car crashes, electric chairs, Marilyn Monroe in Day-Glo series. The silk-screen process removes the artist's hand; the seriality drains the image of expressiveness; the subject matter — celebrity, death, consumer goods — makes the works philosophical propositions about contemporary life. The Factory at 231 East 47th Street employed collaborators ('superstars') who did much of the work. Shot by Valerie Solanas in 1968 and nearly killed. He worked prolifically until his sudden death from a post-surgical complication in 1987. The brand — the name, the signature, the numbered edition — is what the market prices.

famous_works.title: Campbell's Soup Cans
famous_works.notes: Thirty-two canvases displayed in a grid at Ferus Gallery, Los Angeles. The gallery placed them on shelves, as in a supermarket. The installation is the argument.

famous_works.title: Gold Marilyn Monroe
famous_works.notes: Marilyn's face centred in a gold field — a devotional object, a Byzantine icon. Made three months after her death. MoMA collection.

famous_works.title: Eight Elvises
famous_works.notes: Serial Elvis figures in cowboy posture on silver ground. Sold for $100M in 2008. The repetition that drains and then reinvests the image with uncanny intensity.

lesser_known_works.title: Tunafish Disaster
lesser_known_works.notes: A death-and-disaster work based on a newspaper photograph of women who died from contaminated tuna. Warhol's engagement with anonymous mortality alongside celebrity mortality.

lesser_known_works.title: Oxidation Painting
lesser_known_works.notes: Made by urinating (by Warhol and collaborators) onto copper-primed canvas to produce chemical oxidation patterns. The Action Painting parody fully literalised.

lesser_known_works.title: Shadows
lesser_known_works.notes: A series of 102 canvases meant to be shown together — abstract, near-monochromatic, their source a photograph of a studio shadow. Warhol called them 'disco décor'.

top_lot.title: Clockwork Panda Drummer 發條熊貓鼓手

artists.bio:
Zhou Chunya was born in Chongqing in 1955 and studied at the Sichuan Fine Arts Institute and at the Kunstakademie in Kassel, Germany. His encounter with European Expressionism during his German studies was formative, and he synthesised this with Chinese painting traditions to develop the practice for which he is internationally known: large-scale expressionist paintings centred on recurring subjects — rocks, peach blossoms, nude figures, and above all the Tibetan Mastiff dog — executed with raw gestural energy and an intense, personal palette.

Zhou's Green Dog paintings, begun in the 1990s and featuring his beloved Tibetan Mastiff rendered in brilliant greens, became among the most recognisable Chinese paintings of their decade. The choice to paint the dog in non-naturalistic green reflects Zhou's integration of Expressionist colour with the formal traditions of Chinese painting in which colour often carries symbolic rather than descriptive function. His rock paintings engage directly with the Chinese literati painting tradition, updating its scholar-rock subject through an expressionist surface that insists on immediate physical energy alongside aesthetic contemplation.

Zhou has been exhibited internationally and has achieved major auction prices. He represents a generation of Chinese artists for whom European Expressionism and the traditional Chinese painting heritage were both active resources to be synthesised in a practice of individual formal identity.

famous_works.title: Green Dog series
famous_works.notes: Large expressionist paintings of Zhou's Tibetan Mastiff in brilliant non-naturalistic green that synthesise European Expressionist colour practice with the Chinese tradition of symbolic colour use.

famous_works.title: Rock series
famous_works.notes: Expressionist paintings of scholar rocks that update the Chinese literati painting tradition through gestural directness and the intense colour practice of Zhou's mature style.

famous_works.title: Peach Blossom series
famous_works.notes: Large-scale paintings of blossoming peach trees rendered in pink and gestural marks that celebrate seasonal flowering with a directness that recalls the energy of Expressionism within a Chinese natural symbolism.

lesser_known_works.title: Early figurative works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Zhou's formation period in Sichuan and Germany that demonstrate the development of his synthetic approach to Chinese and European painting traditions.

lesser_known_works.title: Nude series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of expressionist figure paintings demonstrating the range of Zhou's subject matter beyond his better-known dog and rock works.

top_ratio_lot.title: Green Dog No. 6 綠狗 六號

artists.bio: The most intellectually articulate of the Abstract Expressionists, Motherwell had studied philosophy at Stanford and Harvard before dedicating himself to painting. He served as the movement's primary editor and anthologist — The Dada Painters and Poets (1951) remains the essential sourcebook. His Elegies to the Spanish Republic, begun in 1948 and continued for over forty years across more than 150 paintings, are the defining sustained series of Abstract Expressionism: large black ovoids pressing against vertical bars in stark bipartite compositions. The Spanish Civil War was the occasion; the elegiac mode became his vehicle for all political tragedy. He was married to Helen Frankenthaler from 1958 to 1971. His Open series — canvases of single colour interrupted by a gestural rectangle — explored the paradox of painting's flatness through near-minimalist means. He was also a prolific printmaker and collagist who brought a European sensibility to the American scene.

famous_works.title: Elegy to the Spanish Republic No. 34
famous_works.notes: One of the most fully achieved of the Elegy series. The black ovoids and bars are at their most formally resolved — minimal in element, monumental in presence.

famous_works.title: At Five in the Afternoon
famous_works.notes: The first fully resolved Elegy, named after García Lorca's poem. Established the vocabulary that Motherwell would pursue for the rest of his life.

famous_works.title: The Voyage
famous_works.notes: A large horizontal canvas of nautical imagery refracted through collage logic. The journey as existential metaphor, rendered with paper and oil.

lesser_known_works.title: Je t'aime (with Gauloises)
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the smallest, most intimate works — a collage with a Gauloise cigarette package fragment. Motherwell's Frenchness, his Europeanness, compressed into a torn rectangle of blue paper.

lesser_known_works.title: Open No. 121
lesser_known_works.notes: From the Open series — a field of deep ochre interrupted by a single gestural rectangle. The simplest possible painting that is still a painting.

lesser_known_works.title: Drunk with Turpentine
lesser_known_works.notes: A late Elegy variant in which the gestural mark breaks free of the canonical oval-and-bar vocabulary — a rare instance of the series relaxing its formal discipline.

artists.bio:
Kohei Nawa was born in Osaka, Japan in 1975 and studied at Kyoto City University of Arts, where he later joined the faculty and established his practice. He has developed a sculptural and installation practice centred on the materiality of surface, skin, and the threshold between the animate and the inanimate, deploying a range of contemporary materials — glass spheres, expanding foam, oil and glycerin — in ways that generate formally compelling and conceptually rich works.

Nawa's PixCell series, begun in 1999, involves covering found objects — stuffed animals, skulls, deer taxidermy — with glass spheres of varying sizes, creating surfaces in which each sphere functions as a refractive lens, multiplying and distorting the object beneath. The works explore how the accumulation of surfaces — screens, skins, lenses — mediates and transforms the experience of objects, connecting material investigation to the contemporary ecology of mediated perception. His foam installations, in which expanding industrial foam is applied to create large organic forms, test the productive tension between control and contingency.

Nawa is the founder and director of Sandwich, a multi-disciplinary design collective based in Kyoto. He has exhibited at Taro Nasu Gallery in Tokyo and internationally, and his large-scale public installations have been commissioned for various architectural contexts.

famous_works.title: PixCell-Deer#24
famous_works.notes: A taxidermied deer whose entire surface is covered in glass spheres of varying sizes, each sphere functioning as a refractive lens that transforms and multiplies the object's appearance.

famous_works.title: Foam
famous_works.notes: A large installation in which expanding industrial foam is applied in massive quantities to create an organic, cave-like environment that explores the boundary between controlled material process and contingent form.

famous_works.title: Force
famous_works.notes: A work in which oil and glycerin liquids create dynamic fluid surfaces that shift and flow according to physical forces, making visible the invisible pressures that shape material existence.

lesser_known_works.title: Thread
lesser_known_works.notes: Works using thread applied to found objects that predate the PixCell series and demonstrate Nawa's early investigation of surface accumulation as a transformative process.

lesser_known_works.title: Direction
lesser_known_works.notes: A sculptural work exploring the materiality of directional force through the formal organisation of material in response to gravitational and centrifugal pressures.

top_lot.title: PixCell-Bambi #15 PIXCELL系列 - 小鹿斑比15號

top_ratio_lot.title: PixCell-Bambi #15 PIXCELL系列 - 小鹿斑比15號

artists.bio:
Adrian Ghenie was born in Baia Mare, Romania in 1977 and studied at the Cluj Academy of Fine Arts, becoming one of the founders of the influential Plan B Gallery in Cluj — a space that catalysed what came to be called the Cluj School, an energetic cluster of painters who brought Central European art world attention back to Romania in the early 2000s. Ghenie's own painting emerged from this milieu but rapidly developed an international profile grounded in its visceral engagement with twentieth-century history and its traumatic imagery.

His paintings fuse photographic source material — archival photographs of historical atrocities, portraits of ideological monsters, stills from mid-century cinema — with broad, smeared gestural passages that seem to eat into the image, creating faces half-dissolved by paint, rooms collapsing into abstraction. Darwin, Hitler, and Duchamp recur as figures precisely because they represent inflection points where ideas produced catastrophic or transformative consequences. Ghenie treats history as inherently illegible: the past is simultaneously insistent and ungraspable, like a photograph dissolving in developer fluid.

He has been exhibited widely including at the Venice Biennale, where he represented Romania in 2015, and at major auction at Christie's and Sotheby's where his works have achieved significant prices. Ghenie is represented by Hauser & Wirth and maintains studios in both Berlin and Barcelona.

famous_works.title: Pie Fight Interior 6
famous_works.notes: Part of a series reworking the pie-fight motif from a Three Stooges short, overlaying slapstick chaos onto architectures reminiscent of Nazi ceremonial halls.

famous_works.title: Self-Portrait as Charles Darwin
famous_works.notes: A monumental self-portrait in which the artist's features are subsumed into those of Darwin, meditating on the violence implicit in the idea of natural selection.

famous_works.title: The Fake Rothko
famous_works.notes: A painting depicting a figure confronting an abstract canvas, implicating questions of authenticity, authority, and the market mythology surrounding Abstract Expressionism.

lesser_known_works.title: Study for Sunflowers
lesser_known_works.notes: A smaller-scale work in which Van Gogh's iconic sunflowers are rendered with Ghenie's characteristically corrosive gestural surface, meditating on artistic inheritance.

lesser_known_works.title: The Collector
lesser_known_works.notes: An early canvas depicting a shadowed figure amid accumulated objects, establishing the claustrophobic interior spaces that recur throughout Ghenie's oeuvre.

artists.bio: Born into a family of sculptors — his father Alexander Stirling Calder and grandfather Alexander Milne Calder were both prominent — he trained as a mechanical engineer before studying at the Art Students League, New York. In Paris in the late 1920s he constructed his Cirque Calder, a miniature performance of wire figures, to which Mondrian, Léger, and Miró came. A visit to Mondrian's studio in 1930 — the primary colours, the rectangles — was, he said, the turning point. He began making kinetic sculptures driven by motors, then discovered that air itself could provide the motive force: the mobile was born. Duchamp named the moving works; Arp named the static ones 'stabiles.' He worked at every scale from jewellery to monumental public commissions (La Grande Vitesse, Grand Rapids, 1969). The wit and formal clarity of the basic vocabulary — coloured steel elements in balance — never failed him.

famous_works.title: Lobster Trap and Fish Tail
famous_works.notes: The first large mobile commissioned specifically for a museum space (MoMA). Steel wire and metal sheets in biomorphic shapes that move continuously.

famous_works.title: Big Red
famous_works.notes: A stabile-mobile in red — Calder's most iconic colour. Demonstrates the synthesis of stability and motion that defines the mature work.

famous_works.title: La Grande Vitesse
famous_works.notes: The first public art work commissioned under the US National Endowment for the Arts. Forty-three tons of red-painted steel in Grand Rapids, Michigan — the city adopted it as a symbol.

lesser_known_works.title: Calder's Circus
lesser_known_works.notes: The miniature performance work in wire, cork, and found objects. Not quite sculpture, not quite theatre — a proto-performance-art object that Calder performed himself.

lesser_known_works.title: Fish
lesser_known_works.notes: A small mobile in which the fish-like biomorphic elements seem genuinely aquatic. One of the most graceful domestic-scale works in the Calder catalogue.

lesser_known_works.title: The Brass Family
lesser_known_works.notes: A wire sculpture depicting an acrobatic family — precursor to the mobiles in its demonstration of balance, tension, and form made from line alone.

artists.bio: Born in Mesa, Arizona and raised in Long Beach, California, Thiebaud worked as a commercial illustrator and cartoon animator before studying at San José State and Sacramento State. He began painting food subjects in the late 1950s — cakes, pies, gumball machines, diner counters — with a formally rigorous attention to light, shadow, and the physical materiality of paint that distinguished his work from the cooler irony of East Coast Pop. The frosting on a layer cake is painted with thick, sensuous impasto; the display-case setting is handled with a Hopper-esque attention to commercial loneliness. He taught at UC Davis for decades, building an influential West Coast pedagogical tradition. His San Francisco cityscape paintings — extreme vertiginous perspectives down hillside streets — achieve a formally ambitious spatial organisation that goes well beyond the food work in structural ambition. He continued painting actively into his late nineties.

famous_works.title: Cakes
famous_works.notes: A row of identical cakes on a display counter. The seriality anticipates Minimalism; the impasto pleasure of the frosting is the counter-argument. Whitney Museum.

famous_works.title: Two Seated Figures
famous_works.notes: A departure from food subjects — two figures in folding chairs, the social awkwardness of commercial seating rendered with the same formal attention as the pastries.

famous_works.title: Steep Street
famous_works.notes: A San Francisco street plunging almost vertically from the picture plane. The spatial vertigo of the cityscape paintings — less famous than the food works but arguably more formally ambitious.

lesser_known_works.title: Jawbreaker Machine
lesser_known_works.notes: A candy machine rendered with the same clinical pleasure as the cake displays. The commercial object as still-life subject with a dignity Chardin would have recognised.

lesser_known_works.title: Ponds and Streams
lesser_known_works.notes: A late aerial landscape — Sacramento valley agricultural terrain viewed from above. The food work and the landscape work are formally related through their common grid-like structure.

lesser_known_works.title: Candy Counter
lesser_known_works.notes: A selection of wrapped candies in commercial display. Less reproduced than the cakes but the formal problem — seriality, colour, light — is treated with equal seriousness.

artists.bio: Born Moishe Shagal in Vitebsk, Belarus, to a Hasidic Jewish family, Chagall absorbed Cubism's spatial fragmentation in Paris (1910–14) while keeping the visual culture of his hometown — floating figures, green-faced fiddlers, lovers suspended above rooftops — as his permanent subject matter. He returned to Russia after WWI, directed the Vitebsk Art School, then fled the Bolshevik purges for Berlin and Paris. The Nazi invasion of France sent him to the United States (1941–48), where he designed sets and costumes for Stravinsky's Firebird. His late Provence years produced the biblical works (the 17 paintings now in the Nice Biblical Message Museum), Paris Opera ceiling (1964), and stained glass commissions for the Hadassah Hospital, Jerusalem and the UN building, New York. His late work shows consistent aesthetic decline but unfailing market strength: the iconic vocabulary — green donkeys, flying couples, Vitebsk rooftops — maintains collector demand regardless of quality.

famous_works.title: I and the Village
famous_works.notes: The great synthesis of Cubist structure and Eastern European folk imagery. A goat and a man face each other across a circular composition containing the memory of Vitebsk.

famous_works.title: The Birthday
famous_works.notes: Bella Rosenfeld (his future wife) floats above the floor bearing flowers. The levitation of joy — Chagall's most characteristic formal metaphor — rendered with intimate directness.

famous_works.title: White Crucifixion
famous_works.notes: Christ on the cross surrounded by scenes of Nazi persecution. The most politically direct of all Chagall's works and one of the few in which the floating figures serve an explicitly critical function.

lesser_known_works.title: The Cattle Dealer
lesser_known_works.notes: A more grounded work than the famous levitating canvases — figures and animals depicted with Fauvist flatness against a winter landscape. Less reproduced but structurally important.

lesser_known_works.title: Solitude
lesser_known_works.notes: A rabbi and a cow on a dark ground. Made in the year of Hitler's appointment as Chancellor — the mood is premonitory and the imagery stripped back to essentials.

lesser_known_works.title: The Tribe of Levi (Hadassah Window)
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the twelve stained glass windows for the Hadassah Hebrew University Medical Center. The medium (stained glass) suits Chagall's colour sensibility better than oil on canvas.

top_lot.title: Fleurs et fruits
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

top_ratio_lot.title: Panier de fruits ou Le panier de fleurs

artists.bio:
Firenze Lai was born in Hong Kong in 1982 and studied at the Chinese University of Hong Kong and the Royal College of Art in London. She works primarily in oil on canvas, developing a figurative practice that explores the emotional and social textures of contemporary Hong Kong life through densely coloured, psychologically charged scenes of everyday urban existence — crowded restaurants, transport, domestic interiors, moments of social interaction and withdrawal.

Lai's figures inhabit their environments with a compressed psychological intensity that recalls German Expressionism and the work of Edvard Munch, while the specific cultural context — the neon light of Hong Kong interiors, the density of urban space, the particular textures of Cantonese social life — is rendered with immediate specificity. Her colour is hot and discordant, the bodies of her figures animated by an emotional energy that verges on anxiety, but the works maintain a formal control that prevents expressionistic excess from dominating their psychological nuance.

She has exhibited at Edouard Malingue Gallery in Hong Kong and has attracted international critical attention as a significant voice in the growing recognition of Hong Kong's contemporary art scene. Her practice provides a richly specific account of social and emotional life in one of the world's most intensely urban environments.

famous_works.title: Congee Shop
famous_works.notes: A densely populated restaurant interior in which figures are compressed by the painting's frame and by social proximity, rendering the quotidian Hong Kong dining experience with charged psychological intensity.

famous_works.title: MTR
famous_works.notes: A painting of passengers in Hong Kong's Mass Transit Railway in which the anonymity and physical proximity of urban transport becomes a meditation on the emotional isolation within collective space.

famous_works.title: Friday Night
famous_works.notes: An urban nightlife scene in which artificial light, compressed figures, and discordant colour evoke the particular energy and unease of Hong Kong weekend social life.

lesser_known_works.title: Domestic Interior
lesser_known_works.notes: A small domestic scene demonstrating Lai's ability to render the psychological atmosphere of private space with the same expressionist intensity she brings to crowded public settings.

lesser_known_works.title: Waiting
lesser_known_works.notes: A figure alone in an urban setting whose stillness within the charged colour environment creates a meditation on solitude within the metropolis.

artists.bio: Born in San Mateo, California, Francis began painting while hospitalised for spinal tuberculosis contracted during WWII service. The enforced horizontal orientation — the ceiling above a bedridden patient — and the light filtering from above are legible in the all-white early compositions. He moved to Paris in 1950 and absorbed Monet's late water-lily paintings alongside Abstract Expressionism, producing luminous chromatic fields in which colour clusters toward the edges, leaving centres of white that appear to radiate light. He later introduced cellular blue and red grids that gave the colour a structural armature. He travelled extensively — Japan, Europe, California — and his late works engage more directly with gestural energy. He established the Lapis Press in Santa Monica in 1981, producing artist's books of considerable quality. His paintings are widely collected in Europe and Japan, where the white-field works with peripheral colour rings are among the most recognisable of all American post-war paintings.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Composition in Blue)
famous_works.notes: An early Paris work of almost pure blue on white — the colour field at its most atmospheric and luminous.

famous_works.title: Around the Blues
famous_works.notes: A large horizontal canvas in which blue and red cells surround a central emptiness. The most structural of the colour-field works; a direct influence on Japanese Gutai artists.

famous_works.title: Summer No. 1
famous_works.notes: Yellow and orange saturate the canvas to the edges while the centre remains open. The light emanates from the void — the most characteristic compositional logic of the mature Francis.

lesser_known_works.title: The Whiteness of the Whale
lesser_known_works.notes: Named after the Moby-Dick chapter. One of the few works with a literary title — it suits the white-field paintings better than any formal description.

lesser_known_works.title: Big Red
lesser_known_works.notes: The early work of red cells on white, before the compositional logic of peripheral colour was fully established. More aggressive and less atmospheric than the mature work.

lesser_known_works.title: Shining Back
lesser_known_works.notes: An almost black canvas with peripheral red and blue flares. Francis's engagement with darkness as a chromatic problem rather than an absence.

artists.bio: Born in Chicago, Johnson studied at Columbia College Chicago and the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. He works across photography, film, painting, sculpture, and installation, using materials with specific cultural and historical resonance: shea butter, black soap, tropical plants, mirrored tiles, books, wax. His practice investigates Black American identity, the history of trauma and beauty, the relationship between the domestic and the cosmological. The Shea Butter and Wax paintings combine these materials in sgraffito-like surfaces that have both Expressionist physicality and archival reference. He has been consistently collected by major US institutions and his market has grown substantially since the mid-2010s. His films and public works extend the same investigation into temporal and environmental dimensions.

famous_works.title: Anxious Men
famous_works.notes: Agitated male faces drawn in black wax and oil stick on mylar tiles arranged in grids. The anxiety of the title is made structural in the jagged, pressured mark-making.

famous_works.title: The Hikers
famous_works.notes: A large mural-scale work combining books, plants, shea butter, and wax. The library and the garden as twin repositories of knowledge — the domestic archive as survival tool.

famous_works.title: Cosmic Slop
famous_works.notes: A video installation combining music (George Clinton's 'Cosmic Slop'), texts about Black American culture, and performance. The title connects George Clinton's cosmic black aesthetic to the material world.

lesser_known_works.title: The New Negro Escapist Social and Athletic Club
lesser_known_works.notes: A photographic series depicting a fictional 1930s social club — history as imaginative reconstruction. Johnson's engagement with the Harlem Renaissance as a model for contemporary Black cultural production.

lesser_known_works.title: Falling Man
lesser_known_works.notes: A photographic self-portrait series in a Superman falling pose — the heroic posture inverted. The superhero and the falling body: agency and vulnerability in the same gesture.

lesser_known_works.title: Nubian Monument
lesser_known_works.notes: A steel tower covered with tropical plants and books. The monument tradition (the column, the obelisk) claimed for Black cultural production.

artists.bio:
Izumi Kato was born in Shimane Prefecture, Japan in 1969 and studied at Musashino Art University in Tokyo. He has developed a distinctive figurative practice in painting, sculpture, and works on paper centred on a recurring cast of simplified, alien-seeming figures — large-headed, large-eyed, with simplified limbs and an ambiguous species identity — that hover between the human and the extraterrestrial, between the childlike and the uncanny.

Kato's figures are rendered in a manner that references both the naive directness of children's art and the formal sophistication of Outsider Art traditions, while his sculptural works — figures carved from wood and stone or modelled in resin — carry an additional dimension of material presence and physical vulnerability. The figures' simplicity is deceptive: their economy of form encodes complex emotional states, and their ambiguous ontological status — are they humans, spirits, aliens, toys? — opens up questions about identity, consciousness, and the boundary between the animate and the inanimate that connect to ancient animist traditions within Japanese culture.

Kato has been exhibited extensively internationally, including at Perrotin Gallery, and his work has attracted significant institutional and collector interest. He represents a singular contribution to contemporary figurative art that draws on both Japanese cultural tradition and global contemporary practice.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Large-Headed Figure)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale painting of one of Kato's characteristic alien figures in which the simplified form achieves an emotional presence that belies its formal economy.

famous_works.title: Sculpture (Stone Figure)
famous_works.notes: A carved stone figure in which Kato's formal vocabulary of simplified, embryonic humanity is translated into the permanent weight of mineral matter.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Group)
famous_works.notes: A multi-figure composition in which Kato's characters occupy a shared space, exploring the dynamics of community and isolation through their simplified, alien-human forms.

lesser_known_works.title: Works on paper series
lesser_known_works.notes: Drawings and works on paper that demonstrate the development of Kato's figurative vocabulary in a more intimate and exploratory format.

lesser_known_works.title: Collaborative installation
lesser_known_works.notes: A site-specific installation in which Kato's figures inhabit an architectural space in ways that extend their interrogation of presence, absence, and the boundaries of the human.

artists.bio: Born in Malden, Massachusetts, Stella studied painting at Princeton before arriving in New York in 1958. His Black Paintings (1958–60) — stripes of black enamel separated by thin lines of raw canvas, in formats that make the support's shape a determinant of the interior composition — became the founding works of Minimalist painting. 'What you see is what you see': the tautology as manifesto. He was 23. He subsequently moved through shaped canvases in aluminium and copper, then into polychrome metallic geometries, and eventually into elaborate relief sculptures and three-dimensional assemblages that introduced colour, gesture, and spatial complexity — a trajectory that some critics read as the exhaustion of Minimalism's premises and others as its liberation. He taught at Yale, and his published lectures Working Space (1986) are the most intellectually serious painter's statement of the late 20th century. He died in May 2024.

famous_works.title: Die Fahne Hoch!
famous_works.notes: From the Black Paintings series. The title ('The Flag on High') is the opening phrase of the Nazi Horst-Wessel-Lied — a confrontational acknowledgement of the work's historical moment.

famous_works.title: Talladega Three I
famous_works.notes: From the Circuits series — curvilinear shapes derived from a racetrack map. The entry into gesture and spatial complexity that divided critical opinion.

famous_works.title: Jarama II
famous_works.notes: Named after the site of a Spanish Civil War battle. The Circuit series works at its most dramatically spatial — painting escaping the flat surface.

lesser_known_works.title: Empress of India
lesser_known_works.notes: A massive V-shaped metallic canvas — four chevron elements that can be arranged in multiple orientations. The shaped canvas at its most radically anti-rectangular.

lesser_known_works.title: Conway I
lesser_known_works.notes: From the Irregular Polygon series — flat, colour-saturated, geometrically complex. The moment of transition between Minimalism's austerity and Stella's subsequent chromatic ambition.

lesser_known_works.title: Guadalupe Island (Caracara)
lesser_known_works.notes: From the Exotic Bird series — welded metal relief with painted surfaces. The three-dimensional turn that abandoned the flat support entirely.

artists.bio: Born in Le Cateau-Cambrésis to a grain merchant, Matisse began painting while recovering from appendicitis at 21. He studied under Gustave Moreau, absorbing Cézanne and Signac's pointillism before leading the Fauve explosion at the 1905 Salon d'Automne. Where Picasso fragments and analyses, Matisse integrates and harmonises: colour creates space, defines volume, constructs the picture's internal logic. The long partnership-rivalry with Picasso ran from 1906 to Matisse's death. He lived and worked in Nice from 1917 — the light, the Mediterranean, the odalisques. The late cut-outs, made when arthritis prevented painting, are among the most formally perfect works of the century: scissors replace brushes, negative space and positive form are held in equilibrium. The Chapelle du Rosaire in Vence (1948–51), designed entirely by Matisse at 80, is the most complete integration of his decorative intelligence into architectural space.

famous_works.title: La Joie de vivre
famous_works.notes: An idyllic scene of nude figures in an Arcadian landscape rendered in pure colour without modelling. Scandalized critics; purchased by Leo and Gertrude Stein.

famous_works.title: Dance (II)
famous_works.notes: Five nude figures in a circle against a flat blue and green ground. Commissioned by Sergei Shchukin — the most monumental statement of Matisse's colouristic programme.

famous_works.title: The Swimming Pool
famous_works.notes: A mural-scale cut-out in which blue paper figures swim around the walls of a room. One of the last and greatest works of the cut-out period.

lesser_known_works.title: The Moroccans
lesser_known_works.notes: A dark, severe painting — unusual in Matisse's oeuvre for its near-monochrome gravity. The Moroccan visits (1912–13) produced his most formally radical work.

lesser_known_works.title: Piano Lesson
lesser_known_works.notes: A boy at a piano in a grey-green room. More architecturally abstract than most Matisse — the spatial logic of the room treated almost like a Mondrian.

lesser_known_works.title: Bathers by a River
lesser_known_works.notes: Worked on for seven years — Matisse's most sustained formal struggle. Four flat figures against vertical bands of grey. The monumental scale and severity are uncharacteristic.

artists.bio: Born in Merion Square, Pennsylvania, Neel studied at the Philadelphia School of Design for Women and spent decades in relative poverty and obscurity painting the occupants of her Spanish Harlem neighbourhood: her children, lovers, Communist Party friends, pregnant women, the elderly, the recently deceased. She refused the Abstract Expressionist turn away from figuration on principle — human figures, human experience, were the only subjects that interested her — and the mainstream art world ignored her for it. Her portraits refuse to flatter: Andy Warhol is shown post-shooting, corseted and scarred, eyes closed; John Perreault lounges nude in an armchair with complete unselfconsciousness. The Metropolitan Museum of Art gave her a retrospective only in 1974, when she was 74. She received the National Medal of Arts from President Reagan shortly before her death. Posthumous revaluation has been dramatic and accelerating: she is now among the most sought-after American figurative painters.

famous_works.title: Andy Warhol
famous_works.notes: Warhol shown with the corseting and surgical scars from the 1968 shooting. The vulnerability is total; the directness is clinical. Now considered one of the great American portraits.

famous_works.title: Pregnant Woman
famous_works.notes: One of Neel's pregnant nude studies. The medical and the intimate held together without sentimentality — the pregnant body depicted with the attention usually reserved for heroic subjects.

famous_works.title: Joe Gould
famous_works.notes: Three figures of Joe Gould — the bohemian intellectual — each with multiple exposed penises. A compositionally radical portrait of masculine exhibitionism made a decade before Abstract Expressionism existed.

lesser_known_works.title: TB Harlem
lesser_known_works.notes: A Harlem man bedridden with tuberculosis. Neel's engagement with poverty and illness as painterly subjects in a period when American art was dominated by either Social Realism's optimism or burgeoning abstraction.

lesser_known_works.title: Rhoda Myers with Blue Hat
lesser_known_works.notes: An early portrait showing the psychological penetration that would define her mature work. Already the formula is clear: no flattery, no evasion, direct confrontation with the sitter's specific reality.

lesser_known_works.title: Last Sickness
lesser_known_works.notes: Neel's mother on her deathbed. The medical precision of the depiction is the form of tenderness Neel permits herself.

artists.bio: Born in London, the daughter of critic David Sylvester (biographer of Francis Bacon and Magritte), Brown studied at the Slade School of Fine Art before moving to New York in 1994. She emerged in the late 1990s with paintings that synthesised gestural abstraction with erotic figuration, taking visual source material from Rubens, Delacroix, and Ingres and dissolving representation into paint at the threshold of legibility. Her densely layered surfaces reward sustained looking: what reads at a distance as pure abstraction resolves on approach into figures, gardens, and hunting scenes. The Old Master technical skills — glazing, layering, colour temperature — are deployed to produce images at the edge of dissolution. She represents the contemporary reclamation of painterly virtuosity after decades of Conceptual dominance, a position that has proved commercially very strong. She is now one of the most collected figurative painters working today.

famous_works.title: Tits and Ass
famous_works.notes: An early canvas of erotic figuration in which the figures dissolve into pure painterly incident. The title is deadpan; the execution is seriously engaged with Rubens.

famous_works.title: The Fugitive
famous_works.notes: A large horizontal canvas of hunting figures in a landscape that becomes pure gesture at the edges. One of the defining works of the late-1990s figurative painting revival.

famous_works.title: Girl with a Martini
famous_works.notes: A single figure, the cocktail glass visible through a web of gestural marks. The title's banality and the painting's seriousness are the point.

lesser_known_works.title: Dead Butterfly
lesser_known_works.notes: A still-life subject treated with the same gestural density as the figure paintings. The vanitas tradition absorbed into the New York painting context.

lesser_known_works.title: Suddenly Last Summer
lesser_known_works.notes: A large diptych of figures in a landscape — the Tennessee Williams title signals the erotic charge and threatened violence that runs through Brown's figure compositions.

lesser_known_works.title: The Emperor of Ice-Cream
lesser_known_works.notes: Named after the Wallace Stevens poem. The connection between abstract pleasure and mortality — ice-cream's sweetness against the corpse in the corner — is the implied subject.

artists.bio:
Zhang Xiaogang was born in Kunming, Yunnan Province in 1958 and studied at the Sichuan Fine Arts Institute. He came to international prominence in the 1990s with his Bloodline: Big Family series — paintings of Chinese families depicted in the faded, sepia-toned manner of Cultural Revolution-era family photographs, the figures' faces expressionless and interchangeable, their familial relationships marked by thin red lines connecting them across the canvas.

These paintings constitute one of the most sustained engagements with collective memory and individual identity in post-Cultural Revolution Chinese art. The photographs on which Zhang based his images were the official and family portraits of the period — documents that enforced conformity and collectivity at the expense of individual expression. By translating them into painting while maintaining their faded, monochromatic quality, Zhang created works that are simultaneously memorial, critique, and meditation on how memory, identity, and political history are inscribed in the body.

Zhang Xiaogang is represented by Pace Gallery and has been exhibited at major institutions internationally. His auction prices have been among the highest achieved by any contemporary Chinese artist, and his practice is recognised internationally as a major contribution to the art of political memory and collective experience.

famous_works.title: Bloodline: Big Family No. 1
famous_works.notes: An inaugural painting in the series that would define Zhang's career, in which a family unit is depicted in Cultural Revolution photographic style with thin red lines marking the biological connections between its members.

famous_works.title: Amnesia and Memory
famous_works.notes: A large-scale work from Zhang's ongoing investigation of memory and identity in which the family photograph format is extended to explore the relationship between individual and collective historical experience.

famous_works.title: Green Wall
famous_works.notes: A series in which figures are presented against institutional green backgrounds derived from the wall colour of Cultural Revolution-era public buildings, investing architectural context with political memory.

lesser_known_works.title: Remnants of Time
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from a later series in which the family photograph format is combined with dreamlike and surrealist elements that expand Zhang's formal vocabulary.

lesser_known_works.title: Youthful Portraits
lesser_known_works.notes: Early paintings from before the Bloodline series that demonstrate Zhang's initial development of his figurative approach.

top_lot.title: Amnesia and Memory 失憶與記憶

top_ratio_lot.title: Amnesia and Memory 失憶與記憶

artists.bio: Born in Dublin, raised in London, Scully studied at Croydon College of Art, Newcastle University, and Harvard. He moved to New York in 1975 where he abandoned the rigorous hard-edge geometry of his earlier work and began developing the looser, more gestural stripe painting that defines his mature practice. His mature work is built from horizontal and vertical stripes of varying width, loosely painted in warm, earthy tones — ochre, burgundy, grey, blue — with considerable gestural energy beneath the geometric structure. He positions himself as heir to Rothko and Mondrian while insisting on a humanist warmth that he sees as their common ground: the stripe as a fundamental unit of visual order, the brushwork as the mark of a body moving in time. He has lived and worked in New York, Barcelona, and Munich. His work is widely collected by European and American institutions.

famous_works.title: Paul
famous_works.notes: Named for Paul Klee. A large canvas of warm horizontal stripes with an inserted vertical stripe panel — the 'wall of light' compositional logic first fully stated.

famous_works.title: Pale Fire
famous_works.notes: Named for the Nabokov novel. A canvas of greys and off-whites — Scully's most austere, the stripe reduced to tonal variation within a nearly monochrome field.

famous_works.title: Landline Blue Pale
famous_works.notes: From the Landline series — horizontal bands of colour that reference both landscape and the body's horizontal plane. The most atmospheric of the recent series.

lesser_known_works.title: Robe
lesser_known_works.notes: An early mature work in which the stripe is introduced as a full compositional language for the first time. The transition from hard-edge to painterly stripe is visible.

lesser_known_works.title: Backs and Fronts
lesser_known_works.notes: A massive work (20 panels) in which alternating vertical and horizontal stripes create a serial rhythm. One of the most ambitious of the early mature works.

lesser_known_works.title: Catherine
lesser_known_works.notes: A large painting named for his son's mother. The personal naming of abstract paintings — Scully's insistence on human content within abstract structure — is the method.

artists.bio:
Ha Chong-Hyun was born in Sancheong, South Korea in 1935 and studied at the Seoul National University College of Fine Arts. He became a founding member of the Avant-garde Association in Seoul in the 1960s and a central figure in the Dansaekhwa movement — a group of Korean painters who, from the early 1970s, developed approaches to painting centred on meditative processes of material application, surface, and the relationship between physical labour and pictorial result.

Ha's distinctive technique, developed in the 1970s and maintained with remarkable consistency since, involves pushing paint through the back of loosely woven hemp fabric to produce a surface that reveals simultaneously the push of material through the weave and the resulting frontal pattern of paint — a process he terms 'Conjunction'. The resulting works are simultaneously abstract and material, the grid of the fabric and the marks of pushing and pulling becoming the subject of the painting. The physical discipline of the process — methodical, labour-intensive, concentrated — reflects an approach to painting as meditative practice that draws on both Korean cultural tradition and an engagement with Western Minimalism.

Ha has been the subject of major international exhibitions as Dansaekhwa's global recognition has expanded in recent decades. His work is held by major museums in Korea and internationally, and he is recognised as one of the foundational figures of Korean abstraction.

famous_works.title: Conjunction 14-12
famous_works.notes: A large hemp-and-oil work in which paint pushed through the back of the canvas produces a frontal surface of regular, cellular marks that embodies Ha's decades-long investigation of material and process.

famous_works.title: Conjunction 77-5
famous_works.notes: An early Conjunction painting that established the definitive form of Ha's mature technique and demonstrated its formal authority.

famous_works.title: Conjunction 90-20
famous_works.notes: A mid-career work in which the material logic of the Conjunction technique is developed to a level of formal refinement and chromatic subtlety.

lesser_known_works.title: Avant-garde period works
lesser_known_works.notes: Early mixed-media and assemblage works from Ha's involvement in the Korean avant-garde before the development of his Conjunction technique.

lesser_known_works.title: Wire series
lesser_known_works.notes: Works incorporating wire and industrial materials from the transition period between Ha's early avant-garde practice and the development of the Conjunction method.

artists.bio: Born in Durham, North Carolina, Barnes played as a professional NFL offensive guard for the San Diego Chargers and New York Jets (1960–66) before dedicating himself to painting. His physical experience of the athlete's body — the specific mechanics of catching, blocking, running — shaped his visual language: elongated figures with closed eyes and extended limbs, straining in athletic and social ritual. The style is immediately recognisable: the elongated bodies derived from El Greco, the movement derived from lived athletic experience. His most famous work, Sugar Shack, appeared on the cover of Marvin Gaye's I Want You (1976) and as the closing credit painting of the television series Good Times. He was named Official Artist of the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics. His prices were modest in his lifetime; posthumous market discovery has been dramatic — two lots in the dataset sold at 5.9× and 8.1× estimate respectively.

famous_works.title: Sugar Shack
famous_works.notes: A dance-hall scene of elongated Black figures in joyful motion — used on the cover of Marvin Gaye's I Want You and in Good Times. The most reproduced image in Barnes's oeuvre.

famous_works.title: At Last
famous_works.notes: In the dataset at 8.1× estimate, $567k. A scene of dignified social gathering — the title and the painting both reference a long-deferred arrival.

famous_works.title: The Winning Shot
famous_works.notes: In the dataset at 5.9× estimate, $592k. A basketball player at the peak of a jump shot — athletic time frozen and extended in Barnes's characteristic elongated gesture.

lesser_known_works.title: The Bench Warmer
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work derived directly from Barnes's NFL experience. The athlete on the sideline — observing rather than acting — rendered with the same elongated urgency as the game itself.

lesser_known_works.title: Inner City Blues
lesser_known_works.notes: Urban figures in a street scene — the Marvin Gaye connection is not accidental. Barnes's neighbourhood painting equivalent to the album's social critique.

lesser_known_works.title: Rite of Spring
lesser_known_works.notes: A spring gathering — the title's art-historical resonance (Stravinsky, Nijinsky) absorbed into an African-American neighbourhood context.

artists.bio: Born Brian Donnelly in Jersey City, New Jersey, KAWS studied illustration at the School of Visual Arts in New York and worked as a cel animator for Disney before his career as a fine artist and commercial figure took over. He began in the mid-1990s by subverting bus-shelter advertisements in New York with his XX-eyed character interventions. He subsequently developed a vocabulary of cartoon-derived figures — most notably Companion, a Mickey Mouse variant with crossed-out eyes and exposed bone — that he deploys across painting, sculpture, toy design, fashion collaboration (Supreme, Uniqlo), and monumental inflatable installation. His work occupies the intersection of street art, Pop Art, and commercial product design. He commands exceptionally strong prices among younger collectors and in Asian markets particularly. His wooden and vinyl toys sell in editions of hundreds of thousands and have created a secondary collectibles market that operates in parallel with the fine art market.

famous_works.title: Companion (Passing Through)
famous_works.notes: A monumental sculpture of Companion in a 'thinking' pose — head on folded hands. The cartoon figure given the scale and material presence of traditional bronze sculpture.

famous_works.title: The KAWS Album
famous_works.notes: A repainting of The Beatles' Yellow Submarine album cover with KAWS characters replacing the original. Sold for HK$115.9M ($14.7M) in 2019 — a record for the artist.

famous_works.title: BFF
famous_works.notes: An inflatable sculpture deployed across urban waterfronts internationally — Hong Kong harbour, Seoul, Melbourne. The public art gesture on the largest scale.

lesser_known_works.title: Bus Shelter Interventions
lesser_known_works.notes: The original KAWS practice — advertising panels in New York, Paris, Tokyo altered with XX-eyed character additions. The document of this period is now studied as foundational street art.

lesser_known_works.title: At This Time
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting of two Companion figures, eyes X'd, in an embrace. The emotional register that KAWS brings to cartoon iconography — the childlike form carrying adult feeling.

lesser_known_works.title: Clean Slate
lesser_known_works.notes: A dark, dense canvas in which KAWS figures are partially obscured by heavy black paint — the erasure of the familiar image. Less pop-brightly cheerful than the canonical works.

artists.bio: Born in San José, California, the son of art historians, Tansey studied at the Art Center College of Design, Pasadena, and the Hunter College, New York. He developed a practice of hyperrealist painting rendered entirely in a single colour — usually a tonal monochrome of ochre or blue — depicting philosophical and art-theoretical allegories staged with photographic precision. His paintings are visual paradoxes: representationally skilled pictures whose subjects are the impossibility of representation. Action Painting II (1984) depicts Jackson Pollock and Clement Greenberg arguing about a painting that turns out to be a map of the French coastline. Triumph of the New York School (1984) shows a WWI surrender ceremony in which the French (European modernism) capitulate to the Americans (Abstract Expressionism). The single-colour technique is essential: it removes the pictorial seduction of colour and forces attention to the conceptual structure.

famous_works.title: Triumph of the New York School
famous_works.notes: American and French artists depicted as combatants in a WWI trench warfare scene. The 'surrender' of European modernism to American Abstract Expressionism — art history as military history.

famous_works.title: Action Painting II
famous_works.notes: Pollock and Greenberg on a cliff edge, a painting between them that turns out to be a map of France. The debates of Abstract Expressionism re-enacted as physical farce.

famous_works.title: Derrida Queries de Man
famous_works.notes: Two mountaineers on a rockface, one using a rope attached to the other's ice axe. A visual pun on deconstruction's intellectual dependencies — theory as mountain climbing.

lesser_known_works.title: The Innocent Eye Test
lesser_known_works.notes: A cow is brought before Paulus Potter's The Bull (1647) while art professionals observe its reaction. Vision, nature, and art-historical convention as simultaneous subjects.

lesser_known_works.title: Landscape with Figures
lesser_known_works.notes: A crowded monochrome landscape of figures that resolve on close inspection into books and words — the library as terrain. Less reproduced than the theory works but equally rigorous.

lesser_known_works.title: Picasso and Braque Arrive at the Gates
lesser_known_works.notes: Two aviators (Picasso and Braque) flying primitive planes above a landscape of text. Cubism as flight, language as ground.

artists.bio: Born in Rotterdam, de Kooning trained at the Rotterdam Academy of Fine Arts in the classical European tradition — life drawing, typography, commercial design — before stowing away to the United States in 1926. He worked as a house painter and commercial illustrator while developing his fine art practice in New York. His mature work holds representation and abstraction in perpetual, violent tension: the Women series (1950–53) generated enormous controversy — the aggressively distorted female figures seemed a rejection of both AbEx's non-figurative orthodoxy and conventional beauty. He continued oscillating between abstraction and figuration for decades. He moved to East Hampton in 1963, building a studio where the scale and physicality of his work expanded. Alzheimer's disease developed in his late career; the late ribbons of flowing colour are simpler than earlier work, and their status — made under diminished capacity — remains contested. He worked until 1990.

famous_works.title: Woman I
