famous_works.notes: Worked on and nearly destroyed multiple times over two years. The most contested American painting of the 1950s — abstract painting's refusal to accommodate the figure, or the figure's refusal to be refused.

famous_works.title: Excavation
famous_works.notes: A large vertical canvas of interlocking flesh-coloured forms that contain partial figurative references. Won first prize at the Art Institute of Chicago's 61st American Exhibition.

famous_works.title: Gotham News
famous_works.notes: A large abstract work in which newsprint is embedded in the paint surface. The city as gesture, the newspaper as texture.

lesser_known_works.title: Pink Angels
lesser_known_works.notes: A biomorphic work of the pre-Women period — flesh-coloured forms arranged with Surrealist automatism. The figuration is there but not yet confrontational.

lesser_known_works.title: Saturday Night
lesser_known_works.notes: A large-scale abstract work in which the energy of the Women series is channelled into pure painterly incident. Less dramatic than the Women paintings but more formally assured.

lesser_known_works.title: Door to the River
lesser_known_works.notes: The slap-and-drag technique at its most spatial — the mark simultaneously depicts and is the gate. The landscape is suggested through the quality of the gesture alone.

artists.bio: Born in Aschaffenburg, Kirchner studied architecture in Dresden before co-founding Die Brücke (The Bridge) in 1905 with Erich Heckel, Karl Schmidt-Rottluff, and Fritz Bleyl — a group dedicated to building from the art of the past a bridge to a new, vital present. He moved to Berlin in 1911, and the Berlin street scenes of 1913–15 — angular, crowded, erotically charged, painted in strident acid greens, magentas, and cadmium — are the most iconic images of Wilhelmine urban modernity's psychological underside. The figures on the Ku'damm are distorted and alienated; the streetwalkers on the Friedrichstrasse are both predatory and prey. WWI military service precipitated a mental and physical collapse from which he never fully recovered. He moved to Davos, painted the Alps with increasing formal abstractness, and managed a partial recovery before the Nazi designation of Expressionism as 'Degenerate Art' destroyed his standing. He killed himself in 1938.

famous_works.title: Street, Berlin
famous_works.notes: Elongated figures in furs crowd a wet street. The spatial compression, acid colour, and psychological unease are the movement's signature — and its most direct legacy for subsequent German and Austrian painting.

famous_works.title: Marzella
famous_works.notes: One of the Dresden period nudes — a young girl painted with raw urgency against a flat decorative ground. The influence of Edvard Munch and African sculpture simultaneously visible.

famous_works.title: The Drinkers
famous_works.notes: Four figures around a table, the wartime atmosphere compressing the composition into near-suffocating density. Kirchner's response to the imminent catastrophe.

lesser_known_works.title: Mountain Forest, Staffelalp
lesser_known_works.notes: A Davos alpine forest — the German Expressionist city nightmare replaced by Swiss landscape. The angularity persists but the colour lightens toward a tentative post-traumatic recovery.

lesser_known_works.title: Winter Moon Landscape
lesser_known_works.notes: A Davos snow scene with extraordinary flat blues — the landscape as psychological space, the cold as mood made form.

lesser_known_works.title: Fränzi before Carved Chair
lesser_known_works.notes: A seated girl before a decoratively carved chair. Kirchner's Brücke period at its most formally complex — the flat pattern of the chair competing with the figure.

top_lot.title: Sonnentänzerin
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Annie Morris was born in London in 1978 and trained at the Slade School of Fine Art and the École des Beaux-Arts in Paris. She came to sustained international attention in the mid-2010s with her 'Stack' sculptures — columns of balanced spheres cast in a variety of materials including iron, unfired earthenware, and silk — that became widely recognised for their combination of formal elegance, physical precariousness, and emotional resonance.

The Stack works emerged from a period of personal loss and were conceived as a response to grief: the stacked spheres represent accumulated days of survival, the precarious balance encoding the effort required to continue under conditions of sorrow. The forms are simultaneously ancient — recalling burial mounds, counting stones, ceremonial objects — and strikingly contemporary. Morris works across sculpture, painting, and works on paper, and her paintings of simplified, brightly coloured female figures have a directness and warmth that share the Stacks' quality of emotion held within formal discipline.

She has exhibited at Hauser & Wirth and Timothy Taylor Gallery and is collected by major institutions and private collectors internationally. Morris represents a strand of contemporary British sculpture that connects formal rigour with biographical and emotional content without sacrificing the formal ambition of the object.

famous_works.title: Stack Series
famous_works.notes: Monumental and intimate columns of balanced spheres in painted iron and earthenware, their precariousness encoding the emotional weight of grief and survival.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Woman)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale painting of a simplified female figure in which flat colour and bold outline produce an image of emotional directness.

famous_works.title: Nine Spheres
famous_works.notes: A stack sculpture in unfired earthenware that makes the material's fragility integral to the work's meaning, literalising the precariousness of emotional survival.

lesser_known_works.title: Small Stack
lesser_known_works.notes: An intimate-scale version of the Stack works that allows close inspection of the surface textures and colour relationships that give the larger works their power.

lesser_known_works.title: The Drawing Collection
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works on paper that extend Morris's formal vocabulary in the medium of drawing, demonstrating the breadth of her practice beyond sculpture.

top_lot.title: Stack 3, Cadmium Red 堆叠 3，鎘紅

top_ratio_lot.title: Stack 3, Cadmium Red 堆叠 3，鎘紅

artists.bio:
Yun Hyong-Keun was born in Cheongwon County, North Chungcheong Province, Korea in 1928 and studied at the Hongik University College of Fine Arts in Seoul. He became one of the central figures of the Dansaekhwa movement, developing a practice of monochrome and near-monochrome painting that has become one of the most internationally recognised Korean artistic traditions.

Yun worked primarily in burnt umber and ultramarine blue — colours he mixed and diluted to create tonally subtle grounds of extraordinary depth — on raw linen or cotton canvas, which he applied paint to and then rotated through the seasons, allowing the thinned oil to soak into the weave and create surfaces of gradual tonal modulation. The works appear simple — often divided into bands or rectangles of related tones — but reward sustained looking with an increasingly complex experience of colour, light, and material surface. His works have been compared to the meditative qualities of Korean celadon ceramics and ink painting while engaging fully with the Western abstract painting tradition.

Yun exhibited internationally throughout his career, with major exhibitions at galleries in Paris, New York, and London. Following his death in 2007, his estate has worked to preserve and promote his legacy, and major institutional retrospectives have confirmed his importance as a central figure in both Korean and international abstract art.

famous_works.title: Burnt Umber and Ultramarine Blue
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which the two signature colours of Yun's palette are juxtaposed in bands of tonal gradation that create a surface of meditative depth and material richness.

famous_works.title: Earth I
famous_works.notes: A monochrome painting in Yun's characteristic burnt umber in which the tonal modulation of a single colour creates a surface of extraordinary contemplative power.

famous_works.title: Conjunction
famous_works.notes: A large diptych in which two panels of related tones create a spatial dialogue that extends Yun's investigation of tonal gradation across an expanded format.

lesser_known_works.title: Early figure works
lesser_known_works.notes: Figurative paintings from Yun's early career that document the academic training from which his subsequent development toward monochrome abstraction departed.

lesser_known_works.title: Works on paper
lesser_known_works.notes: Smaller works on paper that demonstrate the development of Yun's tonal and material investigations in a more intimate and exploratory format.

artists.bio: Born in New York City, Rockwell studied at the Chase Art School, the National Academy of Design, and the Art Students League before becoming art director of Boys' Life magazine at 19. His association with the Saturday Evening Post (1916–1963) — 321 cover illustrations — made him the most widely reproduced American artist of the 20th century. His images of small-town American life, family ritual, and civic virtue were understood for decades as nostalgic sentimentality; his critical rehabilitation, beginning in the 1970s and accelerating with the 1999 retrospective, revealed a more complex and socially engaged practice. The Problem We All Live With (1964) — depicting Ruby Bridges walking to school escorted by federal marshals — is among the most politically direct images in American art. His technique is technically astonishing: the paintings are built on detailed preparatory drawings and photo sessions, the final surface of meticulous tonal control.

famous_works.title: Four Freedoms
famous_works.notes: Four paintings illustrating Roosevelt's Four Freedoms — Freedom of Speech, Freedom of Worship, Freedom from Want, Freedom from Fear. Published in the Saturday Evening Post and used in a US war bond tour that raised $132M.

famous_works.title: The Problem We All Live With
famous_works.notes: Ruby Bridges escorted by federal marshals to school in New Orleans. A work of direct political engagement that overturned the 'sentimental Americana' critical assessment of Rockwell.

famous_works.title: Triple Self-Portrait
famous_works.notes: Rockwell painting himself in the mirror, surrounded by self-portrait masters (Dürer, Rembrandt, Picasso, Van Gogh). A formally sophisticated meditation on the self-portrait tradition.

lesser_known_works.title: Southern Justice (Murder in Mississippi)
lesser_known_works.notes: Civil rights workers murdered in Mississippi, depicted in Rockwell's meticulous illustrational style. The most disturbing image in his catalogue — the technique of comforting normality applied to atrocity.

lesser_known_works.title: The Connoisseur
lesser_known_works.notes: A businessman in a bowler hat confronting what appears to be a Jackson Pollock drip painting. The joke about high and low culture — Rockwell's self-awareness of his position in the cultural hierarchy — is also a formal proposition.

lesser_known_works.title: After the Prom
lesser_known_works.notes: A teenage couple in formal dress at a diner counter. Rockwell's formal mastery is visible in the compositional triangulation — seemingly a simple scene, structurally rigorous.

artists.bio: Born in Brooklyn to a Haitian father and a Puerto Rican-American mother, Basquiat dropped out of school at 17 and began spraying SAMO© (Same Old Shit) tags on Lower Manhattan buildings with friend Al Diaz. Discovered by the art world at the 1980 Times Square Show, he was absorbed into the gallery system within two years and became the most celebrated artist of the 1980s New York Neo-Expressionist boom. His paintings combine street-art energy, graffiti aesthetics, African and Caribbean imagery, anatomy diagrams, historical figures, and aggressive textual elements — a dense, layered archive of Black American experience and art-historical reference. He was 21 when he had his first solo gallery show; 27 when he died of a heroin overdose. His collaboration and close friendship with Warhol was formative for both. His prices have become among the highest in contemporary art — Untitled (1982) sold for $110.5M in 2017.

famous_works.title: Untitled
famous_works.notes: Sold for $110.5M in 2017 — the record price for a Black artist. A skull-like face on a black ground, combining graffiti urgency with formal complexity.

famous_works.title: Hollywood Africans
famous_works.notes: Three figures labelled with text — Toxic, Samo, Hollywood Africans — against a yellow ground. The Black presence in Hollywood, in American culture, examined through inventory.

famous_works.title: In Italian
famous_works.notes: A large canvas combining anatomical drawings, text, and schematic figures. The body as diagram, anatomy as colonial inventory.

lesser_known_works.title: Dos Cabezas
lesser_known_works.notes: A double portrait of Basquiat and Warhol — painted the day after they met. The friendship and artistic relationship compressed into a single confrontational image.

lesser_known_works.title: Horn Players
lesser_known_works.notes: Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie depicted with textual annotations about jazz, anatomy, and race. The jazz genealogy as structural scaffold for the painting.

lesser_known_works.title: Riding with Death
lesser_known_works.notes: A skeleton astride a skeletal horse against red. Made months before his death — the premonitory imagery has been read as self-knowledge, though Basquiat worked with this vocabulary throughout his career.

artists.bio:
Mehdi Ghadyanloo was born in Tehran in 1981 and studied at Tehran University of Art, where he subsequently founded the Mural Painting department. He became known internationally for his large-scale trompe-l'oeil murals painted on the walls of Tehran buildings — works in which architectural illusions of windows, staircases, and fantastical spaces transform the grey concrete surfaces of the city into extensions of the sky, of imagined interiors, of dreamlike public spaces.

Ghadyanloo's murals possess a gentle, surrealistic quality: figures float in impossible spaces, staircases lead to nowhere, clouds enter buildings. The works draw on the tradition of European trompe-l'oeil while developing a specifically Iranian visual sensibility influenced by classical Persian miniature painting and its use of multiple spatial registers. They are public in the most direct sense — paintings for the inhabitants of a city, made on the surfaces of everyday architecture — and reflect a genuine commitment to the idea of art as a democratic public good.

He has expanded his practice internationally, producing murals and studio works in the United States and Europe. In 2016 he was commissioned to create a large mural in the Rose Kennedy Greenway in Boston. His studio paintings on canvas extend his trompe-l'oeil and surrealist spatial investigations into the gallery context.

famous_works.title: Tehran Murals
famous_works.notes: More than a hundred large-scale trompe-l'oeil and surrealist murals painted on the walls of Tehran buildings, transforming the city's urban fabric with fantastical architectural illusions.

famous_works.title: Spaces of Hope (Boston Greenway)
famous_works.notes: A large public artwork commissioned for the Rose Kennedy Greenway in Boston, extending Ghadyanloo's Iranian public art practice into an international context.

famous_works.title: Floating City
famous_works.notes: A studio painting in which the spatial illusions of Ghadyanloo's mural practice are translated into an intimate canvas.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Tehran Street Works
lesser_known_works.notes: The initial murals that established Ghadyanloo's reputation within Tehran's public art context and attracted international critical attention.

lesser_known_works.title: Miniature references series
lesser_known_works.notes: Studio works in which Persian classical miniature painting is engaged as a formal and cultural source for Ghadyanloo's contemporary spatial investigations.

artists.bio:
Tracey Emin was born in London in 1963 and grew up in Margate. She studied at Maidstone College of Art and the Royal College of Art in London, and became one of the defining figures of the Young British Artists generation. Her practice is rooted in an extreme autobiographical candour — using her own life, particularly experiences of abuse, sexual violence, loss, and emotional crisis, as the primary material of works across neon, embroidery, painting, sculpture, and installation.

Emin's most celebrated work, My Bed (1998), presented her unmade bed surrounded by the detritus of several days of emotional crisis — bottles, cigarette packets, underwear — as an artwork. The work divided critics between those who found it self-indulgent and those who recognised it as a formal and confessional act of considerable power. Her neon works — texts rendered in the cursive script of her own handwriting — have become among the most recognised images in contemporary British art: I Promise to Love You, You Forgot to Kiss My Soul, and similar declarations function simultaneously as personal statements and universal emotional propositions.

Emin won the Turner Prize nomination in 1999 and was appointed a Royal Academician in 2007, subsequently becoming the Royal Academy's professor of drawing. She is represented by White Cube and her work is held by Tate and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: My Bed
famous_works.notes: An unmade bed surrounded by the debris of an emotional crisis, presented as a work that insists on the emotional and physical facts of private life as valid art materials.

famous_works.title: Everyone I Have Ever Slept With 1963–1995
famous_works.notes: A tent embroidered inside with the names of everyone with whom Emin had shared a bed — a work that used textile craft to create a confessional record of intimacy across multiple registers.

famous_works.title: I Promise to Love You (Neon)
famous_works.notes: A neon work in Emin's own cursive script that transforms a personal declaration into an architectural-scale public statement of universal emotional resonance.

lesser_known_works.title: The Hotel (film)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early film work demonstrating Emin's autobiographical methodology before her emergence as a celebrated YBA.

lesser_known_works.title: Painting works
lesser_known_works.notes: A body of figurative monoprints and paintings that extends Emin's confessional practice into a more traditional medium.

top_lot.title: I can't Believe (how much) How much I Loved You 我無法相信我曾如此(如此

top_ratio_lot.title: I can't Believe (how much) How much I Loved You 我無法相信我曾如此(如此

artists.bio: Born in Hartford, Connecticut, to Russian Jewish immigrant parents, LeWitt studied at Syracuse University and worked in the offices of I.M. Pei before finding his direction. 'Paragraphs on Conceptual Art' (1967) and 'Sentences on Conceptual Art' (1969) are the founding texts of Conceptual art's theoretical programme. The central premise: the idea of the work is the work; its physical execution is incidental. His wall drawings, beginning in 1968, are sets of written instructions that can be executed by anyone in any location — the artist provides the score, not the performance. He produced over 1,200 wall drawing instructions and thousands of artist's books. His three-dimensional structures — white cubic open lattices — propose an architecture of pure logic without function. He was enormously generous to younger artists, storing, buying, and championing work throughout his career.

famous_works.title: Wall Drawing #260
famous_works.notes: All combinations of arcs from four corners, four sides, and the centre of a square using black, grey, and white crayon on a white wall. The instruction is the work; the execution is its temporary instantiation.

famous_works.title: Serial Project No. 1 (ABCD)
famous_works.notes: A modular structure of open and closed cubes on a grid. The systematic exhaustion of combinatorial possibilities as an artistic programme.

famous_works.title: Sentences on Conceptual Art
famous_works.notes: A text work — 35 numbered sentences. 'Irrational thoughts should be followed absolutely and logically.' The text as the art: instructions without physical realisation.

lesser_known_works.title: Incomplete Open Cubes
lesser_known_works.notes: 122 variations of the open cube with one or more edges removed — a systematic exploration of incompleteness. The database logic as sculpture.

lesser_known_works.title: Arcs, Circles and Grids
lesser_known_works.notes: A series of prints and drawings — the formal vocabulary of LeWitt's wall drawings in portable form. The grid and the arc as fundamental pictorial elements.

lesser_known_works.title: Five Towers
lesser_known_works.notes: Stacked concrete blocks in different heights. LeWitt's engagement with vernacular building materials — the monument made from the most banal possible element.

top_lot.title: Complex Structure in Contained Space 密閉空間中的複合結構

artists.bio:
Henri Hayden was born in Warsaw in 1883 and moved to Paris in 1907, where he studied at the École des Beaux-Arts and became progressively absorbed into the milieu of Montparnasse and the emerging Cubist movement. He was associated with the group of artists and writers centred on the École de Paris — that loose community of predominantly Jewish and immigrant artists who made Montparnasse their home — and developed a Cubist-influenced style that brought geometric structure to bear on landscape and still-life subjects.

Hayden's paintings of the 1920s show a Cubism that has been absorbed and softened into a more lyrical, chromatic idiom, the geometric faceting of the earlier movement's rigour giving way to a structural simplification that retains form and mass while allowing colour greater atmospheric freedom. His Breton landscapes and still-lifes of this period are among the most visually satisfying products of post-Cubist French painting.

During World War II Hayden and his wife hid in the Lot, where Samuel Beckett sheltered with them — a friendship that proved important for both men. After the Liberation he returned to Paris and continued painting until his death in 1970, maintaining a consistent dignity and formal intelligence through a career of more than six decades that deserves fuller critical attention than it has received.

famous_works.title: Still Life with Guitar
famous_works.notes: A Cubist still-life in which musical instruments and domestic objects are analysed into faceted planes with a formal clarity and chromatic warmth that characterise Hayden's post-Cubist manner.

famous_works.title: Breton Landscape
famous_works.notes: A landscape in which Cubist structural simplification meets plein air observation of the Breton coast, producing images of quiet formal authority.

famous_works.title: Portrait of Samuel Beckett
famous_works.notes: A portrait of Hayden's wartime companion and friend, linking two distinguished careers through the specific conditions of occupied France.

lesser_known_works.title: Village Scene, Brittany
lesser_known_works.notes: A mid-career landscape demonstrating the mature post-Cubist style that Hayden maintained with elegant consistency across decades.

lesser_known_works.title: Still Life with Fruit
lesser_known_works.notes: A domestic still-life in which Cézannist structure and warm Post-Impressionist colour create a painting of concentrated visual pleasure.

top_lot.title: La plage
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Kenny Scharf was born in Hollywood, California in 1958 and arrived in New York in 1978 to study at the School of Visual Arts, where he met Jean-Michel Basquiat and Keith Haring and became part of the East Village art scene and the Club 57 community that was central to New York's early 1980s cultural moment. His paintings brought together the visual language of American popular culture — cartoon television, science fiction, consumer products — with a sensibility of cosmic psychedelia and childlike exuberance.

Scharf's canvases are densely populated with cartoon-derived creatures, consumer product logos, outer space imagery, and pop cultural references, all rendered in bright, saturated colours and combined with a compositional exuberance that is at once knowing and genuinely anarchic. The works embrace the mass-cultural vernacular of their moment — particularly the Saturday morning cartoon aesthetics of the 1960s and 1970s — while investing it with an excess of colour, form, and cosmic imagery that transforms kitsch into something more formally complex.

He has been exhibited widely and has maintained a highly productive practice across painting, sculpture, installation, and collaborative environmental works for more than four decades. Scharf's work contributes to the sustained investigation of how popular culture can be simultaneously embraced and transfigured by painterly intelligence.

famous_works.title: When the Worlds Collide
famous_works.notes: A large painting in which cartoon characters, cosmic space imagery, and consumer products collide in a composition of chromatic intensity and anarchic formal energy.

famous_works.title: Cosmic Closet
famous_works.notes: One of Scharf's 'Kosmik Klossets' — room-sized installations of found objects painted and adorned with fluorescent paint — that extend his painterly vocabulary into immersive environments.

famous_works.title: Graffiti Wall (Times Square)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale mural that brought Scharf's cartoon-cosmic language to a public urban context, extending the Street Art public platform that his East Village contemporaries were developing.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Club 57 paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from Scharf's initial New York period that demonstrate the development of his cartoon-based language within the specific cultural context of the East Village community.

lesser_known_works.title: Television paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Works incorporating actual television sets as formal elements, literalising the visual culture of broadcast media within the painting object.

artists.bio: Born in Paris to a wealthy banking family, Degas trained at the École des Beaux-Arts and in Italy before aligning himself with the Impressionists — though he resisted the plein-air doctrine entirely. His subjects — ballet dancers, café-concert singers, jockeys, women bathing — are depicted from unexpected angles and cropped by the frame in ways derived from photography and Japanese woodblock prints. He was interested in movement and the body in action rather than in light on water. His late pastels, made as his eyesight failed, achieve an atmospheric intensity that exceeds his oil paintings. He was politically reactionary (an anti-Dreyfusard), socially reclusive, and increasingly misanthropic in old age. His sculpture, produced privately and mostly cast after his death from wax models found in his studio, anticipates 20th-century attitudes to the sculptured figure.

famous_works.title: The Dance Class
famous_works.notes: Ballet students in rehearsal, the great dancing master Jules Perrot at centre. The casual spatial organisation — figures turned away, partially visible — is the radical move. Musée d'Orsay.

famous_works.title: L'Absinthe
famous_works.notes: Two figures in a café, the woman staring into nothing, the glass of absinthe before her. The image of urban solitude and alcoholic depression that the bourgeois public found offensive.

famous_works.title: Little Dancer Aged Fourteen
famous_works.notes: A wax figure (cast in bronze posthumously) of a young dancer in a real tutu, real hair ribbon, real silk slippers. The inclusion of non-artistic materials scandalised the 1881 exhibition audience.

lesser_known_works.title: Miss La La at the Cirque Fernando
lesser_known_works.notes: A Black circus performer suspended from a rope by her teeth — viewed from below at a vertiginous angle. Race, gender, spectacle, and bodily risk all present simultaneously.

lesser_known_works.title: At the Milliner's
lesser_known_works.notes: Women trying on hats — Degas observed from behind and to the side, the faces averted. The shopping excursion depicted with the same formal attention as the rehearsal room.

lesser_known_works.title: Woman with Chrysanthemums
lesser_known_works.notes: A woman displaced entirely to the right edge of the canvas by an enormous floral arrangement. The compositional off-centering — unprecedented at the time — makes the painting's interest formal rather than portraiture.

top_lot.title: Danseuse, grande arabesque, deuxième temps
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio: Born in Newark, New Jersey, Steir studied at the Pratt Institute under Richard Lindner and Philip Pearlstein before developing a practice that moved through conceptual text works, art-historical quotation, and printmaking into the monumental waterfall paintings for which she is best known. She pours paint down large unstretched canvases, allowing gravity and the paint's viscosity to determine the cascading marks. The waterfalls are both rigorous engagements with painting's relationship to time and Chinese ink-painting tradition and irreducibly beautiful large-scale abstractions. She was associated with the Conceptual art movement and Fluxus in the 1970s (close to Brice Marden, Sol LeWitt, Agnes Martin, and Elizabeth Murray). Her market has strengthened considerably since the 2010s.

famous_works.title: The Brueghel Series (A Vanitas of Style)
famous_works.notes: Sixty-four panels each depicting a flower painting in a different art-historical style — Monet, Matisse, Manet, El Greco. A systematic art-historical investigation of style as subject.

famous_works.title: Waterfall
famous_works.notes: A monumental waterfall painting — poured paint cascading down the full height of a large canvas. The process (gravity-determined flow) is the composition.

famous_works.title: White Through Blue to Red Waterfall
famous_works.notes: A late large waterfall in which three colour pours occupy the canvas in sequence. The colour relationships between the cascades are the formal subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Big Red
lesser_known_works.notes: A monochrome red waterfall of exceptional scale. The reduction to single colour focuses attention on the physical process of the pour — what happens when colour is removed as a variable.

lesser_known_works.title: Lilac Waterfall
lesser_known_works.notes: A softer waterfall work in lavender — the atmospheric rather than dramatic pole of the series.

lesser_known_works.title: December Moon, January Moon, February Moon
lesser_known_works.notes: An early conceptual work before the waterfall series — three paintings documenting the moon through three winter months. The Conceptual structure that underlies all of Steir's work.

artists.bio: Born Vosdanig Manoog Adoian in Khorkom, Armenia, Gorky survived the Armenian Genocide — his mother died of starvation in 1919 — and emigrated to the United States in 1920. He reinvented himself as a Caucasian from Tiflis, adding fictional biography to a false name. In New York he systematically absorbed the history of modernism: Cézanne, Picasso, Miró, and then the Surrealists (Matta, Breton, Léger) who arrived as refugees during WWII. His late paintings — The Liver is the Cock's Comb (1944), Agony (1947), The Betrothal (1947) — use flowing biomorphic forms charged with references to memory, landscape, the Armenian pastoral of his childhood, and the psychic violence of survival. He is the essential bridge between European Surrealism and American Abstract Expressionism. A fire destroyed most of his recent work in 1946; a car crash left him with a broken neck; cancer of the colon was diagnosed in 1948. He hanged himself in July 1948, aged 44.

famous_works.title: The Liver is the Cock's Comb
famous_works.notes: A monumental canvas of biomorphic forms in a charged field of yellows, reds, and greens. Breton considered it the most important painting produced in America to that date.

famous_works.title: Agony
famous_works.notes: Made after the studio fire and car crash — formally stripped down, the colour more acid, the forms more violent. Among the most emotionally direct of the late works.

famous_works.title: The Artist and His Mother
famous_works.notes: Based on a photograph taken in Van before the Genocide. Ten years of reworking produced a monumental image of memory and loss — the mother's hands erased by multiple layers.

lesser_known_works.title: Garden in Sochi
lesser_known_works.notes: A memory of his father's garden in Khorkom, filtered through Miró's biomorphism. The most directly autobiographical of the major works.

lesser_known_works.title: One Year the Milkweed
lesser_known_works.notes: From the same period as Liver. Softer in colour and more suspended in feeling — the forms float rather than press against each other.

lesser_known_works.title: Waterfall
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting where the forms begin to loosen from Miró's influence into something more distinctly Gorky's own. The Virginia countryside in which he was living enters the work as feeling rather than depiction.

artists.bio:
Yu Nishimura was born in Tokyo in 1983 and studied at the Tama Art University before developing a painting practice that combines the traditions of Japanese figuration — particularly the influence of traditional Japanese painting (nihonga) and woodblock prints — with a contemporary approach to the female figure and personal experience. Her work explores private, contemplative states in which young women inhabit spaces of interiority that are rendered with a particular atmospheric quality.

Nishimura's paintings are characterised by their warm palette, their simplified but psychologically resonant figure rendering, and their treatment of interior light. The figures in her canvases are often depicted in moments of absorbed activity — reading, looking out windows, existing in states of quiet concentration — that create images of interior life rendered visible through careful formal attention. Her approach draws on both Japanese and European painting traditions while maintaining a sensibility that is entirely contemporary.

She has exhibited in Japan and has attracted international attention as part of the growing global interest in Japanese figurative painting. Nishimura represents a thoughtful engagement with the intimate scale of personal experience as a subject for painting.

famous_works.title: Woman Reading
famous_works.notes: A figure absorbed in a book rendered in Nishimura's warm palette and simplified figurative style that creates an image of concentrated inner life.

famous_works.title: Interior
famous_works.notes: A domestic scene in which the relationship between figure and space is developed with atmospheric sensitivity characteristic of Nishimura's mature approach.

famous_works.title: Morning Light
famous_works.notes: A painting in which the quality of morning light in a domestic space is the primary formal and emotional concern.

lesser_known_works.title: Early figure studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial paintings demonstrating the development of Nishimura's figurative approach before its full maturation.

lesser_known_works.title: Works on paper
lesser_known_works.notes: Drawings and works on paper that demonstrate the intimacy and exploratory quality of Nishimura's practice beyond the painted canvas.

top_lot.title: A Car Running in the Green 穿過叢林的車子

top_ratio_lot.title: A Car Running in the Green 穿過叢林的車子

artists.bio:
Zeng Fanzhi was born in Wuhan, Hubei Province in 1964 and studied at the Hubei Institute of Fine Arts before settling in Beijing, where he has been based since the early 1990s. He came to international attention with works from three distinctive series that mark the main phases of his early practice: the Hospital series, the Mask series, and the late landscape and expressionist works.

The Hospital paintings of 1991–93 — large expressionistic canvases of flesh, medical instruments, and institutional spaces rendered in acidic pinks and yellows — were among the most viscerally affecting Chinese paintings of their decade. The Mask series that followed, in which figures wearing identical white masks inhabit social spaces (dinner parties, streets, public buildings), used this device to express the anxiety of social performance and the concealment of individual identity within a collectivised society. These works, combining European Expressionism with a specifically Chinese social critique, brought Zeng significant international recognition.

His subsequent large-scale abstract landscape paintings, in which gestural calligraphic marks create complex visual fields that evoke Chinese ink painting while engaging with Western Expressionism, represent a further development of his practice. He is represented by Acquavella Galleries and Gagosian Gallery and his works have achieved major auction records.

famous_works.title: Mask Series No. 6
famous_works.notes: A large painting in which expressionistically rendered figures in identical white masks gather in a social scene whose apparent normality is rendered uncanny by the collective concealment.

famous_works.title: Hospital Triptych
famous_works.notes: A three-panel work from the Hospital series in which medical contexts are rendered in acid expressionist colour, making visible the anxiety and vulnerability of bodies under institutional management.

famous_works.title: Last Supper
famous_works.notes: A monumental reworking of Leonardo's Last Supper in which the masked figures replace the apostles, creating a meditation on collective identity, betrayal, and Chinese social experience.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait of a Doctor
lesser_known_works.notes: A single figure from the Hospital series that demonstrates the psychological intensity of Zeng's expressionist approach to the individual subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Landscape series
lesser_known_works.notes: Late abstract landscape paintings that extend Zeng's expressionist mark-making into a more purely formal register informed by the tradition of Chinese ink painting.

top_lot.title: Idealism Drawing 理想主義繪畫

top_ratio_lot.title: Idealism Drawing 理想主義繪畫

artists.bio:
Mr. is the pseudonym of Masakatsu Iwamoto, born in Japan in 1969. He studied at Joshibi University of Art and Design in Tokyo and became associated with the Superflat movement through his close relationship with Takashi Murakami, though his work takes a considerably more provocative position within that framework. Mr.'s paintings depict adolescent anime-style female figures in states of explicit sexual availability or fantasy, rendered in the flat, candy-coloured aesthetic of otaku culture — the subculture of Japanese manga and anime enthusiasts.

Mr.'s practice sits within the tradition of Japanese Pop Art's engagement with otaku aesthetics, but it does so with a directness about its subject matter — the fetishisation of the schoolgirl figure in contemporary Japanese popular culture — that makes it simultaneously a participation in and a critique of the cultural environment it depicts. The works' technical accomplishment and their formal connection to the sophisticated visual language of anime distinguish them from mere transgression toward a more complex cultural commentary.

He has been exhibited with Blum & Poe in Los Angeles and internationally, and his work has been collected by major institutions. Mr. represents a significant strand in the ongoing international dialogue about the relationship between art and popular culture, particularly within the specific context of Japanese visual culture.

famous_works.title: I Am Pure, I Am Just a Romantic
famous_works.notes: A large painting in the Superflat idiom in which an anime-style schoolgirl figure is depicted with the explicit sexuality and formal sophistication characteristic of Mr.'s mature practice.

famous_works.title: The Fantastic Journey
famous_works.notes: A complex multi-figure composition in which otaku aesthetic elements are combined in a narrative visual field that operates both as pop cultural spectacle and formal investigation.

famous_works.title: Love, Fear, and Beauty
famous_works.notes: A painting in which Mr.'s characteristic flat colour and manga figuration explore the complex emotional economy of adolescent desire within Japanese popular culture.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Superflat works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial paintings that established Mr.'s formal language within the Superflat context before the full development of his distinctive critical position.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait series
lesser_known_works.notes: Works focused on the individual anime-style figure head that demonstrate the psychological dimensions within Mr.'s apparently flat visual language.

artists.bio: Born on the Lower East Side of New York to Jewish immigrant parents, Gottlieb studied at the Art Students League and the Académie de la Grande Chaumière in Paris. He was a founding member of The Ten (1935–40), a group of abstract and expressionist painters, and helped draft the famous 1943 letter to the New York Times (co-signed with Rothko) defending modern art. His Pictograph series (1941–53) — canvases divided into a grid of compartments each containing a symbolic image — drew on archaeology, Native American petroglyphs, and Freudian iconography to create a private mythological visual language. His Burst series (begun 1957) reduced this to two elements: a hovering disc above an exploding mass below. The simplicity and cosmic scale of the Burst works became his most enduring contribution.

famous_works.title: Voyager's Return
famous_works.notes: A Pictograph of extraordinary density — the compartments packed with Jungian archetypes derived from Freud and Frazer's Golden Bough. One of the finest works in the series.

famous_works.title: Burst
famous_works.notes: The first fully achieved Burst — a red disc hovering above an exploding black mass. The binary composition that reduced everything to cosmic opposition.

famous_works.title: The Frozen Sounds, Number 1
famous_works.notes: A transitional work between Pictograph and Burst — the compartments dissolving, the imagery becoming more atmospheric. The emergence of the Burst logic is visible.

lesser_known_works.title: Red Sky
lesser_known_works.notes: A Burst work in which the disc is absent — the explosion alone fills the canvas. The subtraction of one half of the binary makes the remaining element more violent.

lesser_known_works.title: Augury
lesser_known_works.notes: A Pictograph in which bird imagery dominates the compartments. The ornithological reference to augury (divination from bird observation) is the subject and the formal programme simultaneously.

lesser_known_works.title: Green Foreground
lesser_known_works.notes: A late work in which the Burst logic is pushed toward landscape — the disc reads as sun, the explosion as earth. The cosmological becomes environmental.

artists.bio: Born in Asheville, North Carolina, Noland studied at Black Mountain College under Josef Albers and Ilya Bolotowsky, then in Paris with Ossip Zadkine. The encounter with Frankenthaler's Mountains and Sea (1953) — taken to his studio by Morris Louis — was the pivotal event: the soak-stain technique offered a means of making colour saturate the canvas rather than sit on it. His concentric circle paintings (the Target series, 1958–62) and chevron paintings (1962–64) reduced the pictorial problem to one element repeated with precision, using hard-edged masking tape rather than gestural drawing to define the forms. The horizontal stripe paintings (1966–68) pushed this further: the canvas format was extended to extreme horizontal proportions to make the stripe dominant. He was Clement Greenberg's protégé and the most technically precise practitioner of Colour Field painting.

famous_works.title: Beginning
famous_works.notes: An early target painting in which the concentric circles are loosely painted — the hard-edge precision of the mature work not yet established. The spatial ambiguity of the circle between flatness and illusion.

famous_works.title: Turnsole
famous_works.notes: A target painting in saturated teal and orange. The colour relationships are the subject — the concentric rings activate the space between them optically.

famous_works.title: Via Blues
famous_works.notes: A horizontal stripe painting in deep blue — the canvas twelve feet wide. The extreme horizontal format makes the stripes architectural rather than pictorial.

lesser_known_works.title: Half
lesser_known_works.notes: A target bisected by the bottom edge of the canvas — the composition is incomplete by design. The frame as an active compositional element.

lesser_known_works.title: Bloom
lesser_known_works.notes: The target as flower — the central circle and radiating rings of soft pink and green suggest botanical structure without depicting it.

lesser_known_works.title: Grayed Rainbow
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work before the target solution was found — Frankenthaler's influence absorbed but not yet transformed. The soak-stain technique applied to a more compositionally conventional ground.

artists.bio: Born in Limoges, Renoir trained as a porcelain painter before entering the École des Beaux-Arts and meeting Monet, Sisley, and Bazille. He was the most socially optimistic of the Impressionists: his paintings depict pleasure — dances, outdoor luncheons, Sunday afternoons on the Seine, women bathing — with a warmth and sensuousness that distinguished him from Monet's more meteorological programme. The dappled light in his outdoor figure paintings is the technique applied to human social life rather than landscape. His late career, dominated by rheumatoid arthritis that eventually left him unable to hold a brush (his assistants strapped it to his wrist), turned toward an increasingly academic and studio-bound treatment of the female figure. Institutional and collector prices remain very strong globally, particularly in the United States and Japan, where his work entered major collections early.

famous_works.title: Le Moulin de la Galette
famous_works.notes: A Sunday afternoon at the outdoor dance hall in Montmartre. The dappled Impressionist light applied to a scene of bourgeois leisure — one of the movement's defining images. Musée d'Orsay.

famous_works.title: Luncheon of the Boating Party
famous_works.notes: Renoir's friends lunching on a terrace above the Seine at Chatou. The most elaborate of his outdoor figure compositions — Impressionist technique at its most socially immersive.

famous_works.title: The Large Bathers
famous_works.notes: A monumental outdoor nude composition in a more classical style than the Impressionist period — Renoir's attempt to reconcile plein-air observation with the grand tradition.

lesser_known_works.title: The Swing
lesser_known_works.notes: A small painting made in his garden in Montmartre — the same location as Le Moulin. The dappled light effects are even more immediate here than in the larger canvas.

lesser_known_works.title: Young Women at the Piano
lesser_known_works.notes: Commissioned by the French state for the Luxembourg Museum. The domestic interior at its most comfortable — Renoir's bourgeois optimism at its least problematically cheerful.

lesser_known_works.title: The Theatre Box
lesser_known_works.notes: A woman in a theatre box, the male companion a dark background figure. The woman as spectacle within the spectacle of the theatre — the looking and being-looked-at doubled.

top_lot.title: Vache dans un verger
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Ken Price was born in Los Angeles in 1935 and studied at the Chouinard Art Institute and the Otis Art Institute before receiving his MFA from the New York State College of Ceramics at Alfred University. He returned to Los Angeles, where he became associated with the Ferus Gallery group alongside Ed Ruscha, Larry Bell, and Robert Irwin, though his practice in ceramic sculpture was quite distinct from the glossy conceptualism of most of his contemporaries.

Price's work with clay began with biomorphic abstract vessels in the 1960s that drew on both the Surrealist tradition and the specific material possibilities of ceramics, and developed into an increasingly complex investigation of form, colour, and surface that continued for fifty years. His mature sculptures — dense, roughly organic forms covered in hundreds of layers of acrylic paint, sanded between applications — have a colour intensity and surface richness that is unlike anything else in sculpture. The forms are simultaneously abstract and suggestive of biological organisms, geological formations, or fantastical objects.

Price worked slowly and produced relatively few works in his later career, each piece representing months of labour in the application of its many-layered polychrome surface. He is represented posthumously by Matthew Marks Gallery and his work is held by major collections including the Museum of Modern Art and the Los Angeles County Museum of Art.

famous_works.title: Happy's Curios
famous_works.notes: A major installation of ceramic objects displayed in a purpose-built roadside display case, combining craft tradition with a vernacular commercial presentation.

famous_works.title: Snail Cup
famous_works.notes: An early biomorphic ceramic vessel that establishes Price's departure from conventional pottery toward an abstract sculptural investigation of ceramic form.

famous_works.title: Sea and Sunshine
famous_works.notes: A late polychrome sculpture in which hundreds of layers of sanded acrylic paint create a surface of extraordinary chromatic complexity and depth.

lesser_known_works.title: Red Cup
lesser_known_works.notes: An early ceramic work that establishes the simple vessel form as a vehicle for investigation of colour, surface, and the relationship between utility and aesthetic object.

lesser_known_works.title: L.A. Series
lesser_known_works.notes: An early series of ceramics drawing on the specific visual culture of Los Angeles — its drive-ins, its neon, its suburban strangeness — as formal and cultural material.

artists.bio:
Rhee Seundja was born in Cheongju, Korea in 1918 and came to Paris in 1951 after completing her training at the Seoul National University College of Fine Arts. She settled in France permanently and became part of the École de Paris's circle of foreign artists who made Paris their creative home, developing a practice of lyrical abstraction that occupied a distinctive position between the gestural energy of French informal abstraction and an aesthetic sensibility shaped by East Asian aesthetics.

Rhee's paintings are executed in a technique involving numerous layers of applied and partially removed paint, building surfaces of great material complexity in which colour glows from beneath darker upper layers with a quality of inner light that is entirely distinctive. Her favourite subjects and formal preoccupations — light, space, the relationship between mass and emptiness — connect to Korean aesthetic traditions of restraint and suggestion while finding expression through the vocabulary of Western abstract painting.

She received numerous French honours including the Légion d'honneur and was exhibited at the Galerie Jacques Casanova and other Paris venues. Despite a long and distinguished career, she received fuller international recognition only in the years before her death in 2009 and in the subsequent growth of critical attention to Korean abstraction worldwide.

famous_works.title: Composition (Light)
famous_works.notes: A large abstract painting in which Rhee's layered technique creates a surface from which light seems to emanate from within, connecting Western gestural abstraction with Eastern concepts of the luminous void.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Blue Series)
famous_works.notes: A painting from Rhee's most characteristic blue-and-grey palette in which the layering of translucent colour creates a spatial depth of meditative quality.

famous_works.title: Paris Compositions
famous_works.notes: Works from the height of Rhee's Parisian engagement that demonstrate the synthesis of Western lyrical abstraction and Korean aesthetic sensibility at the core of her practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Korean works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from before Rhee's departure for France that document her training and the tradition she subsequently transformed through her encounter with European abstraction.

lesser_known_works.title: Late works
lesser_known_works.notes: Late career paintings demonstrating Rhee's sustained formal development into the final decade of her long career.

artists.bio:
Rita Ackermann was born in Budapest in 1968 and studied at the Hungarian University of Fine Arts before moving to New York in 1992. Her arrival coincided with the vibrant East Village alternative culture, and she quickly established a distinctive practice combining expressive figuration, underground music, and fashion illustration in works that came to define a particular moment in 1990s New York art.

Ackermann's early work featured adolescent female figures in states of vulnerability or aggression — girls with weapons, girls in complex emotional and physical postures — rendered in a gestural, expressionistic manner that combined art historical references (Egon Schiele, Expressionism) with a specifically 1990s sensibility of transgressive girlhood. Over subsequent decades her practice has become more abstract, incorporating collage, burning, and overpainting to create densely layered canvases in which figuration and abstraction coexist in states of productive destruction.

She is represented by Hauser & Wirth and has been exhibited at major institutions internationally. Ackermann's practice represents a significant contribution to the intersection of expressionism, feminism, and the specific cultural energies of New York alternative culture.

famous_works.title: Girl with Knives
famous_works.notes: A painting of an adolescent female figure armed and confrontational that establishes Ackermann's distinctive approach to girlhood as a subject of both empowerment and vulnerability.

famous_works.title: Fire by Days (Harmony and Dissatisfaction)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale work in which collage, overpainting, and burning create a surface of complex material history that reflects Ackermann's development toward more abstract approaches.

famous_works.title: Mauvaise Herbe
famous_works.notes: A painting demonstrating the full range of Ackermann's mature language in which fragmentary figuration and abstraction coexist in a surface of extraordinary complexity.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Budapest works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Ackermann's Hungarian period that demonstrate the European Expressionist sources from which her New York practice developed.

lesser_known_works.title: Fashion illustration works
lesser_known_works.notes: Commercial illustrations that demonstrate the crossover between art and fashion that characterised Ackermann's early New York career.

top_lot.title: Contours in Prime Color Field 原色領域之輪廓

top_ratio_lot.title: Contours in Prime Color Field 原色領域之輪廓

artists.bio:
Diego Giacometti was born in Borgonovo, in the Italian-speaking Swiss canton of Graubünden in 1902, the younger brother of Alberto Giacometti. He arrived in Paris in 1925 and became his brother's essential collaborator, assistant, and model for the next four decades, his face and body appearing in many of Alberto's most important sculptures. He was for much of his life overshadowed by his more famous brother, yet his own distinct practice as a designer of decorative objects — bronzes, furniture, lamps — constitutes a highly original body of work.

Diego's bronzes depict animals, birds, and fantastical composite creatures rendered with a surface texture of remarkable vitality — roughened, encrusted, alive with the marks of the making process. Tables, chairs, and lamps incorporating cast bronze animals and naturalistic forms, made for clients including Dior's Château de la Colle Noire and the Fondation Maeght, occupy a position between furniture, sculpture, and the decorative arts that resists easy categorisation. Their combination of craftsmanship, formal invention, and animistic spirit is entirely his own.

Following Alberto's death in 1966, Diego's independent practice received greater recognition. His work is sought by collectors of both modern sculpture and decorative arts, and its distinctive character — sophisticated but unpretentious, French in craft tradition but Swiss in material directness — has attracted sustained critical appreciation.

famous_works.title: Table with Bird and Snake
famous_works.notes: A bronze table in which the supporting legs and surface are inhabited by naturalistic animals, fusing furniture and sculpture in Diego's characteristically animistic manner.

famous_works.title: Pair of Andirons with Panthers
famous_works.notes: Decorative bronze fireplace objects combining the craftsmanship of the applied arts with sculptural imagination of a high order.

famous_works.title: Cat Table
famous_works.notes: A bronze low table featuring cat forms integrated into the structure, demonstrating Diego's ability to make the decorative carry genuine sculptural weight.

lesser_known_works.title: Studies of Animals for Alberto
lesser_known_works.notes: Working drawings and models made in collaboration with his brother that document the productive intimacy of their decades-long professional relationship.

lesser_known_works.title: Floor Lamp with Herons
lesser_known_works.notes: A bronze and iron floor lamp in which naturalistic bird forms support the functional element with characteristic elegance.

top_lot.title: Carcasse à la chauve-souris
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
David Smith was born in Decatur, Indiana in 1906 and worked in an automobile factory and a locomotive factory before studying painting in New York, where his encounter with the welded iron sculptures of Picasso and González — reproduced in an art magazine — decisively redirected his practice toward sculpture in metal. He was among the first American artists to use industrial welding as a sculptural technique, establishing a tradition that profoundly influenced subsequent generations.

Smith worked at the Terminal Iron Works in Brooklyn and later from his property in Bolton Landing, New York, where he built a studio and outdoor sculpture field. His work developed through numerous series: the Tanktotem works of the early 1950s, in which industrial boiler parts are fused into figures of quasi-totem anthropomorphism; the Voltri-Bolton works made during a residency at an Italian steel factory; and the late Cubi series, which extended Abstract Expressionist energy into large-scale polished stainless steel cuboid forms whose burnished surfaces engage light and landscape with extraordinary sophistication.

Smith died in a truck accident in Vermont in 1965 at fifty-nine, leaving a body of work that occupies a unique position between painting and sculpture, between industrial fabrication and gestural abstraction. His work is held by the Guggenheim, MoMA, and other major institutions, and his influence on Anthony Caro and subsequent generations of British and American sculptors is immeasurable.

famous_works.title: Cubi XXVII
famous_works.notes: One of the monumental stainless steel geometric sculptures completed in Smith's final years, in which burnished facets reflect the sky and landscape with an openness that the massiveness of the forms seems to contradict.

famous_works.title: Hudson River Landscape
famous_works.notes: A welded steel drawing in three dimensions that translates the gestural fluency of Abstract Expressionist drawing into a large-scale openwork sculpture.

famous_works.title: Zig IV
famous_works.notes: A painted steel sculpture in which flat geometric planes are arranged in a dynamic spatial relationship that balances between stability and apparent instability.

lesser_known_works.title: Royal Bird
lesser_known_works.notes: A welded steel bird form that marks Smith's development from his early Surrealist-influenced work toward the more abstract spatial concerns of his mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Voltri VI
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the series made during Smith's Italian residency, incorporating found industrial tools and materials into compositions that retain the trace of their prior utility.

artists.bio:
Angel Otero was born in Santurce, Puerto Rico in 1981 and studied at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago, where he developed a process-based approach to painting that has become his signature. His technique involves pouring oil paint onto glass sheets, allowing it to skin over, then peeling and collaging these dried paint skins onto canvas — a process that produces surfaces of extraordinary complexity, in which the history of the painting's making is preserved in its surface folds and transparencies.

The resulting works oscillate between painting and sculpture, between the figurative traces encoded in their imagery and the pure materiality of paint as substance. Otero's surfaces recall simultaneously the crumpled folds of drapery in Baroque painting, the gestural accidents of Abstract Expressionism, and the worn surfaces of urban Puerto Rico. The tension between these registers — between historical painterly tradition and the contingent materiality of the process — generates the works' visual and intellectual richness.

Otero has been exhibited widely by Lehmann Maupin Gallery in New York, Hong Kong, and London. His work has been collected by major institutions and private collectors, and he represents a significant strand in contemporary painting's ongoing investigation of medium specificity and process.

famous_works.title: Skeletons in the Closet
famous_works.notes: A large-scale paint-skin collage in which overlapping translucent passages create an archaeology of the painting's own history of making.

famous_works.title: New Works (Oil Paintings)
famous_works.notes: A series of process-based paintings exploring the full range of Otero's peel-and-collage technique across varied chromatic registers.

famous_works.title: Puerto Rico Mi Amor
famous_works.notes: A group of works responding to Hurricane Maria's devastation, in which Otero's material process becomes a metaphor for destruction and reconstruction.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Glass Transfer)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating the glass-transfer technique in its initial, more exploratory phase.

lesser_known_works.title: Interior Study
lesser_known_works.notes: A more intimate scale work in which domestic imagery is encoded within the material complexity of the paint-skin surface.

artists.bio: Born in Philadelphia, Whitney studied at the Kansas City Art Institute and the Yale School of Art before moving to New York and then Rome, where he has spent significant periods of his career. He developed his distinctive grid-based abstract painting in Rome in the 1970s: a format of stacked horizontal bands, each containing vertical colour blocks, which he has explored with increasing depth since the 1990s. The grid is fixed but the colour relationships within it are endlessly varied — warm against cool, saturated against muted, advancing against receding. He cites Paul Klee, Miles Davis, and jazz improvisation as structural influences. His market was modest until the late 2010s when a broader revaluation of mid-career Black American abstractionists created rapid appreciation. His work is now in the collections of MoMA and the Whitney.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Venice)
famous_works.notes: A large vertical grid of warm and cool colour blocks — Whitney's mature language at its most confident. Made following his Venice Biennale presentation.

famous_works.title: Blue Nile
famous_works.notes: Named for the Ethiopian song. The blues of the title — music, geography, colour — converge in a canvas of extraordinary chromatic complexity within the constrained grid format.

famous_works.title: Dance the Orange
famous_works.notes: Named for the Rilke poem. The formal vocabulary of the grid freed into a looser organisation — the grid as improvisation rather than constraint.

lesser_known_works.title: Jazz
lesser_known_works.notes: An early mature work that establishes the stacked grid format. The jazz reference — improvisation within a fixed structure — is the method before it is the title.

lesser_known_works.title: Slow Boat to China
lesser_known_works.notes: A mid-career work in deep blues and greens. The long horizontal format that Whitney uses to extend the rhythm of the colour bands.

lesser_known_works.title: American Painting
lesser_known_works.notes: A canvas in which red, white, and blue — the obvious national palette — are distributed across the grid without irony. The American tradition absorbed and answered rather than quoted.

artists.bio:
Mark Grotjahn was born in Pasadena, California in 1968 and studied at the University of Colorado and the University of California Berkeley. He came to international attention in the early 2000s with his Butterfly series — large abstract paintings organised around vanishing-point perspective schemes in which lines radiate from multiple points on a canvas divided into bilateral symmetrical wings — that combined the formal rigour of geometric abstraction with a luminous, chromatic intelligence.

Grotjahn's Butterfly paintings are distinguished by their rich, layered colour and their formal balance: the perspective lines draw the eye inward through colour fields that build from light to dark or dark to light, creating images of concentrated optical energy. His subsequent Face paintings — in which perspective lines and coloured areas are organised around mask-like frontal configurations — extend this investigation into a more figurative register while maintaining the abstract logic of the earlier work. More recent large-scale works in cardboard and oil on linen have been executed on a monumental scale.

He is represented by Blum & Poe in Los Angeles and Gagosian Gallery, and his work has been collected by major institutions and private collectors internationally. Grotjahn's paintings have commanded among the highest prices of any artist of his generation at auction.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Butterfly)
famous_works.notes: A defining Butterfly painting in which bilateral symmetry and multiple perspective vanishing points create a chromatic field of controlled optical energy.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Face)
famous_works.notes: An early Face painting in which perspective lines and colour areas create a mask-like frontal configuration that introduces figurative suggestion without abandoning abstract logic.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Five Eyes)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale Face work in which the perspective system generates a five-eyed mask of considerable complexity and visual intensity.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Perspective works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial paintings in which Grotjahn developed his perspective-line approach before the Butterfly series' full formal clarity was established.

lesser_known_works.title: Cardboard Butterfly
lesser_known_works.notes: A work in which the Butterfly format is adapted to a cardboard support, demonstrating the range of material experiments within Grotjahn's formal system.

top_lot.title: Untitled (Multicolor Wing Butterfly White Background Dec. 31

top_ratio_lot.title: Untitled (Multicolor Wing Butterfly White Background Dec. 31

artists.bio:
Jaune Quick-to-See Smith was born on the Flathead Reservation in Montana in 1940, an enrolled member of the Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes. She studied at Framingham State University and received an MFA from the University of New Mexico, and has become over more than four decades one of the most significant Native American artists working in the United States and a tireless advocate for the representation of Indigenous artists within mainstream art institutions.

Smith works across painting, printmaking, and works on paper, developing a pictorial language that fuses gestural abstraction with symbolic imagery drawn from Native American traditions, newsprint collage, and references to the political history of Indigenous peoples in America. Her large-scale paintings layer these elements — newspaper headlines, petroglyphic symbols, land maps, contemporary commercial imagery — into dense, urgent fields that address history, sovereignty, and cultural survival with both formal sophistication and political directness.

Her retrospective at the Whitney Museum of American Art in 2023 brought her work to the widest audience it has received and generated substantial critical attention confirming her importance as both an artist and an institutional advocate. She has been instrumental in founding artist cooperatives and alternative exhibition spaces that support Indigenous artists.

famous_works.title: Trade (Gifts for Trading Land with White People)
famous_works.notes: A painting incorporating a canoe form laden with contemporary consumer goods — team mascot jerseys, toy tomahawks — that interrogates the continuing exploitation of Native American imagery within American popular culture.

famous_works.title: The Red Mean: Self-Portrait
famous_works.notes: A self-portrait using the Vitruvian Man format to locate the artist's body within both Western art historical tradition and Native American cosmological knowledge.

famous_works.title: Spotted Owl
famous_works.notes: A large painting addressing the intersection of Indigenous land rights, environmental activism, and colonial land use within the specific context of the Pacific Northwest timber debates.

lesser_known_works.title: Paper Dolls for a Post-Columbian World
lesser_known_works.notes: A print series using the paper doll format to examine the stereotypical representations of Native Americans in popular culture and tourist imagery.

lesser_known_works.title: Petroglyphs for the Living
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings in which ancient petroglyphic imagery is brought into dialogue with contemporary concerns, insisting on the continuity of Indigenous symbolic traditions.

artists.bio:
Robert Mangold was born in North Tonawanda, New York in 1937 and studied at the Cleveland Museum of Art School and Yale University School of Art. He came to prominence in the 1960s as one of the central figures of Minimalism, developing a practice of large-scale paintings on shaped canvases or panels that reduced pictorial means to simple geometric forms and limited, matte colour surfaces.

Mangold's paintings consistently engage the relationship between drawn line and painted plane, frame and interior, circle and rectangle — the basic geometric vocabulary of Western pictorial tradition tested against its own limits. He paints in flat acrylic on panels that are often monochrome or bicolour, drawing lines or curves in graphite or chalk directly on the painted surface that create a second layer of visual information in dialogue with the painted ground. The shapes of his supports — trapezoids, ellipses, joined polygons — are themselves formal propositions about the nature of the pictorial field.

Mangold is represented by Pace Gallery and his work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and major collections internationally. He has maintained an extraordinarily consistent practice across six decades, making formal decisions of precision and apparent simplicity that reward sustained attention with increasing complexity.

famous_works.title: Attic Series
famous_works.notes: A group of large-scale paintings on curved-edge panels in which a single sweeping line creates a spatial and compositional drama from the most elementary formal means.

famous_works.title: Frame Paintings
famous_works.notes: Works in which two or four panels are joined to create frame-like structures, investigating the relationship between frame and image as a formal and conceptual problem.

famous_works.title: Column Structure series
famous_works.notes: Large-scale vertical panel paintings in which the architectural metaphor of the column is engaged through purely pictorial formal means.

lesser_known_works.title: Area Studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Early works on shaped panels that establish Mangold's fundamental approach to the relationship between support shape and painted surface.

lesser_known_works.title: Ring Image series
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings in which elliptical drawn forms create spatial relationships with the panel edge that demonstrate the continued formal range of Mangold's mature practice.

artists.bio:
Takashi Murakami was born in Tokyo in 1962 and studied at the Tokyo University of the Arts, from which he received a doctorate in nihonga (traditional Japanese painting). His early work engaged the gap between the highly traditional discipline of his academic training and the contemporary popular cultures — manga, anime, video games — that surrounded him, and by the early 1990s he had developed the theoretical framework of 'Superflat' that became both the name of his movement and a philosophical position.

Superflat posits that Japanese consumer culture — the flat, bright, cute imagery of anime and its associated merchandise — is not merely entertainment but a direct expression of Japan's post-war cultural psychology, the nuclear trauma flattened and made palatable through an aesthetic of extreme cuteness and radical surface-ness. Murakami's paintings, sculptures, and merchandise deploy the conventions of anime and Japanese popular culture with a formal intelligence that simultaneously participates in and critically analyses these conventions.

Murakami operates one of the most ambitious artist-as-industry models in contemporary art: his Kaikai Kiki studio employs dozens of artists and assistants producing paintings, sculptures, prints, and merchandise at a scale that explicitly references the production models of anime and consumer goods. He is represented by Gagosian Gallery and his market has been among the strongest of any living artist. Major retrospectives at the Museum of Contemporary Art Los Angeles and other institutions have confirmed his importance.

famous_works.title: DOB series
famous_works.notes: Paintings and sculptures featuring Mr. DOB, Murakami's alter-ego character derived from anime aesthetics, which became the central figure in his early investigation of Superflat.

famous_works.title: Oval Buddha
famous_works.notes: Monumental gold and platinum sculptures in which traditional Buddhist imagery is combined with the surface aesthetics of anime and the production values of luxury goods.

famous_works.title: The 500 Arhats
famous_works.notes: A one-hundred-metre painting commissioned for the Qatar Museum in which five hundred Buddhist saints are depicted in a synthesis of Japanese classical painting and contemporary pop culture aesthetics.

lesser_known_works.title: My Lonesome Cowboy
lesser_known_works.notes: A fiberglass sculpture of an anime-style male figure in an explicit sexual pose that extends Murakami's investigation of otaku culture's erotic dimensions.

lesser_known_works.title: Superflat Museum
lesser_known_works.notes: A designer collaboration project extending the Superflat concept into consumer product design and demonstrating the deliberate collapse of boundaries between art and commodity.

top_lot.title: Untitled (Mr. Dob - Multicolour) 無題（Mr. Dob - 彩色）

top_ratio_lot.title: Untitled (Mr. Dob - Multicolour) 無題（Mr. Dob - 彩色）

artists.bio:
Mickalene Thomas was born in Camden, New Jersey in 1971 and studied at the Portland School of Art and Yale University School of Art. She developed a practice of large-scale figurative works combining painting, photography, and rhinestone ornamentation to create images of Black womanhood that engage simultaneously with the history of Western portraiture, African American vernacular visual culture, and the aesthetics of Blaxploitation and 1970s popular culture.

Thomas's paintings are distinctive for their combination of acrylic paint and rhinestones applied to the canvas to create surfaces of elaborate decorative richness that simultaneously reference classical European portraiture and the specific visual vocabulary of Black American style and self-adornment. Her subjects — typically Black women posed with a confidence and leisure that claims the history of the odalisque for different subjects — are presented with an insistent material presence that the glittering rhinestone surface amplifies rather than undermines.

She has been represented by Lehmann Maupin Gallery and Galerie Nathalie Obadia, and her work has been exhibited at major institutions including the Musée d'Orsay in Paris, which hosted an extraordinary dialogue between her work and Ingres's great odalisques. Thomas stands as one of the most significant figures in contemporary art's reassessment of the female figure and the politics of visual representation.

famous_works.title: Le déjeuner sur l'herbe: Les Trois Femmes Noires
famous_works.notes: A large rhinestone-and-paint reworking of Manet's controversial picnic scene that recasts the nude female figures as Black women, transforming the canonical source's racial and gender dynamics.

famous_works.title: A Little Taste Outside of Love
famous_works.notes: A large-scale painting of two Black women in a richly decorated domestic interior, in which the combination of paint and rhinestone creates a surface of celebratory material abundance.

famous_works.title: Do I Look Like I Believe in Forever?
famous_works.notes: A work in which a Black female figure's self-possessed gaze challenges the viewer's looking within the formal language of the Western portrait tradition.

lesser_known_works.title: I Can't See You Without Me
lesser_known_works.notes: A work exploring the dynamics of the gaze and recognition within a specifically Black female social context.

lesser_known_works.title: Mama Bush series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works depicting Thomas's mother as subject, exploring the relationship between portraiture, love, and the politics of visibility.

artists.bio: Born in Brooklyn, Longo studied at the Nova Scotia College of Art and Design and at the State University of New York at Buffalo, where he co-founded Hallwalls (1974) with Cindy Sherman and Charles Clough. His Men in the Cities drawings (1976–83) — monumental charcoal figures in business suits, frozen in convulsive contortions as if struck by an invisible force — became the defining images of 1980s urban alienation. The figures were projected from photographs of friends and traced, then drawn with obsessive hyperrealist precision. His more recent large-scale charcoal works — atomic bomb detonations, ocean waves, the American flag, the Capitol building — use photographic source material as the basis for technically virtuosic drawing.
