In the dataset, Study of Eric sold at 14.7× estimate, $441k against a $30k estimate — the dataset's top outperformer.

famous_works.title: Men in the Cities
famous_works.notes: A series of monumental charcoal drawings of figures in business attire, contorted as if by an unseen force. The canonical images of 1980s alienation — reproduced as wallpaper in American Psycho.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Cloud for Patrick)
famous_works.notes: A six-foot mushroom cloud rendered in charcoal with photographic precision. The nuclear sublime — scale, clarity, and dread — as hyperrealist drawing.

famous_works.title: Untitled (After Goya)
famous_works.notes: A hyperrealist charcoal response to Goya's Saturn Devouring His Son. The art-historical source reprocessed through Longo's documentary technique.

lesser_known_works.title: Study of Eric
lesser_known_works.notes: A Men in the Cities figure study — the 14.7× outperformer in the dataset. The preparatory studies for the series carry an immediacy that the final monumental drawings formalise.

lesser_known_works.title: Empirical House
lesser_known_works.notes: A work from Longo's more installation-oriented period — objects and images combined in a theatrical environment. Less well-known than the drawing series.

lesser_known_works.title: Sword of the Pig
lesser_known_works.notes: A relief sculpture from the end of the Men in the Cities period — the move from drawing into three dimensions, from representation into object.

artists.bio:
Joel Shapiro was born in New York City in 1941 and studied at New York University, served in the Peace Corps in India, and returned to New York in the late 1960s where he became associated with the broader Minimalist milieu while developing an approach that increasingly diverged from its orthodoxies. His early works — small cast iron or bronze house forms placed directly on the floor — used geometric architectural forms at a scale that forced intimacy rather than the monumental distance of conventional sculpture.

From the late 1970s, Shapiro developed the vocabulary that has remained central to his practice: geometric wooden elements — beams, planks, L-shapes — assembled into dynamic configurations that suggest human figures in motion, without being representational in any obvious sense. These forms are fabricated and painted in bright, non-naturalistic colours, their angular architectonic geometry evoking both pure spatial composition and the kinetic energy of the human body in movement, falling, running, or reaching.

Shapiro is represented by Pace Gallery and his work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and major collections internationally. He stands as an important figure in the development of post-Minimalist sculpture, maintaining Minimalism's commitment to geometric form while reintroducing the associative and figurative dimensions that strict Minimalism had excluded.

famous_works.title: Untitled (House on a Field)
famous_works.notes: A tiny cast iron house placed on the floor of an enormous gallery, in which scale displacement generates a sense of melancholy and displacement that exceeds the object's apparent simplicity.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Figure in Motion)
famous_works.notes: A configuration of painted wooden beams assembled to suggest a human figure in dynamic movement, demonstrating Shapiro's synthesis of geometric abstraction and figurative association.

famous_works.title: Loss and Regeneration
famous_works.notes: A large-scale bronze work installed at the Jewish Museum Berlin that extends Shapiro's kinetic figurative vocabulary into memorial and historical subject matter.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Small Bridge)
lesser_known_works.notes: A cast iron architectural fragment at miniature scale, demonstrating Shapiro's sustained interest in the relationship between architectural form and the body.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Floating Figure)
lesser_known_works.notes: A work in which the geometric beam-figure appears to be in free suspension, testing the limits of physical balance as a formal and associative element.

artists.bio: Born in Honfleur, Normandy, the son of a sailor, Boudin ran a stationery and framing shop in Le Havre before the artists Troyon, Millet, and Couture encouraged him to paint full-time. He was the painter who introduced the young Claude Monet to plein-air technique on the Norman coast in 1858 — his influence on the artist who would become the centre of Impressionism cannot be overstated. His beach scenes at Trouville (from 1862 onward) — fashionable figures in crinolines against vast sky, the meteorological accuracy of the Normandy atmosphere — established the subject matter that Monet, Renoir, and Sisley would elaborate. Jongkind and Boudin are now understood as the essential pre-Impressionist generation. His work enters the market with reliable strength: collector enthusiasm for 19th-century French plein-air masters — particularly sky studies — has grown substantially since the 2000s.

famous_works.title: The Beach at Trouville
famous_works.notes: Fashionable figures with parasols against a luminous sky. The Impressionist programme — sensation of light and atmosphere rather than narrative — is fully present a decade before the 1874 exhibition.

famous_works.title: Jetty and Wharf at Trouville
famous_works.notes: The harbour infrastructure depicted with documentary precision against a dramatic Normandy sky. Marine painting and meteorology as dual subjects.

famous_works.title: Washerwomen near Trouville
famous_works.notes: Working-class figures on the Norman coast rather than the bourgeois resort. Boudin's range beyond the fashionable beach — the labour behind the leisure economy.

lesser_known_works.title: Study of Sky (Étude de ciel)
lesser_known_works.notes: Rapid plein-air sky studies — Boudin made hundreds. In the dataset, a study sold at 10.24× estimate; the sky study format is among the most sought-after of his work.

lesser_known_works.title: Fishing Boats at Berck
lesser_known_works.notes: The northern French coast rather than Normandy — Boudin's range extended to Pas-de-Calais. The boats and the sky in equal balance.

lesser_known_works.title: The Harbour at Antwerp
lesser_known_works.notes: Industrial port imagery — the steamships and loading docks that Boudin observed on his travels to Belgium. The modernity of the industrial port is acknowledged rather than avoided.

top_lot.title: Étude de ciel
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Otto Freundlich was born in Stolp, Pomerania (now Słupsk, Poland) in 1878 and arrived in Paris in 1908, where he became one of the earliest advocates of abstract painting and a significant figure in the international avant-garde. Working in a mode of figurative and abstract painting that drew on Cubism's geometric analysis while pursuing an independent investigation of colour and form, he developed a personal style of mosaic-like colour fields in strong black outlines that had a particular character.

Freundlich was one of the targets of the Nazis' Entartete Kunst (Degenerate Art) exhibition of 1937, where his large sculpture The New Man was reproduced on the poster for the exhibition as an emblem of everything the Nazi aesthetic rejected. He fled to the French Pyrenees after the German invasion but was arrested and transported to the Maidanek concentration camp, where he was killed in 1943. The post-war rehabilitation of his reputation has been considerable.

His mosaics, sculptures, and paintings represent an important strand of pre-war European abstraction that was systematically suppressed by Fascism. Works that survived — many in French collections — have been subject to sustained critical reassessment confirming his importance to the history of abstract painting.

famous_works.title: The New Man
famous_works.notes: A large stone sculpture of a monumental abstract figure, notoriously reproduced on the poster of the 1937 Degenerate Art exhibition as an emblem of what the Nazis rejected.

famous_works.title: Composition
famous_works.notes: A painting in which segmented colour areas in strong black outlines create a mosaic-like surface of formal complexity and chromatic energy.

famous_works.title: Ascension
famous_works.notes: A large abstract painting in which Freundlich's characteristic mosaic-like colour organisation is applied to a vertical format suggestive of spiritual aspiration.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Figurative works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Freundlich's early Paris period demonstrating his initial development toward abstraction from a Cubist-influenced figurative base.

lesser_known_works.title: Mosaics
lesser_known_works.notes: Decorative mosaic works that translate Freundlich's colour organisation into a material medium with specific cultural and historical resonances.

top_lot.title: Kopf (Selbstbildnis)
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Mohammed Sami was born in Baghdad in 1984 and trained as a painter at the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad, where he was also employed by the secret police to paint portraits of Saddam Hussein — an experience of compelled collaboration with totalitarian power that has profoundly shaped his subsequent practice. He fled Iraq in 2007 and eventually settled in Stockholm, where he received further training at the Royal Institute of Art.

Sami's paintings address the experience of trauma, displacement, and the persistence of violent memory through a formal approach of great subtlety. He typically depicts domestic objects or fragments of interior environments — carpets, curtains, windows, furniture — in a manner that charges these apparently neutral subjects with the weight of what they witnessed. The works are never explicitly documentary; rather, they operate through formal suggestion and atmospheric density, creating images in which the mundane carries an almost unbearable symbolic load.

He has been exhibited at Modern Art Gallery in London and has attracted significant institutional attention, with works collected by Tate and other major institutions. Sami's practice represents a rigorous and formally intelligent engagement with the relationship between political violence, personal memory, and pictorial representation.

famous_works.title: Portrait with Party Background
famous_works.notes: A painting in which a festive party curtain becomes a charged vehicle for the memory of Ba'athist cultural celebrations, investing domestic decorative material with political and personal weight.

famous_works.title: The Silence
famous_works.notes: A large painting of an interior space in which the stillness of the depicted environment carries the weight of absent bodies and suppressed memory.

famous_works.title: Homeland
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which a fragment of Iraqi domestic interior becomes a meditation on the meaning of home for someone whose homeland was destroyed by violence.

lesser_known_works.title: Carpet Study
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work in which the specific pattern of an Iraqi carpet becomes the vehicle for a meditation on cultural identity and displacement.

lesser_known_works.title: Window
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting of a window that frames an absent landscape, demonstrating Sami's characteristic investment of simple architectural elements with complex emotional and political weight.

artists.bio:
Jonas Wood was born in Boston in 1977 and studied at Hobart and William Smith Colleges and the University of Washington before relocating to Los Angeles. He developed a distinctive pictorial language in which interior scenes, potted plants, sports subjects, and domestic still-lifes are rendered in flattened, pattern-conscious compositions that combine the influence of Matisse and Japanese woodblock prints with the specific visual culture of his Los Angeles environment.

Wood's paintings are characterised by their delight in pattern and surface: the competing patterns of wallpaper, fabric, ceramic, and plant form are laid against each other without hierarchical organisation, the space compressed into near-flatness while specific objects retain their identities. His basketball courts, with their geometric floor markings and surrounding spectator areas, his domestic interiors with their proliferation of potted plants and ceramics, and his studio portraits all explore the boundary between representation and decoration without fully committing to either.

He is represented by David Kordansky Gallery in Los Angeles and Gagosian Gallery, and his work has been collected by major museums including the Museum of Contemporary Art in Los Angeles and the Hammer Museum. Wood has established himself as one of the most formally inventive and personally resonant painters of his generation.

famous_works.title: Large Plaid Interior with Plants
famous_works.notes: A monumental interior scene in which a plaid sofa, potted plants, and other domestic elements are flattened into a pattern of competing geometric and organic forms.

famous_works.title: Basketball Court
famous_works.notes: A painting of a gymnasium basketball court whose geometric floor markings and surrounding interior elements are treated as a pattern field of equal interest to any figurative subject.

famous_works.title: Black Pot Studio
famous_works.notes: A studio interior in which Wood's ceramic collection and domestic environment provide the material for an investigation of still-life in a specifically contemporary register.

lesser_known_works.title: Ikea Lamp #2
lesser_known_works.notes: An early still-life that demonstrates Wood's interest in the aesthetic possibilities of ordinary commercial objects within a flattened, pattern-conscious composition.

lesser_known_works.title: Snake Plant
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting focused on a single domestic plant in which Wood's formal vocabulary — flat colour, strong outline, pattern — is applied to a simple horticultural subject with characteristic precision.

artists.bio:
Etel Adnan was born in Beirut in 1925, the daughter of a Greek mother and Syrian father, and grew up speaking Greek and Turkish, learning French and English later, and Arabic still later — a linguistic multiplicity that was formative for a writer and artist whose entire practice engages the relationship between language, image, and the experience of displacement. She studied philosophy at the Sorbonne in Paris and at Harvard before returning to the Arab world as a teacher and journalist.

Adnan came to visual art relatively late, developing in the 1960s the leporello format — folded accordion-book paintings — that became her primary visual medium alongside poetry and prose. These small, jewel-like works contain simple geometric forms — circles, triangles, rectangles — in areas of pure, luminous colour derived from the landscape and light of Northern California (where she eventually settled) and the landscapes of Lebanon and Greece. The forms are neither representational nor purely abstract but hover between horizon, sun, and mountain in a register of condensed poetic notation.

Adnan was awarded major literary prizes and her novel Sitt Marie Rose has become a classic of war literature. Her visual work was exhibited internationally from the early 2000s, including at Documenta, and she was recognised as one of the most original figures at the intersection of poetry, visual art, and the Arab intellectual tradition. She died in Paris in 2021.

famous_works.title: The Arab Apocalypse (leporello)
famous_works.notes: A visual poem in leporello format combining Arabic calligraphy and geometric forms that responds to the Lebanese Civil War with concentrated poetic-pictorial force.

famous_works.title: California Leporello series
famous_works.notes: Ongoing series of accordion-folded paintings in which the geometry of horizon, sun, and mountain is condensed into areas of pure colour that carry the emotional weight of landscape remembered and inhabited.

famous_works.title: Seasons
famous_works.notes: A late series of leporellos in which chromatic harmonies derived from the natural world create meditations on time, change, and the relationship between body and landscape.

lesser_known_works.title: Sea and Fog
lesser_known_works.notes: A leporello painting in which the atmospheric conditions of the Pacific Coast are translated into a poetic geometry of greys, blues, and white.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Mountain Series)
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works in which the triangular form of a mountain is treated as both geometric form and emotionally charged landscape memory.

artists.bio:
Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec was born in Albi in 1864 into the ancient aristocracy of southern France. Genetic complications from his parents' consanguinity, compounded by two childhood fractures that prevented normal bone growth, left him with stunted legs and a body that never exceeded five feet tall. He moved to Montmartre in 1882 to study painting and became over the next two decades the most distinctive visual chronicler of that world: its cabarets, dance halls, brothels, and circuses.

Lautrec brought an observer's intelligence shaped by his own experience of social marginality to bear on the performances and performers of the Belle Époque. His large-format lithographic posters for the Moulin Rouge and other venues transformed commercial graphics through the combination of Japanese compositional strategies — bold outlines, flat colour areas, cropped compositions — with his acute psychological observation. His paintings of women in brothels, made during extended periods of residence in regulated houses, are among the most psychologically penetrating images of female labour in Western art.

Alcoholism and the physical consequences of his condition led to a breakdown in 1899 and his death at thirty-six in 1901. His work is held by the Musée Toulouse-Lautrec in Albi, the Musée d'Orsay, and major collections internationally, and its influence on poster design, graphic art, and the subsequent history of Expressionism is immeasurable.

famous_works.title: Moulin Rouge: La Goulue
famous_works.notes: A lithographic poster for the Moulin Rouge that transformed commercial graphic design through the combination of Japanese spatial organisation, flat colour, and incisive psychological characterisation.

famous_works.title: Au Moulin Rouge
famous_works.notes: A large canvas depicting regulars and performers at the Moulin Rouge, in which the harsh artificial light and the specific social world of Montmartre are rendered with clinical precision.

famous_works.title: The Sofa (Deux Femmes Faisant l'Amour)
famous_works.notes: A painting of two women in intimate contact at a brothel, remarkable for its combination of formal intelligence and non-judgmental human attention.

lesser_known_works.title: Aristide Bruant dans son cabaret
lesser_known_works.notes: A poster of the chansonnier Aristide Bruant in which the dark cape and red scarf become an image of almost heraldic severity.

lesser_known_works.title: The Clownesse Cha-U-Kao at the Moulin Rouge
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting of a female clown adjusting her costume backstage, in which the ambiguous gender identity of the performer is treated with characteristic Lautrec directness.

top_lot.title: L'enfant au chien, fils de Madame Marthe et la chienne Pamel
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

top_ratio_lot.title: L'enfant au chien, fils de Madame Marthe et la chienne Pamel

artists.bio:
Richard Estes was born in Kewanee, Illinois in 1932 and studied at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago before working as a commercial artist in New York. He developed from the late 1960s the Photorealist approach to urban subjects for which he became internationally known: large paintings of New York storefronts, telephone booths, street scenes, and reflected urban surfaces rendered with an exactitude that equals — or arguably exceeds — photographic detail while maintaining the qualities of painted surfaces.

Estes's subjects are characteristically reflective urban surfaces: the chrome of diner counters, the plate glass of shop windows, the polished steel of transit vehicles — all surfaces that multiply and distort their environments in complex reflections. His paintings combine multiple photographic references to create composite views that are hyper-real in their detail but are in fact composite constructions that could not have been photographed from a single viewpoint. The result is a vision of the city that is simultaneously exhaustively specific and subtly impossible.

He has been exhibited at Marlborough Gallery in New York and is collected by the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Museum of Fine Arts Boston, and other major institutions. Estes represents the most sustained and technically accomplished contribution to American Photorealism.

famous_works.title: Food City
famous_works.notes: An early painting of a New York supermarket façade that established Estes's approach to reflective urban surfaces with particular formal precision.

famous_works.title: Hotel Lucerne
famous_works.notes: A painting of a Manhattan hotel façade in which the reflections in the glass create a spatial complexity that exceeds photographic documentation.

famous_works.title: Bus Windows
famous_works.notes: A view through and out of a city bus window in which multiple layers of reflection create a New York cityscape of extraordinary visual complexity.

lesser_known_works.title: Telephone Booths
lesser_known_works.notes: An early painting of glass phone booths that demonstrates the reflective surface investigations central to Estes's photorealist approach.

lesser_known_works.title: D-Train
lesser_known_works.notes: A New York subway car painting demonstrating Estes's sustained interest in transportation vehicles as vehicles for the investigation of reflective urban surfaces.

artists.bio:
Barbara Kruger was born in Newark, New Jersey in 1945 and studied at Syracuse University before transferring to Parsons School of Design in New York, where she trained as a graphic designer under Marvin Israel. She worked for many years as art director and designer at Condé Nast publications — experience that gave her intimate knowledge of how visual rhetoric operates in mass media. When she turned to fine art practice in the late 1970s, she did so armed with the tools of the professional propagandist.

Kruger's mature work takes the form of large-format photographs — typically appropriated from mass media archives — overlaid with declarative text in a bold Futura Bold italic typeface, in black and white with red accents. Slogans such as 'Your body is a battleground', 'I shop therefore I am', and 'We don't need another hero' address the viewer directly in a language that seems to belong simultaneously to advertising and political manifesto. The work implicates the viewer in the structures it critiques: the pronouns 'you', 'your', 'we', 'I' shift and destabilise, refusing the comfortable distance of critique.

Kruger has exhibited and installed her work at an extraordinary range of scales, from gallery photographs to museum-scaled wrap-around installations and public hoardings. Represented by Mary Boone Gallery and Sprüth Magers, her work has been collected by major museums worldwide and has had a documented influence on graphic design, advertising, and feminist cultural theory that extends far beyond the art world.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Your Body Is a Battleground)
famous_works.notes: A billboard-scale work produced for a Washington D.C. pro-choice march, in which a divided female face frames a text that became a defining slogan of feminist visual culture.

famous_works.title: Untitled (I Shop Therefore I Am)
famous_works.notes: A photograph of a hand holding a shopping bag with Kruger's text, compressing commodity culture, Descartes, and identity into a single photographic object.

famous_works.title: Untitled (We Don't Need Another Hero)
famous_works.notes: A photograph of a girl observing a flexing boy's bicep, in which the text critiques the myths of masculine heroism on which both advertising and political culture depend.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Your Gaze Hits the Side of My Face)
lesser_known_works.notes: A photograph of a classical female bust whose title exposes the violence of the objectifying male gaze within art historical tradition.

lesser_known_works.title: Thinking of You
lesser_known_works.notes: An installation wrapping an entire gallery interior in Kruger's text-and-image language, transforming the exhibition space itself into an environment of mediated address.

artists.bio:
Lynne Drexler was born in Newport News, Virginia in 1928 and studied at the Richmond Professional Institute (now Virginia Commonwealth University), the College of William and Mary, and the Hans Hofmann School in New York and Provincetown, where her encounter with Hofmann's colour theory was decisive for the development of her practice. Working in New York and later on Monhegan Island in Maine, she developed a body of abstract painting that has been recognised only recently as one of the significant achievements of mid-century American Colour Field painting.

Drexler's paintings are characterised by a distinctive technique of short, dense brushstrokes applied in rich, closely valued colour combinations to create mosaic-like surfaces of extraordinary luminosity. Working primarily on small to medium canvases, she builds up chromatic environments of great complexity in which no single colour dominates but the overall effect is one of vibrating, life-affirming warmth. The works recall simultaneously Seurat's pointillism, Klimt's decorative surfaces, and Hofmann's colour theory, while remaining entirely their own.

Drexler died in 1999 with her work largely unrecognised by the mainstream art world. The discovery of a large body of work in her Monhegan Island home after her death, and subsequent exhibitions by Kasmin Gallery in New York, have led to her recognition as an important figure in American abstract painting.

famous_works.title: The Catkin
famous_works.notes: A painting in which Drexler's characteristic short, dense brushstroke technique creates a surface of chromatic richness that demonstrates her mastery of optical colour mixing.

famous_works.title: First Folio
famous_works.notes: An early major work demonstrating the fully developed technique that Drexler would maintain with extraordinary consistency throughout her career.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Gold and Pink)
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which warm gold and pink tones create a mosaic surface of extraordinary luminosity, demonstrating the chromatic sophistication of Drexler's mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Monhegan Island Paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Works made during Drexler's years of relative isolation on the Maine island that maintained her practice at a high level throughout the decades of her critical obscurity.

lesser_known_works.title: Hofmann Study
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work from Drexler's time studying with Hofmann that demonstrates the direct absorption of colour theory principles that underpins her mature approach.

artists.bio:
Minoru Nomata was born in Tokyo in 1955 and studied at Musashino Art University. He has developed a highly distinctive painting practice over four decades centred on the depiction of imaginary architectural structures and objects in watercolour and acrylic — structures that are simultaneously utopian and melancholic, constructive and ruinous, familiar and entirely fantastic.

Nomata's paintings show towers, vessels, engines, bridges, and architectural fantasies rendered with a technical precision that gives them the quality of architectural drawings or scientific illustrations while insisting that the depicted objects exist nowhere but within the painting. The structures are often incomplete, in states of construction or apparent disintegration, and are set against empty horizons or sky backgrounds that give them a monumental isolation. The works engage simultaneously with the tradition of the architectural capriccio — the imaginary building that tests the limits of engineering and conception — and with a specifically contemporary anxiety about built form and its relationship to human aspiration.

Nomata has been exhibited in Japan and internationally, including at Blum & Poe Gallery, and has attracted a devoted following of collectors drawn to the unique visual intelligence and formal consistency of his practice.

famous_works.title: Architecture (Tower Study)
famous_works.notes: A painting of an imaginary tower in Nomata's characteristic precise but fantastical manner, in which technical verisimilitude serves an architectural conception that is purely imaginary.

famous_works.title: Vessel
famous_works.notes: A depiction of an imaginary ship or vessel in which the specificity of the structural details creates a convincing presence for an object that has never been built.

famous_works.title: Construction (Imaginary Bridge)
famous_works.notes: A painting of an enormous bridge structure against an empty sky in which scale and isolation create an image of utopian aspiration and melancholy solitude simultaneously.

lesser_known_works.title: Engine Study
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating the precision of Nomata's depiction of imaginary mechanical structures.

lesser_known_works.title: Lighthouse
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting of an isolated architectural structure whose combination of precision and fantasy is typical of Nomata's mature approach.

artists.bio:
Takesada Matsutani was born in Osaka, Japan in 1937 and became a member of the Gutai Art Association in 1963, joining the movement four years after its founding. Under the influence of Gutai's foundational principle of direct material engagement, he developed a highly distinctive technique: applying vinyl glue to canvas and then manipulating it with his own breath — blowing into the adhesive surface to create bulging, bubble-like protrusions that hardened into permanent three-dimensional forms.

This technique, which Matsutani has pursued and refined across more than fifty years, embeds the artist's own body and breath into the physical structure of the work. The resulting paintings — on canvas or paper, in graphite or oil and graphite — have surfaces of extraordinary subtlety and material presence. In his later practice, the vinyl bubble forms are reduced or elaborated in combination with graphite drawing, creating works of great tonal richness in which dark graphite fields are punctuated by the slight protrusions of the adhesive forms.

Matsutani moved to Paris in 1966 and has lived there since, becoming a bridge between the Gutai tradition and the European context. He is represented by Hauser & Wirth and his work has been exhibited extensively internationally as interest in Gutai has grown. His drawings and paintings are held by major museums in Japan, Europe, and the United States.

famous_works.title: Propagation
famous_works.notes: A work in which vinyl glue manipulated by the artist's breath creates a field of organic protrusions that embeds bodily process directly into the painting's physical structure.

famous_works.title: Flow series
famous_works.notes: Works on paper in which graphite and vinyl glue create densely worked surfaces of extreme tonal subtlety and material depth.

famous_works.title: Stream
famous_works.notes: A large work on paper in which Matsutani's mature graphite technique creates a surface of concentrated darkness punctuated by the subtle materiality of the adhesive forms.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Gutai period works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial Gutai works before Matsutani's departure for Paris that demonstrate the development of his breath-and-vinyl-glue methodology within the group's broader experimental context.

lesser_known_works.title: Hiroshima series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works responding to the memory of Hiroshima in which Matsutani's material process takes on additional ethical and historical dimensions.

artists.bio:
Conrad Shawcross was born in London in 1977 and studied at the Ruskin School of Drawing and Fine Art in Oxford and the Slade School of Fine Art. He developed a practice grounded in a fascination with scientific and philosophical systems — mathematics, optics, mechanics, cosmology — and the way that physical models or machines can illuminate or complicate human understanding of these systems.

Shawcross constructs large-scale kinetic and static sculptures — mechanical arms, rotating light devices, architectural interventions — that embody the principles they explore. His Slow Arc Inside a Cube installations use robotic arms that cast complex, slowly changing light-shadows throughout gallery interiors, making time and perception their subject matter. His recent forays into permanent public installation, including the ADA project at Cern in Geneva and large-scale works for One New Change in London, have extended this investigation into site-specific contexts where architectural programme and sculptural ambition converge.

He has been exhibited at Victoria Miro Gallery and has received major public commissions. His work occupies a productive zone between art, engineering, and scientific enquiry, treating the machine not as industrial critique but as a vehicle for philosophical investigation.

famous_works.title: Slow Arc Inside a Cube IV
famous_works.notes: A robotic arm moving through a cubic framework and casting ever-shifting shadows across gallery walls and floor, making light and time the primary formal materials.

famous_works.title: The Dappled Light of the Sun
famous_works.notes: A permanent steel installation at Dulwich Picture Gallery whose interlocking modules generate patterns that evoke both light refraction and architectural ornament.

famous_works.title: Canopy
famous_works.notes: An installation of illuminated tetrahedra filling a large interior space, creating an environment that recalls both natural forms and mathematical abstractions.

lesser_known_works.title: Chord
lesser_known_works.notes: A three-dimensional rope form suspended in space that encodes a musical interval through its physical structure, connecting acoustic and spatial experience.

lesser_known_works.title: The Nervous System (Elaboration)
lesser_known_works.notes: A mechanical drawing machine that produces complex, quasi-organic patterns, turning its mechanical process into a meditation on authorship and biological systems.

top_lot.title: The Dappled Light of the Sun (Study III) 陽光斑點 - 習作三

top_ratio_lot.title: The Dappled Light of the Sun (Study III) 陽光斑點 - 習作三

artists.bio:
Bob Thompson was born in Louisville, Kentucky in 1937 and studied at the University of Louisville before arriving in New York's bohemian art world in the late 1950s. He became a central figure in the Cedar Tavern and Judson Gallery scene, befriending Larry Rivers, Red Grooms, and other artists committed to maintaining figuration at a moment dominated by Abstract Expressionism. His brief career — he died of a heroin overdose in Rome at twenty-eight — produced a body of work of startling energy and sophistication.

Thompson painted mythological, biblical, and pastoral subjects drawn from the Old Masters — Goya, Rubens, Poussin, Bonnard — transposing their compositions into works in which human figures are rendered in flat, intense colour (deep purple, raw sienna, viridian green) against landscape grounds that oscillate between representational setting and pure colour field. The figures themselves are schematised to silhouette, their colour non-naturalistic, creating an effect of dream and ritual that bridges abstract and figurative concerns.

Thompson's work brings together European painterly tradition and a distinctly American urgency, refracted through the experience of a young Black artist navigating both the freedoms and limitations of the New York art world. His paintings are held by the National Gallery of Art in Washington, the Whitney Museum, and the Studio Museum in Harlem, and sustained critical attention has established him as a significant figure in post-war American art.

famous_works.title: Bacchanal
famous_works.notes: A dense multi-figure composition recasting the classical Bacchanal in Thompson's signature flat, jewel-coloured manner, asserting the relevance of mythological subject matter to contemporary painting.

famous_works.title: Bird by the Sea
famous_works.notes: A late work of quiet intensity in which the ornithological subject meditates on freedom and flight — recurring themes in the work of an African American artist of the civil rights era.

famous_works.title: Crucifixion
famous_works.notes: A religious subject in which the traditional iconography is rendered in Thompson's flat, intensely coloured manner, stripping the scene to its essential formal and emotional elements.

lesser_known_works.title: The Garden of Music
lesser_known_works.notes: A pastoral scene recalling Poussin's Arcadian subjects, in which Thompson's colour-field approach transforms the Old Master source into something entirely his own.

lesser_known_works.title: Homage to Nina Simone
lesser_known_works.notes: A late work reflecting Thompson's deep involvement with jazz and blues culture, the community he inhabited in New York alongside his painterly practice.

artists.bio: Born in Macklin, Saskatchewan, Canada, Martin moved to New York after studies in Oregon and Columbia University, where she taught. She developed her mature practice in the early 1960s: small-scale canvases of pencil-drawn grids on white or off-white paint, the lines hand-drawn with a ruler, carrying the slight irregularity of the human hand within a rigid structure. She was associated with the first generation of Minimalists at Elsworth Kelly's building on Coenties Slip in Lower Manhattan, though she rejected the Minimalist label, insisting on the emotional and spiritual dimension of her work. She left New York abruptly in 1967 following the death of Ad Reinhardt and a breakdown, and moved to New Mexico, where she lived in self-built adobe houses and did not paint for six years. The late works are horizontal bands of pale colour — pink, blue, grey — of extraordinary refinement.

famous_works.title: The Islands I–XII
famous_works.notes: A series of small square canvases — the grid at its most intimate and complete. The islands of the title are the canvases themselves.

famous_works.title: With My Back to the World
famous_works.notes: A late work of pale horizontal bands. The title is a statement of position — withdrawal from the social world as the condition of the work.

famous_works.title: Friendship
famous_works.notes: Gold leaf on canvas crossed by thin pencil grid lines. The warmth of the gold and the severity of the grid — the emotional and structural in productive tension.

lesser_known_works.title: Happy Holiday
lesser_known_works.notes: A late canvas of yellow bands — an unusually warm, light work for Martin. The title's unironic positivity and the painting's quiet pleasure are matching.

lesser_known_works.title: Grass
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the last works before the New Mexico departure — thin horizontal lines on pale ground. The landscape of the title is implicit in the colour and the horizon.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled #10
lesser_known_works.notes: A return to grid format after the horizontal-bands period of the 1970s and 1980s. The grid reinterpreted in softer, more atmospheric terms than the early Manhattan works.

artists.bio:
Juan Muñoz was born in Madrid in 1953 and studied at the Croydon College of Art in London and the Pratt Graphic Center in New York before settling in Madrid, where his work brought him significant critical attention from the late 1980s. He died suddenly of heart failure in 2001 at forty-eight, leaving a body of work of extraordinary formal and conceptual richness.

Muñoz's sculptures and installations occupy a space between the human presence and its absence: figures of resin, papier-maché, and bronze populate his spaces in groups, their scale subtly wrong, their faces and expressions ambiguous or absent. They seem to be engaged in private conversation, in silent laughter, in states of concentrated attention to something the viewer cannot access. Muñoz was profoundly interested in the nature of theatrical space and the relationship between performer, audience, and the architectural container of experience, and his installations consistently construct scenes whose narrative is withheld.

He was selected to represent Spain at the Venice Biennale in 1997 and his Tate Modern commission Double Bind, installed in the Turbine Hall in 2001, was one of the most significant large-scale commissions in the history of that institution. He is represented posthumously by Marian Goodman Gallery and his work is held by major museums internationally.

famous_works.title: Double Bind
famous_works.notes: A monumental Turbine Hall commission at Tate Modern in which floors, elevators, and figures create a labyrinthine spatial narrative about presence, absence, and the impossibility of meeting.

famous_works.title: Many Times
famous_works.notes: An installation of thirty bronze figures in Chinese costume engaged in silent laughter, populating a large gallery space with an uncanny social life from which the viewer is excluded.

famous_works.title: Conversation Piece series
famous_works.notes: Small groups of figures arranged in configurations suggesting intimate conversation, the figures' scale and bodily ambiguity generating an atmosphere of theatrical intimacy.

lesser_known_works.title: The Wasteland
lesser_known_works.notes: An early installation in which balconies and handrails create a theatrical architecture of presence and absence, establishing the spatial concerns that would define Muñoz's mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: First Banister
lesser_known_works.notes: A staircase element to which a figure seems to cling, demonstrating Muñoz's early interest in architecture as a framework for psychological and theatrical narrative.

artists.bio: Born in Knoxville, Tennessee, Gribbon studied at the School of Visual Arts in New York. She paints domestic interiors and intimate moments — her partner and family in scenes of reading, sleeping, working, watching television — with a clear art-historical awareness (references to Vallotton, Hopper, and Vermeer are structurally present) but a contemporary frankness that is distinctly her own. The paintings insist on the ordinary as a subject worthy of the sustained attention traditionally reserved for the monumental: a woman watching a screen, a figure reflected in a window, a child asleep in an armchair. The light is specific and photographic but the paint handling is deliberate and Old Master in its patience. Her market trajectory has been rapid: in the dataset, Set List Focus sold at 8.06× estimate — one of the most dramatic outperformances — signalling strong speculative and institutional interest.

famous_works.title: Set List Focus
famous_works.notes: A figure in intimate domestic light — the 8.06× outperformer in the dataset. The painting's quietness makes the market response the loudest element of its history.

famous_works.title: It Doesn't Always Look Like This
famous_works.notes: A self-portrait reflected in a window, the sitter's gaze directed at the viewer through the glass. Vermeer's window light processed through a contemporary domestic moment.

famous_works.title: A Whole New Kind of Morning
famous_works.notes: Her partner reads in morning light — the quiet moment rendered with the formal attention of a seventeenth-century genre painting translated into a present-tense domestic space.

lesser_known_works.title: Lit at Night
lesser_known_works.notes: A figure illuminated by a screen in a dark room. The contemporary light source that Hopper's windows have been replaced by — the painting thinks about what has changed.

lesser_known_works.title: The Voyager Has Landed
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work that established the intimate scale and domestic subject matter before the larger canvases of the mature period.

lesser_known_works.title: Slipping Into Fiction
lesser_known_works.notes: A figure absorbed in a book — the act of reading as the subject of a painting. The reader disappearing into language while the painter watches.

artists.bio:
David Park was born in Boston in 1911 and moved to San Francisco in 1929, where he spent most of his working life teaching at the California School of Arts and Crafts and later at the University of California Berkeley. In 1950, he made one of the more dramatic gestures in post-war American art history: he drove to the Berkeley city dump and discarded all his Abstract Expressionist paintings, announcing his return to figuration at a moment when doing so meant certain marginalisation within the dominant New York discourse.

Park became the central figure of the Bay Area Figurative Movement, a loose grouping that included Elmer Bischoff and Richard Diebenkorn, who responded to their Northern California environment by insisting on the figure and the observable world as primary subjects while maintaining the painterly energy and gestural directness of Abstract Expressionism. His paintings of figures at the beach, musicians, bathers, and domestic subjects are remarkable for their combination of raw physical paint application and a peculiar, saturated light — the golden light of the Bay Area absorbed into the paint surface itself.

Park died of cancer in 1960 at forty-nine, limiting his mature work to a decade. His paintings are held by the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art and other major institutions, and he is recognised as an essential figure in the history of post-war American figuration.

famous_works.title: Kids on Bikes
famous_works.notes: The first major figurative work painted after Park's famous discarding of his abstract paintings, a joyful and formally confident declaration of his return to observable subject matter.

famous_works.title: Bather with Knee Up
famous_works.notes: A figure at the water's edge rendered in saturated colour with gestural brushwork that fuses the Abstract Expressionist heritage with insistent physical presence.

famous_works.title: Four Men
famous_works.notes: A group of male figures in a landscape rendered in the compressed, weighty manner of Park's late work, in which figures become almost geological presences.

lesser_known_works.title: Standing Male Figure
lesser_known_works.notes: A late figurative study in which Park reduces the figure to its essential weight and volume, approaching abstraction through concentration rather than through abandonment of the subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Interior with Two Figures
lesser_known_works.notes: A domestic scene in which the Bay Area light suffuses an ordinary interior with a chromatic richness that elevates the quotidian to the painterly.

artists.bio:
Scott Kahn was born in New York City in 1946 and studied at the Art Students League and the Brooklyn Museum School. He developed a figurative painting practice that has been described as both naïve and sophisticated — a category-defying body of work that depicts dreamlike pastoral and domestic scenes in a manner that combines the formal simplicity of folk art with a genuine painterly intelligence.

Kahn's paintings depict American landscapes, country houses, gardens, and domestic interiors in a style of deliberate flatness and formal simplification that produces images of considerable psychological strangeness. His colour is intense but non-naturalistic, his spatial organisation more emotional than logical, and the result is painting that is simultaneously familiar and uncanny — recognisably American in its subject matter but displaced from realism by a formal vision that insists on its own emotional truth over visual accuracy.

His work spent decades largely outside the mainstream gallery system before being taken up by Karma Gallery in New York and attracting significant critical and collector attention from a generation of younger painters who recognised in his approach a model for how figuration could operate outside the dominant critical frameworks. Kahn's late-found recognition has become an emblem of the art world's tendency to overlook artists whose work doesn't fit prevailing theoretical narratives.

famous_works.title: Country House
famous_works.notes: A painting of an American domestic exterior in which Kahn's simplified, flattened forms and intense non-naturalistic colour create an image that oscillates between nostalgia and unease.

famous_works.title: Landscape with Figures
famous_works.notes: A pastoral composition in which simplified human figures inhabit a landscape of simplified but psychologically charged natural forms.

famous_works.title: Interior (Fireplace)
famous_works.notes: A domestic interior scene demonstrating Kahn's particular ability to create an image of warmth and habitation through the most economic formal means.

lesser_known_works.title: Early New York period
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from Kahn's initial artistic period that demonstrate the development of his simplified figurative language.

lesser_known_works.title: Garden series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of outdoor garden and landscape scenes demonstrating Kahn's sustained engagement with American domestic pastoral subjects.

artists.bio: Born in San Francisco, Serra worked in steel mills during college summers — the material knowledge that became his artistic medium. He studied at UC Santa Barbara, UC Berkeley, and Yale (BA and MFA), then in Florence and Paris on a Yale traveling fellowship. His early props and lead splashes (1969) established a practice in which the physical properties of materials and the force of gravity are the subject: lead rolled from one direction meets itself, propped sheets lean against walls, and weight and balance are made simultaneously visible and precarious. His mature large-scale weathered-steel sculptures — the Torqued Ellipses (from 1996) and the East-West/West-East installation in Qatar (2014) — create experiences of enclosed and open space that make the body's movement through space the work. He remained active until his death in March 2024.

famous_works.title: Tilted Arc
famous_works.notes: A curved weathered steel wall installed in Federal Plaza, New York. Removed in 1989 after a public controversy — the most contested public art installation in American history.

famous_works.title: Torqued Ellipses I, II and III
famous_works.notes: Three steel sculptures whose interior and exterior describe different spatial experiences simultaneously. Made possible only with computer assistance — the first large works where digital design preceded physical fabrication.

famous_works.title: The Matter of Time
famous_works.notes: Eight massive sculptures permanently installed in the Guggenheim Bilbao. Walking through the installation is to walk through a spatial autobiography of Serra's formal preoccupations over forty years.

lesser_known_works.title: Splash Piece: Casting
lesser_known_works.notes: Molten lead thrown into the junction of floor and wall, then removed, creating a negative cast of the angle. The corner of a room as sculpture; process as form.

lesser_known_works.title: Hand Catching Lead
lesser_known_works.notes: A film of a hand catching and failing to catch falling lead splinters. Process documented rather than frozen — the action as the work.

lesser_known_works.title: Circuit
lesser_known_works.notes: Four steel plates placed in the four corners of a large room, visible only from specific positions. The room's geometry activated by the plates' placement — the space of the gallery as material.

artists.bio:
Hannah Höch was born in Gotha, Thuringia in 1889 and moved to Berlin in 1912 to study at the Kunstgewerbeschule, where she remained except for a wartime return to Gotha. She became a central figure in the Berlin Dada movement, participating in the legendary First International Dada Fair in 1920 alongside Raoul Hausmann (her long-term partner), George Grosz, John Heartfield, and others. She was the only woman in this milieu, and her position within it was complicated by the misogyny of several of her fellow Dadaists.

Höch's major contribution was the technique of photomontage, which she developed with extraordinary formal and political sophistication. Her Cut with the Kitchen Knife Dada through the Last Weimar Beer-Belly Cultural Epoch of Germany — exhibited at the First Dada Fair — remains one of the defining works of both Dada and the broader history of montage: a vast, seething collage of images from mass media that constructs a satirical panorama of Weimar Germany's technological modernity and political volatility.

From the mid-1920s, Höch developed a series of works on the 'New Woman' — the modern, independent female figure of Weimar culture — that examine with equal parts celebration and critique the promises and contradictions of female emancipation. She lived quietly outside Berlin during the Nazi period, hiding her collection of Dada works, and resumed exhibiting after the war, receiving belated recognition as a foundational figure of both Dada and feminist art history.

famous_works.title: Cut with the Kitchen Knife Dada through the Last Weimar Beer-Belly Cultural Epoch of Germany
famous_works.notes: A massive photomontage constructing a satirical panorama of Weimar Germany from mass media imagery, a foundational work of Dada and the history of political montage.

famous_works.title: The Beautiful Girl
famous_works.notes: A photomontage in which a woman's body is assembled from advertising images and machine parts, critically examining the commodification of the female body in consumer culture.

famous_works.title: From an Ethnographic Museum series
famous_works.notes: Montages combining African sculptural artefacts with contemporary European images of women that examine colonial appropriation and the construction of gender and race.

lesser_known_works.title: Love
lesser_known_works.notes: A photomontage exploring the representation of romantic relationships in mass media, part of Höch's sustained investigation of Weimar popular culture.

lesser_known_works.title: Journalist
lesser_known_works.notes: A photomontage figure from the New Woman series that celebrates and examines the emergence of the independent professional woman in Weimar Germany.

top_lot.title: Bürgerliches Brautpaar (Streit)
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Josef Albers was born in Bottrop, Westphalia in 1888 and trained as a teacher before enrolling at the Bauhaus in Weimar in 1920. He progressed from student to faculty, teaching the Vorkurs (preliminary course) and the stained glass workshop, remaining at the school through its moves to Dessau and Berlin until its closure by the Nazis in 1933. He emigrated to the United States, where Black Mountain College in North Carolina had been alerted to his availability and immediately invited him to run its art programme.

Albers's teaching at Black Mountain College (1933–49) and subsequently at Yale School of Art (1950–58) was transformative for American art education and through it, for American art. His Bauhaus-derived emphasis on perceptual investigation, material experimentation, and the primacy of visual phenomena over academic convention shaped generations of students including Rauschenberg, Noland, and Frankenthaler.

His own practice culminated in the Homage to the Square series, begun in 1949 and continued until his death in 1976: square canvases in which three or four nested squares of flat colour generate optical effects of extraordinary complexity, the apparent spatial relationships between the squares shifting with every change of colour combination. The series — comprising more than a thousand works — constitutes the most sustained investigation of colour interaction in the history of painting, summarised in his theoretical text Interaction of Color (1963).

famous_works.title: Homage to the Square: Appearing
famous_works.notes: A representative work from the defining series in which nested squares of precisely chosen colours produce spatial and chromatic interactions of remarkable complexity from the most elementary formal means.

famous_works.title: Interaction of Color
famous_works.notes: Not a painting but a book-portfolio, this landmark theoretical text presenting colour perception experiments became one of the most influential works of colour theory in the history of art.

famous_works.title: Structural Constellation series
famous_works.notes: A parallel series to the Homage to the Square, in which line drawings create ambiguous spatial constructions that test the viewer's ability to maintain a single spatial reading.

lesser_known_works.title: Variant/Adobe series
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings made between the Bauhaus and the Homage to the Square series, in which rectangular forms are arranged in colour investigations that prefigure the later, more systematic approach.

lesser_known_works.title: Glass and Metalwork (Bauhaus period)
lesser_known_works.notes: Objects made during Albers's Bauhaus teaching that demonstrate the material intelligence and perceptual investigation that underlies his subsequent commitment to colour painting.

artists.bio:
Chantal Joffe was born in St Albans in 1969 and studied at the Glasgow School of Art before completing an MA at the Royal College of Art. She came to prominence in the early 2000s with large-scale paintings of women and girls rendered in a rapid, gestural manner that balances psychological penetration with formal openness. Her practice has developed consistently across decades, maintaining figuration as its absolute centre while absorbing and transforming influences from Expressionism, Matisse, and the psychological portraiture of Gwen John and Paula Modersohn-Becker.

Joffe typically works from photographs and direct observation, constructing images of her subjects — often her daughter, mother, friends, and herself — that are simultaneously intimate and formally rigorous. Her brushwork is confident and summary: she arrives at a likeness through accumulation rather than refinement, and the unresolved passages in her paintings are as expressive as the precisely observed ones. Women in domestic spaces, children in gardens, self-portraits in the studio — these subjects are treated with a serious attention that refuses the hierarchies that have historically undervalued intimate and feminine subject matter.

Joffe has been exhibited extensively at Victoria Miro Gallery in London and has been the subject of solo exhibitions at institutions including the Scottish National Portrait Gallery. Her practice stands as one of the most committed and consistent investigations of figurative painting in contemporary British art.

famous_works.title: Esme and I in the Studio
famous_works.notes: A large self-portrait with her daughter in which the intimacy of the mother-child relationship is rendered with the same formal rigour Joffe brings to her more distanced subjects.

famous_works.title: Self-Portrait in Gwen John's Coat
famous_works.notes: A self-portrait in which Joffe wears the coat depicted in Gwen John's famous painting, making explicit the line of feminist art historical inheritance she inhabits.

famous_works.title: The Women
famous_works.notes: A large-scale multi-figure composition in which women of various ages occupy a shared space, asserting the variety and substance of female experience as pictorial subject.

lesser_known_works.title: My Mother on Holiday
lesser_known_works.notes: An intimate portrait of Joffe's mother in a holiday setting that demonstrates her particular ability to make the familiar strange through formal attention.

lesser_known_works.title: Vashti (Blue Dress)
lesser_known_works.notes: A portrait of a specific individual that balances personality with the formal qualities of colour, pose, and paint surface.

artists.bio:
Peter Beard was born in New York City in 1938 into a wealthy family and studied at Yale University. He first visited Africa in 1955 and spent much of his life there, developing a practice centred on the wildlife and landscape of Kenya — particularly the Tsavo region — that combined documentary photography with diary-keeping, collage, and drawing into a hybrid form that occupied a unique position between art and activism.

Beard's large-scale works combine his own photographs with found imagery, animal blood, newspaper, insects, and his own handwritten texts to create densely layered objects that function as diaries, manifestos, and artworks simultaneously. His documentation of the overpopulation and mass death of elephants in Tsavo in the early 1960s — published in his seminal book The End of the Game (1965) — combined photographic evidence with a personal, passionate engagement that was both artistically innovative and politically significant.

Beard associated with the creative figures of his time across art, fashion, and literature — Andy Warhol, Francis Bacon, Karen Blixen, the Rolling Stones — and this position at the intersection of art and social spectacle complicated critical assessment of his work. He disappeared near his property in Montauk, New York in 2020 and was found dead some weeks later. His estate maintains a significant archive.

famous_works.title: The End of the Game series
famous_works.notes: Large-format photographs of the mass death of elephants in Kenya's Tsavo National Park, combining documentary evidence with a passionate engagement that initiated the contemporary wildlife conservation photography tradition.

famous_works.title: Diary collages
famous_works.notes: Dense diary-format works combining photography, drawing, handwritten text, animal blood, and found materials into hybrid objects that are simultaneously personal record and public statement.

famous_works.title: Africa portfolio
famous_works.notes: Large-scale African wildlife photographs overworked with drawing and text that established Beard's approach to the combination of photographic and pictorial elements.

lesser_known_works.title: Eyelids of Morning collaboration
lesser_known_works.notes: A photographic collaboration with writer Alistair Graham documenting the Nile crocodile that demonstrates Beard's integration of ecological science and artistic practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Yale Sketchbooks
lesser_known_works.notes: Artist books from Beard's formative period that establish the diary-collage methodology he would develop throughout his career.

artists.bio: Born in Port Arthur, Texas, Rauschenberg studied at Black Mountain College under Josef Albers and John Cage before arriving in New York. He developed the 'Combines' (1954–64) — works that incorporated objects from daily life (stuffed animals, tires, clothing, photographs, street detritus) into painterly surfaces, making the distinction between painting and sculpture moot. He was among the first artists to collaborate with choreographers (Merce Cunningham, Paul Taylor) and the first to explore the relationship between art and technology institutionally (Experiments in Art and Technology, 1966). He won the Venice Biennale Grand Prize in 1964 — the moment American Pop and Conceptual art were officially consecrated by the European establishment. His Tibetan Buddhism, his gay identity (publicly declared), and his Texan populism were all present in the work, which remains among the most generative in post-war American art.

famous_works.title: Bed
famous_works.notes: His own quilt, pillow, and sheet hung vertically on a stretcher and smeared with toothpaste, paint, and nail varnish. The painting as domestic object, the domestic object as painting.

famous_works.title: Monogram
famous_works.notes: A stuffed angora goat with a tire around its middle, standing on a painted combine canvas. The most famous of the Combines — sculpture, painting, and absurdist theatre simultaneously.

famous_works.title: Erased de Kooning Drawing
famous_works.notes: Rauschenberg visited de Kooning and asked for a drawing he could erase. De Kooning chose a difficult, multiply-media drawing. The act of erasure as the art — Dada's destruction turned into a relationship.

lesser_known_works.title: White Paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Pure white canvases that catch shadow and light — precursors to John Cage's 4'33" in their creation of a field for ambient incident rather than a determined composition.

lesser_known_works.title: Barge
lesser_known_works.notes: A monumental transfer drawing — newspaper and magazine images transferred to canvas using solvent. The 22-foot composite of American imagery is the most ambitious single surface in the transfer series.

lesser_known_works.title: Retroactive I
lesser_known_works.notes: Kennedy's photograph layered with an astronaut and a hand-signaling figure. The transfer technique applied to the imagery of the 1960s American moment — assassination and space race in the same picture.

artists.bio:
Maria Taniguchi was born in Bacolod City, Philippines in 1981 and studied at the University of the Philippines and the Université de Paris VIII. She has developed a rigorous minimal painting practice centred on the repetitive brushwork construction of large-scale fields of modulated darkness — works in which thousands or hundreds of thousands of individual brushstrokes in black and dark grey paint build up surfaces of extraordinary density and subtle variation.

Taniguchi's paintings are made by applying small, methodical brick-like brushstrokes in patterns that accumulate over time into fields that, seen at a distance, read as near-monochrome but, approached closely, reveal the incredible intricacy of their construction. The works engage the history of both Abstract Expressionism and Minimalism — the physical labour of Pollock's drip, the reductive ambition of Ad Reinhardt's black paintings — while being rooted in a specifically Southeast Asian practice that connects sustained manual repetition to meditative and social labour traditions.

She has been exhibited at Silverlens Galleries in Manila and internationally, and has been included in major regional and international exhibitions. Taniguchi represents a significant voice in contemporary Asian art's ongoing engagement with the traditions of Western abstraction.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Large Black)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale painting built from hundreds of thousands of tiny black brushstrokes in which the sustained manual labour of construction becomes the work's primary subject and formal achievement.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Gridded Dark)
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which the brick-like pattern of Taniguchi's brushwork creates a subtle grid that mediates between the gridded minimalism of Judd and the painterly surface of gestural abstraction.

famous_works.title: Installation at Yokohama Triennale
famous_works.notes: A large-scale installation of multiple paintings that demonstrates the cumulative visual and physical impact of Taniguchi's labour-intensive practice in a major institutional context.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Grid paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial works establishing Taniguchi's brushwork methodology in smaller-scale canvases that demonstrate the development of her mature approach.

lesser_known_works.title: White Variations
lesser_known_works.notes: A departure from Taniguchi's characteristic dark palette that applies her methodical brushwork construction to lighter tones, demonstrating the formal range within her system.

artists.bio:
Otto Mueller was born in Liebau, Silesia (now Lubawka, Poland) in 1874 and studied at the Dresden Academy of Fine Arts before joining Die Brücke in 1910 — unusually, invited to join rather than being a founder. He was the oldest member of the group and his visual sensibility, more lyrical and less confrontational than Kirchner or Schmidt-Rottluff, gave his work a distinctive character within the Expressionist milieu.

Mueller worked primarily with distemper (a water-based tempera) rather than oil, a medium whose matte, fresco-like quality gave his canvases a warmth and softness quite different from the aggressive impasto of some Die Brücke contemporaries. His subjects were above all the nude in landscape — figures bathing in lakes and rivers, reclining in dunes and grasses — treated with a simplified elongation of form and a muted warmth of colour that relates his figures to the Egyptian and archaic sources he admired. His gypsies and his landscape subjects are among the most immediately beautiful productions of German Expressionism.

Mueller taught at the Breslau Academy until 1933, when he was dismissed by the Nazis. His work appeared in the Degenerate Art exhibition of 1937, and many of his paintings were confiscated from German museums.

famous_works.title: Two Nudes in the Dunes
famous_works.notes: A distemper painting of two female bathers in a landscape in which the elongated simplification of the figures and the warm muted tones create an image of Arcadian harmony.

famous_works.title: Bathers in the Pond
famous_works.notes: An early Die Brücke period painting demonstrating Mueller's distinctive approach to the nude-in-landscape subject within the group's shared formal concerns.

famous_works.title: Gypsy Women with Sunflowers
famous_works.notes: A painting of Romani women in a landscape that demonstrates Mueller's ongoing engagement with gypsies as subjects of both formal interest and ethnographic attention.

lesser_known_works.title: Yellow Nudes
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting in which Mueller's characteristic warm palette and elongated figures are presented with particular chromatic intensity.

lesser_known_works.title: Boy with Cat
lesser_known_works.notes: A smaller-scale figurative work demonstrating Mueller's lyrical approach to intimate subjects outside his more characteristic bather compositions.

top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

top_ratio_lot.title: Sitzender Frauenakt mit Bäumen und Hügeln

artists.bio:
Damien Hirst was born in Bristol in 1965 and grew up in Leeds before studying at Goldsmiths College in London, where he organised the Freeze exhibition in 1988 — a landmark moment for what came to be called the Young British Artists. By the early 1990s, he had become the defining public figure of a generation of British artists who deployed shock, spectacle, and media savvy to make art that was simultaneously critically engaged and commercially spectacular.

Hirst's most significant works are his Natural History series — animals preserved in formaldehyde in steel and glass vitrines — and his spin paintings and spot paintings, which exist in large editions. The shark of The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living and the bisected cow and calf of Mother and Child Divided are among the most discussed works of late twentieth-century art, their confrontation of mortality, beauty, and commodity spectacle generating both genuine philosophical engagement and considerable suspicion about their depth. The diamond-encrusted skull For the Love of God (2007) extended these investigations into the territory of extreme luxury.

Hirst is represented by White Cube and his own company Science Ltd. A celebrated auction of his work directly through Sotheby's in 2008 raised unprecedented sums and coincided almost exactly with the global financial crisis — a piece of dark timing that many commentators found apt. His work has been the subject of major retrospectives at institutions including Tate Modern.

famous_works.title: The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living
famous_works.notes: A tiger shark preserved in formaldehyde in a steel and glass vitrine, the foundational work of Hirst's Natural History series and a defining object of 1990s British art.

famous_works.title: Mother and Child Divided
famous_works.notes: A cow and calf bisected and displayed in four vitrines, winner of the Turner Prize and a key work in contemporary art's engagement with biological life and institutional display.

famous_works.title: For the Love of God
famous_works.notes: A platinum cast of an eighteenth-century human skull set with 8,601 diamonds, posing questions about mortality, luxury, and value with maximal theatrical impact.

lesser_known_works.title: A Thousand Years
lesser_known_works.notes: A steel and glass vitrine containing a fly-breeding box, a rotting cow's head, and an insect-o-cutor, creating a life cycle of reproduction and death without the artist's intervention.

lesser_known_works.title: Pharmacy
lesser_known_works.notes: An installation replicating the interior of a British pharmacy in its entirety, making the aesthetics of medicine and the culture of health-consumption identical terms.

artists.bio:
Loie Hollowell was born in Woodland, California in 1983 and studied at the Savannah College of Art and Design, the New Hampshire Institute of Art, and the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. She came to prominence in the mid-2010s with paintings that combine geometric abstraction — circles, ovals, bulging forms — with a symbolic vocabulary derived from the female body, sexuality, and reproduction, creating works of singular visual and conceptual density.

Hollowell's paintings present vulvic, ovoid, and phallic forms in luminous radiant colour fields that combine the sacred geometry of mandala traditions with an insistently corporeal subject matter. The works are executed in oil and acrylic with a smooth, highly finished surface that gives the colour — typically warm pinks, oranges, purples, and yellows — an interior luminosity. Their formal beauty and their explicit sexual symbolism create a productive tension: they are at once meditative and physical, abstract and bodily.

Her exhibitions at Pace Gallery have attracted considerable critical and collector interest, and her work has been collected by major institutions. Hollowell represents a significant contribution to contemporary painting's ongoing investigation of the female body, sexuality, and the spiritual dimension of physical experience.

famous_works.title: Spread Eagle in Violet, Orange, and Pink
famous_works.notes: A large painting in which vulvic geometry and radiant colour combine to create an image that is simultaneously abstract mandala and explicit bodily form.

famous_works.title: High Tide
famous_works.notes: A painting in which rounded, swelling forms in warm colour create an image of bodily expansion that refers simultaneously to erotic and maternal experience.

famous_works.title: Second Trimester
famous_works.notes: A painting made during the artist's pregnancy in which the bodily changes of gestation are rendered through geometric abstraction as a form of visual autobiography.

lesser_known_works.title: Early circle works
lesser_known_works.notes: The initial paintings in which Hollowell developed her vocabulary of geometric forms related to the female body.

lesser_known_works.title: Day 1
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting related to the artist's experience of childbirth in which bodily extremity is translated into formal intensity.

top_lot.title: Linked Lingam in brown-yellow and maroon

top_ratio_lot.title: Linked Lingam in brown-yellow and maroon

artists.bio:
Jean Hélion was born in Couterne, Normandy in 1904 and arrived in Paris in the early 1920s, initially working as a chemist and architect's draughtsman while painting in his spare time. He became one of the leading figures of European geometric abstraction in the early 1930s, associated with the Abstraction-Création group alongside Mondrian, Arp, Calder, and Vantongerloo, producing paintings of rigorous formal organisation in which curved and angular forms generate a spatial dynamism of considerable elegance.

In 1939, in a move as scandalous within the abstract art world as Picasso's turn to classical figuration had been to the Cubists, Hélion abruptly returned to figurative painting. Captured during the German Occupation, he escaped from a German prisoner-of-war camp and made his way back to France — an experience he documented in the memoir They Shall Not Have Me. His postwar figurative paintings of street vendors, pedestrians, and bakers in Paris are among the most original figurative works of the 1940s and 1950s, their compositional structure informed by his abstract period while their subject matter insists on the everyday as worthy of sustained attention.

Hélion was a significant transatlantic figure, spending considerable time in the United States and exerting influence in both directions across the Atlantic. His work is held by the Musée National d'Art Moderne and major collections, and has attracted growing critical appreciation.

famous_works.title: Composition
famous_works.notes: A major abstract work from Hélion's Abstraction-Création period in which biomorphic and geometric forms create a spatial dynamic of elegant formal complexity.

famous_works.title: Fallen Figure
famous_works.notes: A pivotal transitional work in which abstraction and figuration are held in tension at the precise moment of Hélion's return to the human subject.

famous_works.title: Man at the Newspaper Kiosk
famous_works.notes: A post-war figurative painting in which the ordinary activity of reading a newspaper is elevated through the compositional intelligence of Hélion's abstract period.

lesser_known_works.title: Équilibre (Balance)
lesser_known_works.notes: An abstract canvas from the height of Hélion's Abstraction-Création involvement, in which the balance of forms across the picture plane gives the work its title and its formal principle.

lesser_known_works.title: Le Boulanger (The Baker)
lesser_known_works.notes: A figurative painting of a Parisian tradesperson that demonstrates the warm, observational quality of Hélion's return to representation in the post-war decade.

top_lot.title: Equilibre
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Sadamasa Motonaga was born in Iga, Mie Prefecture in 1922 and became a founding member of the Gutai Art Association in 1955, the group founded by Jiro Yoshihara that became one of the most significant movements in post-war Japanese art. Gutai's founding principle — 'do what has never been done before' — encouraged artists to engage with materials in unprecedented ways, and Motonaga's contributions to Gutai exhibitions were among the most formally inventive.

Monotaga's best-known work involves coloured liquids: in 1956 he suspended plastic tubes filled with coloured water and oil from the ceiling of the Gutai exhibition space, creating installations in which the movement of the liquid and the transparency of the plastic created colour experiences that were simultaneously painterly and sculptural, optical and physical. His later paintings on canvas maintained the interest in colour as a liquid, atmospheric substance — he poured and dripped oil paint to create surfaces of flowing, pooling colour that recall simultaneously Abstract Expressionism, Colour Field painting, and the specific material investigations of Gutai.
