Monotaga's work was exhibited internationally within the Gutai context, including famously in France through Michel Tapié's connections. His contribution to the history of process-based art and environmental installation has been increasingly recognised since the major international reassessment of Gutai from the 1990s onward.

famous_works.title: Work (Tubes)
famous_works.notes: An installation of plastic tubes filled with coloured liquids suspended from gallery ceiling — a work of revolutionary material and spatial innovation within the Gutai context.

famous_works.title: Work (Pour Paintings)
famous_works.notes: Large canvases in which oil paint is poured and pooled to create surfaces of flowing colour that connect Gutai's material investigation to the global colour abstraction conversation.

famous_works.title: Sky Series
famous_works.notes: Late paintings of atmospheric colour that demonstrate the sustained development of Motonaga's investigation of colour as liquid and luminous phenomenon.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Gutai works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial works from Motonaga's participation in the foundational Gutai exhibitions that demonstrate his particular material approach within the group's broader programme.

lesser_known_works.title: Outdoor Installation works
lesser_known_works.notes: Works created for outdoor settings that extend Motonaga's material investigations into environmental and spatial contexts.

top_lot.title: Vertical and Horizontal Fillet Black White 垂直與水平圓角 - 黑白

top_ratio_lot.title: Vertical and Horizontal Fillet Black White 垂直與水平圓角 - 黑白

artists.bio:
Katharina Grosse was born in Freiburg im Breisgau, Germany in 1961 and studied at the Kunstakademie in Düsseldorf and later in Münster. She came to international prominence in the late 1990s and 2000s with her large-scale spray-paint installations, in which she uses an industrial paint spray gun to apply acrylic paint directly to gallery floors, walls, and ceilings, as well as to found objects — soil, rocks, furniture, architectural fragments — creating all-encompassing chromatic environments that dissolve conventional distinctions between painting and its support.

Grosse's installations do not respect the boundaries of the gallery wall or the limits of the canvas: paint spills from one surface to another, crosses corners, spreads across floor and ceiling, follows the contours of piled earth or crumpled fabric. The effect is simultaneously overwhelming and carefully considered: her colour choices and application patterns create works of great visual sophistication despite the apparent disorder of their making. The individual brushstroke is replaced by the spray as a mark-making tool, and the scale of her interventions is often architectural.

She has been commissioned for major installations at institutions including the Massachusetts Museum of Contemporary Art (MASS MoCA), the Australian Museum in Sydney, and the Nationalgalerie in Berlin. Grosse is represented by Gagosian Gallery and her practice stands as one of the most consequential investigations of painting's spatial and environmental possibilities.

famous_works.title: Rockaway!
famous_works.notes: A large-scale installation at MoMA PS1 in which spray paint is applied to building fragments, soil, and the architectural space itself, creating an immersive chromatic environment.

famous_works.title: Wunderblock (MASS MoCA)
famous_works.notes: A monumental installation filling the largest gallery at MASS MoCA with painted soil, fabric, and architectural elements in an overwhelming environment of colour and material.

famous_works.title: It Wasn't Us
famous_works.notes: A painting and installation exhibition in which Grosse's spray paint extends across large canvases and gallery architecture in a synthesis of painting and spatial installation.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled Trumpet
lesser_known_works.notes: An early room installation that established Grosse's method of applying spray paint directly to architectural surfaces, dissolving the conventional boundary of the canvas.

lesser_known_works.title: One Floor Up More Highly
lesser_known_works.notes: An installation in Berlin in which spray paint transforms a disused building interior into a work of extraordinary chromatic complexity.

artists.bio:
Franz West was born in Vienna in 1947 and came of age in the intellectual milieu of Viennese Actionism, though his own practice took a very different direction: toward sculpture, furniture, and objects that question the boundaries between art and utility, formal presentation and informal use. Largely self-taught, he began making his Passstücke (Adaptives) in the late 1970s — small, lumpy plaster objects sized to be held, worn, or balanced against the body by gallery visitors, activating and completing the work through physical participation.

West developed these ideas into larger-scale sculptures and furniture that similarly demanded activation: sculptures to sit on, paper-maché and wire constructions in bright colours that look simultaneously childish and formally sophisticated, collaborative installations with friends and collaborators. His work consistently refuses the preciousness and distance of conventional sculpture, insisting on contact, use, and the social interaction that objects designed for bodies necessarily produce.

West won the Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement at the 2011 Venice Biennale. He was represented by Gagosian Gallery and his work is held by major museums worldwide. His legacy extends beyond individual works to an attitude toward the relationship between art objects and their audience that has influenced numerous younger artists.

famous_works.title: Passstücke (Adaptives)
famous_works.notes: Small plaster objects sized for bodily use by gallery visitors, in which the sculpture is completed only through physical contact and activation by the audience.

famous_works.title: Epoxy works
famous_works.notes: Large-scale painted epoxy sculptures of bulbous, lumpy forms in bright colours that combine formal playfulness with a material directness that resists aesthetic idealisation.

famous_works.title: Furniture Works
famous_works.notes: Wicker, steel, and found-material furniture that functions simultaneously as art and seating, collapsing the distinction between the aesthetic and the utilitarian.

lesser_known_works.title: Auditorium
lesser_known_works.notes: An installation in which West's characteristic furniture-sculptures are arranged into a collective seating arrangement that transforms the gallery into a social space.

lesser_known_works.title: Collages
lesser_known_works.notes: A body of works on paper in which magazine images are rearranged and annotated in a manner that reflects West's intellectual engagement with psychoanalysis and Viennese cultural life.

artists.bio:
Huang Yishan was born in Guangdong Province, China in 1984 and studied at the Guangzhou Academy of Fine Arts before pursuing postgraduate work in the United States. She works primarily in oil paint, developing a figurative approach that draws on both Western academic figuration and the long history of Chinese figure painting to create images of contemporary women in domestic and interior settings.

Huang's figures are depicted with a psychological specificity and physical ease that represents a significant departure from the ideological figuration of Chinese Socialist Realism, instead insisting on the individual woman — her inner life, her relationship to her environment, her bodily presence — as the primary subject of the work. The domestic interiors she depicts are rendered with attention to the specific textures and colours of contemporary Chinese middle-class life, creating images that are simultaneously universal in their emotional register and culturally particular in their visual detail.

She has exhibited internationally and has attracted growing critical attention as part of the global recognition of contemporary Chinese painting's diversity and vitality. Her work contributes to ongoing conversations about female representation, domesticity, and the possibilities of figurative painting in the twenty-first century.

famous_works.title: Interior with Woman
famous_works.notes: A painting of a woman in a contemporary Chinese domestic interior in which psychological specificity and careful attention to material surfaces combine in an image of quiet formal authority.

famous_works.title: Rest
famous_works.notes: A reclining figure in a domestic setting that demonstrates Huang's particular ability to invest a conventional subject with psychological depth through the quality of painterly attention.

famous_works.title: Afternoon
famous_works.notes: A scene of domestic leisure in which the quality of afternoon light and the specificity of the surrounding objects create an atmosphere of concentrated sensory awareness.

lesser_known_works.title: Study in Green
lesser_known_works.notes: A figure in a predominantly green interior that demonstrates the chromatic sophistication underlying Huang's apparently straightforward figurative approach.

lesser_known_works.title: Two Women
lesser_known_works.notes: A double-figure composition that extends Huang's investigation of female subjectivity into the domain of relationship and social interaction.

artists.bio:
Robert Nava was born in Aurora, Illinois in 1985 and studied at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. He came to rapid international prominence in the early 2020s with large-scale paintings of mythological creatures, warriors, and angelic figures rendered in a gestural, neo-expressionistic manner that combines the formal directness of cave painting, comic book illustration, and children's art with the scale and physical presence of monumental painting.

Nava's figures — dragons, winged beings, armoured warriors, demons — are painted in a deliberately rough, apparently naive manner: thick black outlines, flat colour areas, figures that seem to emerge from the canvas surface with a primitive vitality that belies the formal intelligence of their placement and the relationship between figure and ground. The roughness is calibrated: Nava's figures have the quality of ancient or folkloric imagery that has survived by being transmitted through many generations of hands, each adding or removing something.

He is represented by Pace Gallery and has exhibited at major art fairs and institutions internationally, achieving commercial success at a speed that has placed him at the centre of conversations about the current market for expressive figurative painting. His practice engages with the deep human need for mythological imagery in a self-aware contemporary manner.

famous_works.title: Angel
famous_works.notes: A large-scale painting of a winged figure rendered in Nava's characteristic rough-outlined, childlike manner that combines primitive vitality with monumental scale.

famous_works.title: Dragon
famous_works.notes: A mythological creature painted with the formal directness of ancient image-making, in which the simplicity of the means creates an image of considerable psychological presence.

famous_works.title: Warrior
famous_works.notes: A large figurative work in which an armoured figure's formal clarity and physical presence demonstrate the range of Nava's mythological vocabulary.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Chicago works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Nava's formation period at the School of the Art Institute that demonstrate the development of his figurative and gestural approach before his commercial success.

lesser_known_works.title: Small mythology works
lesser_known_works.notes: Smaller-scale works demonstrating the intimate register within which Nava developed his mythological figure vocabulary.

artists.bio:
Keith Haring was born in Reading, Pennsylvania in 1958 and arrived in New York in 1978 to study at the School of Visual Arts. He became one of the defining visual voices of the 1980s through his chalk drawings made on the blank paper panels covering unused advertising spaces in the New York subway system, drawings that were seen daily by millions of commuters and that translated the energy and accessibility of graffiti into a language of simple, almost universal symbolic figures.

Haring's visual vocabulary is immediately recognisable: radiant babies, barking dogs, dancing figures, hearts, flying saucers — outlined in the thick black line that became his signature — rendered in a manner that is simultaneously playful and politically charged. His work addressed the AIDS crisis, apartheid, drugs, nuclear threat, and the Reagan administration's indifference to minority communities with a directness that made art available as political communication without requiring any specialist knowledge. He opened the Pop Shop in 1986, selling merchandise at affordable prices as an extension of his democratic commitment to accessibility.

Haring was diagnosed with HIV in 1988 and died of AIDS-related complications in 1990 at thirty-one. The Keith Haring Foundation, established before his death, continues to support social programmes for children and AIDS research. His work is held by major museums worldwide and his visual language has had an influence on subsequent culture that far exceeds the art world.

famous_works.title: Radiant Baby
famous_works.notes: Haring's most reproduced image — a crawling infant surrounded by radiant lines — which became an emblem of innocence, energy, and creative possibility.

famous_works.title: Crack Is Wack
famous_works.notes: A large-scale mural painted on a handball court in New York City addressing the crack cocaine epidemic, demonstrating Haring's commitment to using his public platform for political communication.

famous_works.title: Once Upon A Time (Cooper Square installation)
famous_works.notes: An explicit mural painted on the walls of a men's room in the LGBT Community Center in New York, addressing sexuality, desire, and the AIDS crisis with characteristic directness.

lesser_known_works.title: New York Public Library installation
lesser_known_works.notes: An early gallery-context installation that translated Haring's subway chalk work into a formal institutional setting while maintaining its popular energy.

lesser_known_works.title: Apocalypse series
lesser_known_works.notes: A series of prints made in collaboration with William S. Burroughs that extends Haring's symbolic vocabulary into the register of cultural apocalypse.

artists.bio:
Ji Xin was born in China in 1990 and has developed a figurative painting practice that explores the ambiguities of contemporary Chinese identity, urban experience, and the psychology of the individual within collective social frameworks. Working primarily in oil, she constructs compositions of considerable formal sophistication in which figures inhabit spaces that are simultaneously specific and dreamlike.

Ji's work engages with the particular emotional texture of contemporary Chinese urban life — its aspirations, its anxieties, its negotiations between tradition and modernity — through images that maintain a psychological realism while employing formal strategies of compression and dislocation that give the works an uncanny quality. Her figures are often depicted in moments of social performance or private withdrawal, their psychological states rendered through posture, facial expression, and the atmospheric quality of the surrounding environment.

She has exhibited internationally and represents a generation of young Chinese painters whose work addresses both the specific conditions of contemporary China and the universal registers of human experience that connect them to a global artistic conversation.

famous_works.title: Interior with Figure
famous_works.notes: A painting of a figure in a contemporary Chinese domestic space in which the relationship between person and environment encodes the psychological tensions of modern urban life.

famous_works.title: Gathering
famous_works.notes: A social scene in which the formal organisation of figures in space reflects the complex dynamics of collectivity and individual interiority within contemporary Chinese social life.

famous_works.title: Night
famous_works.notes: A nocturnal scene in which artificial light and the figure's relationship to its environment create an atmosphere of meditative intensity.

lesser_known_works.title: Study after Old Master
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating Ji's engagement with Western art historical sources as a foundation for her contemporary figurative practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait
lesser_known_works.notes: A direct figurative work that demonstrates Ji's technical facility and psychological penetration within the conventional portrait format.

top_lot.title: Portrait of a Young Woman 少女肖像

top_ratio_lot.title: Portrait of a Young Woman 少女肖像

artists.bio:
Qin Qi was born in Chongqing, China in 1984 and studied at the Sichuan Fine Arts Institute, one of the significant centres of Chinese contemporary art known for its experimental and often socially engaged approach to figuration. She has developed a painting practice centred on the female figure in domestic and personal spaces that engages with the psychological experience of contemporary Chinese womanhood with directness and formal sophistication.

Qin's paintings explore the inner lives of her subjects through compositions in which the relationship between the figure and its spatial context is carefully constructed to suggest psychological states of concentration, anxiety, or repose. Her colour is distinctive: warm, sometimes acid, always carefully calibrated to the emotional register of the scene. The formal intelligence of her compositions — the way figures are placed within architectural and domestic spaces — reflects a sophisticated awareness of both Chinese painting traditions and international contemporary figurative art.

She has exhibited in China and has attracted increasing international attention as part of the growing global recognition of contemporary Chinese figuration's diversity and quality. Qin Qi represents an important voice in the representation of Chinese women's psychological experience within contemporary painting.

famous_works.title: Afternoon Study
famous_works.notes: A painting of a woman in domestic interior space in which the quality of afternoon light and the figure's psychological absorption create an image of concentrated inner life.

famous_works.title: Solitude
famous_works.notes: A work in which a female figure's solitary state is rendered through spatial organisation and chromatic atmosphere rather than explicit narrative.

famous_works.title: Two Women
famous_works.notes: A composition exploring the dynamics of female social interaction in a contemporary Chinese domestic context.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Sichuan works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Qin's initial professional period demonstrating the development of her figurative approach.

lesser_known_works.title: Night Interior
lesser_known_works.notes: A nocturnal domestic scene demonstrating Qin's particular sensitivity to the psychological atmosphere of artificial light environments.

artists.bio:
Yuan Yuan was born in Jilin, China in 1973 and studied at the Zhejiang Academy of Fine Arts before developing a painting practice that has established him as one of the most distinctive figural painters in contemporary China. His large-scale oil paintings depict elaborate interior architectural spaces — theatres, lobbies, corridors, stairwells, bathing rooms — rendered with extraordinary technical virtuosity and a saturated colour that gives the depicted spaces an uncanny, hyperreal quality.

Yuan's interiors are typically devoid of human inhabitants, or contain only barely visible figures, making the architectural space itself the primary subject. These spaces are derived from the visual culture of early-to-mid twentieth-century China — the art deco and modernist interiors of Shanghai's colonial-era hotels and public buildings — and are rendered with an attention to tile, fabric, wood, and metal surfaces that makes the works extraordinary demonstrations of painterly technique. The combination of technical mastery and psychological unease gives Yuan's paintings a quality of both seduction and melancholy.

He is represented by Sperone Westwater Gallery in New York and has been exhibited widely in China and internationally. Yuan Yuan represents a significant contribution to the global dialogue about the possibilities of hyperrealist figuration as a vehicle for psychological and historical investigation.

famous_works.title: The Corridor
famous_works.notes: A large painting of a Shanghai deco-era corridor in which the intricate pattern of floor tiles and wall surfaces creates an image of overwhelming material richness and psychological unease.

famous_works.title: Bathroom Series
famous_works.notes: Paintings of elaborately tiled and appointed bathing spaces whose combination of luxury and isolation creates an atmosphere of charged, uninhabited intimacy.

famous_works.title: The Theatre
famous_works.notes: A large interior painting of an art deco theatrical space in which the empty seats and ornate architecture create a melancholy meditation on spectacle and absence.

lesser_known_works.title: Early interior works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial paintings establishing Yuan's investigation of historical Chinese architectural interiors before his mature technical approach was fully developed.

lesser_known_works.title: Small format studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Smaller-scale works that demonstrate the intimacy and detailed study underlying Yuan's large-scale compositions.

top_lot.title: The Circular Ruins 環形廢墟

top_ratio_lot.title: The Circular Ruins 環形廢墟

artists.bio:
Salman Toor was born in Lahore, Pakistan in 1983 and studied at the Ohio Wesleyan University and the Pratt Institute in New York. He developed a figurative painting practice centred on the social lives of young South Asian men — drinking, dancing, using phones, navigating public space, existing in states of intimacy and vulnerability — that has attracted widespread critical attention for its combination of formal sophistication and its insistence on representing a community rarely seen in contemporary art.

Toor's paintings are executed in a distinctive manner that draws on Indian miniature painting, the intimacy of eighteenth-century genre painting (Watteau, Fragonard), and the gestural expressiveness of contemporary painting. His interiors are lit with a warm, amber-brown light that gives them the quality of memory or firelight, and within these luminous spaces his figures move with an ease that is simultaneously casual and psychologically charged. The paintings address queer South Asian identity — the negotiation between cultural traditions, family expectations, and personal desires — with a warmth and intelligence that refuses both the sensational and the didactic.

Toor is represented by Toby Webster's The Modern Institute in Glasgow and Luhring Augustine in New York. A major survey exhibition at the Whitney Museum of American Art in 2021–22 established him as one of the significant figurative painters of his generation.

famous_works.title: Hammam I
famous_works.notes: A painting of South Asian men in a bathhouse that draws on the tradition of the male nude and the hammam as a social space while addressing the specific dimensions of queer South Asian male sociality.

famous_works.title: How Lovely It All Was
famous_works.notes: A large figurative work in which a group of young men inhabit a domestic space with the ease of a Watteau fête that is simultaneously a celebration and a melancholy meditation on belonging.

famous_works.title: Boy with Arms Akimbo
famous_works.notes: A single-figure portrait in which Toor's characteristic warm light and intimate psychological register create an image of considerable emotional presence.

lesser_known_works.title: Early New York works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Toor's initial New York period demonstrating the development of his figurative language before the full refinement of his mature manner.

lesser_known_works.title: Lahore Memory paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Works addressing childhood and family memory from Toor's Pakistani background that demonstrate the autobiographical depth underpinning his social subject matter.

artists.bio:
Danielle McKinney was born in Montgomery, Alabama in 1981 and came to painting later than many of her peers, previously working in graphic design before committing fully to fine art. Her oil paintings depict solitary Black women in domestic spaces — smoking, reading, sitting at windows, listening to music — in conditions of private absorption that are rendered with an intimacy and psychological depth that has generated widespread critical attention.

McKinney's compositional approach is carefully controlled: she typically works with a limited palette of warm, smoky tones, constructing figures whose features are often left slightly indistinct while the atmosphere of the surrounding space — a dim lamp, a glass of wine, the glow of a screen — is rendered with precise attention. The solitude of her figures is not depicted as loneliness but as sovereignty: these are women in possession of their own inner lives, claiming private space and private experience as subjects fully worthy of the painted surface.

She has been exhibited at Marianne Boesky Gallery in New York and has attracted rapid critical and commercial attention since her emergence. McKinney's work contributes to a broader reconsidering of whose interiority is granted pictorial attention, insisting on Black women's psychological and emotional complexity as a primary subject of contemporary art.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Smoking)
famous_works.notes: A painting of a Black woman smoking in a warmly lit interior, in which the intimacy of the private moment and the quality of ambient light are treated with equal formal care.

famous_works.title: Company
famous_works.notes: A composition in which a solitary figure and a pet together occupy a domestic interior, extending McKinney's exploration of private companionship and quiet self-possession.

famous_works.title: Nesting
famous_works.notes: A figure at ease in a domestic environment rendered in McKinney's characteristic warm palette, asserting the value of private experience as pictorial subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Night Reading
lesser_known_works.notes: An intimate scene of a woman reading by lamplight in which the quality of artificial light is the primary formal and atmospheric concern.

lesser_known_works.title: By the Window
lesser_known_works.notes: A figure pausing at a window in a work that demonstrates McKinney's ability to invest a simple posture with complex psychological weight.

artists.bio:
Günther Förg was born in Füssen, Bavaria in 1952 and studied at the Akademie der Bildenden Künste in Munich. He developed a multifaceted practice encompassing painting, photography, sculpture, and graphic design whose common thread was an investigation of modernism's legacy — its forms, its ambitions, its failures — pursued through a combination of rigorous formal intelligence and wry, affectionate irony.

Förg's paintings range from monochrome lead works applied directly to wall to gestural abstract canvases in bright, raw colour that simultaneously invoke and undermine the heroic ambitions of Abstract Expressionism and its European counterparts. His photographic series of modernist architecture — Bauhaus buildings, Fascist-era Italian public works, Le Corbusier structures — approach these monuments with a documentary precision that is also an act of aesthetic reckoning, neither condemning nor celebrating the ideological content of the buildings' forms.

Förg died at sixty in 2013, leaving a body of work that has attracted growing institutional attention in the years since his death, with major retrospectives at the Museum of Modern Art in New York and the Haus der Kunst in Munich confirming his position as one of the most consistently intelligent and formally inventive German artists of his generation. He is represented posthumously by Hauser & Wirth.

famous_works.title: Lead Paintings
famous_works.notes: A series of monochrome paintings executed in lead applied directly to the wall, whose material heaviness and surface modulation engage modernism's utopian aspirations through their most elemental formal means.

famous_works.title: Athen series (Photography)
famous_works.notes: Photographs of modernist architecture in Athens that combine documentary precision with an aesthetic attention that transforms buildings into formal compositions.

famous_works.title: Grid Paintings
famous_works.notes: Abstract canvases in which grids of gestural colour application create surfaces that reference the history of modernist geometric painting while maintaining a distinctly raw, anti-perfectionistic quality.

lesser_known_works.title: Bronze Sculptures
lesser_known_works.notes: Small bronze works that extend Förg's investigation of modernist form into three dimensions.

lesser_known_works.title: Bauhaus Photography series
lesser_known_works.notes: Early photographic works focused on Bauhaus buildings that establish the terms of Förg's ongoing dialogue with German and European modernist heritage.

artists.bio:
Kei Imazu was born in Osaka, Japan in 1975 and studied at Kyoto City University of Arts before pursuing further study in Germany at the Hochschule für Bildende Künste in Braunschweig. Her paintings combine a rigorous formal intelligence — informed by both Western geometric abstraction and Japanese aesthetic traditions — with a highly distinctive approach to colour and surface that creates works of quiet, concentrated visual power.

Imazu's canvases are often built up through multiple translucent layers of oil paint, creating surfaces that have a particular depth and luminosity — colour that seems to emanate from within the painting rather than lying on its surface. Her compositional language is abstract but not purely geometric: organic and architectural forms coexist in works that suggest both structural diagrams and emotional weather, the relationship between the two never entirely resolved.

She has exhibited in both Japan and Europe, and her practice represents a sophisticated navigation of the cross-cultural dialogue between Eastern and Western abstract painting traditions. Her work has attracted particular attention in Germany and Switzerland, where institutional and private collectors have recognised the formal intelligence and meditative quality of her paintings.

famous_works.title: Landscape (Pink)
famous_works.notes: A layered oil painting in which translucent passages of warm colour create a spatial depth that oscillates between landscape suggestion and pure chromatic event.

famous_works.title: Structure Study
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which geometric forms are inflected with organic deviation, creating a formal tension between architectural order and natural variation.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Deep Blue)
famous_works.notes: A painting in which deep blue colour is built up through multiple transparent layers to create a surface of extraordinary chromatic density and inner luminosity.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Translucent Works
lesser_known_works.notes: The initial development of Imazu's layered translucent methodology in works of smaller scale and more exploratory chromatic range.

lesser_known_works.title: Drawing Series
lesser_known_works.notes: Works on paper that demonstrate the compositional thinking underlying Imazu's painted surfaces.

artists.bio:
Invader is the pseudonym of a French street artist born in 1969 who has maintained strict anonymity throughout a career of more than twenty-five years. Beginning in Paris in 1998, he has installed ceramic tile mosaics modelled on Space Invaders and other early arcade game characters on walls, buildings, and public spaces in cities worldwide — his 'invasions' documented obsessively and published in books with titles corresponding to each city's count (Paris 10, London 2, LA 3, etc.).

Invader's practice is conceptually precise: the works mimic the low-resolution pixel grid of 1970s and 1980s video games through the medium of ceramic tile — a material of ancient craft tradition associated with permanence and architectural ornament. This juxtaposition of technological ephemerality (the Space Invader game) and material permanence (ceramic tile) within the unauthorised public space of street art generates a rich set of conceptual tensions. The game iconography is itself a form of cultural archaeology: the Space Invader image is simultaneously nostalgic kitsch and a recognisable image of popular culture's global reach.

Invader has extended his practice into gallery exhibitions, where large-scale mosaic works and framed pieces are sold, and into virtual reality and cryptocurrency-related projects. His work has been auctioned at major houses and has achieved prices that make him among the most commercially successful street artists internationally.

famous_works.title: Paris Invasion (ongoing)
famous_works.notes: The original series of Space Invader tile mosaics placed across the city of Paris, which established Invader's practice and the methodology of urban invasion he has maintained globally.

famous_works.title: Hollywood Invasion
famous_works.notes: A series of particularly ambitious mosaic works placed in culturally significant locations across Los Angeles, including sites linked to the film industry.

famous_works.title: Space 2 (large mosaic)
famous_works.notes: A large gallery-scale mosaic work demonstrating how Invader's street practice translates into institutional settings at a scale and complexity not possible in public urban space.

lesser_known_works.title: Rubik Cubism series
lesser_known_works.notes: Portraits and images constructed from Rubik's cubes rather than ceramic tile, extending the pixel-grid logic into a different found material.

lesser_known_works.title: Point scores
lesser_known_works.notes: An ongoing conceptual element of Invader's practice in which each installation is assigned a point value corresponding to its difficulty and significance, turning the urban invasion into a game.

top_lot.title: Radiant, Apple, Space 光芒四射，蘋果，宇宙

top_ratio_lot.title: Radiant, Apple, Space 光芒四射，蘋果，宇宙

artists.bio:
Mark Bradford was born in Los Angeles in 1961 and worked as a hairdresser in his mother's salon before studying at the California Institute of the Arts, from which he received his BFA and MFA. He came to prominence in the early 2000s with large-scale abstract paintings made from salvaged commercial paper materials — end rolls of billboard paper, pharmacy advertisement sheets, signs from his South Los Angeles neighbourhood — that are layered, wet, bleached, and abraded to create surfaces of extraordinary complexity.

Bradford's process transforms the visual detritus of the Los Angeles commercial street into abstract painting of great formal power. His works often begin with maps or newspaper text, whose residual imagery survives through the layers of application and removal as trace, ghost, and formal element. The resulting canvases are simultaneously abstract paintings and social documents — records of the advertising economy and vernacular visual culture of a specific American neighbourhood — and they carry this duality without resolving it.

He represented the United States at the 2017 Venice Biennale and has been exhibited at Hauser & Wirth and White Cube. His work is held by major museums worldwide including the Museum of Modern Art and the Tate. Bradford has also developed socially engaged projects addressing incarceration, economic inequality, and community development.

famous_works.title: Niagara
famous_works.notes: A large-scale work in which layers of billboard paper and text are built up and abraded to create a surface that is simultaneously abstract painting and urban archaeological excavation.

famous_works.title: Los Moscos
famous_works.notes: A major painting in which the layered commercial paper materials of Bradford's Leimert Park neighbourhood create a canvas of documentary and formal richness.

famous_works.title: Spiderman (Homeland)
famous_works.notes: A large abstract work in which superhero imagery is absorbed into Bradford's layered surface, implicating popular culture mythology in the social realities of urban America.

lesser_known_works.title: Early pharmacy sheets works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial paintings using the pharmaceutical advertisement sheets that characterise Bradford's early South Los Angeles materials, demonstrating the development of his collage-and-abrasion methodology.

lesser_known_works.title: Scorched Earth
lesser_known_works.notes: A large-scale painting in which bleaching and burning create surface effects that evoke environmental destruction alongside Bradford's formal concerns.

artists.bio:
Louise Nevelson was born Leah Berliawsky in Pereiaslav in the Russian Empire (now Ukraine) in 1899 and emigrated with her family to Rockland, Maine as a child. After her marriage she moved to New York, where she studied under Hans Hofmann and Diego Rivera before developing the sculptural practice that made her reputation from the late 1950s. Her signature works are large-scale wall assemblages — accumulations of found wooden objects (chair legs, architectural fragments, blocks, boxes) organised within a grid of wooden boxes and painted uniformly in black, white, or gold.

Nevelson's assemblages are among the great formal achievements of post-war American sculpture. The uniform colour (which she described as making order out of disorder, one colour being the equaliser) transforms the disparate found elements into a complex visual music of shadow and form. The works are wall-scaled environments rather than objects, and their relationship with the viewer is architectural — they are entered as much as viewed. Her use of black in particular gives the works a gravity and spatial depth that continues to affect viewers with immediate force.

She came to major critical attention relatively late, achieving international recognition in her sixties. Her work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and major collections internationally. She was awarded the National Medal of Arts and the Presidential Medal of Freedom.

famous_works.title: Sky Cathedral
famous_works.notes: A monumental wall assemblage of found wooden elements in painted black boxes that established Nevelson's mature language and made her an immediate sensation in the 1958 New York art world.

famous_works.title: Dawn's Wedding Feast
famous_works.notes: A large white-painted assemblage intended to evoke the ritual of matrimony through accumulated wooden forms, demonstrating the emotional and symbolic range of Nevelson's vocabulary.

famous_works.title: Royal Tide I
famous_works.notes: A gold-painted assemblage that invests the found wooden elements with a ceremonial, precious quality that complicates Nevelson's more customary mortuary black.

lesser_known_works.title: First Personage
lesser_known_works.notes: An early freestanding painted wood figure that prefigures the wall assemblages while demonstrating Nevelson's individual approach to sculpture as a gestural, accumulative practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Atmosphere and Environment series
lesser_known_works.notes: Later works in Cor-ten steel that extend Nevelson's formal concerns into outdoor public and architectural contexts.

artists.bio:
Jean Metzinger was born in Nantes in 1883 and arrived in Paris in 1903, initially painting in a Pointillist manner before encountering Cubism through his association with Braque and Picasso and the developing milieu around Apollinaire. He became a central figure in the theoretical articulation of Cubism, co-authoring with Albert Gleizes the landmark text Du Cubisme in 1912 — the first serious theoretical treatment of the movement — and participating in all the major Cubist group exhibitions of the period.

Metzinger's own paintings are distinguished from those of Braque and Picasso by their tendency toward more refined, even elegant execution and a greater use of colour, reflecting his Divisionist and Fauvist roots. His multi-viewpoint compositions apply the Cubist analysis of simultaneous viewpoints to figurative subjects — portraits, nudes, still-lifes — with a clarity that made his work particularly influential in spreading Cubist ideas to artists working outside Paris.

After World War I Metzinger continued developing his Cubist approach, and later in his career explored a more synthetic, decorative application of Cubist principles. He was a significant teacher and remained active until his death in 1956, his work now held by the Museum of Modern Art and major European collections.

famous_works.title: Le Goûter (Tea Time)
famous_works.notes: A Cubist figure of a woman with a spoon and cup in which multiple viewpoints are combined in a manner that simultaneously analyses and aestheticises the subject.

famous_works.title: Dancer in a Café
famous_works.notes: A painting that applies Cubist simultaneity to a modern urban subject, demonstrating Metzinger's ability to synthesise the new pictorial language with contemporary life.

famous_works.title: Woman with a Fan
famous_works.notes: A figure painting in which Cubist multi-viewpoint analysis is applied with particular formal elegance and chromatic sophistication.

lesser_known_works.title: Two Nudes
lesser_known_works.notes: An early Cubist nude that marks Metzinger's departure from his Pointillist roots toward the multi-viewpoint analysis that defined his mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Composition (Still Life)
lesser_known_works.notes: A post-war synthetic Cubist still-life demonstrating Metzinger's continued formal development after the armistice.

top_lot.title: Nature morte: pommes, coupe à fruits et pichet
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

top_ratio_lot.title: Nature morte: pommes, coupe à fruits et pichet

artists.bio:
Yves Klein was born in Nice in 1928 to parents who were both painters. He trained as a judo practitioner in Japan, where he achieved a fourth-dan black belt, before returning to Europe and developing the artistic practice that would make him one of the most original and provocative figures of post-war art. He died at thirty-four of a heart attack, leaving a body of work of extraordinary formal and conceptual audacity.

Klein's central preoccupation was colour as a physical and spiritual substance. His International Klein Blue (IKB) — a specific ultramarine pigment in a synthetic resin binder that maintained the pigment's intensity without the dulling effect of traditional binders — was the medium through which he investigated the possibility of colour as pure feeling, pure space, pure immateriality. His Monochromes, single canvases covered entirely in IKB, remain among the most immediately affecting and conceptually rigorous works of the post-war period. His Anthropometries, in which naked women covered in IKB paint pressed their bodies against canvas, were performed before live audiences to musical accompaniment.

Klein's other interventions included selling 'Zones of Immaterial Pictorial Sensibility' (empty space) for gold leaf and then requiring the buyer to throw the gold into the Seine; his Leap into the Void photograph (1960); and his Fire Paintings. His relationship with the Nouveaux Réalistes is complicated — he participated in the movement but remained distinct from it. The Musée National d'Art Moderne in Paris holds major collections of his work.

famous_works.title: IKB 191
famous_works.notes: A monochrome painting in International Klein Blue whose intense, dust-free ultramarine surface embodies Klein's investigation of colour as pure immaterial presence.

famous_works.title: Anthropometry of the Blue Period (ANT 82)
famous_works.notes: A canvas covered in blue body prints made by live models painted in IKB who pressed themselves against the canvas during a public performance with orchestral accompaniment.

famous_works.title: Leap into the Void
famous_works.notes: A photograph of Klein apparently leaping from a second-floor window into the street, staged as a photomontage that enacts his aspiration toward physical levitation.

lesser_known_works.title: Fire Paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Works created by applying flame to paper or canvas, in which carbonisation and fire become the material of painting.

lesser_known_works.title: Gold Monochrome (MG 1)
lesser_known_works.notes: A gold-leaf monochrome that extends Klein's investigation of pure colour beyond IKB to the precious metal associated with spiritual light.

artists.bio:
Blanche Hoschedé was born in Paris in 1865, the daughter of Alice Hoschedé, who became Claude Monet's companion after both their spouses died. Growing up at Giverny, Blanche became the only one of the Hoschedé children to develop a serious artistic practice, painting alongside Monet in the fields and gardens from her teenage years. She married Monet's son Jean in 1897, further consolidating her relationship to the great Impressionist household.

Blanche's paintings demonstrate a deep understanding of Impressionist plein air method absorbed from direct proximity to Monet, combined with a more structured compositional approach that reflects her awareness of Post-Impressionist developments. Her subjects are characteristically the landscape around Giverny — the meadows, orchards, and river banks she knew intimately — rendered in light-filled colour with a confident directness that is both indebted to and independent of her step-father's example.

After her husband's death in 1914, Blanche devoted herself to caring for the ageing and increasingly blind Monet, and after his death in 1926 she became the guardian of his legacy at Giverny, overseeing the preservation of his studio and gardens. Her own considerable artistic achievement was long overshadowed by this role, and a thorough critical assessment of her work remains a task for future scholarship.

famous_works.title: The Banks of the Epte at Giverny
famous_works.notes: A plein air riverscape demonstrating Blanche's deep assimilation of Impressionist light handling in the landscape immediately surrounding the Monet family home.

famous_works.title: Apple Trees in Blossom
famous_works.notes: A flowering orchard subject rendered with a vibrant, confident brushwork that shows the full development of her independent plein air practice.

famous_works.title: Meadow at Giverny
famous_works.notes: A sun-filled field painting that demonstrates both her debt to and independence from the Impressionist methods she absorbed from daily proximity to Monet.

lesser_known_works.title: Study after Monet
lesser_known_works.notes: One of several early works in which Blanche painted the same subjects as Monet, documenting her apprenticeship while establishing her own confident pictorial voice.

lesser_known_works.title: The Garden at Giverny
lesser_known_works.notes: A late work depicting the famous garden that she helped preserve after Monet's death, its loose handling showing an independent development of the Impressionist plein air tradition.

top_lot.title: Bords de Seine (La Seine à Croisset)
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

top_ratio_lot.title: Bords de Seine (La Seine à Croisset)

artists.bio:
Victor Vasarely was born Győző Vásárhelyi in Pécs, Hungary in 1906 and studied at the Mühely School in Budapest, a Bauhaus-inspired institution where he absorbed the principles of geometric abstraction and design. Moving to Paris in 1930, he worked as a graphic designer and commercial artist, developing through his advertising work a mastery of visual perception and optical effect that would become the foundation of his fine art practice.

From the late 1940s, Vasarely developed the systematic investigation of optical phenomena — colour interaction, figure-ground ambiguity, spatial illusion — that made him the founding father of Op Art. His paintings and prints use grids of geometric forms in precisely calibrated colour relationships to create effects of vibration, movement, and three-dimensional illusion on flat surfaces. His ambition was not merely optical sensation but a democratic art: he believed that his visual investigations could be translated into architectural environments, serial multiples, and design applications that would bring aesthetic experience to everyone.

Vasarely established the Fondation Vasarely in Aix-en-Provence in 1976, a Bauhaus-inspired centre for visual research housed in a building he designed, and his work was exhibited internationally throughout the 1960s and 1970s as Op Art attracted worldwide popular attention. His work is held by the Fondation Vasarely and major museums internationally.

famous_works.title: Vega-Nor
famous_works.notes: A large painting in which a grid of squares distorted at the centre creates an illusion of a spherical form projecting from or receding into the picture plane.

famous_works.title: Zebra
famous_works.notes: An early optical painting in which black and white stripes create a figure-ground ambiguity that announces Vasarely's central concerns twenty years before Op Art was named.

famous_works.title: Kettes
famous_works.notes: A painting in which the subtle modulation of a geometric grid creates a pulsing spatial dynamic characteristic of Vasarely's mature optical investigations.

lesser_known_works.title: Orion MC
lesser_known_works.notes: An optical work in which the interaction of circular and square forms creates spatial illusions of considerable visual complexity.

lesser_known_works.title: Gestalt series
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from Vasarely's late career in which the optical investigations of the earlier work are pursued at increasingly large scale and chromatic complexity.

artists.bio:
Robert Mapplethorpe was born in Floral Park, Queens, New York in 1946 and studied at the Pratt Institute in Brooklyn, where he began working in photography. He moved into the Chelsea Hotel in 1969, where he formed the seminal friendship with Patti Smith documented in her memoir Just Kids. By the mid-1970s he had developed the studio photography practice — exploiting a large-format camera and precise studio lighting — that defined his mature career.

Mapplethorpe worked across portraiture, still-life flowers, and explicit male sexual imagery, bringing to all these genres a formal rigour and aesthetic classicism that drew on Old Master tradition. His flower photographs are among the most exquisite in the history of the medium: calla lilies and bird-of-paradise rendered in a lighting so precise and a compositional control so complete that they seem both more beautiful and more menacing than the flowers of any Dutch still-life. His portraits of figures from the New York arts world — Warhol, Cindy Sherman, Brooke Shields — have the gravitas of classical portraiture applied to contemporary subjects.

Mapplethorpe died of AIDS-related complications in 1989. A posthumous retrospective at the Cincinnati Contemporary Arts Center in 1990, which included explicit sadomasochistic photographs, resulted in an obscenity trial that became a defining moment in American culture war debates about arts funding and freedom. The Mapplethorpe Foundation supports arts education and AIDS research.

famous_works.title: Self-Portrait with Whip
famous_works.notes: A photograph in which Mapplethorpe presents himself in a sadomasochistic scenario with the formal composure of a classical self-portrait, insisting on the dignity of transgressive imagery.

famous_works.title: Calla Lily
famous_works.notes: A studio photograph of a single lily in which Mapplethorpe's formal perfection and precision lighting create an image of overpowering sensuous beauty.

famous_works.title: Ken Moody and Robert Sherman
famous_works.notes: A portrait of two men — one Black, one white — whose physical beauty is presented with the same classical formality as any historical depiction of the ideal male body.

lesser_known_works.title: Patti Smith (cover)
lesser_known_works.notes: A portrait of Smith used for her debut album Horses that exemplifies Mapplethorpe's approach to the artist portrait as both intimate record and classical image.

lesser_known_works.title: Orchid
lesser_known_works.notes: A flower photograph demonstrating the range of Mapplethorpe's botanical subjects beyond the more frequently reproduced lilies.

artists.bio:
Constantin Brâncuși was born in Hobiţa in the Carpathian region of Romania in 1876, the son of peasant farmers. He walked much of the way from Bucharest to Paris on foot, arriving in 1904 and briefly working in Rodin's studio — an experience he terminated after two months with the famous remark that nothing grows under big trees. From this rejection of Rodin's influence he constructed one of the most radical sculptural programmes of the twentieth century: a sustained reduction of forms toward their essential, primal state.

Brâncuși carved and cast a small number of recurring themes — the bird, the column, the head, the kiss — returning to them obsessively across decades, each iteration approaching more closely the pure form that seemed to embody the essence of the subject. Endless Column, Bird in Space, The Kiss: these works do not represent their subjects but aspire to become them, to capture the fundamental energy or principle that the subject represents. The stone, marble, and polished bronze he worked in are chosen and finished with exquisite attention to material quality.

His studio in Paris, reconstructed adjacent to the Centre Pompidou, remains one of the extraordinary artistic environments of the twentieth century, the works installed within it in relationships that Brâncuși considered integral to their meaning. He influenced virtually every sculptor of the subsequent century who was concerned with the relationship between form, material, and meaning.

famous_works.title: Bird in Space
famous_works.notes: A polished bronze form of extraordinary simplicity that reduces the idea of flight to a single upward-surging volume, triggering a famous US customs dispute about whether it constituted sculpture.

famous_works.title: Endless Column
famous_works.notes: The ultimate iteration of Brâncuși's column motif, installed at Târgu Jiu in Romania at nearly thirty metres, a form that implies vertical extension beyond any physical limit.

famous_works.title: The Kiss
famous_works.notes: A limestone block in which two figures embrace with a formal integration so complete that the distinction between the bodies nearly disappears, making their unity literal.

lesser_known_works.title: Sleeping Muse
lesser_known_works.notes: A polished bronze oval that reduces the sleeping human head to an almost geometric form, anticipating the radical simplification of Brâncuși's mature work.

lesser_known_works.title: Princess X
lesser_known_works.notes: A polished bronze form of ambiguous erotic suggestion, withdrawn from the 1920 Salon des Indépendants as obscene, demonstrating that Brâncuși's abstractions were not merely formal.

artists.bio:
David Wojnarowicz was born in Red Bank, New Jersey in 1954 and spent much of his childhood in conditions of acute poverty and domestic violence before arriving in New York as a teenager, living on the streets and turning to sex work for survival. These experiences of marginalisation formed the biographical ground from which one of the most urgent and formally inventive artistic practices of the 1980s emerged — a practice that combined painting, photography, film, performance, writing, and installation in an assault on the social and political conditions that were producing mass death from AIDS.

Wojnarowicz painted maps, used photographic overlays, stenciled, collaged newsprint and industrial materials, and used his own body and the bodies of his community as subjects. His work is simultaneously autobiographical and political, personal testimony and cultural critique, formal experiment and activist provocation. Images of buffalo herds tumbling over cliffs, collaged maps of America, portraits of his dying friends and lover Peter Hujar — all carry the urgency of someone acutely aware of mortality, both his own and that of an entire community being destroyed by indifference and stigma.

Wojnarowicz died of AIDS-related illness in 1992 at thirty-seven. His work is held by the Whitney Museum, which mounted a major retrospective in 2018, and by numerous other collections. He is recognised as a foundational figure in queer art history and in the history of AIDS cultural activism.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Buffalo)
famous_works.notes: A photograph of a toy buffalo tumbling toward the viewer, which became one of the defining images of AIDS-era art in its meditation on death, extinction, and political abandonment.

famous_works.title: When I Put My Hands on Your Body
famous_works.notes: A photographic work overlaying text on an image of a skeleton, confronting the viewer with the language of violence and erasure surrounding AIDS and marginalised communities.

famous_works.title: Arthur Rimbaud in New York
famous_works.notes: A photographic series in which Wojnarowicz wears a mask of the French poet at various New York sites, linking urban poverty and artistic outsider-hood across a century.

lesser_known_works.title: The Weight of the Earth
lesser_known_works.notes: A collage combining scientific diagrams, found imagery, and painted elements into a meditation on ecological and social devastation.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Genet, After Brassaï)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early photograph establishing the appropriation of literary and artistic heritage as a method of locating the self within a tradition of queer creative resistance.

top_lot.title: The Boys Go Off to War

top_ratio_lot.title: The Boys Go Off to War

artists.bio:
Auguste Rodin was born in Paris in 1840 and spent his early career working as a decorative craftsman, having been rejected three times by the École des Beaux-Arts. A visit to Italy in 1875 transformed his understanding of figure sculpture through his encounter with the work of Michelangelo, and the full-scale figure he produced on his return — The Age of Bronze — was so anatomically accurate that he was accused of casting from a live model. The controversy established him as a radical presence within the French sculpture establishment.

Rodin's working method was itself revolutionary: he did not carve but modelled, and he insisted on the fragment — the headless torso, the disembodied hand — as a legitimate subject rather than a failure of completion. His surfaces preserve the mark of the modelling process, embracing accident and the trace of creative struggle as expressive content. The Gates of Hell, commissioned in 1880 and worked on for the rest of his life, served as a laboratory for most of his major figures; The Thinker, The Kiss, and numerous others were conceived as components of this monumental threshold before achieving independent life.

Rodin attracted both devotion and scandal throughout his career, most notably with his Monument to Balzac, rejected by the commissioning body for its uncompromising abstraction of the writer's bulky form. He employed numerous studio assistants, including Camille Claudel, with whom he had an intense and professionally formative relationship. The Musée Rodin in Paris, established during his lifetime, holds the definitive collection of his work.

famous_works.title: The Thinker
famous_works.notes: Originally conceived as a figure of Dante for The Gates of Hell, The Thinker became the twentieth century's most recognisable image of intellectual contemplation.

famous_works.title: The Burghers of Calais
famous_works.notes: A monument to six medieval civic heroes facing death, revolutionary in its placement of the figures at ground level and its refusal of heroic idealisation.

famous_works.title: The Kiss
famous_works.notes: A marble group depicting the forbidden embrace of Paolo and Francesca from Dante's Inferno, which became an iconic image of romantic love.

lesser_known_works.title: Iris, Messenger of the Gods
lesser_known_works.notes: A headless, spread-eagled bronze figure of aggressive sexual explicitness that demonstrates the transgressive energy concealed within Rodin's more celebrated works.

lesser_known_works.title: The Walking Man
lesser_known_works.notes: A headless, armless figure in the act of walking that anticipates twentieth-century concerns with the body as pure movement and mass rather than individual expression.

top_lot.title: L'un des Bourgeois de Calais, Jean d'Aire vêtu, réduction
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

top_ratio_lot.title: Masque de Madame Rodin dit aussi Masque de Rose Beuret

artists.bio:
Gabriele Münter was born in Berlin in 1877 and studied at the Munich Kunstgewerbeschule before enrolling at the Phalanx School where she met Wassily Kandinsky, with whom she formed both a professional and intimate relationship that lasted until his departure for Russia in 1914. The two travelled extensively together and Münter's photographic experiments — she was an accomplished photographer — fed directly into her developing painterly practice.

Münter was a founding member of the Neue Künstlervereinigung München and Der Blaue Reiter, contributing paintings to the landmark 1911–12 exhibitions. Her distinctive style — flat, simplified forms in bold colours with dark outlines that reflect her absorption of Bavarian folk painting and the hinterglasmalerei (painting behind glass) tradition she encountered in the village of Murnau — was reached earlier and more decisively than that of Kandinsky himself. Her Murnau landscapes of 1908–11 are among the most original works produced within the Expressionist circle.

After Kandinsky's departure she withdrew from public life, eventually hiding a large collection of his pre-war works and her own paintings in her Murnau house. Their rediscovery in the 1950s provided a crucial document of the Blaue Reiter's formative period. The Städtische Galerie im Lenbachhaus in Munich holds the most significant collection of her work.

famous_works.title: Boating
famous_works.notes: A Murnau-period painting of figures in a boat in which flat colour areas and simplified forms achieve a formal independence from representational description that anticipates abstraction.

famous_works.title: Village Street in Winter
famous_works.notes: A landscape in which the hinterglasmalerei-derived dark outlines and flat colour fields of Münter's mature manner are applied to the Murnau village environment she painted repeatedly.

famous_works.title: Still Life with Saint
famous_works.notes: A domestic interior with devotional objects in which folk art sources are absorbed into a modernist formal language without losing their cultural rootedness.

lesser_known_works.title: Self-Portrait
lesser_known_works.notes: A direct self-portrait in Münter's mature manner that demonstrates her application of simplified form and flat colour to the genre of self-representation.

lesser_known_works.title: Listening (Portrait of Jawlensky)
lesser_known_works.notes: A portrait of fellow Blaue Reiter member Alexej von Jawlensky that records the intimate community of artists who gathered at Murnau in the formative pre-war years.

top_lot.title: Abendlandschaft mit weißem Mond
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Jean Dubuffet was born in Le Havre in 1901 and arrived in Paris in 1918, entering the Académie Julian but quickly abandoning formal art education. He spent years working in his family's wine business before committing fully to painting in his forties — a late start that paradoxically gave his practice an independence from contemporary fashions that those trained in art schools often lack. He became the most radical primitivist in twentieth-century art and the theorist and collector of Art Brut.

Dubuffet's work, beginning in the mid-1940s, deliberately sought to escape the refinements of Western high culture by working with crude materials — gravel, sand, coal dust, tar — and by developing a drawing style that mimicked the untrained mark-making of children, the mentally ill, and outsider creators. He coined the term 'Art Brut' (raw art) for work made outside cultural conventions, amassing a collection of several thousand works that is now housed at the Collection de l'Art Brut in Lausanne.

Dubuffet's own painting moved through numerous phases: the Hautes Pâtes (thick paste) works, the Hourloupe cycle of the 1960s–70s (red, blue, white, and black puzzle-like forms derived from telephone doodles), and late large-scale theatre and sculpture projects. He remains among the most consequential and provocative artists of the post-war period, and his influence on subsequent generations of artists interested in the primitive, the outsider, and the anti-aesthetic is profound.

famous_works.title: Corps de Dame
famous_works.notes: A series of paintings of the female body in which the figure is rendered in thick, crude paste with a deliberate anti-aesthetic brutality that attacks conventional beauty standards.

famous_works.title: The Busy Life
famous_works.notes: A dense, primitive urban scene in which Paris pedestrian life is depicted in the flat, graffiti-like manner Dubuffet developed from children's drawing and urban wall art.

famous_works.title: Pissing Figure (Hourloupe)
famous_works.notes: A large Hourloupe-cycle painting in which the red, blue, and white puzzle-forms create a complex figure within a visual system that denies both illusion and conventional form.

lesser_known_works.title: Minuit Hurlant (Howling Midnight)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating Dubuffet's initial move toward primitive figuration before the development of the dense material surfaces of his mature Hautes Pâtes period.

lesser_known_works.title: Coucou Bazar
lesser_known_works.notes: A theatrical event involving performers in Hourloupe-costumed interaction with Hourloupe stage elements, extending the visual system into time and performance.

artists.bio:
John Currin was born in Boulder, Colorado in 1962 and studied at Carnegie Mellon University and Yale University School of Art, from which he graduated in 1986. He came to prominence in the early 1990s with paintings that combined highly accomplished Old Master technique with subject matter that was deliberately provocative: aging women in awkward poses, aggressively pneumatic female figures, men in domestic contexts. The combination of technical virtuosity and apparent bad taste generated considerable critical attention and debate.

Currin's mature work engages with a very specific genealogy of European figurative painting — Cranach, Spranger, the Dutch Golden Age — applying the technical methods of Flemish paint application and glazing to subjects that range from the grotesque to the pornographic. His paintings of domestic life, scenes with multiple figures, and erotic subjects are executed with a technical finesse that insists on their seriousness as painting while their subject matter tests the viewer's willingness to find that seriousness merited.

He is represented by Gagosian Gallery and his work has been the subject of major retrospectives at the Museum of Contemporary Art Chicago and other institutions. Currin occupies a singular position in contemporary art as an artist for whom technical virtuosity is inseparable from a willingness to inhabit positions of moral and aesthetic discomfort.

famous_works.title: Thanksgiving
famous_works.notes: A domestic scene of women preparing food in which the virtuoso technical handling and the unsettling ambiguity of the figures' psychological relationship test the viewer's habitual categories.

famous_works.title: The Bunnies
famous_works.notes: A painting of two young women in which Playboy Bunny associations are fused with Old Master technical sophistication to create an image of deliberate and searching discomfort.

famous_works.title: Honeymoon Nude
famous_works.notes: A figure painting combining Flemish flesh-painting technique with a subject drawn from contemporary erotic imagery, forcing a reckoning with the persistence of the male gaze across art historical periods.

lesser_known_works.title: The Old Fence
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating Currin's application of Old Master glazing technique to a deliberately unglamorous domestic subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Heartless
lesser_known_works.notes: An early New York painting in which the provocations of Currin's subject matter and his emerging technical sophistication are first clearly articulated together.

artists.bio:
Jack Whitten was born in Bessemer, Alabama in 1939 and came of age during the civil rights movement, studying briefly at the Tuskegee Institute and meeting Martin Luther King Jr. before moving north to New York, where he enrolled at Cooper Union and graduated in 1964. He became part of the downtown art world, befriending Philip Guston and Ad Reinhardt and developing through the 1960s and 1970s a practice of rigorous formal experimentation that remained largely outside mainstream critical attention.

Whitten's work went through numerous distinct phases, each involving technical innovation. In the 1970s he developed an approach using a large homemade squeegee to drag mixed paint across large canvases in a single decisive gesture, creating surfaces of extraordinary tonal complexity. Later he invented what he called 'tesserae' — dried slices of acrylic paint used as mosaic elements — which he pressed into large compositions to create surfaces that combine the history of Byzantine mosaic with the materiality of abstract painting. His work was explicitly concerned with being both a Black American artist and a modernist painter, holding these identities not in tension but in productive synthesis.

Late recognition came with a major retrospective at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in 2014, the year before his death, which confirmed his status as one of the most significant and original painters of his generation. His works are held by major museums including the Guggenheim and the Whitney.

famous_works.title: Delta Group
famous_works.notes: A large squeegee painting in which a single horizontal gesture creates a surface of complex tonal modulation that brings together Jackson Pollock's physical engagement with painting and a more concentrated formal restraint.
A tessera painting created in response to the September 11 attacks, in which fragmented acrylic slabs are combined into an elegy that locates abstract painting in relation to collective trauma.

famous_works.title: Atopolis (For Édouard Glissant)
famous_works.notes: A large tessera painting dedicated to the Caribbean philosopher, in which the mosaic surface becomes a metaphor for the creolised, rhizomatic cultural identity that Glissant theorised.

lesser_known_works.title: Doctor Egypt (and the Witchdoctor's Problem)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early painting that demonstrates Whitten's engagement with African and African-American cultural sources as formal and intellectual content.

lesser_known_works.title: Greek Alphabet series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of squeegee paintings in which the gestural system is applied with particularly rigorous consistency, creating works of concentrated formal intelligence.

artists.bio:
Al Held was born in Brooklyn, New York in 1928 and studied at the Art Students League before travelling to Paris on the GI Bill, where he encountered the post-war European avant-garde and produced his first gestural paintings under the influence of Abstract Expressionism. Returning to New York in the early 1950s, he became part of the downtown scene but gradually moved away from gestural painting toward hard-edged, large-scale geometric abstraction.

By the early 1960s, Held had developed a distinctive pictorial language: massive geometric forms — circles, triangles, interlocking bands — pushing insistently against the canvas edge, generating spatial tension through scale and compression rather than illusionistic depth. In the 1970s he introduced a further development, painting complex three-dimensional geometric structures — cubes, spheres, cylinders in impossible spatial relationships — in black and white, creating a vertiginous illusionism that challenged the viewer's spatial cognition. Late in his career he reintroduced colour, producing sumptuous, jewel-like variations on these architectural fantasies.

Held taught at Yale University for many years and his influence on subsequent generations of geometric painters is considerable. His work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and numerous other major collections.

famous_works.title: Flemish XIV
famous_works.notes: A large black-and-white canvas presenting interlocking geometric solids in impossible spatial configurations, exemplifying Held's investigation of illusionistic space through strict formal means.

famous_works.title: Taxi Cab series
famous_works.notes: Bold gestural works using commercial yellow house paint, bridging Held's Abstract Expressionist roots with the harder-edged direction of his mature practice.

famous_works.title: The Yellow X
famous_works.notes: A monumental canvas in which a single massive X-form crowds the picture plane, demonstrating Held's ability to generate overwhelming spatial presence through elementary geometry.

lesser_known_works.title: Greek Garden
lesser_known_works.notes: A transitional work in which elementary geometric forms float against saturated grounds, marking the shift from raw gestural painting to Held's signature hard-edge style.

lesser_known_works.title: Ivan the Terrible series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works named with characteristic sardonic wit, in which large letter-forms and geometric bands create compressed, heraldic compositions.

artists.bio:
Cristina de Miguel, who works under the name Cristina BanBan, was born in Barcelona in 1989 and studied at the Escola Massana in Barcelona and the Wimbledon College of Arts in London before relocating to New York. She has developed a distinctive figurative practice centred on the female body rendered at monumental scale in a palette of warm, saturated colour that draws simultaneously on the tradition of Spanish painting — Velázquez, Goya, Murillo — and on a more immediate sensibility indebted to fashion, beauty culture, and contemporary femininity.

BanBan's figures are large, voluminous, and emphatically present: women with rounded forms, strong gazes, and an ease of occupying their own physical reality. They inhabit richly coloured grounds — deep reds, ochres, Mediterranean blues — and are frequently depicted in states of leisure, intimacy, or self-possession that assert female subjectivity without recourse to either classical idealisation or contemporary irony. The works' combination of art-historical gravitas and immediate sensory pleasure has made them among the more visually compelling contributions to the recent resurgence of figurative painting.

BanBan has been exhibited at Almine Rech Gallery and her work has attracted significant critical and commercial attention. She represents a strand of contemporary painting that finds its power in an unapologetic celebration of beauty, physicality, and female presence.

famous_works.title: Two Figures on Red
famous_works.notes: A monumental canvas in which two female figures in close proximity inhabit a saturated red ground, asserting both individual presence and female sociality.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Golden Ground)
famous_works.notes: A single figure against a warm golden background in which BanBan's characteristic warm palette and confident brushwork create an image of almost iconic directness.

famous_works.title: Family
famous_works.notes: A multi-figure composition exploring the dynamics of familial groupings within the large-scale figurative format BanBan has made her own.

lesser_known_works.title: Woman in Blue
lesser_known_works.notes: An early large-scale work establishing BanBan's chromatic approach and her interest in depicting women in states of ease and self-possession.

lesser_known_works.title: Three Women
lesser_known_works.notes: A group composition in which the relationships between figures are expressed through colour, proximity, and gaze rather than explicit narrative.

top_lot.title: The Venus of the Lower East Side

top_ratio_lot.title: The Venus of the Lower East Side

artists.bio:
Pablo Ruiz Picasso was born in Málaga, Spain in 1881, the son of an art teacher from whom he received formal instruction from childhood. His technical precocity was evident by his early teens, when he produced academic figure paintings of a quality that few trained adults could match. Arriving in Paris at the turn of the twentieth century, he absorbed the city's extraordinary artistic ferment — post-Impressionism, primitive and Iberian sculpture, Cézanne's structural analysis — and by 1907 produced Les Demoiselles d'Avignon, a painting so radical in its fragmentation of pictorial space and its synthesis of African and Iberian visual sources that it effectively inaugurated modern art.

With Georges Braque, Picasso developed the two phases of Cubism (Analytic and Synthetic) between 1908 and 1914, transforming the fundamental premises of Western pictorial representation that had governed it since the Renaissance. These are among the most consequential formal innovations in the history of painting. But Picasso's career is not reducible to Cubism: his Classical period of the 1920s, his savage Neo-Expressionist Guernica of 1937, his late autobiographical works of the 1960s and 1970s, his ceramics at Vallauris — the range and energy of his production over seven decades has no parallel in modern art history.

Picasso died at Mougins in 1973. His work is held by every major museum in the world. The Musée Picasso in Paris and the Museu Picasso in Barcelona hold the most comprehensive collections of his work, documenting a career that fundamentally changed the possibilities of visual art.

famous_works.title: Les Demoiselles d'Avignon
famous_works.notes: The foundational painting of modern art, in which five female figures are rendered in a style that fuses Iberian and African sculptural sources with Cézannist spatial analysis to destroy the conventions of Western pictorial representation.

famous_works.title: Guernica
famous_works.notes: A monumental response to the bombing of the Basque town by Nazi aircraft, in which Cubist fragmentation is combined with the imagery of screaming horses, weeping women, and dismembered bodies to produce the most powerful anti-war painting in Western art.

famous_works.title: Girl Before a Mirror
famous_works.notes: A painting of his lover Marie-Thérèse Walter in which the mirror simultaneously shows and transforms its subject, creating a meditation on female identity, sexuality, and self-knowledge.

lesser_known_works.title: La Vie
