lesser_known_works.notes: A major Blue Period painting depicting two couples that meditates on the relationships between love, poverty, and mortality with a melancholy that recalls the great Spanish painters.

lesser_known_works.title: Seated Woman with Wrists Crossed (Françoise)
lesser_known_works.notes: A late painting of his partner Françoise Gilot that demonstrates the sustained intensity of Picasso's engagement with portraiture across all periods of his career.

top_lot.title: Danseuse
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
John Chamberlain was born in Rochester, Indiana in 1927 and studied at the Art Institute of Chicago and Black Mountain College, where his encounter with the community around Robert Creeley and Charles Olson confirmed his interest in a practice rooted in direct physical engagement with materials. He began making sculptures from crushed automobile parts in the late 1950s, a development that placed him at the intersection of Abstract Expressionist painting's gestural energy and the three-dimensional world of junk sculpture.

Chamberlain's works in compressed and welded automotive steel — the chromium-plated, enamel-painted, sometimes rusted surfaces of car bodies from American mid-century production — generate an abstract sculptural language of considerable formal sophistication. The works are not simply crushed cars: they are constructed from selected and manipulated components whose colour relationships, forms, and spatial organisation have been carefully arranged. The gestural energy of the bending and crushing is preserved in the final object, but it is subordinate to the compositional intelligence that gives each work its visual identity.

From the late 1960s Chamberlain also worked with urethane foam, galvanised steel, brown paper, and aluminium foil as sculptural materials. He is represented posthumously by Gagosian Gallery and his work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Guggenheim, and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: Nutcracker
famous_works.notes: An early automobile-part sculpture that established Chamberlain's compressed-steel vocabulary and demonstrated its formal richness in a relatively contained scale.

famous_works.title: Delores James
famous_works.notes: A large assembled automotive-steel sculpture in which the relationship between the chromatic and formal qualities of the car-body components achieves particular complexity.

famous_works.title: Fantan
famous_works.notes: A late large-scale work demonstrating the development of Chamberlain's automotive-steel method toward increasingly monumental scale and spatial ambition.

lesser_known_works.title: Foam Sculptures
lesser_known_works.notes: Works in crumpled urethane foam that extend Chamberlain's compression methodology into a softer, lighter material that produces different spatial and chromatic effects.

lesser_known_works.title: Papier Maché Works
lesser_known_works.notes: Small-scale works on paper that demonstrate the range of Chamberlain's formal and material experimentation beyond his better-known automotive steel.

artists.bio:
Jim Hodges was born in Spokane, Washington in 1957 and studied at Fort Wright College and Pratt Institute in New York, where he has been based throughout his career. He came to prominence in the early 1990s with work that combined materials of delicate beauty — flowers, fabric, mirrors, spider webs — with an emotional directness that addressed loss, love, and mortality within the specific context of the AIDS crisis.

Hodges's practice resists any single medium or method, ranging from large-scale mirror installations to works on paper, fabric flowers, neon, and found photography. His most celebrated pieces include large chandeliers of silk flowers, circles of silk scarves cut into leaves, and mirror installations in which viewers see themselves within fragmentary reflections of the world. The recurring concern with impermanence — the cut flower, the aging reflection — is approached with a formal elegance that makes beauty itself a strategy rather than a retreat from difficulty.

He has been exhibited by D'Amelio Terras Gallery and Corvi-Mora Gallery, and a major retrospective was mounted at the Walker Art Center in Minneapolis. Hodges occupies a distinctive position in contemporary art as an artist for whom sensory beauty and emotional directness are not in opposition but are mutually constitutive.

famous_works.title: A Piece of Heaven
famous_works.notes: A large floor installation of thousands of silk and polyester flower blossoms that creates an environment of overwhelming floral beauty while invoking funerary associations.

famous_works.title: Don't Be Afraid
famous_works.notes: A text work whose simple declarative instruction — rendered in silver on black — addresses the viewer directly with the emotional register that characterises all of Hodges's work.

famous_works.title: every touch
famous_works.notes: An early work in which spider webs are fixed to paper with gold and silver leaf, creating an image of extraordinary delicacy that makes visible the invisible infrastructure of connection.

lesser_known_works.title: Changing Things
lesser_known_works.notes: An installation of silk scarves cut into leaf forms and distributed across a gallery floor, in which the relationship between seasonal change and personal loss is articulated through material and gesture.

lesser_known_works.title: Along the Way
lesser_known_works.notes: A large mirror work in which fragmented reflections multiply the viewer's image within a field of visual complexity that is both seductive and disorienting.

artists.bio:
Lynda Benglis was born in Lake Charles, Louisiana in 1941 and studied at Newcomb College at Tulane University before moving to New York in 1964. She came to prominence in the late 1960s with a body of work that directly engaged and challenged the hegemony of Minimalism: where Minimalism insisted on geometric order, industrial fabrication, and emotional neutrality, Benglis poured latex, wax, and pigmented foam directly onto gallery floors, creating forms of exuberantly irregular biomorphic excess that implied organic life, sexuality, and bodily process.

Her Sparkle Knots and metallic sculptural works of the 1970s extend this investigation into more permanent materials: cast aluminium, bronze, and gold leaf applied to knotted forms of extraordinary vitality. The forms simultaneously reference ancient decorative traditions (Greek torcs, Celtic metalwork) and maintain the physical energy of their bodily origins. A 1974 advertisement she placed in Artforum — a nude self-portrait wielding a dildo — was among the most provocative feminist gestures in art world history, generating letters of protest and cancellation of advertising contracts.

Benglis is represented by Paula Cooper Gallery and has been the subject of major retrospectives at the Moderna Museet and the New Museum. Her work stands as a foundational contribution to the development of Post-Minimalism, feminist art, and process-based sculpture.

famous_works.title: Fallen Painting
famous_works.notes: A poured latex floor piece in which pigmented rubber creates a pooled, fleshy form that directly challenges Minimalism's structural authority with its bodily excess.

famous_works.title: Excess
famous_works.notes: A large poured wax floor piece in which the material process generates forms of organic exuberance that are named with characteristic self-awareness.

famous_works.title: Blatt
famous_works.notes: A poured foam and latex floor work that extends Benglis's investigation of material process and bodily form.

lesser_known_works.title: Wing
lesser_known_works.notes: A poured rubber and pigment work that demonstrates the range of Benglis's early material investigations within the poured-process methodology.

lesser_known_works.title: Torc series
lesser_known_works.notes: Cast metallic sculptures in knotted forms that translate the energy of Benglis's poured works into permanent, precious-seeming objects.

artists.bio:
José Parlá was born in Miami in 1973 to Cuban immigrant parents and came to painting through the street art and graffiti culture of his native city in the 1980s and 1990s. He moved to New York, where his practice evolved from street-based work toward large-scale studio paintings that maintain the calligraphic energy and layered surface quality of graffiti within the institutional context of the art world gallery and museum.

Parlá's paintings are built up through complex, multi-layered processes: text (usually cursive, sometimes illegible), gestural marks, and stains of paint and pigment are applied, removed, and reapplied over multiple sessions to create surfaces that resemble the archaeological strata of urban walls — the accumulated history of marking and erasure that characterises any surface in a city with a long life. His work treats calligraphy, urban archaeology, and the history of writing as interconnected subjects, approaching mark-making as a form of cultural memory.

He has been exhibited at Brewer Street Garage and has collaborated with architectural practices on large-scale mural and installation projects in New York, Miami, and internationally. Parlá's work bridges the worlds of street art and the contemporary gallery with a formal sophistication that insists on the connection between the two.

famous_works.title: One: Unity of Many
famous_works.notes: A monumental mural commissioned by One World Trade Center that extends Parlá's layered calligraphic surfaces to architectural scale in one of the most symbolically charged sites in America.

famous_works.title: Aeons
famous_works.notes: A large-scale painting in which layers of calligraphic marks and stains create a surface that reads as both abstract painting and cultural archaeological deposit.

famous_works.title: Writing with Letters
famous_works.notes: A studio painting in which Parlá's layered text and gestural mark-making achieve a particular density and historical resonance.

lesser_known_works.title: Havana Club works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings made in response to Cuban cultural imagery that connect Parlá's calligraphic practice to his family's Caribbean heritage.

lesser_known_works.title: Early studio paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from the transition between street practice and studio context that demonstrate the development of Parlá's layered methodology in its early institutional form.

artists.bio:
Pierre Bonnard was born in Fontenay-aux-Roses near Paris in 1867 and studied at the École des Beaux-Arts and the Académie Julian, where he met Paul Sérusier, Maurice Denis, and other members of what became the Nabis — a group of painters who, inspired by Gauguin's flat, symbolic use of colour, developed an approach to painting as a two-dimensional arrangement of colour and form that led Bonnard toward the particular vision he maintained for the rest of his life.

Bonnard's mature paintings are among the most chromatically extraordinary in Western art. Working from memory and from colour notes rather than direct observation, he constructed domestic interiors, garden scenes, and nudes of his companion Marthe (whom he eventually married) in colour combinations of seemingly arbitrary intensity — oranges next to purples, greens next to reds — that are somehow always precisely right, the colour creating the space and the emotion simultaneously. Marthe, who bathed compulsively due to a health condition, appears in numerous canvases floating in bathtubs of purple and gold — images of domestic intimacy that are simultaneously quotidian and transcendent.

Bonnard worked largely apart from the major movements of twentieth-century art — Cubism, Surrealism, Abstract Expressionism passed him by — maintaining a domestic, intimate subject matter that many critics long dismissed as minor. The rehabilitation of his reputation has been substantial, and major retrospectives at the Tate, MoMA, and the Fondation Beyeler have confirmed his standing as one of the great colourists in the history of painting.

famous_works.title: The Bath
famous_works.notes: A painting of Marthe in the bathtub in which the body, the water, and the warm-tiled room are dissolved into a single chromatic field of extraordinary radiance.

famous_works.title: The Dining Room in the Country
famous_works.notes: A large interior painting in which domestic space, garden, and figure are united by the chromatic logic that makes every spatial distinction simultaneously a colour decision.

famous_works.title: Nude in the Bath and Small Dog
famous_works.notes: A late painting of Marthe, from which she has recently died, in which her form in the tub is suffused with a luminosity that has the quality of memory rather than observation.

lesser_known_works.title: The Almond Tree in Blossom
lesser_known_works.notes: A late landscape from Bonnard's Le Cannet studio in which the almond tree's flowering is rendered with the trembling chromatic precision of his final years.

lesser_known_works.title: The Breakfast Table
lesser_known_works.notes: A domestic scene in which the still-life arrangement of breakfast objects and the morning light create a composition of characteristic chromatic intimacy.

top_lot.title: Paysage, arbres fruitiers
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Henri-Edmond Cross was born Henri-Edmond Joseph Delacroix in Douai in 1856 and took his pseudonym to avoid confusion with the great Romantic painter. He moved to Paris and worked in an Impressionist manner before encountering the work of Georges Seurat and Paul Signac in the late 1880s, which converted him to the Neo-Impressionist technique of applying pure colour in small separated strokes — pointillism — to achieve optical mixing and luminous colour effects.

Cross became one of the most committed and technically accomplished practitioners of Neo-Impressionism, developing his own approach to the method that tended toward larger mosaic-like strokes and a more lyrical, less systematic application than Seurat's strict scientific approach. He settled in the south of France, first at Cabasson and then at Saint-Clair on the Mediterranean coast, and the intense light and colour of the Midi became the primary subject of his mature work. His paintings of Mediterranean landscapes, bathers, and coastal scenes achieve a chromatic radiance that pushed the Neo-Impressionist system toward an almost Fauvist intensity.

Cross's friendship with Signac was central to the development of Neo-Impressionism after Seurat's death, and the paintings of his late period, in which colour is used with increasing freedom and expressiveness, had a direct influence on the young Matisse and other Fauvists who encountered his work in the early 1900s.

famous_works.title: The Golden Isles (Les Îles d'Or)
famous_works.notes: A large Mediterranean seascape in which the Neo-Impressionist technique achieves a chromatic intensity that anticipates the Fauvist use of colour.

famous_works.title: The Bathers (Les Baigneuses)
famous_works.notes: A large figurative composition of bathing women in a Mediterranean setting that demonstrates Cross's application of Neo-Impressionist colour theory to the classical subject of the nude.

famous_works.title: Evening Breeze
famous_works.notes: A coastal scene in which large mosaic-like strokes of colour create an atmospheric shimmering quality that was particularly influential on early Fauvism.

lesser_known_works.title: Venice
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting of the canal city in which Cross's Mediterranean colour palette and mosaic stroke create an unusually luminous interpretation of this much-painted subject.

lesser_known_works.title: The Farm, Morning
lesser_known_works.notes: A Provençal landscape demonstrating the range of Cross's Neo-Impressionist practice beyond his better-known coastal subjects.

top_lot.title: Étude pour Nymphes (Femme au chapeau, nu en plein air)
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

top_ratio_lot.title: Étude pour Nymphes (Femme au chapeau, nu en plein air)

artists.bio:
Karl Schmidt-Rottluff was born Karl Schmidt in Rottluff, Saxony in 1884 and added his hometown name to distinguish himself from other Schmidts — a gesture typical of the assertive cultural self-fashioning of the Die Brücke group he co-founded with Ernst Ludwig Kirchner, Fritz Bleyl, and Erich Heckel in Dresden in 1905. The group drew inspiration from the tribal art of Oceania and Africa, the prints of Albrecht Dürer and Lucas Cranach, and the anti-bourgeois spirit of German Expressionism.

Schmidt-Rottluff's style is among the most forceful within Die Brücke: angular, large-planed, reduced to elemental forms of great physical impact. His woodcuts in particular — in which the grain and cut of the block are integral to the image's energy — are among the finest achievements of Expressionist printmaking. His paintings, which share this formal reduction, are characterised by strong outlines, non-naturalistic colour, and a compositional boldness that sometimes approaches near-abstraction.

The Nazis declared his work degenerate, confiscating hundreds of his paintings from German museums. He survived the war and was appointed professor at the Hochschule für Bildende Künste in Berlin in 1947, continuing to paint until his death at ninety-one. The Brücke Museum in Berlin, established in 1967, holds a major collection of the group's work.

famous_works.title: Self-Portrait with Monocle
famous_works.notes: A self-portrait of fierce formal directness in which the simplified angular planes and non-naturalistic colour of Schmidt-Rottluff's mature style are already fully present.

famous_works.title: Two Women
famous_works.notes: A figure composition in which the reduction of the human form to geometric planes and the non-naturalistic colour combination create an image of striking primitive force.

famous_works.title: Moonlit Night at the Sea
famous_works.notes: A landscape in which the simplified planes of Schmidt-Rottluff's mature style are applied to the natural world, creating an image of elemental power.

lesser_known_works.title: Head of Christ (Woodcut series)
lesser_known_works.notes: A series of woodcuts in which religious subjects are treated with the same formal severity as secular material, the angular cut of the block creating an image of primitive spiritual force.

lesser_known_works.title: Landscape with Railway
lesser_known_works.notes: A German landscape in which the industrial subject is rendered in the simplified, angular manner that makes Die Brücke painting so formally distinctive.

top_lot.title: Weisse Vase und grüne Flasche
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Albert Gleizes was born in Paris in 1881 and came to Cubism through an earlier engagement with Impressionism and the Abbaye de Créteil, a utopian artistic community he helped found in 1906 that sought to unite art and social reform. By 1910 he had become a central figure in the Salon Cubists, the group gathered around Metzinger, Léger, Delaunay, and others who exhibited collectively at the major Paris salons while Picasso and Braque pursued their analytic experiments in relative privacy.

Gleizes and Metzinger co-authored Du Cubisme in 1912, the first significant theoretical treatment of the movement, which attempted to provide a philosophical and perceptual grounding for the new pictorial language. His own paintings of this period apply Cubist fragmentation to large-scale figurative and architectural subjects — bridges, cities, workers — with an ambition toward the monumental that distinguishes him from the more intimate analytic works of the movement's inventors.

Following World War I, Gleizes deepened his spiritual and philosophical concerns, developing a theory of 'simultanism' and eventually converting to Catholicism, after which his work increasingly engaged with religious subjects. He settled in rural France and spent his later decades writing theoretical texts, teaching, and producing a body of work that increasingly diverged from Parisian fashions. He remains an essential figure for understanding the dissemination and theorisation of Cubism across Europe.

famous_works.title: Brooklyn Bridge
famous_works.notes: A monumental Cubist reworking of the iconic New York structure, painted during Gleizes's American period and demonstrating his ambition to apply the new pictorial language to contemporary industrial subjects.

famous_works.title: Harvest Threshing
famous_works.notes: An ambitious large-scale canvas exhibiting at the 1912 Salon d'Automne, depicting agricultural labour through a fractured Cubist lens that insists on the social dimension of the new painting.

famous_works.title: Portrait of Igor Stravinsky
famous_works.notes: A Cubist portrait of the composer that demonstrates Gleizes's ability to apply geometric fragmentation to the individual human subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Football Players
lesser_known_works.notes: An energetic multi-figure composition applying Cubist simultaneity to the dynamism of sport, anticipating the Futurist interest in movement.

lesser_known_works.title: Composition for Jazz
lesser_known_works.notes: A late work that shows Gleizes's post-Cubist development toward a rhythmic, colour-based abstraction influenced by his simultanism theory.

top_lot.title: Composition
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Liu Ye was born in Beijing in 1964 and studied at the Central Academy of Fine Arts before spending time in Berlin at the Hochschule der Künste. He has developed one of the most distinctive figurative painting practices in contemporary Chinese art: intimate, jewel-like canvases in which simplified figures — children, illustrated characters, abstract shapes — inhabit luminous colour fields in a manner that combines the influence of Western modernism (particularly Mondrian and Malevich) with the poetic register of Chinese aesthetics.

Liu's paintings are characteristically small to medium scale, executed in oil with a smooth, lacquer-like surface that gives the colour extraordinary depth. His figures — often a recurring boy character or child-like forms — appear in states of reverie or absorption, surrounded by books, abstract shapes, or simple geometric environments. The works have a quality of quietness and concentration that recalls both the spiritual intensity of Russian Constructivism and the meditative atmosphere of Chinese ink painting, while operating within the international contemporary art context.

He is represented by Sperone Westwater Gallery in New York and has been exhibited widely in Asia and internationally. Liu Ye is one of the most significant and original voices in the generation of Chinese artists who came of age after the Cultural Revolution.

famous_works.title: Book Lovers
famous_works.notes: A painting of a simplified child figure with a book in which the stillness of the reading act and the luminosity of the coloured ground create an image of concentrated inner life.

famous_works.title: Small World
famous_works.notes: A composition in which abstracted child figures inhabit a Mondrian-like grid of colour that combines the visual languages of Western abstraction and Chinese narrative.

famous_works.title: The Painter
famous_works.notes: A self-referential image of the artist figure at work that meditates on the solitude and absorption of painting practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Berlin Works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings made during Liu's time in Germany that demonstrate the initial synthesis of Western modernist influence with his developing pictorial language.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Red Background)
lesser_known_works.notes: A small canvas in which a figure is presented against a saturated red ground, demonstrating the chromatic intensity characteristic of Liu Ye's mature work.

artists.bio:
Blinky Palermo was born Peter Heisterkamp in Leipzig in 1943 and took his pseudonym — borrowed from a Mafia figure — during his studies at the Kunstakademie Düsseldorf under Joseph Beuys. His career was brief and ended with his death at thirty-three during a trip to the Maldives, but the work he produced across twelve years remains among the most quietly influential of post-war German art.

Palermo's practice resisted easy categorisation: he worked in shaped canvases, fabric paintings, wall drawings, metal pictures, and works on cloth that combine influences from Mondrian and Malevich's geometric abstraction with the material sensibility of Arte Povera and the spatial concerns of American Minimalism, filtered through his particular sensitivity to colour relationships and physical context. His fabric works — lengths of commercially woven textile in two or three colours, joined and hung on the wall — are perhaps his most original contribution: they treat colour not as applied paint but as woven material, not as illusion but as physical fact.

The wall drawings that Palermo created specifically for architectural spaces, destroyed after each exhibition, extended his investigation of colour and form into a direct relationship with a specific room, a specific light. Joseph Beuys described him as the most gifted of all his students. His work is held by the Dia Art Foundation, which has been particularly important in preserving and promoting his legacy.

famous_works.title: Blue Triangle
famous_works.notes: A shaped canvas in which a single geometric form in flat colour asserts the primacy of shape and hue over illusionistic pictorial space.

famous_works.title: Cloth Pictures
famous_works.notes: A series of works made from commercially woven fabric in two or three colours, in which material and colour are identical, refusing the distinction between support and surface.

famous_works.title: To the People of New York City
famous_works.notes: An installation of metal pictures installed across New York in his final American years, treating colour as an environmental and urban experience.

lesser_known_works.title: Schwarzer Keil (Black Wedge)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early shaped canvas demonstrating Palermo's rejection of the rectangular format in favour of forms that assert geometry as spatial rather than merely pictorial.

lesser_known_works.title: Bauhaus Stairway (Study)
lesser_known_works.notes: A work in which Palermo engages directly with the Bauhaus heritage, testing its colour and geometry theories against his own more intuitive approach.

artists.bio:
Diane Arbus was born Diane Nemerov in New York City in 1923, into a prosperous Jewish fur-merchant family. She married Allan Arbus at eighteen and worked with him for years as a fashion photographer before studying under Lisette Model in the late 1950s, an encounter that licensed her to pursue what she had always been most drawn to: the marginal, the transgressive, the aberrant, and the ordinary rendered strange through her particular quality of attention.

Arbus's subjects — nudists at their camps, twins in identical outfits, carnival performers, the developmentally disabled, suburban couples in their living rooms — were chosen not out of voyeuristic condescension but from a genuinely democratic fascination. She approached her subjects directly, spent time with them, and made photographs that give her sitters a peculiar dignity precisely through the unflinching directness of her gaze. Her use of the medium-format Rolleiflex camera allowed direct eye contact, and her deadpan, centred compositions invest every subject with the same unsettling gravity.

Arbus died by suicide in New York in 1971. Her posthumous exhibition at the 1972 Venice Biennale introduced her work to an international audience, and she subsequently became one of the most influential photographers of the twentieth century. The Diane Arbus estate is managed by her daughter Doon, and her photographs are held by major museums worldwide.

famous_works.title: Identical Twins, Roselle, New Jersey
famous_works.notes: Perhaps the most famous photograph in the history of documentary photography, its presentation of identical siblings as simultaneously identical and uncannily different encapsulates Arbus's entire sensibility.

famous_works.title: Child with Toy Hand Grenade in Central Park, New York City
famous_works.notes: A portrait of a boy in a moment of unguarded tension, in which the toy weapon and the grimacing face collapse the boundary between childhood and adult violence.

famous_works.title: A Jewish Giant at Home with His Parents in the Bronx, NY
famous_works.notes: Eddie Carmel with his parents in their modest apartment, in which height and scale make the domestic uncanny while insisting on familial tenderness.

lesser_known_works.title: Woman with a Veil on Fifth Avenue, NYC
lesser_known_works.notes: A street portrait that demonstrates Arbus's ability to find the strangeness of convention in a figure who believes herself to be entirely unremarkable.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled series
lesser_known_works.notes: Late photographs made at a home for developmentally disabled adults in New Jersey, in which the subjects' uninhibited self-presentation became a meditation on performance and authenticity.

top_lot.title: Teenage couple on Hudson Street, N.Y.C. 1963

top_ratio_lot.title: Teenage couple on Hudson Street, N.Y.C. 1963

artists.bio:
Caroline Walker was born in Dunfermline, Scotland in 1982 and studied at Glasgow School of Art and the Royal College of Art in London. Her paintings depict women in domestic and service environments — hotel rooms, spas, laundry rooms, kitchens, nail salons — with a concentrated attention to light, colour, and spatial architecture that consistently elevates the mundane scene into something approaching the lyrical.

Walker's principal interest is in the economics and aesthetics of female service work: the women she depicts are caregivers, hospitality workers, domestic employees, and her treatment of them refuses the sentimentality of social realism while insisting on their full presence within richly lit, formally considered pictorial spaces. The light in her paintings — particularly the artificial light of interior spaces, halogen and neon falling across fabric, skin, and reflective surfaces — is handled with a quality that recalls Vermeer and Edward Hopper while remaining entirely contemporary.

She has been exhibited by Parafin Gallery in London and Stephen Friedman Gallery, and her work is held by major collections including the Arts Council Collection. Walker's practice represents a considered contribution to the feminist project of making women's labour and domestic life visible within fine art representation.

famous_works.title: Late Checkout
famous_works.notes: A painting of a hotel housekeeper at work in which the geometry of the hotel room and the quality of artificial light together produce an image of almost formal perfection.

famous_works.title: Solarium
famous_works.notes: A spa interior scene in which the saturating light of a sun bed creates a hallucinatory colour environment that transforms a mundane service interaction.

famous_works.title: Pool (Night)
famous_works.notes: A nocturnal outdoor pool scene in which artificial lighting transforms water and skin into surfaces of extraordinary chromatic intensity.

lesser_known_works.title: The Laundry
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work in the series depicting service environments, in which the folding of linen becomes an occasion for careful study of repetitive physical labour.

lesser_known_works.title: Night Shift
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting of a woman working a night shift in a domestic context, in which interior artificial light generates the colour field within which the figure operates.

artists.bio:
Genieve Figgis was born in Dublin in 1972 and came to painting in her late thirties after a career in other fields, beginning to post her work on Twitter in 2013 where she was discovered by Richard Prince, who purchased her work and brought her to the attention of Almine Rech Gallery. This unconventional entry into the art world has become part of her story, but the work itself needs no biographical crutch: it is immediately identifiable and formally assured.

Figgis paints scenes drawn from eighteenth-century portraiture, aristocratic domestic interiors, and pastoral landscapes, subjecting this material to a process of controlled dissolution. Her figures are rendered in thin, runny paint that causes features to bleed and merge, producing faces and bodies in various states of liquefaction that are simultaneously beautiful and grotesque. The effect evokes decay, intoxication, and psychological dissolution — the decadent underside of the decorative world she invokes — while the basic pictorial structure of the Old Master sources remains legible beneath the ruin.

Figgis has been exhibited at Almine Rech Gallery in Paris, New York, and London, and her work is held by major collections. She represents a particularly original strand in contemporary painting's dialogue with art historical sources, finding in their deliberate corruption a language for exploring the comedy and horror of social aspiration.

famous_works.title: Tea Party
famous_works.notes: A group scene derived from eighteenth-century conversation pieces in which the dissolving faces and running paint transform elegant social ritual into something vertiginous and comic.

famous_works.title: Baby Shower
famous_works.notes: A contemporary domestic subject treated in Figgis's runny, dissolving manner, in which the celebration of new life takes on an unsettling undertow.

famous_works.title: Portrait of a Family
famous_works.notes: A group portrait in which the formal conventions of aristocratic family portraiture are undermined by the liquefaction of the faces, suggesting the instability within the social order being depicted.

lesser_known_works.title: The Hunt
lesser_known_works.notes: A hunting scene derived from Old Master sources in which the aristocratic outdoor ritual is rendered in Figgis's corrosive manner, the paint dissolving the social pretension of the genre.

lesser_known_works.title: Landscape with Figures
lesser_known_works.notes: A pastoral landscape in which the Watteau-derived setting of elegant figures in nature is subjected to the same comic-grotesque transformation as Figgis's interior scenes.

artists.bio:
Antony Gormley was born in London in 1950, studied at Trinity College Cambridge, and later at the Central School of Art and Goldsmiths College. A period spent in India studying Buddhism in the mid-1970s was formative, deepening his preoccupation with the body as a site of consciousness and spiritual experience. He came to prominence in the 1980s with sculptures cast from his own body — initially in lead, later in iron and steel — that use the artist's own morphology as a universal template for exploring human presence and absence.

Gormley's work consistently treats the body not as a representation of a particular individual but as a vessel, a volume of space occupied by consciousness. The lead works of the 1980s show figures in positions of extreme physical vulnerability — crouching, curled, pressed against walls — that suggest both physical sensation and spiritual interior life. His later iron sculptures, multiplied in large installations across landscapes and urban spaces, explore collective presence: the singular body scattered across an environment, becoming population, horizon, and landscape simultaneously.

Angel of the North, installed near Gateshead in 1998, is among the most visited works of public art in Britain. Gormley has exhibited at the White Cube gallery and had major retrospectives at institutions including the Hayward Gallery, the Royal Academy of Arts, and the Hermitage in St Petersburg. His practice stands as one of the most sustained contemporary investigations of the phenomenology of embodied experience.

famous_works.title: Angel of the North
famous_works.notes: A twenty-metre steel figure with vast wing-like arms installed near the A1 motorway in Gateshead, becoming an iconic symbol of post-industrial northern England.

famous_works.title: Another Place
famous_works.notes: One hundred cast iron figures installed in the tidal flats of Crosby Beach, Merseyside, gazing out to sea in a meditation on migration, horizon, and collective human aspiration.

famous_works.title: Event Horizon
famous_works.notes: An installation of cast iron figures deployed across rooftops and on the ground in various cities, making the unseen edges of urban space suddenly present to the viewer.

lesser_known_works.title: Learning to Think
lesser_known_works.notes: A lead body case in a crouching position that embodies Gormley's early investigation of the body as a container of consciousness.

lesser_known_works.title: One & Other
lesser_known_works.notes: A participatory work on the Fourth Plinth in Trafalgar Square in which 2,400 members of the public each occupied the plinth for one hour over 100 days.

artists.bio:
Hilary Pecis was born in Chico, California in 1979 and studied at the California College of the Arts. She lives and works in Los Angeles, where the particular quality of California light — warm, diffuse, saturating — has become a primary element of her paintings. Her subjects are domestic interiors, gardens, and still-lifes populated by books, plants, ceramics, and the accumulated objects of a cultivated life, rendered in a flat, pattern-conscious style that acknowledges the influence of Matisse, Bonnard, and David Hockney while remaining rooted in her specific Californian context.

Pecis works with acrylic, building up richly layered surfaces in which the relationship between figure and ground, object and environment, is made into an occasion for visual pleasure. Her interiors are inhabited by women (often the artist herself and her friends) in states of casual leisure — reading, sitting in sun-drenched rooms, tending plants — and the atmosphere of these scenes communicates a specifically Californian vision of the good life rendered without irony or nostalgia, simply appreciated with painter's attention.

She has been exhibited with Rachel Uffner Gallery in New York and Almine Rech Gallery, and has attracted strong critical and collector interest as part of the current revival of interest in intimate figurative painting with a feminist sensibility. Her work participates in a reassessment of domesticity and female experience as primary pictorial subjects.

famous_works.title: Backyard with Pool
famous_works.notes: A California backyard scene in which the flat pattern-making of Pecis's mature style transforms domestic outdoor space into a tapestry of chromatic pleasure.

famous_works.title: Still Life with Books and Plants
famous_works.notes: An interior still-life in which the accumulation of cultural and natural objects creates a portrait of intellectual and aesthetic life without depicting a human figure.

famous_works.title: Woman Reading
famous_works.notes: A figure in a domestic interior in which the woman and the objects around her are treated with equal visual attention, the human presence becoming one element within an overall pictorial harmony.

lesser_known_works.title: Afternoon Light
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting focused on the quality of California afternoon light falling through a domestic interior, demonstrating the primacy of light as formal element in Pecis's work.

lesser_known_works.title: Garden Study
lesser_known_works.notes: A plein air-influenced garden painting in which the flat colour and pattern-consciousness of Pecis's indoor subjects is extended into the outdoor environment.

artists.bio:
Eddie Martinez was born in Connecticut in 1977 and studied at the University of Hartford before establishing himself in New York. His practice has developed across painting, works on paper, and ceramics, maintaining throughout a gestural energy and material directness that acknowledges multiple predecessors — Philip Guston, Jim Dine, Jean-Michel Basquiat, outsider art — while remaining distinctly his own.

Martinez's paintings range from dense, layered abstractions to figurative works featuring recurring motifs — turtles, insects, hands, cartoon-like figures — that appear and disappear across his canvases in a manner that suggests automatism and psychological association rather than deliberate composition. He works rapidly and productively, covering surfaces in multiple layers of paint, drawing, and collage to produce works that have the quality of archaeological strata: each visible layer implies a history of previous decisions, revisions, and additions.

He has been exhibited with Mitchell-Innes & Nash in New York and Blum & Poe in Los Angeles, and his work has attracted strong collector interest particularly among those drawn to the expressionist current in contemporary painting. Martinez represents a generational continuation of the gestural figurative tradition that passes through Guston's late work and the graffiti-influenced painting of the 1980s.

famous_works.title: Caulking Gun (The Real Thing)
famous_works.notes: A large, densely layered canvas in which found objects, gestural paint, and graphic marks accumulate into an image that bridges abstraction and symbolic figuration.

famous_works.title: Double Turtle
famous_works.notes: A figurative painting featuring the recurring turtle motif central to Martinez's iconographic vocabulary, rendered with the physical directness of his mature gestural manner.

famous_works.title: The Push and The Pull
famous_works.notes: A large abstract-figurative canvas demonstrating the dialogue in Martinez's work between gestural mark-making and the emergence of legible imagery.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Hand Study)
lesser_known_works.notes: A smaller-scale work focusing on the hand as both mark-making tool and recurring image within Martinez's symbolic vocabulary.

lesser_known_works.title: Face Drawing
lesser_known_works.notes: A work on paper that demonstrates the relationship between Martinez's drawing practice and the larger painted canvases it informs.

artists.bio:
Lucy Bull was born in Chicago in 1990 and lives and works in Los Angeles. She studied at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago before moving to California, where the particular quality of Los Angeles light and her exposure to the traditions of West Coast abstraction have contributed to her development of a highly distinctive painterly language. Working in oil and acrylic, she creates layered abstract canvases that suggest interior spaces, nocturnal environments, and atmospheric conditions of extreme strangeness and beauty.

Bull's paintings are built through a process of layering, glazing, and obscuring — passages of warm colour are covered with darker translucent veils, which are in turn inflected with drawn marks and areas of lighter paint. The resulting surfaces have a depth that suggests both physical depth (rooms, environments, spaces) and temporal depth (the accumulation of pictorial decisions, revisions, and elaborations). Her colour is distinctive: purples, blues, greens, and warm oranges and pinks combine in works that feel simultaneously psychedelic and intimate.

She has been exhibited at 67 Ludlow Gallery in New York and has attracted significant critical and collector interest as part of the current generation of painters working in large-scale abstract idioms. Her paintings represent a rigorous investigation of the atmospheric and spatial possibilities of layered painting.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Purple Interior)
famous_works.notes: A large abstract canvas in which layered purples and blues create a spatial environment of dreamlike depth and atmospheric richness.

famous_works.title: Night Study
famous_works.notes: A painting in which darker atmospheric passages are punctuated by warm colour that reads as artificial light within an indeterminate interior space.

famous_works.title: Cave Painting
famous_works.notes: A large canvas in which Bull's layered translucent methodology creates a surface that recalls both primordial cave walls and contemporary digital light environments.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Chicago Works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Bull's time at the School of the Art Institute that demonstrate the development of her layered atmospheric approach before she relocated to Los Angeles.

lesser_known_works.title: Green Atmosphere
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting in which a dominant green establishes the atmospheric character of Bull's mature canvases while demonstrating the chromatic range within which she works.

artists.bio:
Albert Marquet was born in Bordeaux in 1875 and arrived in Paris at seventeen, studying under Gustave Moreau at the École des Beaux-Arts alongside Matisse, Manguin, and Rouault — a cohort that would become the Fauves. Marquet participated in the 1905 Salon d'Automne exhibition that scandalised critics and named the movement, yet his own use of colour was always more restrained than that of Matisse or Derain; his great subject was water.

From the early 1900s, Marquet painted harbours, rivers, and coastal towns with a luminous economy of means that owes something to Japanese woodblock prints and the spatial clarity of Velázquez. His views of the Seine from Parisian quays and windows, his North African harbour scenes painted during his many visits to Algiers, and his Norwegian fjord paintings constitute a sustained meditation on light falling on still and moving water, rendered in muted, harmonious tones that distinguish him sharply from his Fauvist peers.

Marquet travelled widely throughout his life — North Africa, Scandinavia, Russia, and Egypt — always returning to his essential subject: the relationship between architecture and water, figure and reflected light. Though less celebrated than Matisse, his work possesses a quiet, concentrated intelligence that has attracted sustained critical reassessment in recent decades.

famous_works.title: The Harbour at Hamburg
famous_works.notes: A luminous harbour scene in which industrial shipping is reduced to silhouette and tonal contrast, demonstrating Marquet's ability to make poetry from commercial modernity.

famous_works.title: Quay at Algiers
famous_works.notes: One of many Algerian harbour subjects in which Mediterranean light dissolves architectural mass into shimmering tonal relations.

famous_works.title: Notre-Dame in the Rain
famous_works.notes: A view from a Parisian window in which wet pavements and grey sky create the silvery tonal unity characteristic of Marquet's Seine series.

lesser_known_works.title: The Beach at Fécamp
lesser_known_works.notes: A Fauvist-period coastal scene that shows Marquet at his most chromatic before his palette settled into its characteristic restraint.

lesser_known_works.title: Port of Naples
lesser_known_works.notes: An Italian harbour subject that extends the range of Marquet's water-and-architecture theme beyond his more familiar northern European and North African locales.

top_lot.title: Poissy, le clocher
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Born in Richmond, Virginia in 1984, Adam Pendleton developed a practice rooted in the overlapping histories of the avant-garde and Black radical thought. Working across painting, silkscreen, video, and performance, he coined the term 'Black Dada' as both a theoretical frame and an artistic methodology — a way of bringing together the anti-bourgeois negation of Dada, the formal austerity of Constructivism, and the intellectual lineages of the Black Arts Movement.

Pendleton's paintings are characterised by bold silkscreened text and geometric abstraction, often overlaying language fragments drawn from radical political speeches, manifestos, and his own poetic writings onto fields of black and white. The collision of text and image refuses easy legibility, demanding that the viewer inhabit the gap between language as form and language as meaning. His multi-channel video works extend these concerns into durational time.

He has been the subject of major solo exhibitions at the Museum of Contemporary Art in Los Angeles, the Museum of Modern Art in New York, and the Baltimore Museum of Art. Pendleton is represented by Pace Gallery and has established himself as one of the most rigorously theorised voices of his generation, insisting that abstraction and political thought are not merely compatible but mutually constitutive.

famous_works.title: Black Dada (A)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale silkscreen painting that inaugurated Pendleton's Black Dada series, layering fragmented text over geometric fields to enact the collision of avant-garde form and Black radical politics.

famous_works.title: We Are Not
famous_works.notes: Multi-panel silkscreen work employing negation and erasure as formal strategies that echo the political condition of bodies rendered invisible by systemic violence.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Blackness White Squares)
famous_works.notes: A monumental painting in which white geometric forms punctuate dense black grounds, operating simultaneously as abstract painting, political statement, and visual poem.

lesser_known_works.title: Notebook
lesser_known_works.notes: An early artist book that established the research methodology — collaging theory, poetry, and image — that underpins Pendleton's entire practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Band)
lesser_known_works.notes: A silkscreen painting in which a horizontal band of text bisects the canvas, foregrounding the graphic design heritage within Pendleton's pictorial language.

artists.bio:
Kehinde Wiley was born in Los Angeles in 1977 and studied at the San Francisco Art Institute and Yale School of Art, where he received his MFA. He developed a practice of large-scale figurative painting that inserts Black figures — initially men recruited from the streets of New York — into the compositional frameworks of Old Master portraiture, European Baroque and Rococo painting, and official state portraits, surrounding them with ornate botanical patterns drawn from textiles, stained glass, and wallpaper.

Wiley's work performs a direct intervention into the European tradition of the portrait as a vehicle for the assertion of social power and identity: by placing Black men in the spatial and compositional positions historically reserved for European aristocracy, military commanders, and patrons, he simultaneously reveals the racial exclusivity of those traditions and claims them for different subjects. The tension between the monumental grandeur of the compositions and the contemporary clothing and postures of the figures generates both visual pleasure and political statement.

His commission to paint the official portrait of President Barack Obama for the National Portrait Gallery in 2018 brought his work to its widest audience and confirmed his importance beyond the art world. He is represented by Sean Kelly Gallery and has studios in Brooklyn, Beijing, and Dakar. His work has been exhibited at major institutions worldwide.

famous_works.title: Napoleon Leading the Army over the Alps
famous_works.notes: A direct recasting of David's famous equestrian portrait with a Black male subject, whose contemporary clothing and the displacement of historic triumph from white to Black body makes explicit the power dynamics encoded in the original.

famous_works.title: Barack Obama (Official Presidential Portrait)
famous_works.notes: The official portrait of the 44th President of the United States, installed in the National Portrait Gallery, in which Obama sits before a floral ground in a composition that references both portraiture tradition and Wiley's own formal vocabulary.

famous_works.title: Rumors of War
famous_works.notes: A monumental bronze equestrian sculpture permanently installed in Times Square, New York, based on Confederate monuments in Richmond, Virginia, recast with a young Black male rider.

lesser_known_works.title: The World Stage: Africa series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of paintings in which Wiley's portrait methodology is applied to subjects encountered in Lagos and Dakar, extending his investigation of Black representation across the African diaspora.

lesser_known_works.title: Sleep
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting referencing Courbet's famous lesbian sleeping figures, demonstrating the range of Wiley's engagement with Western art historical canons.

artists.bio:
Anthony Caro was born in New Malden, Surrey in 1924 and served as Henry Moore's assistant from 1951 to 1953, an experience that confirmed both his dedication to sculpture and his eventual need to define himself against Moore's dominant influence. A pivotal visit to the United States in 1959, where he met David Smith and Kenneth Noland and absorbed the directness of American formalist thinking, transformed his practice: he returned to London and in 1960 began producing the welded steel sculptures, placed directly on the ground without plinths, that made his international reputation.

These works — long, horizontal assemblages of I-beams, girders, mesh, and sheet steel, painted in single colours or left bare — were revolutionary in their rejection of both representation and the traditional elevated plinth, insisting on a relationship with the viewer at floor level, as presences rather than objects of display. Works such as Early One Morning, in which red-painted steel elements seem to draw the eye across the gallery floor like a musical phrase, are among the most original sculptural achievements of the 1960s.

Caro subsequently explored table sculptures, bronze works, and late large-scale pieces that incorporated architectural thinking. He taught at St Martin's School of Art, where his influence on a generation of British sculptors — including Philip King, William Tucker, and later the students of the New Generation — was profound. He was knighted in 1987 and continued producing significant work until shortly before his death in 2013.

famous_works.title: Early One Morning
famous_works.notes: A groundbreaking red-painted steel sculpture placed directly on the floor, in which cantilevered and horizontal elements compose a work of remarkable spatial musicality.

famous_works.title: Prairie
famous_works.notes: A low, horizontal sculpture in which burnished steel elements seem to hover just above the ground, creating a sense of floating expanse that gives the work its title.

famous_works.title: The Window
famous_works.notes: A large sculpture incorporating a steel mesh element that introduces transparency and dematerialisation into Caro's otherwise solid and weighty formal vocabulary.

lesser_known_works.title: After Summer
lesser_known_works.notes: A painted steel work that demonstrates the lyrical range of Caro's floor sculptures alongside their better-known more robust counterparts.

lesser_known_works.title: Midday
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the earliest post-plinth sculptures, marking Caro's decisive break from Moore-influenced figuration toward pure abstract assemblage.

artists.bio:
Lisa Yuskavage was born in Philadelphia in 1962 and studied at the Tyler School of Art and Yale University School of Art. She came to prominence in the 1990s with paintings of exaggerated female figures — pneumatic forms in states of semi-undress or sexual availability — rendered with a technical virtuosity that deliberately combined Old Master glazing techniques with subject matter drawn from commercial pornography and the kitsch of pin-up culture.

Yuskavage's work is one of the most sustained and serious engagements with the eroticised female image in contemporary art. Her figures — which have evolved from grotesquely exaggerated adolescent forms in her early work toward more complex, psychologically ambiguous characters in recent years — are presented in atmospheric colour fields of remarkable beauty, the luminous glazed surfaces placing the provocative imagery within a tradition of painterly seriousness that complicates any simple reading. The work forces the viewer to negotiate simultaneously the beauty of the painting, the discomfort of the imagery, and the question of who controls the gaze and for whose benefit.

Yuskavage is represented by David Zwirner Gallery and her work has been the subject of major survey exhibitions at institutions including the Rose Art Museum and the Frye Art Museum. She stands as one of the most important figurative painters of her generation.

famous_works.title: Teenie Weenie
famous_works.notes: An early painting in which an exaggerated adolescent female figure is rendered in Yuskavage's characteristic glazed technique, forcing a confrontation with the politics of the erotic image.

famous_works.title: The Ones That Don't Want To
famous_works.notes: A large multi-figure painting demonstrating the development of Yuskavage's mature style, in which psychologically complex figures inhabit an atmospheric landscape.

famous_works.title: Little Bitch
famous_works.notes: A painting in which Yuskavage's confrontational approach to the erotic female image is combined with her characteristic formal virtuosity to create a work that is simultaneously beautiful and disturbing.

lesser_known_works.title: Room for Cream (Warm)
lesser_known_works.notes: An atmospheric interior work in which Yuskavage's investigation of light and glazed surface creates an environment of immersive colour within which her figure subjects operate.

lesser_known_works.title: Bruiser
lesser_known_works.notes: A large painting from Yuskavage's mature period in which the formal vocabulary of her earlier provocations is applied with new psychological depth.

artists.bio:
Katsura Funakoshi was born in Morioka, Iwate Prefecture, Japan in 1951, the son of a wood sculptor, and studied at Tokyo University of Fine Arts. He has developed one of the most distinctive sculptural practices in contemporary Japanese art: small-to-medium scale figures carved from camphor wood and marble, combining materials and formal traditions from both Eastern and Western sculpture to create figures of profound psychological stillness.

Funakoshi's figures — standing, contemplating, eyes cast slightly downward or gazing into middle distance — have a quality of suspended interior life that is rare in contemporary figurative sculpture. The combination of the warm golden-brown of camphor wood (often used for the torso and limbs) with polished marble (used for specific features) creates a contrast of materials that is simultaneously warm and cool, organic and mineral, suggesting a complexity of inner constitution that the simplified exterior forms belie.

He has been exhibited by Galerie Thaddaeus Ropac in Paris and internationally, and his work is held by major European and Japanese museums. Funakoshi represents a significant contribution to the dialogue between Eastern and Western sculptural traditions in contemporary practice.

famous_works.title: Carrying the Sky
famous_works.notes: A carved wood and marble figure in which the characteristic stillness and psychological depth of Funakoshi's mature practice are fully articulated.

famous_works.title: The Boundary of Time
famous_works.notes: A standing figure in which the combination of camphor wood and marble creates a meditation on the relationship between temporal existence and enduring material.

famous_works.title: Facing the Light
famous_works.notes: A figure oriented toward an implied light source that demonstrates Funakoshi's interest in the relationship between the embodied self and its orienting relationship to the world.

lesser_known_works.title: Head Study
lesser_known_works.notes: An early carved portrait head that demonstrates the formal foundations of the psychological and material investigations of Funakoshi's mature sculpture.

lesser_known_works.title: Floating Figure
lesser_known_works.notes: A horizontal figure that introduces an unusual spatial orientation into Funakoshi's customarily standing or slightly inclined figure repertoire.

artists.bio:
André Derain was born in Chatou, near Paris, in 1880 and met Matisse in 1899, a friendship that would prove foundational for both painters. Together they spent the summer of 1905 in Collioure, producing the explosive colour canvases that, when exhibited that autumn at the Salon d'Automne alongside works by Vlaminck, Marquet, and others, provoked the critic Louis Vauxcelles to describe the room's contents as 'Fauves' — wild beasts. Derain was among the most technically accomplished of the group, bringing to Fauvist colour a structural ambition that quickly drew him toward Cézanne and then toward Cubism.

His London paintings of 1905–06, commissioned by the dealer Ambroise Vollard, are among the great achievements of early twentieth-century painting: views of the Thames and Westminster rendered in chromatic intensity that transforms the grey English capital into a Mediterranean vision. Derain subsequently moved through a 'Gothic' period of darker, more archaic figuration, and after World War I he settled into a conservative, classicising mode that dismayed many of his earlier admirers and led to accusations of collaboration after World War II.

This trajectory — from revolutionary youth to conservative maturity and moral ambiguity — has made Derain a complex figure. His early work remains exceptional, and the full range of his career, encompassing painting, sculpture, set design for the Ballets Russes, and book illustration, represents one of the most restlessly intelligent artistic personalities of the early twentieth century.

famous_works.title: Charing Cross Bridge
famous_works.notes: One of the great London paintings, in which the Thames is flooded with improbable chromatic intensity that transforms industrial modernity into Fauvist spectacle.

famous_works.title: The Dance
famous_works.notes: A large figure composition in which Fauvist colour and archaic formal simplification converge to produce a work of monumental barbaric energy.

famous_works.title: Mountains at Collioure
famous_works.notes: One of the key canvases from the Collioure summer with Matisse, in which Cézanniste structure is supercharged with colour that approaches pure sensation.

lesser_known_works.title: The Old Tree
lesser_known_works.notes: A pre-Fauvist painting that reveals Derain's debt to Van Gogh and announces the chromatic ambitions he would pursue at Collioure.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait of Matisse
lesser_known_works.notes: A small but historically significant portrait exchanged with Matisse's portrait of Derain, testifying to the intimacy of the two painters' dialogue.

top_lot.title: Baigneuses dansantes
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Bernar Venet was born in Château-Arnoux-Saint-Auban in the south of France in 1941. Arriving in Nice at seventeen, he encountered the milieu of the Nouveau Réalisme — Klein, Arman, Raysse — which confirmed his conviction that art had to engage directly with the real rather than represent it. Moving to Paris and then New York in 1966, he developed a conceptual practice that used mathematical equations, industrial materials, and diagrammatic notation as both subject and formal language.

Venet's early conceptual works presented mathematical formulas and acoustic sounds as artworks, making abstraction literal: the work was not a representation of mathematical thought but an instance of it. He spent the 1970s entirely withdrawing from art-making — a conceptual act of suspension — before returning in 1976 with the Angles and Lines works in Cor-ten steel that have become his most recognisable sculptures. These monumental arcs and angles of industrial steel, deployed in public spaces worldwide, treat the line as both mathematical entity and spatial presence.

His installations have been exhibited in major public spaces from the Esplanade des Invalides in Paris to Central Park in New York. Venet's practice remains one of the most rigorous and consistent examples of Conceptual Art's investigation of mathematical and logical systems as both subject matter and formal structure.

famous_works.title: Indeterminate Line
famous_works.notes: A massive Cor-ten steel arc that curves in three-dimensional space with apparent structural randomness, embodying the mathematical concept of the indeterminate.

famous_works.title: Major Accident (87.5° Arc × 19)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale installation of interlocking steel arcs in Paris, in which apparent structural collapse becomes a controlled meditation on geometric accident.

famous_works.title: Degree 238.5 (Angle)
famous_works.notes: A steel sculpture in which a single angular form is defined by its precise mathematical description, insisting on equation and physical presence as identical terms.

lesser_known_works.title: Black Tar
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work presenting tar directly on canvas — material without transformation — establishing Venet's refusal of metaphor in favour of literal material reality.

lesser_known_works.title: Télex
lesser_known_works.notes: A conceptual work using industrial communication systems as artistic medium, extending Venet's investigation of language and notation into institutional critique.

artists.bio:
Jesse Mockrin was born in San Francisco in 1981 and studied at the University of California Davis and the University of California San Diego. She lives and works in Los Angeles and has developed a painting practice that engages directly with the history of Western figurative painting — particularly the Baroque and Rococo — through strategies of radical cropping, scale distortion, and isolation that transform art-historical imagery into something simultaneously familiar and estranged.

Mockrin isolates fragments of Old Master paintings — a hand reaching toward a face, a cascade of drapery, a pair of lips in extreme close-up — and repaints them at large scale in oil on linen, preserving the technical sophistication of her sources while stripping away their narrative context. The effect is to make visible the formal and erotic qualities embedded in traditional figuration that the narrative context usually organises and directs: without the story, the bodies and gestures themselves become primary.

She has been exhibited with Night Gallery in Los Angeles and Rachel Uffner Gallery in New York and has attracted critical attention as a painter whose engagement with art historical sources is technically serious, formally intelligent, and conceptually considered. Her work participates in a broader reexamination of the Western figurative tradition through the lens of contemporary feminism.

famous_works.title: Thirst
famous_works.notes: A close-up of a figure drinking from a vessel isolated from its Baroque narrative context, in which the extreme cropping reveals the erotic charge embedded in a conventional devotional gesture.

famous_works.title: Tenderness
famous_works.notes: An enlarged fragment of an Old Master work focusing on two figures in close proximity, in which the removal of narrative context makes the physical and emotional relationship between bodies the primary subject.

famous_works.title: Abundance
famous_works.notes: A large-scale painting derived from a Rubens figure in which the baroque treatment of flesh and drapery is presented at a scale that transforms decorative tradition into intense physical presence.

lesser_known_works.title: After Boucher
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work establishing the methodology of Rococo source isolation that became central to Mockrin's practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Grasp
lesser_known_works.notes: A fragment focused on a hand gesture isolated from its compositional context, demonstrating the range of Mockrin's attention across different register of the Old Master figurative vocabulary.

artists.bio:
Spencer Lewis was born in Texas in 1984 and has developed a painting practice that combines gestural abstraction with process-based methodologies to create large-scale canvases of considerable energy and formal intelligence. Working in Los Angeles, he builds up his paintings through successive applications of paint, using brushes, rollers, and physical gestures to create layered surfaces that preserve the history of their own making.

Lewis's paintings are characterised by their scale, their physical directness, and their engagement with the history of gestural abstraction — particularly the New York School and its successors. His process involves creating multiple stages of painting that are partially or fully covered by subsequent applications, producing surfaces in which earlier marks appear as ghost images beneath later additions. The result is painting that reads as both archaeological deposit and living surface.

He has been exhibited with Nino Mier Gallery and has attracted attention as a painter committed to the possibilities of gestural abstraction in the contemporary moment. Lewis's work participates in the ongoing investigation of what painting can do within an expanded field of visual and cultural practice.

famous_works.title: Large Process Work
famous_works.notes: A monumental canvas in which multiple stages of gestural painting are layered to create a surface of complex material and visual history.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Red and Black)
famous_works.notes: A large abstract painting in which the dialogue between warm and dark colours creates a spatial tension characteristic of Lewis's mature approach.

famous_works.title: Process Study
famous_works.notes: A work demonstrating the layered methodology central to Lewis's practice, in which earlier stages of painting remain visible beneath later additions.

lesser_known_works.title: Early gestural works
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Lewis's formation period demonstrating the development of his gestural approach before its full maturation.

lesser_known_works.title: Small format studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Works in smaller format that demonstrate the intimacy and directness of Lewis's mark-making removed from monumental scale.

artists.bio:
Chuck Close was born in Monroe, Washington in 1940 and studied at the University of Washington, the Yale School of Art, and the Akademie der Bildenden Künste in Vienna. Returning to New York in the mid-1960s, he developed the photorealist practice for which he became internationally known: large-scale portraits painted from photographic sources, initially in black and white and then in colour, that match — and in their scale dramatically exceed — photographic detail.

Close worked methodically, transferring information from a photograph to canvas through a grid system that broke both image and execution into systematic units. The resulting works have the paradoxical quality of being both hyper-realistic (when seen from a distance) and clearly constructed from visible marks (when approached closely). After a spinal injury in 1988 that left him largely paralysed and dependent on a wheelchair, Close adapted his method to paint with a brush strapped to his wrist, producing even more openly gestural and markedly constructed surfaces that pushed toward abstraction while maintaining the photographic schema.

Close was represented by Pace Gallery and his works are held by major museums worldwide including the Museum of Modern Art and the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York. His practice stands as a major contribution to post-war American painting's investigation of image, process, and the boundary between photographic and painted representation.

famous_works.title: Big Self-Portrait
famous_works.notes: A monumental nine-foot black-and-white self-portrait that established Close's photorealist method and announced a new approach to the relationship between painting and photography.

famous_works.title: Phil
famous_works.notes: A large-scale portrait of the composer Philip Glass that exemplifies Close's early photorealist manner at its most technically demanding.

famous_works.title: Self-Portrait
famous_works.notes: A post-injury self-portrait in which Close's adapted technique produces a surface of visible, circular marks that register both the photograph and the physical fact of making.

lesser_known_works.title: Fanny (Fingerpainting)
lesser_known_works.notes: A portrait made by applying paint directly with the artist's fingerprints, a technique that makes the body itself the mark-making instrument.

lesser_known_works.title: Lyle
lesser_known_works.notes: One of Close's most explicit grid paintings, in which the constructive logic of his method is left fully visible as an integral part of the work's meaning.

artists.bio:
Deborah Butterfield was born in San Diego in 1949 and studied at the University of California Davis under the sculptor Manuel Neri. She began making horse sculptures in 1973 while pregnant with her first child, and the horse has remained the subject — and in some sense the obsession — of her sculptural practice for more than five decades. The choice of subject was personal and specific: she is a horse owner and rider and brings to her sculptures a knowledge of equine anatomy, movement, and psychology that is rare among artists who depict animals.

Butterfield's early horses were made from mud, sticks, and found materials in rough, low-to-the-ground configurations that emphasised the animal's weight and earthiness. Over time her materials evolved toward found metal — steel pipes, scrap iron, car parts — assembled into life-size or larger horses whose surfaces retain the visual fragmentation of their component materials while conveying the specific stance, alertness, or repose of the individual animal. In the 1980s she began casting these assembled found-material horses in bronze, disguising the bronze to appear as though it were actually wood — a remarkable act of material deception that raises questions about authenticity, nature, and fabrication.

Butterfield is represented by Tippet Rise Art Center and her work is held by major museums throughout the United States. She represents an exceptional case in contemporary sculpture of a practice of extraordinary consistency and depth centred on a single subject.

famous_works.title: Woodrow
famous_works.notes: A life-size horse assembled from found tree branches and sticks, combining the natural material of the forest with precise knowledge of equine form.

famous_works.title: Paladin
famous_works.notes: A bronze horse cast to appear as though made from rough wooden branches, one of the first works in which Butterfield deployed the material deception of disguised bronze.

famous_works.title: Monekana
famous_works.notes: A large-scale assembled-metal horse whose fragmented industrial surface describes the specific alertness of a horse in a state of watchful attention.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Large Red Horse)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early steel-pipe horse that marks the transition from Butterfield's initial mud-and-found-material works toward the assembled metal constructions of her mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Horse with Rider
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the rare works in which Butterfield includes a human presence, examining the complex dynamic of equestrian partnership through her characteristic assembled-material approach.

artists.bio:
Camille Claudel was born in Fère-en-Tardenois in 1864 and demonstrated exceptional sculptural ability from childhood. She arrived in Paris in 1882 and joined Alfred Boucher's studio, subsequently becoming a pupil, collaborator, model, and lover of Auguste Rodin — a relationship that was professionally transformative and personally devastating for both parties. Working in Rodin's studio from approximately 1884 to 1898, she contributed to several of his major works while simultaneously developing her own independent practice.

Claudel's sculptures demonstrate a psychological intensity and formal mastery entirely her own. Where Rodin's work tends toward the monumental and mythological, Claudel often worked on a more intimate scale, capturing specific emotional states — jealousy, age, abandonment — with a directness that is sometimes more psychologically searching than her mentor's work. The Waltz, The Mature Age (L'Âge mûr), and The Gossips all demonstrate her ability to render complex human relationships in three dimensions with simultaneous formal elegance and emotional urgency.

After her break with Rodin in 1898, Claudel worked in increasing isolation, eventually destroying much of her own work. In 1913, her family had her committed to a psychiatric asylum, where she remained until her death in 1943, despite no evidence of clinical mental illness beyond the acute distress that her circumstances warranted. The Musée Camille Claudel in Nogent-sur-Seine holds the most comprehensive collection of her work.

famous_works.title: The Waltz
famous_works.notes: A bronze group of a dancing couple in which the figures' spiralling movement is captured with a physical and emotional intimacy that goes beyond Rodin's influence toward Claudel's own expressive language.

famous_works.title: L'Âge mûr (The Mature Age)
famous_works.notes: A profoundly personal triptych figure group generally read as an allegory of Rodin's departure from Claudel for Camille Rose Beuret, rendered with anguished psychological acuity.

famous_works.title: The Gossips
famous_works.notes: Four women huddled in intimate conference, carved in onyx and bronze with a jewel-like refinement that demonstrates Claudel's technical mastery independently of Rodin.

lesser_known_works.title: Young Woman with a Sheaf
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating the naturalistic precision that Claudel brought to figurative sculpture before her style developed its characteristic psychological intensity.

lesser_known_works.title: Perseus and the Gorgon
lesser_known_works.notes: A marble relief combining mythological subject with a psychological reading that has been interpreted as a meditation on artistic rivalry and domination.

top_lot.title: Rêve au coin du feu
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
David Salle was born in Norman, Oklahoma in 1952 and studied at the California Institute of the Arts under John Baldessari — a formative encounter with Conceptual Art's critique of modernism. Moving to New York, he became a central figure in the Neo-Expressionist movement of the early 1980s alongside Julian Schnabel, Eric Fischl, and Francesco Clemente, and was among the most theoretically sophisticated painters of that generation.

Salle's paintings are constructed through the juxtaposition of multiple image registers on a single canvas: images from art history, advertising, pornography, cartoon, and his own previous paintings are placed in proximity without being formally integrated, creating works that operate through disjunction rather than coherence. Unlike the emotional expressionism of some contemporaries, Salle's work maintains a cool, analytical quality — images are quotations, and the relationship between them is the subject of the work, not any of the images individually.

He was at the centre of 1980s art market activity and has been represented by Mary Boone Gallery and Gagosian Gallery.
