His work has been the subject of sustained critical debate about the nature of postmodern painting and its relationship to irony, appropriation, and the legacy of pictorial meaning. Despite periods of critical reevaluation, his early paintings remain historically significant documents of their moment.

famous_works.title: Tennyson
famous_works.notes: A large diptych combining Ingres-derived academic figuration with a schematic furniture image, demonstrating Salle's method of pictorial juxtaposition at its most unresolved and provocative.

famous_works.title: His Brain
famous_works.notes: A multi-panel work in which fragments of figurative imagery are distributed across a large canvas without pictorial hierarchy, asserting the equivalence and exchangeability of all image sources.

famous_works.title: King Kong
famous_works.notes: A late-decade canvas in which the density of image-quotation reaches a level of almost baroque complexity, testing the limits of the disjunctive method Salle had developed.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Product
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work establishing the image-quotation method before it received the art market attention that would complicate its reception in the following decade.

lesser_known_works.title: Sextant in Dogtown
lesser_known_works.notes: A mid-career work in which the disjunctive method is applied with particular formal precision, the navigational reference of the title adding a dimension of ironic purposefulness.

artists.bio:
Grant Yun was born in New Jersey in 1994 to Korean-American parents and studied at New York University and the New York Studio School, where his deep engagement with the history of Western painting coexists with the specificity of his own cultural background and contemporary experience. He paints intimate scenes — figures at tables, in kitchens, around food — in a manner that combines the careful observation of Dutch and Flemish genre painting with a more directly emotional approach to colour and light.

Food occupies a central place in Yun's practice, serving as both subject and metaphor: the preparation and sharing of meals, the specific visual textures of Korean and American cooking, the social rituals organised around eating. These scenes are painted with genuine formal ambition, the compositional intelligence and tonal sensitivity of his canvases demonstrating a serious engagement with the material and historical conditions of painting.

Yun has been exhibited with Tara Downs Gallery and has received critical attention as part of a generation of artists for whom the intimate, domestic, and culturally specific are primary pictorial concerns. His work contributes to an expanding field of contemporary painting that insists on the adequacy of ordinary, unspectacular subject matter to the ambitions of serious art.

famous_works.title: Banchan
famous_works.notes: A painting of the small side dishes of Korean cuisine arranged on a table in which the formal organisation of the still-life tradition is brought to bear on specifically Korean culinary culture.

famous_works.title: Kitchen Study
famous_works.notes: A domestic kitchen scene in which the preparation of food becomes an occasion for careful investigation of the relationship between light, space, and the specificity of familiar objects.

famous_works.title: Family Meal
famous_works.notes: A multi-figure composition organised around shared food that explores the social and emotional dimensions of Korean-American domestic ritual.

lesser_known_works.title: After Dinner
lesser_known_works.notes: A quiet scene of aftermath — empty plates, glasses — in which Yun's attention to light and surface texture transforms residue into subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Still Life with Persimmons
lesser_known_works.notes: A traditional still-life subject in which the choice of specifically Korean fruit locates the genre within Yun's cultural particularity.

artists.bio:
William Eggleston was born in Memphis, Tennessee in 1939 and grew up in Sumner, Mississippi, in the social world of the mid-century American South. He took up photography seriously in the early 1960s, initially working in black and white before switching to colour — specifically the dye transfer printing process that saturated his images with a richness no other colour printing technology could match. His relationship with John Szarkowski, curator of photography at the Museum of Modern Art, led to his pivotal 1976 exhibition there.

Eggleston's MoMA show, presenting colour photographs of seemingly banal subjects — a tricycle on a residential street, a freezer in a kitchen, a light bulb on a red ceiling — was one of the most controversial photography exhibitions in the museum's history. Critics who dismissed the work as 'snapshots' failed to understand its profound formal intelligence: each image is a study in pictorial structure of absolute precision, and the colour — the saturated greens and reds and blues of Kodachrome and dye transfer — gives the Mississippi and Tennessee world it documents a heightened reality that is simultaneously specific and archetypal.

Eggleston is one of the most influential photographers of the twentieth century, whose legitimisation of colour photography and vernacular subject matter transformed the medium. He continues to photograph and is represented by David Zwirner Gallery.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Tricycle)
famous_works.notes: The photograph known as 'The Democratic Forest's most iconic image' in which a child's tricycle photographed from a low angle becomes a formal composition of extraordinary precision.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Red Ceiling)
famous_works.notes: A photograph of a single bare light bulb on a vivid red ceiling that demonstrates Eggleston's mastery of dye transfer colour and his ability to make the utterly quotidian formally overwhelming.

famous_works.title: Sumner, Mississippi (Flies)
famous_works.notes: A photograph of two men at a counter with the title noting the location that locates the vernacular American South within a formal structure of considerable precision.

lesser_known_works.title: Black and White series
lesser_known_works.notes: Early work in monochrome that demonstrates the formal intelligence Eggleston brought to colour photography and that he had developed first in the more conventional medium.

lesser_known_works.title: Nashville (1972 series)
lesser_known_works.notes: Photographs made in Nashville that extend Eggleston's documentation of Southern vernacular life across a different urban context.

artists.bio:
Maysha Mohamedi was born in 1979 and received a PhD in neuroscience from Stanford University before pursuing a career as a visual artist — a dual background that has shaped her approach to painting and her understanding of perception, attention, and the relationship between cognitive process and visual experience. Working primarily in oil and mixed media, she develops abstract canvases of considerable complexity that draw on both scientific thinking about perception and a sustained engagement with the history of abstract painting.

Mohamedi's paintings are characterised by their layered, gestural complexity and their particular chromatic sensibility — a combination of warm and cool, dense and translucent passages that creates surfaces of both immediate visual appeal and sustained pictorial depth. Her practice refuses any single referential framework — the works are not illustrational abstractions of scientific concepts but independent formal objects whose intelligence is painterly rather than conceptual.

She has been exhibited at Night Gallery in Los Angeles and has attracted attention as a painter whose unusual background enriches rather than dominates her formal practice. Mohamedi represents a thoughtful engagement with the question of what abstract painting can do in the contemporary moment.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Warm/Cool)
famous_works.notes: A large abstract painting in which the dialogue between warm and cool colour passages creates a spatial and chromatic dynamic of considerable formal intelligence.

famous_works.title: Deep Study
famous_works.notes: A densely layered oil painting in which multiple gestural passes create a surface that rewards sustained looking with increasing formal and chromatic complexity.

famous_works.title: Field Notes
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which the painting's development process — additions, subtractions, revisions — is preserved in the final surface as a visible record of formal investigation.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Gestural Studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Paintings from Mohamedi's early practice demonstrating the development of her layered gestural methodology.

lesser_known_works.title: Atmospheric Study
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting focused on the relationship between tonal atmosphere and gestural mark that demonstrates Mohamedi's particular interest in the cognitive dimensions of visual perception.

artists.bio:
Marisa Merz was born in Turin in 1926 and was the only woman associated with the Arte Povera movement, though her relationship to it was never programmatic — she worked in ways that were simultaneously aligned with the group's concerns and entirely her own. Her practice began to develop publicly in the mid-1960s alongside the work of her husband Mario Merz, whose Igloo constructions are among the better-known Arte Povera works, but Marisa's own work maintained a consistent otherness even within this context.

Merz worked with materials of great fragility and intimacy — copper wire woven into delicate three-dimensional forms, raw clay heads of extraordinary psychological intensity, wax, aluminium sheets cut into organic shapes, thread, and drawing. Her Bietigheim heads — clay or wax portrait heads of a recurring female face in states of psychological concentration — are perhaps her most immediately striking works: objects of modest physical scale that carry a quality of inner life far beyond what their materials would seem to permit.

Her practice was consistently under-recognised during the decades when her male counterparts received major institutional attention, and it was only in the last decade of her life that retrospectives at the Castello di Rivoli, the Hammer Museum, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art confirmed her importance. She was awarded the Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement at the Venice Biennale in 2013.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Bietigheim Head)
famous_works.notes: A wax and clay portrait head of Merz's recurring female face in a state of concentrated interiority, one of many such heads that constitute the most sustained meditation on female inner life in Arte Povera.

famous_works.title: Living Sculpture
famous_works.notes: A copper wire construction made in the domestic space of the Merz home that established the private and intimate scale that characterises Marisa's work throughout her career.

famous_works.title: Nylon Spirals
famous_works.notes: Ceiling installations of nylon spirals that transform domestic space into an immaterial sculpture of extreme delicacy, establishing Merz's particular contribution to Arte Povera's investigation of domestic and spatial experience.

lesser_known_works.title: Drawing series
lesser_known_works.notes: A sustained body of works on paper in which drawing maintains the intimate, handmade quality characteristic of all Merz's practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Aluminium Leaves
lesser_known_works.notes: Early cut aluminium works that demonstrate the combination of industrial material and organic form that characterises Arte Povera's approach to material culture.

artists.bio:
Leonardo Drew was born in Tallahassee, Florida in 1961 and grew up in Bridgeport, Connecticut. He studied at the Parsons School of Design and the Cooper Union in New York, where he developed a sculptural practice centred on large-scale wall-mounted installations constructed from salvaged, deteriorating materials — rusted steel, cotton, rope, wood, paper — that explore themes of entropy, decay, history, and cultural memory.

Drew's installations are typically vast: walls of accumulation in which layers of material are burned, rusted, oxidised, or otherwise subjected to processes of transformation that give them the quality of geological or archaeological deposits. The works evoke without illustrating — the cotton references the history of slave labour without being explicitly illustrational, the rusted steel recalls industrial America without being documentary. This quality of resonance rather than illustration, of emotional and historical weight carried by material and formal means rather than explicit imagery, is what distinguishes Drew's practice.

He has been exhibited at Sikkema Jenkins & Co. Gallery in New York and has received major commissions for permanent public installations. His work is held by major museums including the Museum of Modern Art and the Brooklyn Museum, and he has been the recipient of numerous awards confirming his importance within American sculpture.

famous_works.title: Number 8
famous_works.notes: A large wall installation of rusted steel and cotton in which the accumulated material creates a surface of historical and emotional weight that resonates with the history of African American labour and endurance.

famous_works.title: City
famous_works.notes: A monumental installation that fills an entire wall with densely layered and transformed materials creating an abstract cityscape of decay and regeneration.

famous_works.title: Number 106
famous_works.notes: A large-scale work in which burned and blackened materials create a surface of profound formal complexity and historical resonance.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Rope)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work using rope as a primary material that demonstrates Drew's initial investigation of salvaged and deteriorated material as formal and historical subject.

lesser_known_works.title: Number 47
lesser_known_works.notes: A mid-career installation in which cotton — the material of American slavery — is transformed through burning and oxidation into a work of complex formal and historical resonance.

artists.bio:
Jennifer Guidi was born in Chicago in 1972 and studied at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago and the Loyola Marymount University in Los Angeles. She came to international attention in the 2010s with a body of work that fuses the spiritual traditions of cosmic abstraction — Hilma af Klint, Agnes Martin, Giorgio de Chirico — with the specific sensory environment of the California coast, producing paintings of quiet, enveloping luminosity.

Guidi's most characteristic works feature circles or radiant star-forms built up from thousands of tiny marks applied in sand-inflected paint, layer upon layer, creating surfaces of almost hallucinatory depth and warmth. The marks — made with a small palette knife or brush — suggest meditation, ritual, and the accumulation of time as much as they constitute a pictorial image. Her colour palette is keyed to warmth: gold, pink, orange, cream, with occasional passages of night blue — colours of sunrise and sunset, of the liminal moments between states.

She has been exhibited with Gagosian Gallery and Hauser & Wirth and her work has been collected by major institutions and private collectors internationally. Guidi represents a significant strand in contemporary abstract painting that is simultaneously formally rigorous and spiritually oriented.

famous_works.title: Alchemy (Universal Beyond 8, 1T, Illuminated Sand SF no. 89)
famous_works.notes: A large circular radiant form built up from thousands of tiny marks in warm sand-inflected paint, creating an image of concentrated luminous energy.

famous_works.title: Painted Desert (UM SF no. 41)
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which the accumulation of mark-making creates a surface that evokes both the specific topography of the California desert and a more generalised sense of cosmic depth.

famous_works.title: Universal Beyond series
famous_works.notes: An ongoing series of radiant circle paintings in which the accumulation of small marks creates fields of luminous energy that reference both mystical traditions and the physicality of sustained artistic labour.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Gold Ground)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early radiant work that established Guidi's mature vocabulary of mark accumulation and warm chromatic field before the development of her sand-inflected technique.

lesser_known_works.title: Night Sky
lesser_known_works.notes: A darker-palette work in which the radiant form is set against deep blue-black, demonstrating the range of Guidi's investigation of luminosity across different atmospheric registers.

artists.bio:
Leonard Hilton McGurr, who works as Futura 2000, was born in New York City in 1955 and began painting on subway cars in the early 1970s, developing a distinctive abstract approach within the figurative graffiti world of that era. His tags and throw-ups gave way to complex, swirling, non-objective compositions that brought him recognition as one of the most formally innovative writers working on the New York subway system before the Metropolitan Transit Authority's graffiti eradication campaigns effectively ended that era.

In the early 1980s, Futura made the transition from subway cars to gallery walls, exhibiting at the Annina Nosei Gallery alongside Jean-Michel Basquiat and other artists emerging from the street into the commercial art world. His work of this period — large canvases of shifting, nebular abstraction in spray paint and acrylic — attracted critical attention from writers including Rene Ricard as a significant bridge between street culture and the post-war abstract tradition. He served as a founding member of the Rock Steady Crew's artistic orbit and collaborated extensively with the music industry.

Futura has continued painting, designing, and collaborating across multiple cultural domains including fashion, skateboarding, and music for decades, maintaining a position at the intersection of street culture and fine art that he helped define. His work is collected internationally and has achieved significant auction prices.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Pointman)
famous_works.notes: A large canvas in which Futura's characteristic nebular abstract forms are animated by his signature Pointman character, bridging the worlds of graffiti iconography and abstract expressionist energy.

famous_works.title: Break
famous_works.notes: A major early gallery canvas that established Futura's language of swirling atmospheric abstraction within the context of the early 1980s New York art world.

famous_works.title: Atom Bomb
famous_works.notes: A canvas from the transitional period between subway and gallery that applies the physical scale and energy of street painting to the context of institutional display.

lesser_known_works.title: Subway Car Piece
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the subway pieces from Futura's formative period that established his abstract approach within the predominantly figurative landscape of New York graffiti.

lesser_known_works.title: Study in Grey
lesser_known_works.notes: A more restrained mid-decade canvas demonstrating the range of tonal and chromatic approaches within Futura's abstract spray-paint methodology.

artists.bio:
Ed Clark was born in New Orleans in 1926 and moved to Chicago as a child, later studying at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago and in Paris at the École des Beaux-Arts, where he was exposed to the developing post-war European avant-garde. Returning to New York in the 1950s, he became a member of the Cedar Tavern circle and a participant in the Tanager Gallery, one of the downtown cooperative galleries of the period — the only Black artist regularly associated with this milieu.

Clark's early paintings included shaped canvases — he was one of the first artists to present a painting that extended beyond the rectangle, in 1957 — but his most significant contribution was his development of the broom stroke: he applied paint with an industrial-scale push broom, sweeping across large canvases on the floor to produce broad, sweeping gestures of colour that generated a sense of vast atmospheric space. These works are simultaneously gestural and colour-field in their logic, the physical energy of application producing luminous, cloud-like expanses of tone.

Clark worked largely outside the commercial gallery system until late in his life, when sustained critical attention — particularly from younger Black artists who identified him as a crucial and systematically overlooked predecessor — brought his work the institutional recognition it deserved. The Studio Museum in Harlem and major private collections have played a central role in preserving and promoting his legacy.

famous_works.title: Bigness
famous_works.notes: A monumental broom-stroke painting in which sweeping gestures of colour create an atmosphere of immense spatial expansion, demonstrating Clark's synthesis of gestural and colour-field approaches.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Shaped Canvas)
famous_works.notes: An early shaped canvas that placed Clark among the first artists to challenge the conventional rectangular format, predating the shaped canvas explorations of Frank Stella and others.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Sweep)
famous_works.notes: A large broom-stroke canvas in which the physical scale of the gesture is matched by the scale of the atmospheric colour field it produces.

lesser_known_works.title: Composition in Blue
lesser_known_works.notes: An early painting predating the broom technique that demonstrates Clark's initial engagement with Abstract Expressionist gesture and colour.

lesser_known_works.title: Morning Star
lesser_known_works.notes: A late painting in which Clark's atmospheric broom-stroke method achieves particular luminosity in the handling of warm orange-gold tones.

artists.bio:
Johan Barthold Jongkind was born in Lattrop, Netherlands in 1819 and moved to Paris in 1846, where he studied under Eugène Isabey. He became closely associated with the developing plein air tradition and was a pivotal figure in the transition between the Barbizon School's nature-based realism and the full emergence of Impressionism. Monet himself later acknowledged Jongkind as a key teacher and predecessor.

Jongkind's paintings and watercolours focus primarily on water, sky, and light — the estuaries of the Seine and Schelde, the harbours of Dutch and Norman towns, the urban landscapes of Paris — rendered with a freshness and informality that anticipates Impressionist concerns. His handling of light on water, his interest in the atmospheric effects of particular times of day and weather conditions, and his willingness to leave passages of a work in a state of apparent incompleteness all point directly toward what Monet and his contemporaries would develop.

He suffered from mental instability for much of his life, retreating eventually to Grenoble, where he died in an asylum in 1891. His watercolours in particular demonstrate an extraordinary lightness and economy of observation that makes them among the finest achievements of nineteenth-century graphic practice.

famous_works.title: The Seine and Notre Dame in Paris
famous_works.notes: A view of the Paris river and cathedral from the quays in which Jongkind's treatment of light on water anticipates the Impressionist approach that Monet would develop in the following decade.

famous_works.title: Outskirts of Paris
famous_works.notes: A plein air painting of the urban fringe in which the specific quality of Parisian light and the informal compositional approach distinguish Jongkind from his Barbizon predecessors.

famous_works.title: Dutch Canal with Windmills
famous_works.notes: A watercolour of the Dutch landscape that demonstrates the extraordinary lightness and freshness of Jongkind's observational practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Etching: View of the Seine
lesser_known_works.notes: One of many etchings in which Jongkind translated his plein air visual intelligence into the printmaking medium with remarkable facility.

lesser_known_works.title: The Harbour of Honfleur
lesser_known_works.notes: A harbour scene from the Norman coast where Jongkind worked alongside Monet and Boudin in the crucial summers that formed French Impressionism.

top_lot.title: Patineurs près d'Overschie
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Franz Kline was born in Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania in 1910 and studied at Boston University and the Heatherley School of Fine Art in London before arriving in New York in 1938, where he initially painted figural works — landscapes, portraits, café scenes — in a conventional realist manner. The transformation of his practice came around 1949–50, when — according to art world legend — he used a Bell-Opticon projector to enlarge one of his small pen-and-ink drawings to room scale and recognised in the magnified detail an abstract spatial energy entirely independent of the representational content that had generated it.

From this moment, Kline devoted himself to large-scale abstract paintings executed in black and white with a housepainter's brush: massive interlocking bars and beams of black, applied with tremendous physical energy onto white grounds, creating compositions of enormous spatial tension. The works appeared to many critics to reference the industrial landscape of Kline's Pennsylvania childhood — bridges, steel structures, railroad ties — but Kline resisted this reading, insisting on the formal and spatial reality of the marks themselves.

Kline was a central member of the first generation of Abstract Expressionists and an important presence in the Cedar Tavern community. He died young, at fifty-one, leaving a body of work that is among the most physically commanding in post-war American painting. His works are held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: Chief
famous_works.notes: A defining example of Kline's mature method in which interlocking black bars on white create a composition of industrial scale and gestural force.

famous_works.title: Mahoning
famous_works.notes: Named for a river in his native Pennsylvania, a large black-and-white canvas in which the structural metaphor — if it is one — is subsumed in pure spatial energy.

famous_works.title: Nijinsky
famous_works.notes: Named for the dancer and reportedly drawn from a childhood painting of Nijinsky, a work in which the gestural mark retains its original figural energy in abstracted form.

lesser_known_works.title: Orange and Black Wall
lesser_known_works.notes: One of Kline's rare colour paintings in which the structural logic of his black-and-white works is tested against the addition of warm chromatic elements.

lesser_known_works.title: New Year Wall: Night
lesser_known_works.notes: A large late canvas in which Kline's characteristic formal tension is maintained while achieving a particular spatial depth.

artists.bio:
Ivy Haldeman was born in 1986 and studied at the Pratt Institute and the New York Studio School. She works in oil on canvas, developing a figurative practice centred on an idiosyncratic cast of characters — hot dogs in tuxedos, androgynous human figures in muted interiors, objects and figures in ambiguous social situations — rendered in a style that combines Old Master technical sophistication with a quietly surrealistic sensibility.

Haldeman's paintings resist obvious categorisation. Her anthropomorphised hot dog figures, rendered with the tonal sophistication of academic oil painting, occupy an art-historically serious space while generating a humour that never entirely resolves into irony. The human figures in her paintings — elegant, stylised, often faceless or with simplified features — inhabit ambiguous interiors and social scenarios with a poise that is equally readable as confidence or vacancy. The formal intelligence of her compositions and her technical virtuosity in the manipulation of paint create an unsettling tension between sophistication and absurdity.

She has been exhibited with JTT Gallery in New York and has attracted critical attention as an original voice within contemporary painting's broad figurative current. Her work suggests that the comic and the serious need not be mutually exclusive in painting, and that absurdist content can be pursued with absolute technical conviction.

famous_works.title: Hot Dog (Seated)
famous_works.notes: A painting of an anthropomorphised hot dog figure seated in a formal pose, in which the technical seriousness of Old Master oil painting is applied to an absurdist subject with straight-faced conviction.

famous_works.title: Figure in Blue
famous_works.notes: A human figure in muted interior space rendered in Haldeman's characteristically elegant, simplified manner that hovers between psychological specificity and anonymous type.

famous_works.title: Dinner Party
famous_works.notes: A social scene in which human and hot dog figures share a table, combining the tradition of the conversation piece with Haldeman's absurdist dramatis personae.

lesser_known_works.title: Still Life with Hot Dog
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work that established the hot dog as a recurring character within Haldeman's practice by placing it within the genre of the traditional still-life.

lesser_known_works.title: Standing Figure
lesser_known_works.notes: A more purely figurative work demonstrating the technical foundation in Old Master oil painting that underlies Haldeman's more idiosyncratic imagery.

artists.bio:
Rembrandt Bugatti was born in Milan in 1884, the younger brother of Ettore Bugatti, the automobile manufacturer, and the son of Carlo Bugatti, the celebrated designer of furniture and jewellery. He displayed an exceptional talent for sculpture from childhood and by his early twenties had established himself as the most gifted animal sculptor of his generation, producing works of extraordinary vitality and immediacy that transform the modelling of animal subjects into something approaching artistic revelation.

Bugatti worked directly from live animals at the Antwerp and Paris zoos, sometimes spending hours or days observing a single animal before modelling it in clay. His bronzes capture the essential character of each species with an immediacy that suggests not careful academic study but a direct empathetic understanding: the drooping languor of a pelican, the alert concentration of a cheetah, the massive dignity of an elephant — each is rendered in a surface texture of remarkable vitality, the rough clay marks preserved in the final bronze creating an impression of living skin.

He died in Paris in 1916, possibly by suicide, at thirty-one — his brief career producing fewer than three hundred works that are now among the most sought-after animal bronzes in the auction market. The Czestochow collection at the Musée d'Orsay holds significant examples.

famous_works.title: Pelican
famous_works.notes: A bronze in which the bird's drooping, heavy-limbed languor is captured with a formal intelligence that makes the animal's specific essence immediately palpable.

famous_works.title: Elephant
famous_works.notes: A small bronze in which Bugatti's direct modelling preserves the massive dignity and wrinkled skin of the elephant in a remarkably immediate translation of observation into material.

famous_works.title: Cheetah Walking
famous_works.notes: A bronze of a striding cheetah in which the predator's combination of speed and elegance is fixed in a single arrested moment of characteristic grace.

lesser_known_works.title: Two Vultures
lesser_known_works.notes: A bronze of two birds in which the social dynamics of the species — the hierarchy of feeding — is captured in the composition's formal organisation.

lesser_known_works.title: Greyhound
lesser_known_works.notes: A bronze of a standing greyhound that demonstrates Bugatti's equal mastery of domestic animals alongside the exotic zoo subjects for which he is better known.

top_lot.title: Chien lévrier
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Donald Judd was born in Excelsior Springs, Missouri in 1928 and studied philosophy at Columbia University before establishing himself as an influential critic — his essay 'Specific Objects' (1965) remains among the defining theoretical texts of Minimalism — and as the movement's most rigorous practitioner. His work began with painted relief works and evolved rapidly toward the three-dimensional objects, or 'specific objects', that he insisted were neither painting nor sculpture but something irreducibly themselves.

Judd's signature forms are stacks of identical units — boxes in anodised aluminium, Plexiglas, and other industrial materials — mounted on walls at precise intervals, and floor pieces of metal forms in series. The works reject composition (each unit is identical), illusionistic space (the interior is as real as the exterior), and handmade craft (they are fabricated by industrial manufacturers to Judd's specifications). What remains is pure material presence: the colour of the anodised surface, the reflectivity of the metal, the depth of the interval between units — these are the work, without metaphor or reference.

In 1974 Judd began acquiring buildings in Marfa, Texas, which he converted into permanent installations of his own and others' work. The Chinati Foundation that emerged from this project is among the most significant sites for the permanent installation of Minimalist art. Judd is represented posthumously by David Zwirner, and his work is held by virtually every major art museum.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Stack)
famous_works.notes: A vertical series of identical galvanised iron box units mounted on a wall at equal intervals, among the purest realisations of Judd's principle that the work exists entirely in its material facts.

famous_works.title: 100 untitled works in mill aluminum
famous_works.notes: A permanent installation of one hundred identical aluminium boxes in two artillery sheds at Chinati in Marfa, Texas — the fullest realisation of Judd's vision of art in dedicated architectural space.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Progression)
famous_works.notes: A floor piece in which the intervals between box units follow a mathematical progression, introducing serial variation as a structural principle.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Purple Anodized Aluminum)
lesser_known_works.notes: A late stack work in which the richly coloured anodised surface demonstrates Judd's investigation of industrial colour as a material quality rather than applied decoration.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Bernstein)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early painted wood relief that represents the transitional moment between Judd's painting practice and his development of the three-dimensional specific object.

artists.bio:
María Berrío was born in Bogotá, Colombia in 1982 and moved to the United States to study at the School of Visual Arts in New York, where she has been based. She works primarily in large-scale mixed media works combining Japanese tissue paper (washi), watercolour, and gouache to create works of lyrical beauty that draw on the visual traditions of both Japanese art and Latin American colonial painting.

Berrío's works depict women and children in fantastical natural environments — dense forests populated by birds and flowers, dreamlike landscapes suspended between the earthly and the symbolic — that combine the intimacy of intimate narrative painting with a monumental scale. The technique of collaging layers of washi creates a surface texture that is simultaneously material and translucent, giving the works a quality of light that is unique and immediately recognisable. Her subject matter engages with female experience, migration, displacement, and the relationship between the body and the natural world.

She has been exhibited with Victoria Miro Gallery and has attracted widespread critical and collector attention. Berrío's work represents a distinctive synthesis of multiple cultural traditions within a practice of unusual formal sophistication.

famous_works.title: The Journey of the Fireflies
famous_works.notes: A large-scale work on paper in which female figures and natural elements are combined in a dreamlike nocturnal landscape of extraordinary formal beauty.

famous_works.title: When All the Trees Will Be Silent
famous_works.notes: A monumental work in which women and children inhabit a dense, fantastical forest environment rendered in Berrío's characteristic washi-and-watercolour technique.

famous_works.title: The Age of Growth
famous_works.notes: A large figurative work in which the relationship between human figures and the natural world is explored through the specific formal language of Berrío's layered paper technique.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Washi works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial experiments with the Japanese tissue paper technique that established the formal language of Berrío's mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: The Forest Women
lesser_known_works.notes: A work in which the relationship between women and an imagined natural environment is explored at an intimate scale before Berrío's subsequent move toward monumental dimensions.

artists.bio:
Berthe Morisot was born in Bourges in 1841 into a cultured bourgeois family that actively supported her artistic education. She studied under Camille Corot, who taught her the importance of plein air observation, and subsequently forged a close professional friendship with Édouard Manet, who became her brother-in-law when she married his brother Eugène in 1874. Morisot was a founding member of the Impressionist group and participated in seven of their eight exhibitions — a more consistent participation than any other member.

Her subjects were drawn largely from the domestic world available to women of her class: nurseries, gardens, balconies, women reading or nursing children, leisure scenes at the sea. Far from limiting her, this world provided material for one of the most formally innovative of Impressionist practices. Her brushwork is among the freest and most daring within the group — long, flickering strokes that describe form while asserting the painter's physical presence — and her handling of light on fabric, water, and skin is unsurpassed.

Morisot's reputation suffered the characteristic fate of women artists: admired during her lifetime, she was subsequently underestimated and overshadowed by her male counterparts. The feminist art history of the 1970s and 1980s reclaimed her as a major figure, and major retrospectives at the Musée d'Orsay and elsewhere have confirmed her standing as one of the essential voices of French Impressionism.

famous_works.title: The Cradle
famous_works.notes: A painting of Morisot's sister watching over her sleeping child, in which the transparency of the cradle's muslin veil becomes a demonstration of Impressionist light-handling.

famous_works.title: Summer's Day
famous_works.notes: Two women in a rowing boat on a lake, rendered in flickering brushstrokes that dissolve the boundary between figures, water, and reflected sky.

famous_works.title: The Harbour at Lorient
famous_works.notes: Morisot's first submission to the Salon, a plein air view of her sister at a harbour that Manet admired and from which he removed a detail for her.

lesser_known_works.title: Woman at Her Toilette
lesser_known_works.notes: An intimate depiction of a woman at her dressing table in which the mirror, powders, and fabrics are rendered in a cascade of broken colour of extraordinary delicacy.

lesser_known_works.title: Girl in a Boat with Geese
lesser_known_works.notes: A late-career plein air work in which child, water, and birds are united in a shimmering field of light that marks the full development of Morisot's mature manner.

top_lot.title: Plage de la Comtesse dans la brume
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Chana Orloff was born in Tsarekonstantinivka, Ukraine in 1888 and emigrated first to Palestine, then to Paris in 1910, where she studied at the École nationale supérieure des arts décoratifs and at the studio of Injalbert. She became one of the most prominent sculptors in Paris in the 1920s and 1930s, moving in the milieu of Montparnasse that included Modigliani, Soutine, and Jacob, who were among her subjects.

Orloff's sculpture combines a modernist simplification of form — influenced by Cubism and by the archaic sculpture she encountered in museums — with a humanistic warmth and psychological acuity that makes her portrait busts and figurative works immediately engaging. She worked primarily in bronze, stone, and wood, and developed a particularly distinctive treatment of the female body: her maternal figures, dancers, and female portraits assert volume, weight, and presence with an authority that resists the idealisation of much contemporary sculpture.

Her studio in Paris was ransacked during the German Occupation and much of her work destroyed; she fled to Geneva before returning after the Liberation to rebuild her practice. She is represented in major French and Israeli public collections, and a museum devoted to her work exists in Tel Aviv. Her reputation, long eclipsed by better-documented male contemporaries, has undergone sustained critical rehabilitation.

famous_works.title: Maternité
famous_works.notes: A bronze mother-and-child group in which formal simplification and tactile warmth combine to produce an image of maternal love grounded in physical reality.

famous_works.title: Portrait of Modigliani
famous_works.notes: A bronze portrait of her Montparnasse contemporary that captures the elongated melancholy of Modigliani's physical presence with affectionate precision.

famous_works.title: La Danseuse
famous_works.notes: A bronze figure of a dancer in which the simplified, generalised forms of modernist sculpture and the specific energy of movement are held in productive tension.

lesser_known_works.title: Femme à l'enfant
lesser_known_works.notes: An early maternal subject that establishes the formal vocabulary of simplified volume and psychological warmth that Orloff would develop throughout her career.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait d'Ilya Ehrenburg
lesser_known_works.notes: A bronze portrait of the Russian writer demonstrating Orloff's ability to render intellectual vitality within the reduced formal means of modernist portraiture.

top_lot.title: Maternité enceinte
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
Simon and Nikolai Haas were born in Los Angeles in 1984 and have developed a collaborative sculptural practice that combines craft traditions — ceramic, glass, bronze, textile — with biological and fantastical imagery to create objects of extraordinary visual and tactile richness. Working under the name The Haas Brothers, they produce furniture, sculptures, and decorative objects that occupy the territory between art and design without fully belonging to either.

Their work is characterised by its biological exuberance: creatures with multiple limbs, legs, and textures; furniture legs that grow fur; vessels covered in ceramic scales; objects that seem simultaneously ancient and entirely fantastical. The brothers draw on a wide range of cultural sources — Oceanic and African tribal objects, Science Fiction imagery, natural history collections, Surrealism — synthesising them into an aesthetic of deliberate excess that celebrates the strangeness of organic form.

They are represented by Hauser & Wirth and have exhibited internationally, attracting a devoted collector base among those drawn to the intersection of craft excellence and conceptual ambition. The Haas Brothers represent a significant strand in contemporary decorative arts that insists on the autonomy and intelligence of the object beyond mere function.

famous_works.title: Afreaks series
famous_works.notes: Bronze and mixed-media sculptures in which composite creatures combining African, alien, and organic imagery create objects of deliberately excessive and humorous fantasy.

famous_works.title: Fur Furniture
famous_works.notes: Table and chair forms with legs covered in real fur that transform functional objects into unsettling biological presences.

famous_works.title: Baba Yaga's House
famous_works.notes: An elaborate architectural-sculptural fantasy drawn from Slavic mythology, in which the brothers' combination of craft excellence and fantastical imagery achieves particular complexity.

lesser_known_works.title: Early ceramic works
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial ceramics from the brothers' early practice demonstrating the development of their biological and fantastical formal language.

lesser_known_works.title: Collaboration with textile artists
lesser_known_works.notes: Collaborative works in which the Haas Brothers' formal vocabulary is developed through partnership with specialist craft practitioners.

artists.bio:
Georg Baselitz was born Hans-Georg Kern in Deutschbaselitz, Saxony in 1938 and grew up in Soviet-occupied East Germany, fleeing to West Berlin in 1956 to study at the Hochschule für bildende Künste. His early paintings of the 1960s provoked scandal for their raw sexuality and anarchic imagery — two were confiscated by authorities — and established his commitment to provoking the normative expectations of both political systems within which he moved.

The decisive formal development of his career came in 1969, when he began painting his figures upside-down, a procedure he has maintained consistently for more than fifty years. The inversion refuses narrative: the viewer cannot construct a scene, cannot determine foreground from background in the conventional sense, and is forced to attend to the painting as a painted object — to its gestural surface, its colour, its marks — rather than to its depicted content. The figures are in the painting, but the painting is emphatically about itself.

Baselitz has been a central figure in German art for six decades, associated with the resurgence of figuration in the late 1970s and 1980s, and his work has attracted sustained critical debate about the relationship between German cultural tradition, trauma, and the possibilities of Neo-Expressionism. He is represented by Gagosian Gallery and White Cube, and major retrospectives have been mounted at institutions including the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao.

famous_works.title: The Big Night Down the Drain
famous_works.notes: A notorious early work confiscated by West Berlin authorities for obscenity, establishing Baselitz's commitment to transgressive imagery in his pre-inversion period.

famous_works.title: Motif: The Shepherd (Der Hirte)
famous_works.notes: One of the first inverted paintings, marking the decisive formal decision that has defined Baselitz's practice for more than five decades.

famous_works.title: Adler (Eagle)
famous_works.notes: A monumental inverted eagle in which the national symbol of Germany is subjected to Baselitz's characteristic formal disruption.

lesser_known_works.title: Remix series
lesser_known_works.notes: A late series in which Baselitz revisits and repaints his own earlier works, making the history of the practice itself the subject of the paintings.

lesser_known_works.title: Elke I
lesser_known_works.notes: An early inverted portrait of his wife Elke demonstrating the application of the inversion principle to the specific genre of portraiture.

artists.bio:
Doron Langberg was born in Israel in 1985 and moved to the United States, where he studied at Yale School of Art. His oil paintings depict his partner, friends, and family in domestic interiors and intimate physical proximity — sleeping, touching, resting together — with a quality of light and physical attention that recalls both Bonnard's colour-saturated interiors and the more recently precedent of Lucian Freud's sustained attention to flesh and fabric.

Langberg's approach to the male body, and particularly to gay intimacy, is distinguished by its insistence on treating its subjects with the same formalist seriousness that Western painting has historically reserved for heterosexual subjects. The bodies in his paintings are rendered in thick, tactile paint that registers light falling across skin and sheets with sensuous precision. These are not political paintings in any programmatic sense, but their existence as art-historically serious figurative paintings that centre queer domesticity carries political implication in the tradition it quietly but confidently claims.

He has been exhibited with Yossi Milo Gallery and has attracted significant critical attention as part of the current resurgence of interest in intimate figurative painting. His work participates in the broader conversation about expanding representation within the canonical tradition of Western figure painting.

famous_works.title: Ray and I
famous_works.notes: A large painting of the artist and his partner intertwined in bed, in which the thick, sensuous handling of paint registers the physicality of intimate contact with formal conviction.

famous_works.title: Hands
famous_works.notes: A close-up study of intertwined hands in which the intimate gesture becomes the occasion for an investigation of light, skin tone, and paint surface.

famous_works.title: Morning
famous_works.notes: A domestic interior scene in which two figures in various states of undress occupy a warmly lit space, extending the tradition of intimate interior painting into queer experience.

lesser_known_works.title: Sleeping Figures
lesser_known_works.notes: An early painting establishing the intimate register and attentive treatment of sleeping bodies that became central to Langberg's practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait of Ray
lesser_known_works.notes: A direct portrait demonstrating Langberg's ability to combine psychological specificity with the broader formal concerns of his figurative practice.

artists.bio:
Hans Hofmann was born in Weissenburg, Bavaria in 1880 and spent a formative decade in Paris (1904–14), where he absorbed the lessons of Cézanne, Matisse, and the Cubists at first hand, forming friendships with Picasso, Braque, and Delaunay. He opened his first school in Munich in 1915, and after the rise of National Socialism he emigrated to the United States in 1932, eventually settling in New York and Provincetown, Massachusetts, where his school became the primary transmission point for European modernist ideas to a generation of American artists.

Hofmann's teaching and his theory of 'push and pull' — the notion that spatial recession and advance in a painting should be generated through colour relationships rather than perspective or modelling — was profoundly influential on Abstract Expressionism, even though Hofmann himself did not produce his most significant paintings until relatively late in life. His mature works combine gestural passages with rectangular areas of pure flat colour — what he called 'slabs' — generating spatial tension through the opposition of these different pictorial modes.

His students included Lee Krasner, Helen Frankenthaler, and Nell Blaine. Though as influential as a teacher as any figure in twentieth-century American art, his achievement as a painter has sometimes been underestimated; major retrospectives have increasingly confirmed the independent significance of his paintings, held by the Museum of Modern Art and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: The Gate
famous_works.notes: A painting in which two large rectangles of yellow and orange float within a denser gestural ground, demonstrating the 'push and pull' principle through chromatic spatial tension.

famous_works.title: Memoria in Aeternum
famous_works.notes: A late masterwork in which flat rectangular colour planes and gestural marks achieve a spatial complexity and chromatic resonance that vindicates Hofmann's decades of theoretical investigation.

famous_works.title: Exuberance
famous_works.notes: A large canvas in which gestural marks and pure colour areas coexist in a state of productive tension that exemplifies Hofmann's unique synthesis of European colour theory and American gestural energy.

lesser_known_works.title: Fantasia
lesser_known_works.notes: A wartime painting that shows Hofmann developing the vocabulary of gestural abstraction that would culminate in his mature works.

lesser_known_works.title: The Vanquished
lesser_known_works.notes: A canvas in which gestural passages of dark paint create an atmospheric ground against which lighter colour forms achieve particular spatial prominence.

top_lot.title: Flux No. 4—A Cycle of Oil Paintings

top_ratio_lot.title: Flux No. 4—A Cycle of Oil Paintings

artists.bio:
Asuka Anastacia Ogawa is a Japanese-born painter whose work explores the lived experience of cultural hybridity, desire, and feminine subjectivity through a richly layered figurative language. Working across painting, drawing, and mixed media, she constructs intimate scenes in which figures — often solitary women in domestic or undefined spaces — navigate the ambiguities of identity, belonging, and emotional life.

Ogawa's pictorial language draws on multiple visual traditions: Japanese illustration and manga inform the fluency of her draughtsmanship, while the intimate scale and psychological nuance of her compositions recall the Northern European cabinet picture. This synthesis produces images that are at once immediately accessible and quietly complex, figures whose apparent stillness encodes a rich interior drama. Her colour use is restrained but precise, creating atmospheres of both warmth and melancholy.

She has exhibited with various galleries in New York and Tokyo and has attracted attention as part of a generation of painters working through the international art world while maintaining a deep engagement with their specific cultural inheritances. Her work contributes to an expanding conversation about the multiple modernities visible within contemporary figurative painting.

famous_works.title: Woman at Window
famous_works.notes: A quietly luminous interior scene in which a female figure's gaze through glass becomes a meditation on interiority and threshold.

famous_works.title: The Bath
famous_works.notes: An intimate depiction of a bathing figure in which the domestic ritual is invested with a psychological depth that recalls the cabinet pictures of Vermeer.

famous_works.title: Two Sisters
famous_works.notes: A double figure composition exploring the dynamics of closeness and separateness between female bodies in shared space.

lesser_known_works.title: Night Garden
lesser_known_works.notes: A small-scale painting combining landscape and figure in a nocturnal setting that demonstrates Ogawa's particular atmospheric sensitivity.

lesser_known_works.title: Self-Portrait with Flowers
lesser_known_works.notes: An early self-portrait in which botanical imagery functions as psychological extension of the artist's own presence.

artists.bio:
Michaela Yearwood-Dan was born in London in 1994 to Trinidadian and Barbadian parents and studied at the University of the Arts London's Central Saint Martins and the Slade School of Fine Art. She has developed a painting practice characterised by exuberant, floral richness and a commitment to the celebration of Black womanhood, queer identity, and collective joy as primary pictorial subjects.

Yearwood-Dan's canvases are large, densely planted fields of floral imagery — flowers painted with a gestural, almost ecstatic abandon — within which figures (typically Black women) are partially immersed, their identities merging with the botanical environment. The combination of portrait and botanical painting traditions within large, expressive canvases creates works that feel simultaneously intimate and monumental. Her use of colour is confident and celebratory: warm pinks, oranges, and greens create an atmosphere of tropical abundance that has its own political valence as a counter to the austerity of institutional spaces.

She has been exhibited with Tiwani Contemporary in London and has attracted significant critical and commercial attention since her emergence. Yearwood-Dan represents a generation of British Black artists for whom joy, celebration, and the affirmation of marginalised identities are serious painterly subjects.

famous_works.title: Reverie
famous_works.notes: A large canvas in which a Black female figure is immersed in a dense field of painted flowers, celebrating the subject's presence within an environment of natural abundance.

famous_works.title: For the Love of...
famous_works.notes: A monumental floral painting in which the exuberance of the botanical imagery creates an atmosphere of joyful celebration that is simultaneously intimate and public.

famous_works.title: Garden of Eden
famous_works.notes: An early major work in which the lush botanical environment of Yearwood-Dan's mature style is first fully developed around the presence of a Black female figure.

lesser_known_works.title: Study in Pink
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating the development of Yearwood-Dan's floral language before the full chromatic richness of her mature canvases was established.

lesser_known_works.title: Love Letter
lesser_known_works.notes: A smaller-scale work in which the intimate register of Yearwood-Dan's figuration is developed in a more concentrated pictorial space.

artists.bio:
Katherine Bradford was born in New York City in 1942 and came to painting relatively late, after raising a family and pursuing other work. She studied at the Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture and the Portland School of Art, and has developed a practice of abstract figurative painting centred on swimmers, boats, and bodies in or near water that has attracted growing critical attention over recent decades.

Bradford's paintings are executed in a direct, loosely gestural manner in which simplified figure forms — usually depicted from above or at water level — are submerged in or emerge from fields of atmospheric colour. Her palette is warm and resonant: ochre, rose, coral, and deep blue creating colour fields within which human figures are both present and dissolving. The works explore the relationship between the individual body and a larger encompassing element — water, sky, light — with a formal generosity that recalls both the Colour Field tradition and a more specifically figurative tradition of the figure in landscape.

Bradford has been exhibited at Canada Gallery in New York and her work has attracted renewed attention as part of the current reassessment of second-generation abstract painting that has been developing outside mainstream institutional attention. Her late recognition has become part of her story, evidence of how ageism and the art world's preference for youth have marginalised significant practices.

famous_works.title: Super Heroes
famous_works.notes: A painting of floating or flying figures — simplified, almost cartoonish — in a warm atmospheric field that transforms the superhero fantasy into an image of collective aspiration and bodily freedom.

famous_works.title: Night Swimmers
famous_works.notes: A painting of figures swimming in darkness in which the dissolution of individual form into water and light becomes the subject of the work.

famous_works.title: Pink Boat
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which a simplified boat form in a luminous pink ground creates an image of extraordinary formal simplicity and atmospheric richness.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Boat paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: The first extended series of boat and water subjects that established the thematic territory of Bradford's mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Floating Figure
lesser_known_works.notes: A work in which a single figure in an atmospheric colour field exemplifies Bradford's investigation of the relationship between the body and its encompassing environment.

artists.bio:
Ashley Bickerton was born in Barbados in 1959 and grew up in a peripatetic household — Hawaii, England, New York — before studying at the California Institute of the Arts. He arrived in New York's art world in the mid-1980s as one of the central figures of the Neo-Geo movement alongside Jeff Koons, Peter Halley, and Meyer Vaisman, associated with Sonnabend Gallery. His early work engaged directly with the commodity logic of the art market: objects covered in logos, brand names, and institutional identifiers of the art world, turning the gallery and the artist's career into explicit subjects.

In 1993, Bickerton relocated to Bali, where he has remained, and this move fundamentally transformed his work. The corporate brand logos gave way to fantastic hybrid creatures — part human, part animal, part tribal deity — rendered in garish colour against exotic backdrops that simultaneously parody and celebrate the seductions of the tropics. These later works oscillate between genuine visual pleasure and a satirical awareness of the Western fantasy of the primitive paradise, implicating the viewer's desire alongside the artist's own.

Bickerton has been exhibited at Lehmann Maupin Gallery and his work has been the subject of a critical reassessment that recognises the coherence of a practice that appears, on the surface, to have made a radical break but in fact maintains a consistent interest in the seductions and deceits of image culture.

famous_works.title: Tormented Self-Portrait (Susie at Arles)
famous_works.notes: A wall-mounted object covered in the logos of corporate sponsors and art world institutions, using the artist's own practice as a readymade commodity.

famous_works.title: Sea Chanty
famous_works.notes: A large Bali-period painting featuring a fantastic creature in a tropical seascape, blending Polynesian visual culture with lurid Western fantasy.

famous_works.title: Logo Motel
famous_works.notes: An early Neo-Geo assemblage object that deploys corporate branding and consumer culture as pictorial content, confronting the commodity status of art.

lesser_known_works.title: Slant Step Descendant
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the early box constructions that established Bickerton's approach of treating the artwork as a branded, portable object.

lesser_known_works.title: Beautiful Island
lesser_known_works.notes: A Balinese-period canvas in which the artist's characteristic fantastic figures inhabit an unabashedly seductive tropical landscape.

artists.bio:
Avery Singer was born in New York in 1987 and studied at the Cooper Union and the Städelschule in Frankfurt. Her paintings are produced through a hybrid process: she first constructs scenes in 3D modelling software, rendering simplified architectural and figural forms in a digital environment, then airbrushes the resulting compositions in acrylic on canvas in an accentuated chiaroscuro of greys and blacks, with occasional passages of colour that function as chromatic events within an otherwise monochrome field.

The subjects of her paintings are typically social scenes — artists' studios, gallery openings, domestic interiors, architectural spaces — in which figures interact in ways that are simultaneously naturalistic and uncanny. The process of digital modelling produces figures that look vaguely like wooden mannequins or simplified video game characters, stripped of individual identity but retaining posture and social gesture. The result is a painting that reads as both documentation and critique of the social world it depicts, particularly the art world itself.

Singer has been exhibited by Hauser & Wirth and Kraupa-Tuskany Zeidler Gallery and has received significant institutional recognition including exhibitions at the Stedelijk Museum in Amsterdam and the Kunsthalle Zürich. Her practice represents a rigorous investigation of painting's relationship to digital image production and social performance.

famous_works.title: Chasm
famous_works.notes: A monumental grey-and-black airbrush painting depicting figures in a cavernous architectural space, in which social interaction is rendered as theatrical and vaguely menacing.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Studio)
famous_works.notes: A painting of an artist's studio in which the figures' digital-model provenance is visible as an epistemological condition of how we now inhabit and picture creative space.

famous_works.title: Rogue Wave
famous_works.notes: A large-scale work in which a wave of simplified figures seems to surge through a shared social space, applying Singer's technique to the dynamics of collective movement.

lesser_known_works.title: She Killed It
lesser_known_works.notes: An early airbrush work in which Singer's method of digitally constructing then manually rendering is visible in its most exploratory form.

lesser_known_works.title: The Gathering
lesser_known_works.notes: A mid-size canvas depicting a social gathering in Singer's characteristic grey-scale palette, examining the performance of intellectual sociality.

artists.bio:
Robert Ryman was born in Nashville, Tennessee in 1930 and moved to New York in 1952 intending to become a jazz musician, working as a guard at the Museum of Modern Art where his encounter with the collection transformed him into a painter. Largely self-taught, he spent years making experimental works in his MoMA guard's booth before developing the practice that defined his career: white paintings on various supports that are investigations of paint's physical qualities, the nature of supports, and the experience of looking itself.

Ryman's paintings are all white — or more precisely, they explore the infinite variety within whiteness. Over a fifty-year career he used dozens of different white pigments on dozens of different supports (canvas, steel, fibreglass, paper, linen) applied with different tools (brush, palette knife, squeegee), generating a body of work of extraordinary formal intelligence that demonstrates how much is contained in what is conventionally considered the absence of painting. The works' relationship to their wall supports — the brackets and fasteners are always visible — insists on the painting as an object in space rather than a window.

Ryman is represented posthumously by Pace Gallery and his work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Dia Art Foundation, and virtually every major collection interested in Minimalism. He is one of the most consequential painters of the second half of the twentieth century.

famous_works.title: Standard
famous_works.notes: A large painting on steel in which Ryman's examination of white paint on a specific support creates a work of extreme formal precision and sensory richness.

famous_works.title: Classico IV
famous_works.notes: A painting on coated paper in which the support's particular absorbency creates a surface quality entirely different from canvas or metal, demonstrating the formal range within Ryman's apparently restricted vocabulary.

famous_works.title: Pace
famous_works.notes: A work in which the painted surface, the support, and the fastening elements are coordinated into a composition of integrated spatial intelligence.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from Ryman's period of experimental self-teaching at MoMA that demonstrate the development of his engagement with paint and surface before the fully mature white paintings.

lesser_known_works.title: Winsor series
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works on paper that demonstrate the range of Ryman's investigation across different support types and paint application methods.

artists.bio:
Nan Goldin was born in Washington D.C. in 1953 and grew up in suburban Massachusetts following the suicide of her sister Barbara, an event that became a defining trauma in both her personal and artistic history. She studied photography at the School of the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston and began photographing her circle of friends — drag queens, gay men, women in various states of intimacy and crisis — in the Cambridge and Boston underground in the mid-1970s before moving to New York.

Goldin's work is rooted in the autobiographical and the intimate: she photographs the people she loves and lives among, documenting domestic scenes, sexual intimacy, violence, addiction, AIDS deaths, and the texture of a specific subcultural life in New York of the 1970s and 1980s with a directness that is simultaneously aesthetically sophisticated and emotionally raw. Her major work, The Ballad of Sexual Dependency, first presented as a slideshow in 1986 and published as a book in the same year, is among the most important photographic works of the twentieth century: a record of love, loss, and community rendered in a visual language that broke definitively with photographic distance.

In more recent years Goldin has become a prominent activist against the Sackler family's opioid marketing practices, organising protest actions at major museums. She is represented by Matthew Marks Gallery and her work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: The Ballad of Sexual Dependency
famous_works.notes: A slideshow-and-book that documented Goldin's immediate circle of friends across a decade, presenting intimacy, violence, sex, and death within a visual language of extraordinary directness and beauty.

famous_works.title: Nan One Month After Being Battered
famous_works.notes: A self-portrait in the aftermath of domestic violence whose confrontational directness made it one of the most important photographs of the 1980s.

famous_works.title: Cookie at Tin Pan Alley
famous_works.notes: A portrait of her close friend Cookie Mueller that exemplifies Goldin's ability to render intense psychological presence within the codes of documentary intimacy.

lesser_known_works.title: I'll Be Your Mirror
lesser_known_works.notes: A survey exhibition and book that extended the Ballad's project across the artist's full photographic practice to that point.

lesser_known_works.title: The Other Side
lesser_known_works.notes: A book of photographs of drag queens and transgender women that demonstrates the breadth of Goldin's community beyond the more frequently reproduced Ballad images.

top_lot.title: Nan and Brian in Bed, N.Y.C., 1983

top_ratio_lot.title: Nan and Brian in Bed, N.Y.C., 1983

artists.bio:
Emmanuel Radnitzky, known as Man Ray, was born in Philadelphia in 1890 and grew up in Brooklyn, where his early exposure to fine arts and the avant-garde milieu of the New York Armory Show of 1913 shaped his ambitions. He was a founding member of the New York Dada group and moved to Paris in 1921, where he became a central figure in the Surrealist movement and an intimate of Duchamp, Picasso, Gertrude Stein, and the most significant artistic personalities of inter-war Paris.

Man Ray's practice was deliberately nomadic across media: painting, photography, assemblage, film, and drawing were all pursued with equal seriousness. His photograms — which he called Rayographs — were made by placing objects directly on photosensitive paper and exposing them to light, producing abstract images of poetic beauty from everyday objects. His airbrush paintings and his objects — including the famous Gift, a flatiron with nails attached to its base — translated Dada's confrontational wit into formally inventive objects.

As a photographer, Man Ray worked extensively in portraiture and fashion, producing some of the most significant portrait photographs of the twentieth century while simultaneously developing his experimental photographic practice. He returned to the United States during World War II before settling permanently in Paris, where he died in 1976. His work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Centre Pompidou, and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: Rayographs
famous_works.notes: Camera-less photographs made by placing objects on photosensitive paper — the most sustained and original body of experimental photography in the Dada and Surrealist tradition.

famous_works.title: Le Violon d'Ingres
famous_works.notes: A photograph of the model Kiki de Montparnasse in which f-holes are painted onto her back, transforming the female body into a musical instrument with a wit that is simultaneously tender and critical.

famous_works.title: Gift
famous_works.notes: A readymade consisting of a flat iron with nails glued to its base, which converts a domestic tool into an object of surrealist menace.

lesser_known_works.title: Rope Dancer Accompanies Herself with Her Shadows
lesser_known_works.notes: An early painting from Man Ray's pre-Paris period demonstrating the formal intelligence and spatial experimentation that would underpin his Dada and Surrealist work.

lesser_known_works.title: Observatory Time (The Lovers)
lesser_known_works.notes: A surrealist painting in which Lee Miller's lips float above a Parisian landscape as vast red lips in the sky.

artists.bio:
Sam Gilliam was born in Tupelo, Mississippi in 1933 and raised in Louisville, Kentucky. He studied at the University of Louisville and arrived in Washington D.C. in 1962, where he became associated with the Washington Color School — a group of painters including Morris Louis, Kenneth Noland, and Gene Davis who were developing an approach to colour abstraction through the staining of raw canvas. Gilliam absorbed these concerns while radically transforming them.

In 1965, Gilliam removed the canvas from its stretcher bars and began draping, folding, and hanging it from walls and ceilings in three-dimensional configurations — a decisive innovation that transformed painting from a flat object to a sculptural environment. His draped paintings are executed in the Colour Field method of saturating raw or sized canvas with thinned acrylic paint, but the configuration of the resulting painting is architectural rather than pictorial. These works, shown at the Corcoran Gallery and subsequently at major institutions worldwide, were immediately recognised as a fundamental contribution to the possibilities of painting.

Gilliam continued to develop his practice over a sixty-year career, returning at various points to both stretcher-based and draped configurations. He died in 2022 having recently received a major retrospective at the Hirshhorn Museum confirming his importance. His work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: Carousel Change
famous_works.notes: A major draped painting installation in which saturated canvas is suspended from ceiling in sweeping folds that transform the relationship between painting, space, and the viewer's body.

famous_works.title: Swing series
famous_works.notes: A group of draped paintings that extend Gilliam's investigation of the unstretched canvas as a sculptural element in architectural space.

famous_works.title: Watercolor No. 5
famous_works.notes: A stretched canvas painting from the period immediately before Gilliam's decisive move to the draped format, demonstrating the Colour Field foundations from which that innovation emerged.

lesser_known_works.title: Slat series
lesser_known_works.notes: Works on slat-like supports that represent one of Gilliam's investigations of alternative support formats beyond both stretchers and draped canvas.

lesser_known_works.title: Late constructions
lesser_known_works.notes: Three-dimensional works in which canvas, metal, and other materials are combined in constructions that continue Gilliam's investigation of painting as spatial object.

artists.bio:
Rebecca Ness was born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania in 1989 and studied at the Rhode Island School of Design. She works in oil on canvas, developing a figurative practice that combines influences from Italian Renaissance fresco painting, Northern European tapestry traditions, and the dense, decorative flatness of Gustav Klimt to create images of considerable formal intelligence and immediate visual pleasure.

Ness's paintings are characterised by their elaborate, patterned backgrounds — abstract formations of colour and line that flatten pictorial space while simultaneously creating environments of material richness — against which figurative subjects (typically female) are set in poses that suggest both classical precedent and contemporary ease. The relationship between the figure and the patterned ground is managed with great compositional sophistication: the two elements hold each other in productive tension rather than resolving into either pure abstraction or academic figuration.

She has been exhibited at Situations Gallery in New York and has attracted critical attention as a painter whose historical references are absorbed into a practice that is entirely contemporary in sensibility. Ness represents a thoughtful engagement with the decorative tradition within painting's history.

famous_works.title: Woman with Pattern
famous_works.notes: A large canvas in which a female figure is presented against an elaborate patterned ground that draws on tapestry and mosaic traditions while maintaining contemporary pictorial directness.

famous_works.title: Seated Figure
famous_works.notes: A composition in which Renaissance fresco-influenced figure organisation is combined with Ness's characteristic decorative ground.

famous_works.title: Figure in Garden
famous_works.notes: A painting in which the boundary between figure and natural environment is mediated through pattern and decoration in a manner that creates formal ambiguity of considerable beauty.

lesser_known_works.title: Early RISD works
lesser_known_works.notes: Student works demonstrating the development of Ness's historical and decorative references before their synthesis in her mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Two Figures
lesser_known_works.notes: A composition exploring the relationship between two female figures within a patterned environment.

artists.bio:
László Moholy-Nagy was born in Bácsborsód, Hungary in 1895 and arrived in Berlin in 1920, where his encounter with the Russian Constructivist movement and with Dada transformed his practice from painting toward a multimedia investigation of light, movement, and modern materials. He joined the Bauhaus faculty in 1923 at the invitation of Walter Gropius, taking over the preliminary course from Johannes Itten and fundamentally reorienting the school's approach toward industrial and technological concerns.

At the Bauhaus, Moholy-Nagy developed his Constructivist experiments with reflective materials, light, and kinetic elements — most notably the Light-Space Modulator, a kinetic sculpture of chromium, nickel, and glass that generated complex light and shadow effects when set in motion. His photograms and manipulated photographs extended his investigation of light into the photographic medium, producing images of extraordinary formal beauty through camera-less processes. He also designed typography, exhibitions, and industrial objects, embodying the Bauhaus ideal of the integration of art and life.

Emigrating from Nazi Germany via London, he arrived in Chicago in 1937 to found the New Bauhaus, which became the Institute of Design. He died of leukemia in 1946, leaving an enormously influential legacy as both artist and educator.
