His work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Art Institute of Chicago, and major collections internationally.

famous_works.title: Light-Space Modulator
famous_works.notes: A kinetic sculpture of chromium, nickel, and glass that generates complex patterns of light and shadow when set in motion, embodying Moholy-Nagy's investigation of time, space, and light as artistic materials.

famous_works.title: Photogram series
famous_works.notes: Camera-less photographs made by placing objects on photosensitive paper exposed to light, creating abstract images of great formal elegance from the physics of photographic exposure.

famous_works.title: Space Modulator (Aluminium)
famous_works.notes: A late painting on aluminium in which reflective surface and translucent overlapping forms create a spatial environment characteristic of Moholy-Nagy's light investigations.

lesser_known_works.title: Composition A XXI
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting in which geometric forms and a limited palette create a Constructivist composition of formal economy and spatial intelligence.

lesser_known_works.title: Jealousy
lesser_known_works.notes: A typographic work that demonstrates Moholy-Nagy's integration of visual design and pictorial art within the Bauhaus context.

artists.bio:
Sarah Crowner was born in San Francisco in 1974 and studied at the College of the Atlantic in Maine and the Hunter College of the City University of New York. She has developed a distinctive practice centred on sewn and painted canvas works that engage the histories of geometric abstraction, textile design, and organic form in a synthesis of considerable formal originality.

Crowner's paintings are made by cutting painted canvas into curved and geometric forms and sewing them together at the seams — a process that is simultaneously a painting method, a textile technique, and an act of formal composition. The sewn seams are left visible, insisting on the material process of making as an integral part of the finished work. Her forms — large arabesques, spirals, and organic curves — combine the formal vocabulary of 1930s and 1940s biomorphic abstraction (Arp, Matisse's cut-outs) with an insistence on the physical reality of the canvas as material.

She has been exhibited at Victoria Miro Gallery and Casey Kaplan Gallery and has received significant institutional attention including commissions for site-specific works. Crowner's practice represents a sophisticated engagement with painting's material history that is simultaneously historical in its references and formally inventive in its methods.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Spiral)
famous_works.notes: A large sewn canvas work in which curved forms in contrasting colours are combined through visible stitching to create a composition of both graphic immediacy and material directness.

famous_works.title: Wave
famous_works.notes: A work in which the sewn canvas technique is applied to organic wave-like forms that recall both Matisse's cut-outs and the physical dynamics of water and fabric.

famous_works.title: Terracotta Floor (installation)
famous_works.notes: A site-specific tile floor installation in which Crowner's formal vocabulary is translated from sewn canvas into ceramic tile.

lesser_known_works.title: Early experiments
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial works in which Crowner developed the sewn canvas methodology before its full formal maturation.

lesser_known_works.title: Flag works
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of sewn canvas works engaging the formal language of national flags as a vehicle for investigating geometric abstraction.

artists.bio:
Alex Katz was born in Brooklyn, New York in 1927 and studied at Cooper Union and the Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture in Maine, where he would return repeatedly throughout his career. Coming to maturity in the 1950s, he resisted the dominant pull of Abstract Expressionism, insisting on the figure at a moment when this was an unfashionable position — yet his figuration absorbed the lessons of the New York School in its bold flatness, chromatic intensity, and monumental scale.

Katz developed a signature idiom: large-scale paintings in which flat fields of colour define forms with a precise, graphic economy that acknowledges advertising, fashion photography, and Japanese woodblock prints without being reducible to any of them. His subjects — his wife Ada, friends from the New York arts world, landscape and flora from Maine — are treated with a cool, affectionate attention that refuses psychological probing in favour of surfaces rendered luminously present. The scale of his canvases forces an intimacy: cropped close, the figures exceed human size and the viewer inhabits their field of light.

Katz has also worked extensively in cut-out painted steel sculptures and on a prolific body of prints. Represented by Gavin Brown's Enterprise and Timothy Taylor Gallery, he has achieved international institutional recognition relatively late, with retrospectives at major museums including the Guggenheim Bilbao and the Albertina in Vienna, confirming the singular achievement of a practice spanning more than seven decades.

famous_works.title: Ada Ada
famous_works.notes: One of the early portraits of Katz's wife Ada that established the cropped, flat figuration that became his signature approach.

famous_works.title: Black Dress 2
famous_works.notes: A monumental close-up portrait in which a woman in a black dress is rendered with the graphic precision and chromatic intensity of Katz's mature manner.

famous_works.title: Coca-Cola Girls
famous_works.notes: A multi-panel work referencing commercial imagery that explicitly acknowledges the dialogue between Katz's pictorial language and American visual culture.

lesser_known_works.title: Good Afternoon II
lesser_known_works.notes: A landscape work from Katz's Maine series demonstrating his ability to apply his flat, graphic aesthetic to natural subjects with equal authority.

lesser_known_works.title: The Black Jacket
lesser_known_works.notes: A double portrait combining Katz's interest in fashion-inflected figuration with the flatness and scale that distinguish his approach from conventional portraiture.

artists.bio:
Hank Willis Thomas was born in Plainfield, New Jersey in 1976 and studied at New York University's Tisch School of the Arts and at the California College of the Arts. He works across photography, sculpture, video, and installation, developing a practice centred on the investigation of race, advertising, and the construction of Black identity in American visual culture.

Thomas's work frequently appropriates advertising imagery — particularly Nike and other sports company campaigns — removing brand logos to expose the racial economies of exploitation and aspiration that underlie them. His B®anded series peels back the surface of sports advertising to reveal the historical continuities between the branding of enslaved people and the commercial 'branding' of Black athletes. This methodology — using the tools of appropriation art developed by the Pictures Generation to expose specifically racial systems of image production — has made Thomas one of the more trenchant analysts of American visual culture.

More recent works incorporate reflective materials, quilts, and participatory elements that expand his practice beyond the critique of advertising into broader investigations of collective memory, sports, protest, and the visual legacy of the civil rights movement. He is represented by Jack Shainman Gallery and has exhibited at major institutions including the International Center of Photography and the Smithsonian National Portrait Gallery.

famous_works.title: B®anded series
famous_works.notes: Appropriated advertising images from which brand logos are removed, exposing the racial economies of Black athletic labour within the global sports industry.

famous_works.title: Unbranded: A Century of White Women 1915–2015
famous_works.notes: A companion series examining the construction of white femininity through the history of advertising, extending Thomas's method of logo-removal to different racial and gender dynamics.

famous_works.title: Strange Fruit
famous_works.notes: A reflective silver basketball bearing the image of a lynching photograph, condensing the histories of racial violence and Black athletic spectacle into a single object.

lesser_known_works.title: Priceless
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work from the B®anded series in which the format of a MasterCard advertisement is used to calculate the monetary value placed on Black lives.

lesser_known_works.title: Retroreflective series
lesser_known_works.notes: Works using retroreflective fabric to create images that transform depending on the presence or absence of direct light, making visibility itself a formal and political variable.

top_lot.title: Our Enemies Are Like Fish Caught in Dragnet, That is, Easily

top_ratio_lot.title: Our Enemies Are Like Fish Caught in Dragnet, That is, Easily

artists.bio:
Ross Bleckner was born in New York City in 1949 and studied at New York University and the California Institute of the Arts. He came to prominence in the late 1970s and early 1980s as part of a group of New York painters who, counter to the prevailing winds of Minimalism and Conceptual Art, insisted on the possibility of serious abstract painting — and who faced both dismissal and, by the mid-1980s, enormous commercial success.

Bleckner's early stripe paintings of the late 1970s were among the most critically discussed works of the period, their engagement with Op Art's perceptual experiments and the formal legacy of 1960s abstraction making them simultaneously historical and contemporary. His subsequent work — large paintings of floating lights and cells, of architectural arches and domes filled with luminous orbs, of trophy cups inscribed with dates — engaged with the AIDS crisis through imagery of cells, damaged structures, and memorial conventions while maintaining a visual language of dark, light-suffused beauty.

He has been exhibited at Mary Boone Gallery and Pace Gallery and is collected by major museums including the Museum of Modern Art and the Guggenheim. Bleckner's practice stands as an important contribution to the question of how abstract painting can carry historical and political weight.

famous_works.title: Stripe paintings
famous_works.notes: A group of large paintings in which vertical stripes of paint create Op Art-like perceptual effects that engage the history of post-war abstraction with both seriousness and irony.

famous_works.title: Architecture of the Sky series
famous_works.notes: Paintings of domed interiors filled with floating light orbs that developed Bleckner's visual language for addressing AIDS mortality through architectural and biological imagery.

famous_works.title: Count No Count
famous_works.notes: A memorial painting incorporating trophy cup forms with engraved dates, combining personal grief with a formal language of commemorative melancholy.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Pattern paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial abstract works from Bleckner's pre-stripe period that demonstrate his engagement with the patterns and optical effects that would develop into his mature vocabulary.

lesser_known_works.title: After Years of Hearing Others' Fears
lesser_known_works.notes: A large painting from the AIDS period that demonstrates the full development of Bleckner's luminous, memorial pictorial language.

artists.bio:
Sigmar Polke was born in Oels, Silesia (now Oleśnica, Poland) in 1941. His family fled West Germany in 1953, eventually settling in Düsseldorf, where he studied at the Kunstakademie under Karl Otto Götz, alongside Gerhard Richter. In 1963 Polke and Richter announced 'Capitalist Realism', a German response to American Pop that used commercial and mass-media imagery not with Pop's cool detachment but with a corrosive, often scatological humour.

Polke's practice is among the most formally inventive and intellectually restless in post-war art. He worked across decades in a staggering range of materials and methodologies — Raster dot paintings (enlarging commercial printing screens to canvas), paintings on patterned fabric, works using toxic and transformative chemicals, photographic darkroom experimentations, large-scale works on transparent polyester fabric — rarely settling into any single approach long enough for it to become a style. His use of Raster dots anticipated and complicated digital pixelation; his chemical works produced surfaces that continued to change after the work was 'finished'.

Polke won the Golden Lion at the Venice Biennale in 1986, with an installation that filled the German Pavilion with iridescent chemical surfaces. He is represented posthumously by Michael Werner Gallery and his work is held by the Museum of Modern Art, the Museum Ludwig in Cologne, and major collections worldwide.

famous_works.title: Higher Powers Command: Paint the Upper Right Corner Black!
famous_works.notes: A painting in which Polke executes an apparently nonsensical instruction, simultaneously testing authority structures and the conventions of abstract painting.

famous_works.title: Watchtower
famous_works.notes: A series of paintings of watchtowers — associated with both the Nazi concentration camp system and the East German border — in which multiple layered materials create images of political resonance and formal complexity.

famous_works.title: Alice in Wonderland
famous_works.notes: A work on patterned fabric in which the interference between the applied image and the ground's pattern creates a pictorial complexity that is simultaneously humorous and formally sophisticated.

lesser_known_works.title: Bunnies
lesser_known_works.notes: An early Raster dot painting in which Playboy bunnies are reproduced at enlarged dot-screen scale, connecting commercial printing technology to questions of desire and reproduction.

lesser_known_works.title: Transformers Paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Works using chemicals that alter and transform the painted surface over time, making change itself a constitutive element of the work.

artists.bio:
Anselm Kiefer was born in Donaueschingen, Germany in 1945 — the year of Germany's military defeat — and studied under Joseph Beuys at the Kunstakademie Düsseldorf, an encounter that confirmed his sense that German art had an obligation to confront the traumatic history from which it emerged. His early work, including the notorious photographs of himself performing the Nazi salute in various European locations, produced scandal and incomprehension; in retrospect it is understood as an attempt to exorcise rather than celebrate what the gesture represented.

Kiefer's paintings are among the most physically imposing and intellectually demanding of the post-war era. Executed on enormous canvases and wooden panels, they incorporate lead, straw, ash, wire, sand, and dried plants into surfaces of extraordinary material density. His subjects are drawn from German mythology, Kabbalah, Norse cosmology, and the history of Nazi ideology — not to aestheticise these things but to subject them to a process of interrogation through painting's own alchemical transformations. Vast landscapes scar with the marks of agricultural labour and military destruction; leaded libraries burn; the names of poets, philosophers, and historical catastrophes are inscribed across blackened surfaces.

Kiefer has worked in Germany, France, and his studio complex at Barjac in the Cévennes, and later in Croissy-sur-Seine. Major retrospectives have been mounted at the Royal Academy of Arts in London, the Centre Pompidou in Paris, and the Uffizi in Florence. His work stands as the most sustained artistic reckoning with German history and mythological consciousness in the second half of the twentieth century.

famous_works.title: To the Unknown Painter
famous_works.notes: A monumental canvas in which a heroic architectural interior is saturated in ash and straw, interrogating the relationship between artistic genius and ideological complicity.

famous_works.title: Margarethe / Shulamith
famous_works.notes: Two monumental paintings responding to Paul Celan's 'Death Fugue', contrasting golden straw (Margarethe) with charred lead and ash (Shulamith) to embody the poem's two women.

famous_works.title: The Orders of the Night (Die Ordnung der Engel)
famous_works.notes: A lead-encrusted canvas incorporating books and astronomical imagery, exploring the Kabbalistic concept of angelic hierarchies as a metaphor for cosmic order and human ambition.

lesser_known_works.title: Parsifal series
lesser_known_works.notes: Early works on burlap engaging with Wagnerian mythology and the attic interiors of German cultural heritage as spaces of nationalist mythology.

lesser_known_works.title: Lot's Wife
lesser_known_works.notes: A lead-and-salt installation in which the biblical transformation of a woman into a pillar of salt becomes a meditation on the petrification of memory.

artists.bio:
Born Aboudia Abdoulaye Diarrassouba in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire, in 1983, Aboudia emerged from the city's noisy street culture and its tradition of popular painting to forge one of contemporary Africa's most viscerally immediate pictorial languages. He trained informally, absorbing graffiti aesthetics, the raw energy of Abidjan's underground music scene, and the legacy of the self-taught Saint-Soleil painters of Haiti, before relocating between Abidjan and New York.

His canvases erupt with densely layered figures — wide-eyed children, fractured crowds, spectral bodies — rendered in agitated gestural strokes over grounds collaged with newspaper, fabric, and urban detritus. The work gained international attention in the wake of Côte d'Ivoire's 2010–11 post-election crisis, which Aboudia witnessed directly; the chaos and resilience of civilian life under political violence became central subject matter, filtered through an aesthetic that is simultaneously playful and anguished.

Aboudia has exhibited at the Venice Biennale and with major galleries including Nathalie Obadia in Paris and White Cube in London. His paintings occupy a charged intersection between reportage and expressionist catharsis, insisting that the street-level experience of ordinary Abidjanais carries both aesthetic dignity and historical weight.

famous_works.title: After Election Series
famous_works.notes: A sequence of large paintings produced in the immediate aftermath of Côte d'Ivoire's post-election violence, documenting civilian displacement with expressionist urgency.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Children)
famous_works.notes: Dense multi-figure composition in which wide-eyed children crowd the picture plane, combining collaged newsprint with gestural oil to evoke both innocence and precarity.

famous_works.title: Nouchi Series
famous_works.notes: Named for Abidjan street slang, these paintings translate urban youth culture — its music, fashion, and bravado — into explosive chromatic tableaux.

lesser_known_works.title: La Rue
lesser_known_works.notes: An early street-influenced work on paper that prefigures the dense figural layering of his mature canvases.

lesser_known_works.title: Ivory Coast Market
lesser_known_works.notes: A quieter, more intimate scene depicting market vendors in Abidjan, showing the artist's range beyond crisis imagery.

artists.bio:
Philip-Lorca diCorcia was born in Hartford, Connecticut in 1951 and studied at the School of the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston and Yale University School of Art. He has developed one of the most sophisticated photographic practices in contemporary art, working at the boundary between documentary and staged photography in ways that consistently question the supposed truth-value of each.

diCorcia's most celebrated series include Hustlers (1990–92), for which he photographed male sex workers in Los Angeles, paying each subject the same fee he was paid for sex and using the commercial transaction to frame a photographic encounter of unusual psychological complexity. His Heads series (1999–2001) used a hidden camera and powerful flash strobe set up in a New York street to capture pedestrians' faces in a pool of dramatic light without their knowledge, creating portraits of unanticipated formal beauty from unwitting subjects. Both methods destabilise assumptions about consent, documentation, and the nature of the photographic act.

diCorcia is represented by David Zwirner Gallery and his work has been exhibited at the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and major institutions internationally. He is one of the most influential photographers of his generation, and his investigation of the relationship between staged and documentary photography continues to generate critical discussion.

famous_works.title: Hustlers series
famous_works.notes: Staged documentary photographs of male sex workers in Los Angeles in which the commercial transaction of payment complicates and enriches the psychological dynamics of the photographic encounter.

famous_works.title: Heads
famous_works.notes: Street photographs of New York pedestrians caught in a powerful hidden strobe light, in which the dramatic artificial illumination transforms the unwitting subjects into figures of almost cinematic intensity.

famous_works.title: Streetwork series
famous_works.notes: Street photographs that use carefully prepared artificial lighting to transform observed urban scenes into images that hover between documentation and cinematic staging.

lesser_known_works.title: A Storybook Life
lesser_known_works.notes: A selection from diCorcia's long-term photographic autobiography that documents his personal life with the same formal intelligence he brings to his public projects.

lesser_known_works.title: Lucky Thirteen
lesser_known_works.notes: Photographs of strippers and lap dancers using the same payment-based methodology as the Hustlers series.

artists.bio:
Philip Tsiàras was born in Massachusetts in 1952 to Greek immigrant parents and has developed a practice that spans photography, painting, and digital media, combining classical art historical references with contemporary subjects and techniques. His work engages with the body — both the living human body and the microscopic biological world — as a site of beauty, vulnerability, and the spectacle of life at various scales.

Tsiàras is perhaps best known for his work in medical visualization: his collaborations with scientists to produce images of biological processes within the human body have resulted in both scientific publications and art world recognition. His photographic works combine this interest in biological spectacle with references to classical and Renaissance art, staging human subjects within tableaux that invoke Old Master compositions while using the body's contemporary and medical dimensions.

His practice maintains an ambitious range across multiple disciplines and has been exhibited at galleries and institutions in New York, Europe, and Asia. Tsiàras represents a distinctive strand in contemporary art's engagement with scientific imagery as a source of aesthetic and philosophical reflection.

famous_works.title: The Architecture and Design of Man and Woman
famous_works.notes: A landmark publication combining medical visualization and art photography that explores the human body's architecture through images of extraordinary biological beauty.

famous_works.title: From Conception to Birth series
famous_works.notes: Visual documentation of human development from fertilization through birth, using medical imaging technology to create images of the body's interior life.

famous_works.title: Greek Heritage series
famous_works.notes: Works exploring the visual and cultural heritage of classical Greece through a contemporary photographic lens.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Greek Documentary work
lesser_known_works.notes: Early photographic works documenting Greece that established the cultural foundations of Tsiàras's ongoing engagement with his Greek heritage.

lesser_known_works.title: Medical Visualization Studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Early scientific visualization work that preceded the large-scale publications for which Tsiàras became known.

artists.bio:
Wang Yuyang was born in Harbin, China in 1979 and studied at the Central Academy of Fine Arts in Beijing, where he subsequently joined the faculty. He has developed a practice that positions itself at the intersection of technology, media art, and philosophical inquiry, using custom-built electronic and optical devices to investigate the nature of light, time, perception, and the relationship between the human and the technological.

Wang's works often involve the construction of purpose-built machines or optical systems that produce images or experiences with specific phenomenological properties. His Breath series uses breathing apparatus connected to light systems to create works in which the artist's own respiration drives the visual output. Other works investigate the experience of specific light frequencies, the nature of digital and analogue display, and the phenomenology of vision itself.

He has been exhibited internationally including at Ullens Center for Contemporary Art in Beijing and at major international art fairs. Wang represents a sophisticated engagement with technology as a medium for philosophical and aesthetic investigation rather than mere spectacle, and his practice contributes to an important strand in Chinese contemporary art's engagement with science and technology.

famous_works.title: Breath series
famous_works.notes: Works in which the artist's breathing drives LED light displays, making respiratory biology into a generative system for light-based visual experience.

famous_works.title: Artificial Moon
famous_works.notes: A large-scale installation in which a spherical light source recreates the specific quality of moonlight within an interior space, investigating the phenomenology of natural and artificial light.

famous_works.title: Digital Evolution
famous_works.notes: A video work examining the transformation of analogue into digital image as a philosophical event with implications for how we understand nature, time, and human perception.

lesser_known_works.title: Early technology experiments
lesser_known_works.notes: Initial works in which Wang developed his methodology of using custom electronic systems as artistic tools.

lesser_known_works.title: Light Studies
lesser_known_works.notes: A group of works investigating specific light frequencies and their perceptual and emotional effects on the viewer.

artists.bio:
Brian Calvin was born in Visalia, California in 1969 and studied at the University of California Berkeley and the California College of Arts and Crafts. He came to prominence in the early 2000s with paintings of solitary figures — young women and men in vaguely defined interior or exterior spaces — whose combination of flatness, psychological ambiguity, and a quietly melancholic cool distinguished them from both the gestural figuration and the ironic postmodernism of the preceding generation.

Calvin's figures occupy a peculiar temporal suspension: they seem to be waiting, or have just arrived, or are about to leave, their psychological state encoded in posture and gaze without being explicitly narrated. He paints in a reduced, matte palette — pinks, greens, ochres that suggest both sun-faded California and something more interior and timeless — applying paint in flat areas defined by precise, confident contour. The works acknowledge Matisse's decorative flatness and the economy of George Condo's figures while remaining stylistically singular.

Calvin has been exhibited with Corvi-Mora in London and Anton Kern Gallery in New York, and his work is collected by major museums and private collectors. His practice has quietly influenced a generation of younger figurative painters for whom psychological atmosphere and formal restraint are primary concerns.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Standing Woman)
famous_works.notes: A large-scale figure painting in which a woman in a simple dress occupies a flattened, colour-field ground with a quietness that reads as simultaneously empty and charged.

famous_works.title: Here Comes
famous_works.notes: A double-figure composition in which two figures in an undefined exterior space are related by gaze and posture in a way that suggests narrative without specifying it.

famous_works.title: In Your Shoes
famous_works.notes: A single figure in a domestic interior that condenses Calvin's characteristic suspension of narrative time into a formally precise and emotionally resonant image.

lesser_known_works.title: Long Way Around
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work that establishes Calvin's mature matte palette and his interest in the psychological weight of the solitary figure in undefined space.

lesser_known_works.title: Through the Wire
lesser_known_works.notes: A landscape-inflected figure painting in which the environment begins to dissolve into pattern, expanding Calvin's pictorial range.

artists.bio:
Jonathan Lyndon Chase was born in Philadelphia in 1989 and received a BFA from the Philadelphia College of Art. Working across painting, drawing, and collage, he develops images of Black male and non-binary bodies in intimate and expressive states — bathing, dancing, embracing, existing in states of vulnerability or pleasure — that challenge both the historical representation of Black men in art and contemporary expectations about the acceptable emotional range of Black male subjects.

Chase's work is characterised by its chromatic vivacity and its emotional directness: his figures inhabit spaces suffused with warm, intense colour, their bodies rendered with a fluency that simultaneously references the Western nude tradition and refuses its assumptions about what bodies can be shown doing and feeling. The works create a space in which Black queerness, tenderness, and bodily pleasure are present without apology or qualification, claiming the full range of human experience as available to pictorial representation.

He has been exhibited with Massimo De Carlo in Milan and London and has attracted significant critical attention as part of the broader reassessment of whose bodies and experiences contemporary painting makes visible. Chase's work represents an important contribution to the ongoing expansion of the figurative tradition.

famous_works.title: Don't Cry Baby I Got You
famous_works.notes: A large painting of two embracing male figures in which the tenderness of physical contact between Black men is rendered with warmth and formal confidence.

famous_works.title: Wash
famous_works.notes: A bathing scene in which the domestic ritual of washing becomes an occasion for exploring the beauty and vulnerability of the Black male body in an intimate register.

famous_works.title: Brown Skin
famous_works.notes: A figurative work in which Chase's characteristic luminous colour and direct engagement with Black physicality achieve a particular formal intensity.

lesser_known_works.title: Dancing Figures
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work establishing Chase's figurative vocabulary and his interest in depicting Black bodies in states of joy and physical liberation.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled (Embrace)
lesser_known_works.notes: A two-figure composition in which physical closeness between male figures is rendered with an intimacy that challenges mainstream representations of Black masculine sociality.

artists.bio:
Robert Colescott was born in Oakland, California in 1925 and studied at the University of California Berkeley and at the École des Beaux-Arts in Paris under Fernand Léger. He developed a practice of figurative painting that combined expressionistic energy with a sustained satirical engagement with American racial history and the conventions of Western art. He was the first African American artist to represent the United States at the Venice Biennale solo, in 1997.

Colescott's paintings engage directly with canonical works of Western art history — Delacroix, Manet, van Gogh, Lichtenstein — replacing their figures with Black characters and in doing so exposing the racial assumptions underlying these canonical images. His George Washington Carver Crossing the Delaware (1975), which replaces Washington's European troops with Black stereotypes in a parody of Leutze's famous painting, remains one of the most provocative and significant interventions in the American art historical tradition.

Beyond the appropriation works, Colescott developed a body of large, densely painted canvases that deploy popular culture, comic book aesthetics, and expressionist colour to address racism, sexuality, and the absurdities of American social life. He taught at the University of Arizona and his work is held by the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, the Smithsonian, and other major collections.

famous_works.title: George Washington Carver Crossing the Delaware
famous_works.notes: A parody of Leutze's iconic painting in which the heroic European figures are replaced by Black stereotypes, exposing the racial exclusions encoded in American patriotic mythology.

famous_works.title: Les Demoiselles d'Alabama: Vestidas
famous_works.notes: A reworking of Picasso's foundational painting in which the French artist's primitivist African sources are reversed — Black women are depicted in the style of Picasso's European avant-garde original.

famous_works.title: Eat Dem Taters
famous_works.notes: A parody of Van Gogh's Potato Eaters in which the Dutch peasant subjects are replaced by Black American figures, connecting the two traditions of dignifying working-class subjects.

lesser_known_works.title: Shirley Temple Black and Bill Robinson White
lesser_known_works.notes: A painting examining race and popular entertainment through the famous dancing partnership, deploying Colescott's characteristic analytical reversal.

lesser_known_works.title: American Fever
lesser_known_works.notes: A late painting demonstrating the expressionist energy and satirical depth of Colescott's mature practice outside the appropriation works.

artists.bio:
Joan Brown was born in San Francisco in 1938 and studied at the California School of Arts and Crafts, where she came under the influence of Elmer Bischoff and was part of the second generation of the Bay Area Figurative Movement. She developed an immediately recognisable style: densely impastoed, vibrantly coloured figurative paintings in which the autobiographical subject — herself, her son Noel, her animals, her swimmer's body — is rendered with a directness and physical energy that has no equivalent in Bay Area painting.

Brown's early work of the late 1950s and early 1960s is remarkable for its sheer material extravagance: paint laid on so thickly that the figures seem to emerge from geological formations, the colour raw and assertive. By the late 1960s she had simplified her surfaces while maintaining the autobiographical directness, producing large figurative paintings in flat colour and precise outline that combined personal narrative with a range of cultural sources from Egyptian painting to the imagery of her own spiritual development through Meher Baba.

Brown died in 1990 when a decorative obelisk fell on her during the installation of a public mural in India. Her work is held by the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art and the Smithsonian American Art Museum, and she has been increasingly recognised as one of the most original American painters of her generation.

famous_works.title: Noel at One Year
famous_works.notes: A painting of her infant son rendered in the thick impasto of Brown's early period, in which maternal feeling and material extravagance are combined with characteristic directness.

famous_works.title: Girl in Striped Bathing Suit
famous_works.notes: A self-portrait in a swimming costume that exemplifies Brown's early mature style — thick, gestural, vibrantly coloured — within a directly autobiographical subject.

famous_works.title: Egyptian Series
famous_works.notes: A group of paintings inspired by Brown's deepening engagement with ancient Egyptian culture and her spiritual development, in which flat colour and profile figures create a synthesis of personal and ancient symbolism.

lesser_known_works.title: The Bride (Self-Portrait)
lesser_known_works.notes: An autobiographical canvas from the transitional period in which Brown simplified her surface while maintaining the personal directness that defines her work.

lesser_known_works.title: Dog and Cat with Primus
lesser_known_works.notes: A domestic scene with animals and human figure that demonstrates Brown's sustained attention to the ordinary inhabitants of her daily life as worthy pictorial subjects.

artists.bio:
Angela Heisch is an American painter whose work explores the female body, desire, and domestic space through a pictorial language that combines influences from Pop Art, illustration, and the history of erotic imagery. Working primarily in acrylic on canvas, she creates compositions that are at once seductive and unsettling: figures occupy flattened, vibrantly coloured spaces in poses that reference both pin-up culture and the classical nude, freighted with an ambivalence about the conventions being invoked.

Heisch's formal vocabulary emphasises line, flat colour areas, and a graphic clarity that gives her canvases an immediate visual appeal while encoding more complex negotiations with the politics of looking and being looked at. Her female figures are often depicted in interior spaces that read as simultaneously private and theatrical, spaces in which femininity is performed before an implied audience. The works invite and interrogate desire in the same gesture.

She has exhibited with Nino Mier Gallery and other venues in New York and Los Angeles, and has attracted attention as part of a generation of painters reassessing the female figure through a lens that is simultaneously art-historically informed and culturally critical. Her work participates in broader conversations about women's self-representation and the reclamation of erotic imagery as a site of female agency.

famous_works.title: Pink Interior
famous_works.notes: A vividly coloured canvas in which a reclining female figure inhabits a domestic space that oscillates between private sanctuary and theatrical stage.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Boudoir)
famous_works.notes: A composition recalling pin-up illustration in its graphic flatness while investing the scene with psychological complexity through ambiguous spatial staging.

famous_works.title: Night Scene
famous_works.notes: A darker-palette work in which interior light becomes a formal element as charged as the figure it illuminates.

lesser_known_works.title: Study in Red
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work demonstrating the graphic clarity and chromatic boldness that became Heisch's signature approach.

lesser_known_works.title: Two Figures
lesser_known_works.notes: A composition expanding Heisch's usual solo-figure focus to explore the dynamics between two female bodies in shared space.

artists.bio:
Anne Collier was born in Los Angeles in 1970 and studied at UCLA and the California Institute of the Arts before relocating to New York, where she has developed a photography practice rooted in the staging and re-photographing of found objects — album covers, magazine images, posters, mirrors — that constitute a sustained inquiry into image culture, feminism, and the emotional economies of pop music and mass media.

Collier's practice belongs to the tradition of Pictures Generation artists who questioned the authority of the original image, yet her work carries an emotional directness that distinguishes it from purely deconstructive appropriation. She typically photographs objects against neutral studio grounds, treating them with a quiet formality that allows both their physical materiality and their cultural meaning to register simultaneously. A woman crying on an album cover, a record sleeve depicting female longing, a self-help book about relationships: these found objects are containers of feeling that Collier's careful attention activates without sentimentality.

She has been exhibited extensively at Anton Kern Gallery in New York and Gagosian Gallery, and her work has been included in major institutional exhibitions exploring photography's relationship to image culture and feminism. Collier's practice represents a model of how appropriation can be maintained as a method while being reinvested with emotional and political content.

famous_works.title: Woman Crying series
famous_works.notes: A long-running series photographing album and magazine images of weeping women, excavating the cultural scripts that shape how female emotion is represented and consumed.

famous_works.title: Open
famous_works.notes: A photograph of a hardback book opened flat, in which the spine's tension and the page's blankness create a meditation on vulnerability and exposure.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Mirrors)
famous_works.notes: A series of photographs of mirrors whose reflective surfaces capture the studio and the photographer's equipment, turning the tool of self-reflection into a subject of formal inquiry.

lesser_known_works.title: Cat Stevens
lesser_known_works.notes: A photograph of a Cat Stevens album cover that exemplifies Collier's method of treating pop cultural objects as emotionally loaded archives.

lesser_known_works.title: Negative Space
lesser_known_works.notes: A work in which the empty space around a photographed object becomes as formally and conceptually significant as the object itself.

artists.bio:
Sandro Chia was born in Florence in 1946 and studied at the Istituto d'Arte di Firenze and the Accademia di Belle Arti before moving to Rome. In the late 1970s he emerged as one of the central figures of the Transavanguardia, the movement theorised by critic Achille Bonito Oliva that brought together a group of Italian painters — including Francesco Clemente, Enzo Cucchi, and Mimmo Paladino — who returned to expressive figuration, mythological subject matter, and an explicit engagement with the history of Italian painting at a moment when Conceptual Art and Minimalism dominated international discourse.

Chia's paintings are large, vigorously painted canvases in which robust male figures — typically in action, often heroic or absurdist in their posturing — inhabit landscapes or dreamlike environments with an exuberant physicality that references both Italian Renaissance painting and the gestural energy of Abstract Expressionism. His figures have a comic seriousness: they are obviously painting, obviously rhetorical, yet the genuine painterly intelligence of their surfaces makes the rhetoric something more than emptily decorative.

Chia has been exhibited by Sperone Westwater and has maintained a substantial international profile since his emergence in the late 1970s. He has studios in New York, Florence, and the Bahamas and has been a prolific producer of paintings, drawings, and sculpture.

famous_works.title: Rabbit and Smoke
famous_works.notes: A large painting of a striding male figure with a rabbit that demonstrates Chia's combination of mythological gesture with expressionist painterly surface.

famous_works.title: The Idleness of Sisyphus
famous_works.notes: A mythological subject rendered in Chia's characteristic large-scale figurative manner, using the classical myth as a vehicle for painterly investigation.

famous_works.title: Three Boys on a Raft
famous_works.notes: A painting of youthful male figures in a landscape that exemplifies the combination of Renaissance formal tradition and contemporary expressionist energy at the core of Chia's approach.

lesser_known_works.title: Water Carrier
lesser_known_works.notes: A figurative work from Chia's peak Transavanguardia period demonstrating the full development of his robust male figurative language.

lesser_known_works.title: Campagna painting
lesser_known_works.notes: A landscape-figure composition from Chia's mature period demonstrating the range of his engagement with Italian landscape tradition.

artists.bio:
Sterling Ruby was born in Bitburg, Germany, to American parents in 1972 and grew up in Pennsylvania before studying at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago and the California Institute of the Arts. He has developed a sprawling, multi-disciplinary practice — encompassing painting, sculpture, video, ceramics, textile, and costume — that engages with violence, power, subcultural communities, and the material culture of American life.

Ruby's work resists any single medium or formal approach: his spray paint and acrylic canvases share territory with ceramic vessels decorated with the imagery of gang culture and American vernacular ornament; his fabric collages and urethane spray sculptures share an aesthetic of controlled abandon; his costumes and textile works connect to fashion while maintaining the autonomy of art objects. What unifies the practice is an intense engagement with the physical and psychological dimensions of materials, and a commitment to working at a scale and ambition that refuses intimacy in favour of the overwhelming.

He is represented by Hauser & Wirth and has been exhibited at major institutions internationally. Ruby maintains a large studio complex in Vernon, California, with multiple departments operating simultaneously across the range of media he employs.

famous_works.title: SP
famous_works.notes: A long-running series of spray paint and acrylic canvases in which aerosol paint creates atmospheric grounds layered with gestural marks, exploring the territory between graffit aesthetics and abstract painting.

famous_works.title: BASIN THEOLOGY
famous_works.notes: A monumental installation at Hauser & Wirth filling the gallery's entire space with a combination of sculpture, fabric, and ceramic works that create an overwhelming material environment.

famous_works.title: Ceramics series
famous_works.notes: Large ceramic vessels and urns decorated with imagery drawn from gang culture, violent iconography, and American vernacular design.

lesser_known_works.title: DRFTWR (driftwood sculptures)
lesser_known_works.notes: Works using driftwood and found materials that demonstrate the range of Ruby's material investigations beyond his more visible spray paint and ceramic works.

lesser_known_works.title: Textile works
lesser_known_works.notes: Fabric and clothing works that connect Ruby's practice to the history of craft traditions and the specificity of tactile material.

artists.bio:
Emmanuel Taku was born in Ghana in 1995 and is based in Accra, where he has developed a figurative painting practice centred on the psychological and social lives of Ghanaian youth. Working primarily in oil, he creates large-scale portraits and group scenes of young people in contemporary urban and domestic settings, giving equal pictorial authority to individuals whose daily experience falls outside the representational hierarchies of Western art.

Taku's formal vocabulary is direct and confident: rich colour, strong figural presence, compositions that assert the dignity and interiority of his subjects without aestheticising their circumstances. He draws on the West African tradition of studio portraiture — the formal photograph as an assertion of social standing — while deploying the language of contemporary international figurative painting. His subjects engage the viewer's gaze with an ease that refuses both deference and exoticism.

He has been exhibited internationally, including in exhibitions organised to introduce emerging African artists to European and American audiences, and has attracted attention as part of a broader recognition of the richness of contemporary painting from West Africa. Taku's work represents a generation for whom the international art world and a rooted local identity are not in contradiction.

famous_works.title: After School
famous_works.notes: A group portrait of Ghanaian schoolchildren in which the formal organisation of the composition and the psychological specificity of individual figures combine to produce an image of both community and individuality.

famous_works.title: The Friend Group
famous_works.notes: A contemporary social scene in Accra in which the painting's scale and formal ambition insist on the pictorial equivalence of everyday Ghanaian youth culture to any other subject of serious painting.

famous_works.title: Portrait of Kwame
famous_works.notes: A direct individual portrait that demonstrates Taku's particular ability to render psychological presence within a confidently constructed pictorial space.

lesser_known_works.title: Street Corner, Accra
lesser_known_works.notes: An urban scene in which the specific atmosphere of contemporary Accra is rendered as the ground from which Taku's figural subjects emerge.

lesser_known_works.title: Family Gathering
lesser_known_works.notes: A multi-generational domestic scene that extends Taku's investigation of Ghanaian social life into the register of familial relationships.

artists.bio:
Shona McAndrew was born in 1987 and has developed a painting practice in oil and alkyd that draws on the figurative tradition — particularly the history of portraiture, mythological painting, and the representation of women — to create images of psychological complexity and formal sophistication. Her work explores the representation of women's bodies, emotions, and social identities through both historical and contemporary imagery.

McAndrew's paintings combine references to historical art — the conventions of nineteenth-century academic figuration, Victorian portraiture, genre painting — with contemporary subjects and a sensibility that interrogates rather than merely revives these traditions. Her figures often inhabit spaces of domestic or theatrical ambiguity, their psychological states encoded in posture, costume, and the relationship between figure and setting.

She has exhibited in New York and has attracted attention as part of the current generation of painters engaged in a critical but formally committed dialogue with the history of figurative painting. McAndrew's work participates in the ongoing reassessment of whose stories are told within that tradition and how.

famous_works.title: Portrait in Victorian Dress
famous_works.notes: A contemporary figure in historical costume that creates a dialogue between present and past representations of femininity within the genre of portrait painting.

famous_works.title: Interior with Woman
famous_works.notes: A domestic scene in which psychological complexity is encoded within the formal conventions of the Victorian interior genre painting.

famous_works.title: Mythological Scene
famous_works.notes: A painting drawing on historical mythological subject matter and recasting it through a contemporary feminist lens.

lesser_known_works.title: Study after Old Master
lesser_known_works.notes: A work engaging directly with the historical figurative tradition as a formal and conceptual source.

lesser_known_works.title: Domestic Interior
lesser_known_works.notes: An interior scene demonstrating McAndrew's combination of historical formal conventions with contemporary psychological content.

artists.bio:
Robert Frank was born in Zurich, Switzerland in 1924 and learned photography through apprenticeships in Basel before emigrating to New York in 1947. Working as a fashion photographer for Harper's Bazaar and other publications, he began documenting the texture of daily American life on the margins of his commercial assignments, developing a visual sensibility increasingly at odds with the optimistic, well-lit imagery of post-war American mass media.

In 1955–56, with a Guggenheim Fellowship, Frank travelled across the United States making the photographs that were published as The Americans in France in 1958 and America in 1959. The book — introduced by Jack Kerouac — presented an America that was isolated, racialized, and melancholy, rendered in a deliberately rough, anti-pictorialist style of tilted horizons, blur, and chance that was completely unlike the polished documentary photography of the period. It was initially poorly received in the United States, where its critical perspective on American life felt unpatriotic; it subsequently became the most influential photography book of the twentieth century.

Frank largely abandoned photography for filmmaking after The Americans, producing experimental films and later returning to photography in a more personal, autobiographical mode. He died at his home in Inverness, Nova Scotia in 2019 at ninety-four.

famous_works.title: The Americans
famous_works.notes: The most influential photography book of the twentieth century, presenting an America of racial segregation, isolation, and melancholy in a deliberately rough, anti-monumental visual style.

famous_works.title: Trolley — New Orleans
famous_works.notes: A photograph from The Americans in which a segregated streetcar's windows frame faces by race — a single image that condenses the entire book's social analysis.

famous_works.title: Parade — Hoboken, New Jersey
famous_works.notes: A photograph of American flag and figures in which the national emblem seems to obscure rather than celebrate its implied subjects.

lesser_known_works.title: Black White and Things
lesser_known_works.notes: An early photobook that predates The Americans but demonstrates Frank's developing interest in the social textures of daily life.

lesser_known_works.title: Me and My Brother (Film)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early experimental film that demonstrates Frank's engagement with cinema as a medium for the same investigations he pursued in photography.

artists.bio:
Tyler Ballon was born in 1993 and has developed a painting practice that explores Black masculine identity, leisure, and joy through large-scale figurative works rendered in a warm, expressive manner. His practice participates in the broader reassessment of whose bodies and experiences are made visible in contemporary painting, centring Black male figures in states of pleasure, rest, and social connection.

Ballon's formal approach combines the gestural directness of Neo-Expressionism with a sensitivity to colour and atmosphere that gives his canvases their particular warmth. His figures inhabit parks, beaches, domestic spaces, and social environments in configurations that celebrate the full range of human experience — intimate, communal, physical, contemplative — while insisting on Black subjects as fully deserving of the attention and formal dignity that painting has historically reserved for different populations.

He has exhibited in New York and has attracted critical attention as part of a generation for whom the expansion of figuration to include previously marginalised subjects and experiences is both a political and a formal imperative. Ballon's work contributes to this project with evident painterly talent and genuine formal ambition.

famous_works.title: Park Scene
famous_works.notes: A large painting of Black men at leisure in a public park space, rendered with warmth and formal confidence that asserts the dignity of everyday pleasure as a painterly subject.

famous_works.title: Beach
famous_works.notes: A figurative work placing Black male bodies in a recreational coastal environment with the same aesthetic attention that traditional painting has brought to idealised European figures.

famous_works.title: The Gathering
famous_works.notes: A multi-figure composition exploring the dynamics of Black male sociality and community within a richly painted environment.

lesser_known_works.title: Early studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from Ballon's formation period establishing the figurative vocabulary and warm palette of his mature practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait
lesser_known_works.notes: A direct figurative portrait demonstrating Ballon's technical facility within the genre.

artists.bio:
Sherrie Levine was born in Hazelton, Pennsylvania in 1947 and studied at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. She came to prominence in the late 1970s and early 1980s as one of the central figures of the Pictures Generation, a group of artists including Cindy Sherman, Robert Longo, and Richard Prince who used strategies of appropriation, quotation, and reproduction to challenge the authority of original images and the mythology of artistic originality.

Levine's most celebrated early works were rephotographs: she photographed reproductions of canonical photographs by Walker Evans, Edward Weston, and Alexander Rodchenko from art books, titled the results After Walker Evans, etc., and exhibited the prints as her own work. The gesture simultaneously attacked photographic authority, the myth of the unique artwork, and the patriarchal canon of art history by pointing out that all three were contingent constructions rather than natural facts.

Subsequent work has extended these investigations through lead casts of Duchamp's readymades, watercolour copies of Schiele and Mondrian, and gold-gleamed plywood replicas of Brancusi bronzes. In each case, the act of repetition with a difference exposes what was concealed by the myth of origin. Levine is represented by David Zwirner Gallery and her work has been exhibited at major institutions including the Whitney Museum, where she is a central figure in the history of American Conceptual and postmodern art.

famous_works.title: After Walker Evans: 4
famous_works.notes: A rephotograph of one of Evans's Farm Security Administration photographs that through its act of replication questions the authority of the original and exposes the conventions that produce it.

famous_works.title: Fountain (After Marcel Duchamp: A.P.)
famous_works.notes: A cast bronze urinal that repeats Duchamp's readymade in a precious, authoritative medium, demonstrating how the appropriation of appropriation exposes its own conventions.

famous_works.title: After Egon Schiele
famous_works.notes: Watercolour copies of Schiele's figure drawings that examine how the reproduction of canonical Expressionist work implicates questions of gender, authority, and artistic identity.

lesser_known_works.title: Gold Knots series
lesser_known_works.notes: Works in which commercially produced plywood sheets with their own random grain patterns are framed as representations of abstract painting.

lesser_known_works.title: Lead Checks
lesser_known_works.notes: Lead casts of commercially produced check-patterned fabrics that continue Levine's investigation of reproduction, value, and the conventions of abstract pattern.

artists.bio:
Ksenia Dermenzhi was born in Russia in 1991 and is based in New York, where she has developed a painting practice that explores mythological, fantastical, and psychological subjects through a densely layered oil-on-canvas technique. Her work draws on the traditions of Russian Symbolism, European Romanticism, and Northern Renaissance painting — sources that inform the atmospheric density and the charged psychological atmosphere of her canvases.

Dermenzhi's paintings are inhabited by figures — often female, often in states of transformation or suspension — whose relationship to their environments has a dreamlike quality: bodies dissolving into flowers, figures suspended in atmospheric space, scenes whose narrative logic is emotional rather than literal. Her technical approach involves building up multiple translucent layers of oil paint to create surfaces of depth and atmospheric resonance, a methodology that connects her to the glazing traditions of the Old Masters even as her imagery is entirely contemporary.

She has exhibited in New York and has attracted attention from collectors and institutions interested in the current revival of interest in symbolically rich, technically ambitious figurative painting. Her work represents a rigorous engagement with painting's capacity to render the psychological and mythological dimensions of experience.

famous_works.title: Metamorphosis
famous_works.notes: A painting of a figure in the process of transformation into a natural form, rendered in Dermenzhi's characteristic atmospheric oil layers.

famous_works.title: Night Garden
famous_works.notes: A densely painted nocturnal scene in which figures and botanical forms are woven together in a composition of symbolic complexity.

famous_works.title: The Mirror
famous_works.notes: A painting exploring the psychological theme of self-reflection through the device of a mirror, rendered in Dermenzhi's resonant layered manner.

lesser_known_works.title: Early Symbolist Studies
lesser_known_works.notes: Works demonstrating the development of Dermenzhi's technical approach and her engagement with Symbolist pictorial traditions.

lesser_known_works.title: Botanical Figure
lesser_known_works.notes: A work in which the human figure and plant form are merged in a way that demonstrates Dermenzhi's particular interest in states of transformation.

top_lot.title: Flowers in Vase in the Blue Room

top_ratio_lot.title: Flowers in Vase in the Blue Room

artists.bio:
Piero Dorazio was born in Rome in 1927 and studied at the Faculty of Architecture at the University of Rome. He was a co-founder of the Italian avant-garde group Forma Uno in 1947, which promoted abstract art against the Socialist Realist positions that dominated the Italian left, and subsequently developed a distinctive practice of pure colour abstraction that placed him within an international conversation about the possibilities of non-representational painting.

Dorazio's paintings from the late 1950s onward are characterised by grids, webs, and networks of coloured lines, strokes, and bands that create vibrating optical effects of great chromatic intensity. He was deeply involved in the international dialogue between European concrete abstraction and American Colour Field painting, visiting New York and establishing connections with the Abstract Expressionist generation. His work explores the resonances between closely related hues and the optical effects created by the layering and intersection of colour lines.

He taught at the University of Pennsylvania from 1960 to 1969 and maintained close connections with American abstract painting throughout his career, while remaining a significant figure in the Italian context. His work is held by major European and American museums, and he received the prestigious Leone d'Oro for lifetime achievement at the Venice Biennale.

famous_works.title: Painting (Thread series)
famous_works.notes: A canvas in which networks of brightly coloured thread-like strokes create vibrating optical fields that exemplify Dorazio's mature investigation of colour interaction.

famous_works.title: Net Series
famous_works.notes: A sustained investigation of grid and net structures in which the intersection of coloured lines creates optical phenomena of great complexity.

famous_works.title: Composition in Blue
famous_works.notes: An early abstract work demonstrating the chromatic intensity and formal intelligence that would develop into Dorazio's distinctive mature language.

lesser_known_works.title: Forma Uno Period works
lesser_known_works.notes: Early abstract paintings from Dorazio's formative group association that demonstrate his initial development away from figuration toward pure abstraction.

lesser_known_works.title: Late career investigations
lesser_known_works.notes: Works from the final phase of Dorazio's career in which the network and thread structures are pursued with continued formal clarity and chromatic ambition.

artists.bio:
Alvin Armstrong is an American painter whose work centres on the Black male body as a site of beauty, vulnerability, and cultural inscription. Working primarily in oil on canvas, Armstrong constructs compositions that draw on the traditions of Western figuration — the nude, the portrait, the interior scene — while insisting that these traditions be reckoned with on new terms, ones that acknowledge the historical exclusion and stereotyping of Black subjects within them.

Armstrong's figures occupy richly coloured domestic and imagined spaces, often rendered with a luminosity that recalls the intimacy of Vuillard or Bonnard while asserting a distinctly contemporary sensibility. His approach to the male nude is particularly notable for its tenderness: bodies are depicted in moments of repose, contemplation, or casual intimacy, refusing both the heroic idealization of classical tradition and the objectification that has shaped so much representation of Black men in visual culture.

He has exhibited at galleries in New York and Los Angeles and has attracted attention as part of a broader reassessment of figurative painting centring on artists committed to expanding whose stories are told through the genre. Armstrong's work participates in a conversation about visibility, dignity, and the politics of who gets to be rendered beautiful.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Reclining Figure)
famous_works.notes: A quietly monumental oil depicting a Black male figure at rest, saturated with warm colour and an intimacy that reclaims the tradition of the Western nude.

famous_works.title: Interior with Two Men
famous_works.notes: A domestic scene in which two figures occupy a shared space, demonstrating Armstrong's interest in Black masculine sociality rendered with painterly tenderness.

famous_works.title: Blue Room
famous_works.notes: A richly coloured interior in which the figure and the ambient space are treated with equal attention, recalling Post-Impressionist interior traditions within a contemporary framework.

lesser_known_works.title: After the Bath
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work that establishes the intimate figural vocabulary Armstrong would develop in his subsequent practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Portrait of the Artist's Friend
lesser_known_works.notes: A direct portrait demonstrating Armstrong's particular gift for psychological warmth within a formally precise pictorial structure.

artists.bio:
Henri Cartier-Bresson was born in Chanteloup-en-Brie, Seine-et-Marne in 1908 and came from a prosperous family. He studied painting with André Lhote, whose Cubist-influenced teaching shaped the compositional rigor that would characterise his photographic vision. After a year in Côte d'Ivoire and a period of convalescence from blackwater fever, he acquired a Leica 35mm camera in 1932 and discovered the medium that would become his life's work.

Cartier-Bresson articulated the concept of 'the decisive moment' — the instant at which form and content are simultaneously perfect, when the visual and psychological elements of a scene align into a coherent image. Working exclusively with small cameras and available light, refusing to crop his negatives, he developed a practice of extraordinary formal intelligence: his images are composed with a geometrical precision that recalls Mondrian or Cézanne while capturing the spontaneous, unrepeatable energy of lived time. He photographed across five continents, documenting Gandhi's funeral, the liberation of Paris, China's communist revolution, and the daily life of ordinary people with equal attention.

In 1947 he co-founded Magnum Photos with Robert Capa and others. He retired from photography in 1975 to concentrate on drawing and painting. The Fondation Henri Cartier-Bresson in Paris preserves and promotes his legacy, and his photographs are held by major museums worldwide.

famous_works.title: Behind the Gare Saint-Lazare
famous_works.notes: A photograph of a man leaping over a flooded space, his reflection in the water below, in which the geometry of the image and the energy of the moment achieve perfect coincidence.

famous_works.title: Hyères, France
famous_works.notes: A cyclist descending a spiral staircase, in which the formal perfection of the composition makes the fleeting subject appear inevitable.

famous_works.title: Gandhi's Funeral, New Delhi
famous_works.notes: A monumental photograph made at the cremation of Mahatma Gandhi that demonstrates Cartier-Bresson's ability to operate as both historical witness and formal artist simultaneously.

lesser_known_works.title: Mexico City
lesser_known_works.notes: Early photographs from Cartier-Bresson's Latin American period demonstrating the development of his compositional approach before his style reached full maturity.

lesser_known_works.title: Ivory Coast
lesser_known_works.notes: Among the earliest photographs from Cartier-Bresson's first Leica period, made during a year of hunting and adventure that preceded his serious photographic commitment.

artists.bio:
Francesca Woodman was born in Denver, Colorado in 1958 into an artistic family — her mother Betty Woodman is a celebrated ceramicist, her father George Woodman a painter and photographer — and began photographing herself at thirteen. She studied at the Rhode Island School of Design from 1975 to 1978, where she produced much of the work for which she is remembered, and spent a year in Rome before returning to New York, where she died by suicide in 1981 at twenty-two.

Woodman photographed almost exclusively herself and female friends in interior spaces — abandoned houses, her own studio — using long exposures that blur figures into their environments, obscure faces behind peeling walls, merge bodies with corners and floors. The resulting images occupy a peculiar ontological space between presence and absence, visibility and concealment, as though the subject is in the process of being absorbed by or emerging from the material world. They have an intimacy and intensity that makes their autobiographical dimension inescapable, yet the formal intelligence governing every element of composition, light, and exposure is that of a mature artist, not a troubled young woman.

Woodman's posthumous reputation has grown steadily and her work is now held by major museums including the Guggenheim and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art. Her influence on subsequent generations of photographers working with self-portraiture, feminism, and the body is considerable, and she is recognised as one of the most original photographic voices of the late twentieth century.

famous_works.title: Space 2, Providence, Rhode Island
famous_works.notes: A blurred self-portrait in an abandoned interior in which the figure's dissolution into the architectural surface encapsulates Woodman's central investigation of presence and disappearance.

famous_works.title: House #3, Providence, Rhode Island
famous_works.notes: A photograph of Woodman behind a partially peeled section of wallpaper, in which the spatial ambiguity — is she within the wall or before it? — generates the characteristic ontological uncanny.

famous_works.title: From Angel series, Rome, Italy
famous_works.notes: The Rome series in which wings and architectural spaces create a more deliberately mythological register within Woodman's investigation of the female body in interior space.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled, Boulder, Colorado
lesser_known_works.notes: Early self-portraits made before Woodman arrived at RISD that show her precocious engagement with self-representation, space, and the body.

lesser_known_works.title: MacDowell series
lesser_known_works.notes: Photographs made during a residency at the MacDowell Colony in New Hampshire, among the last works Woodman produced and marked by an increasing formal austerity.

top_lot.title: Untitled, Providence, Rhode Island (Woman with Seashell)

top_ratio_lot.title: Untitled, Providence, Rhode Island (Woman with Seashell)

artists.bio:
Rudolf Bauer was born in Lindenwald, Germany in 1889 and moved to Berlin as a young man, establishing himself as a painter whose early work belonged to the Expressionist milieu before developing toward a purely abstract approach influenced by his close relationship with Wassily Kandinsky and the spiritual dimensions of abstraction the Russian painter theorised.

Bauer's relationship with the American art dealer and collector Hilla Rebay — who would become the founding curator of the Guggenheim Museum — was decisive for his career and his historical fate. Rebay promoted Bauer's work with extraordinary enthusiasm and was instrumental in persuading Solomon Guggenheim to collect it extensively. The Guggenheim Foundation's original collection consisted almost equally of Bauer and Kandinsky, and for a period Bauer was more prominent in American eyes than his Russian counterpart.

His arrest by the Nazis and subsequent release (partly through Rebay's intervention) led to his emigration to the United States, where he lived until his death in 1953. The subsequent direction of the Guggenheim under James Johnson Sweeney, who replaced Rebay, effectively removed Bauer from the museum's active programme, and his reputation declined sharply. The rediscovery of his work and its relationship to the early Guggenheim's founding vision has been a subject of continued scholarly interest.

famous_works.title: Das Geistige (The Spiritual)
famous_works.notes: An abstract composition that embodies Bauer's investigation of spiritual content in non-representational painting, closely related to Kandinsky's theoretical concerns.

famous_works.title: Adagio
famous_works.notes: A large abstract work from Bauer's mature period in which musical terminology in the title reflects the Kandinsky-influenced investigation of synesthetic relationships between music and visual form.

famous_works.title: Improvisation VII
famous_works.notes: A painting in which geometric and biomorphic elements create a spatial dynamic characteristic of Bauer's development of spiritual abstraction.

lesser_known_works.title: Circle paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Abstract compositions centred on circular forms that connect Bauer's work to the broader European geometric abstraction current of the inter-war period.

lesser_known_works.title: American period works
lesser_known_works.notes: Late paintings made in the United States after Bauer's emigration, showing the continuation of his abstract practice in a new context.

top_lot.title: Ohne Titel (Points)
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio:
R.H. Quaytman was born in Boston in 1961 and studied at Bard College and the Academy of Fine Arts in Munich. She has developed over four decades one of the most rigorously conceived painting practices in contemporary art: a body of work organised into numbered 'Chapters' in which all paintings are executed on gessoed plywood panels in standard sizes defined by a specific ratio, and in which the paintings' subjects, formats, and material treatments relate to the specific historical and architectural context of each exhibition.

Quaytman's paintings combine screened images (photographs silk-screened onto the panel surface), geometric patterns, and painted areas in a manner that treats the painting simultaneously as image, object, and document. Her use of the silkscreen allows the incorporation of photographic imagery — of exhibition spaces, artworks, historical events, architectural details — while maintaining the painting's identity as a material object rather than an illusionistic window. The Chapters accumulate over time into a book-like structure in which the history of her own practice is inscribed.

She is represented by Gladstone Gallery and has been the subject of major institutional exhibitions and retrospectives that have confirmed her importance as a rigorous and original voice within contemporary painting's engagement with photography, materiality, and institutional context.

famous_works.title: Chapter 25: HOSPITALITY (for M.O.C.A.)
famous_works.notes: A chapter of paintings produced for a specific institutional context in which the architectural and historical specifics of the Museum of Contemporary Art engage with Quaytman's formal and photographic systems.

famous_works.title: Distracting Distance, Chapter 17
famous_works.notes: A chapter in which photographs of the landscape around Midway Atoll are silk-screened onto panels alongside geometric and optical patterns, connecting American military history to painting's formal concerns.

famous_works.title: Morning: Glory, Chapter 30
famous_works.notes: A chapter produced in response to a specific exhibition context that demonstrates the depth and formal intelligence of Quaytman's accumulated practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Chapter 1
lesser_known_works.notes: The inaugural chapter that established Quaytman's book-like organisational structure and the standard panel proportions that have defined her practice.

lesser_known_works.title: Spine
lesser_known_works.notes: A work addressing the binding-like element that connects Quaytman's Chapters to each other, making explicit the book-metaphor that structures her entire practice.

top_lot.title: Exhibition Guide, Chapter 15 (ICA Archive 5, Art for Corpora

top_ratio_lot.title: Exhibition Guide, Chapter 15 (ICA Archive 5, Art for Corpora

artists.bio:
Jim Dine was born in Cincinnati, Ohio in 1935 and studied at the University of Cincinnati and the Boston Museum School before arriving in New York, where he became a central figure in the Happenings movement of the late 1950s alongside Claes Oldenburg and Allan Kaprow. His participation in this performative milieu fed into a painterly practice that combined Abstract Expressionist gesture with the attachment of real objects — tools, clothing, household items — to the canvas surface, creating works that occupied a position somewhere between painting, assemblage, and theatre prop.

Dine is particularly associated with his recurring motifs — hearts, robes, tools, Pinocchio — which he returns to obsessively across decades, treating them as vehicles for painterly and emotional investigation rather than stable symbols. His paintings of hearts, for example, range from delicate and lyrical to raw and aggressively gestural, the constant subject providing stability within which the full range of painterly possibility can be explored. His tool paintings of the early 1960s, in which real hardware tools are affixed to thickly painted surfaces, combine the physical directness of everyday objects with Abstract Expressionist painterly energy.

Dine has worked extensively as a printmaker and sculptor and has been represented by Pace Gallery for many years. His influence on subsequent generations of painters who have maintained emotionally invested subject matter within a broadly abstract painterly language is considerable.

famous_works.title: Five Feet of Colorful Tools
famous_works.notes: A painting from which thirty-two real tools hang across the lower portion of the canvas, directly integrating the hardware store with the gallery and the painterly surface.

famous_works.title: Heart for Anne Moses
famous_works.notes: An early heart painting that established the motif that has remained central to Dine's practice for six decades.

famous_works.title: The Robe series
famous_works.notes: A series of paintings of a bathrobe — Dine's own —  used as a surrogate self-portrait that explores themes of absence, presence, and the body.

lesser_known_works.title: Car Crash (Happening)
lesser_known_works.notes: An early Happening performance that established Dine's participation in the movement before his practice consolidated around painting.

lesser_known_works.title: Pinocchio paintings
